topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Web Mining

**Web Mining** is the automated process of discovering and extracting meaningful, non-trivial patterns, structures, knowledge, or useful information from web documents, web services, hyperlinks, server logs, and other web-based repositories using techniques drawn from **Data Mining**, **Machine Learning**, **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, and **Information Retrieval (IR)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Web Mining is formally defined as the application of **data mining techniques** to discover **hidden patterns** from the **World Wide Web (WWW)**. The web is treated as a vast, heterogeneous, semi-structured, distributed, and dynamically evolving database, and web mining provides the methodologies to transform this chaotic data ocean into structured, actionable intelligence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "Web Mining is the process of using data mining techniques to extract useful knowledge/information from web data, where at least one of the data sources is the World Wide Web."

The official three-fold taxonomy of Web Mining as per KTU syllabus:

| # | Sub-Field | Data Source Mined | Primary Goal |
|---|-----------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Web Content Mining (WCM)** | Text, images, audio, video, structured records inside web pages | Extract useful knowledge from the *contents* of web pages |
| 2 | **Web Structure Mining (WSM)** | Hyperlinks (inlinks, outlinks), DOM tree, site map | Discover useful knowledge from the *structure* of the web |
| 3 | **Web Usage Mining (WUM)** | Server logs, cookies, click-streams, user sessions | Discover useful knowledge from the *usage* patterns of users |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Gold Rush" Analogy** 💰⛏️
>
> Imagine the World Wide Web is a gigantic, ever-shifting gold mine.
> - The **rocks and ore** are web pages, hyperlinks, and click-logs.
> - The **gold nuggets** are the hidden patterns, user behaviors, and meaningful insights buried inside.
> - A **miner** is a web mining algorithm (like clustering, classification, or association rule mining).
> - The **tools** (pickaxe, pan, sieve) are techniques from data mining, NLP, and graph theory.
>
> Just as a traditional miner sifts tons of dirt to find a few grams of gold, web miners sift **petabytes of web data** to find **actionable knowledge** — like "what users want to buy," "how pages link to each other," or "what topics are trending."

## 1.3 Why Web Mining? — The Motivation

The web contains over **5 billion users** and **billions of pages**, generating massive volumes of:
- **Unstructured data** (free text in blogs, forums)
- **Semi-structured data** (HTML, XML)
- **Structured data** (tables, forms, databases behind web APIs)

Traditional data mining tools cannot directly handle this data because it is **heterogeneous**, **noisy**, **dynamic**, and **distributed**. This necessitates a dedicated discipline: **Web Mining**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Web Characteristics Driving Web Mining:**
> - **Volume:** Zettabytes of data
> - **Velocity:** Real-time updates, streaming logs
> - **Variety:** Text, images, video, links
> - **Veracity:** Noisy, incomplete, contradictory
> - **Value:** Hidden business and research value
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Web Mining — Deep Analysis

### Pillar 1: Web Content Mining (WCM)
Web Content Mining deals with the **discovery of useful information** from the *actual content* of web pages. The content may be:
- **Text** (news articles, blogs, product reviews)
- **Multimedia** (images, audio, video)
- **Structured records** (lists, tables, forms)
- **Semantic data** (ontologies, RDF, knowledge graphs)

**WCM Techniques:**
- **Information Retrieval (IR):** TF-IDF, BM25, vector space model.
- **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Tokenization, POS tagging, NER, sentiment analysis.
- **Text Mining:** Clustering, classification, topic modeling (LDA).
- **Image/Audio Mining:** Feature extraction, CNN-based classification.

### Pillar 2: Web Structure Mining (WSM)
Web Structure Mining discovers knowledge from the **interconnection/link topology** of the web. The famous saying: *"The web is not just a collection of documents; it is a graph."*

**WSM Analyzes:**
- **Hyperlinks** (incoming links, outgoing links, anchor text)
- **DOM tree** of a web page
- **Site maps** and **navigation graphs**
- **Social network graphs** (followers, friends)

**Famous WSM Algorithm:** **PageRank** — used by Google to rank web pages. A page is important if important pages point to it.

### Pillar 3: Web Usage Mining (WUM)
Web Usage Mining uncovers patterns in **user interaction data** with websites. It answers questions like:
- "What did users buy after viewing Product A?"
- "Which path leads to a purchase?"
- "What causes users to abandon a cart?"

**Data Sources for WUM:**
- **Web server access logs** (Apache/Nginx logs)
- **Proxy server logs**
- **Browser cookies** and **session data**
- **Application-level logs** (e-commerce transactions)

**WUM Process:** Preprocessing → Pattern Discovery → Pattern Analysis.

## 2.2 Web Mining vs. Data Mining vs. Text Mining vs. Information Retrieval

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison is a high-frequency KTU board question (asked almost every year).**

| Aspect | **Data Mining** | **Text Mining** | **Information Retrieval** | **Web Mining** |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| **Data Source** | Structured databases, data warehouses | Plain text documents, corpora | Indexed document collections | WWW (web pages, logs, links) |
| **Data Type** | Highly structured, relational | Unstructured natural language | Mostly unstructured text | Heterogeneous (text, links, logs) |
| **Goal** | Discover hidden patterns from *data* | Extract high-quality information from *text* | Retrieve *relevant* documents for a query | Discover useful knowledge from *web* data |
| **Techniques** | Clustering, classification, association rules | NLP, NER, sentiment, topic modeling | Indexing, ranking, query processing | All of the above + link analysis + log mining |
| **Data Scale** | Gigabytes to terabytes | Megabytes to gigabytes | Gigabytes | Petabytes to exabytes |
| **Nature of Data** | Static, clean, schema-defined | Static, noisy, linguistic | Semi-static, indexed | Dynamic, distributed, evolving |
| **Output** | Patterns, models, rules | Knowledge, summaries, entities | Ranked list of documents | Patterns, rankings, recommendations |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Web Mining

| Application Domain | Web Mining Sub-Field | Business/Engineering Use |
|--------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)** | WUM + WCM | Product recommendation engines ("Customers who bought X also bought Y") |
| **Search Engines (Google, Bing)** | WSM + WCM | Page ranking, search result relevance |
| **Social Media Analytics (Twitter, Facebook)** | WCM + WSM | Sentiment analysis, trend detection, influencer identification |
| **Cybersecurity** | WUM | Anomaly detection in user behavior, intrusion detection |
| **Digital Marketing / SEO** | WSM | Backlink analysis, page authority scoring |
| **Healthcare / Epidemiology** | WCM | Mining medical forums, disease outbreak detection (e.g., Google Flu Trends) |
| **News Aggregation** | WCM | Automatic news clustering, summarization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Modern search engines like Google use **all three** sub-fields simultaneously. PageRank uses **WSM**, snippet generation uses **WCM**, and personalized search uses **WUM**.

## 2.4 The Web Mining Process Flow (High-Yield for Board)

The standard KTU 2024 process model for web mining consists of **four sequential phases**:

1. **Resource Discovery (Data Collection):** Locate and retrieve relevant web documents, logs, or link structures from the WWW.
2. **Information Extraction (Preprocessing):** Clean, normalize, transform raw web data into a structured format (e.g., parse HTML, remove stop words, identify sessions).
3. **Pattern Discovery (Mining):** Apply machine learning and statistical algorithms (clustering, classification, association rules, PageRank).
4. **Pattern Analysis & Visualization (Validation):** Interpret, filter, and visualize the discovered patterns for the end user.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of Web Mining Taxonomy

Since this is an introductory topic, the "derivations" here take the form of **logical decomposition** and **algorithmic trace** of how the web mining taxonomy is constructed and how each sub-field operates in practice.

## 3.1 Logical Derivation of the Web Mining Taxonomy

We begin with the **Universe of Web Data**, denoted as $\mathcal{W}$.

$$
\mathcal{W} = \mathcal{D}_{\text{content}} \cup \mathcal{D}_{\text{structure}} \cup \mathcal{D}_{\text{usage}}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{D}_{\text{content}}$ = set of all textual, visual, and multimedia content inside web pages
- $\mathcal{D}_{\text{structure}}$ = set of all hyperlinks, DOM trees, and site maps
- $\mathcal{D}_{\text{usage}}$ = set of all server logs, click-streams, and user interaction data

These three sets are **mutually non-exclusive but collectively exhaustive** of the meaningful data on the web.

From this formal decomposition, we derive the **three branches of Web Mining**:

$$
\text{Web Mining} \;=\; f(\mathcal{D}_{\text{content}}) \;\cup\; g(\mathcal{D}_{\text{structure}}) \;\cup\; h(\mathcal{D}_{\text{usage}})
$$

Where:
- $f$ = Web Content Mining function (text/NLP/image analysis)
- $g$ = Web Structure Mining function (graph theory, link analysis)
- $h$ = Web Usage Mining function (log mining, pattern discovery)

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Any web mining task can be uniquely classified by **which component of $\mathcal{W}$** it targets. This is the rigorous mathematical justification for the standard 3-way taxonomy that KTU expects students to reproduce.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Tracing a Web Usage Mining Pipeline

Consider an e-commerce website with a single web server producing Apache access logs. A student must be able to trace how a raw log line becomes a useful pattern.

**Step 1 — Raw Web Server Log Entry (Data Source $\mathcal{D}_{\text{usage}}$):**

A single log line from `access.log` looks like:

$$
\text{127.0.0.1} \;-\; \text{frank} \;[\text{10/Oct/2024:13:55:36} \;-\text{0700}] \; \text{"GET /product/phone42 HTTP/1.1"} \; 200 \; 1024
$$

This single record contains the **IP address**, **user-id**, **timestamp**, **HTTP request method and URL**, **response status**, and **bytes transferred**.

**Step 2 — Data Cleaning (Preprocessing):**

Remove failed requests, bot traffic, and CSS/image fetches. The cleaned record becomes a structured tuple:

$$
\text{Record} = (\text{userID},\; \text{timestamp},\; \text{URL},\; \text{status})
$$

**Step 3 — Session Identification:**

Group all records belonging to a single user visit. A session $\mathcal{S}_i$ is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{S}_i = \{ r_1, r_2, r_3, \ldots, r_n \} \quad \text{where} \quad \forall r_k \in \mathcal{S}_i,\; \text{userID}(r_k) = \text{userID}(r_1) \;\land\; t(r_{k+1}) - t(r_k) \leq \theta
$$

Where $\theta$ is the **session timeout threshold** (typically **30 minutes** as per industry standard). If the gap between two consecutive requests exceeds $\theta$, a new session begins.

**Step 4 — Pattern Discovery via Association Rule Mining:**

Apply the **Apriori algorithm** on sessionized click-streams to find rules of the form:

$$
X \;\Rightarrow\; Y \quad \text{where} \quad X, Y \subseteq \text{URLs},\; X \cap Y = \emptyset
$$

For example, the discovered rule might be:

$$
\{\text{/product/phone}\} \;\Rightarrow\; \{\text{/product/case}\} \quad \text{with Support} = 0.15,\; \text{Confidence} = 0.72
$$

**Step 5 — Pattern Analysis (Business Interpretation):**

A confidence of **0.72** means **72% of users who view a phone also view a phone case**. The e-commerce manager uses this to bundle products in the recommendation widget.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain web usage mining with an example," the KTU board expects this 5-step trace — raw log → cleaning → sessionization → pattern discovery → business interpretation. Memorize this pipeline.

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis — When to Use Which Sub-Field

| Problem Statement | Recommended Sub-Field | Technique Used |
|-------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| "Find trending topics on Twitter" | **WCM** | Topic modeling (LDA), clustering |
| "Rank web pages by importance" | **WSM** | PageRank, HITS algorithm |
| "Detect fraudulent login patterns" | **WUM** | Anomaly detection on click-streams |
| "Identify influential users in a network" | **WSM** | Social network analysis, centrality |
| "Extract product prices from shopping sites" | **WCM** | Wrapper induction, web scraping + NER |
| "Recommend movies on Netflix" | **WUM + WCM** | Collaborative filtering + content similarity |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

### **Question 1: Define Web Mining. List and briefly explain its three main categories.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Web Mining is the application of data mining techniques to discover useful, previously unknown, and potentially valuable information from web data, where at least one of the data sources is the World Wide Web.

**Three Categories (2 Marks):**
1. **Web Content Mining (WCM):** Extracts useful knowledge from the textual, visual, and multimedia *content* of web pages using techniques from information retrieval and natural language processing.
2. **Web Structure Mining (WSM):** Discovers patterns from the *link topology* of the web using graph-based algorithms such as PageRank and HITS.
3. **Web Usage Mining (WUM):** Mines user *behavior patterns* from server logs, cookies, and click-streams to understand navigation, preferences, and intent.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Web Mining and Data Mining.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Data Mining** | **Web Mining** |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Data Source** | Structured databases, data warehouses | Heterogeneous web data (HTML, logs, links) |
| **Data Nature** | Static, clean, schema-bound | Dynamic, distributed, semi-structured, noisy |
| **Scale** | Gigabytes to terabytes | Petabytes and beyond |
| **Techniques** | Traditional ML and statistics | Data mining + IR + NLP + graph theory |
| **Challenges** | Mostly technical | Includes privacy, legal, ethical, and technical issues |
| **Example** | Mining customer purchase records from a relational DB | Mining click-streams of users on an e-commerce site |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Pattern, Module 1, Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Question on Introduction to Web Mining**

**Q.A (a)** Define Web Mining in detail. Explain the three categories of Web Mining with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Web Mining is the process of applying data mining, machine learning, information retrieval, and natural language processing techniques to extract meaningful, non-trivial, and previously unknown patterns from the vast, heterogeneous, and dynamically evolving data on the World Wide Web.

**Three Categories (5 Marks):**

**(i) Web Content Mining (WCM):** It targets the actual *content* of web pages — text, images, audio, video, and structured records. The goal is to discover, extract, and organize useful knowledge from the inside of pages. For example, a news aggregator uses WCM to cluster news articles on the same topic from different sources. Techniques used include TF-IDF, NLP, text clustering (k-means), and topic modeling (LDA).

**(ii) Web Structure Mining (WSM):** It mines the *link structure* connecting web pages. The web is modeled as a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of pages and $E$ is the set of hyperlinks. By analyzing inlinks, outlinks, and anchor text, WSM identifies authoritative pages. The classic example is **Google's PageRank algorithm**, which ranks pages by the recursive principle: *"a page is important if important pages link to it."*

**(iii) Web Usage Mining (WUM):** It mines *user interaction data* captured in web server access logs, proxy logs, cookies, and application logs. After preprocessing (cleaning, sessionization), algorithms like association rule mining and sequential pattern mining are applied. For example, an e-commerce site discovers that 70% of users who buy a laptop also buy a laptop bag within the same session — a classic association rule used for cross-selling.

---

**Q.A (b)** Explain the standard process of Web Mining with a neat block diagram. List at least four real-world applications of Web Mining across different industries. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**The Web Mining Process (4 Marks):**

The standard four-phase process is:

1. **Resource Discovery:** Identify and collect the relevant web data — fetching HTML pages via crawlers, collecting server logs, or extracting hyperlink graphs.
2. **Information Extraction & Preprocessing:** Parse HTML, remove stop words and HTML tags, filter bot traffic, perform sessionization, and convert raw heterogeneous data into a structured, clean format suitable for mining algorithms.
3. **Pattern Discovery:** Apply data mining algorithms (clustering, classification, association rules, PageRank) to the cleaned data to extract hidden patterns.
4. **Pattern Analysis & Knowledge Delivery:** Interpret, visualize, and present the discovered patterns to the end user via dashboards, recommendations, or reports.

**Real-World Applications (3 Marks):**

1. **E-Commerce:** Amazon uses WUM to build "Frequently Bought Together" recommendation engines.
2. **Search Engines:** Google uses WSM (PageRank) combined with WCM (snippet generation) to deliver ranked search results.
3. **Healthcare:** WCM of medical forums and search queries is used for early detection of disease outbreaks (e.g., Google Flu Trends).
4. **Cybersecurity:** WUM of login logs detects anomalous access patterns indicating intrusion or credential theft.
5. **Digital Marketing:** WSM via backlink analysis powers SEO tools like Ahrefs and Moz.
6. **Social Media:** WCM performs sentiment analysis on Twitter to gauge public opinion on brands and policies.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Comprehensive Question**

**Q.B (a)** Compare and contrast Web Mining, Data Mining, Text Mining, and Information Retrieval in terms of data source, techniques used, and end goal. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | **Data Mining** | **Text Mining** | **Information Retrieval** | **Web Mining** |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| **Data Source** | Structured databases, warehouses | Plain text documents and corpora | Indexed document collections | WWW — pages, links, logs |
| **Data Type** | Relational, clean, schema-defined | Unstructured natural language | Semi-structured indexed text | Heterogeneous, semi/unstructured, dynamic |
| **Primary Goal** | Discover hidden patterns and predictive models from *data* | Extract high-quality information, entities, and sentiments from *text* | Retrieve *relevant* documents matching a user query | Discover *useful knowledge* from the entire web ecosystem |
| **Core Techniques** | Clustering, classification, association rules, regression | NLP, NER, topic modeling, sentiment analysis | Indexing (inverted index), ranking (TF-IDF, BM25) | All data mining + IR + NLP + graph theory |
| **Data Scale** | GB to TB | MB to GB | GB | PB to ZB |
| **Example** | Customer churn prediction from CRM DB | Extracting drug names from medical papers | Google Scholar returning research papers | Amazon's "Customers who bought X also bought Y" |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Web Mining is the most comprehensive discipline — it is essentially a *super-set* that subsumes data mining (on web data), text mining (on web text), and information retrieval (on web documents), while adding unique techniques like link analysis and log mining.

---

**Q.B (b)** Discuss the major challenges and issues in Web Mining. Why is traditional data mining insufficient for web data? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Why Traditional Data Mining is Insufficient (3 Marks):**

Traditional data mining algorithms were designed for:
- **Structured, clean, schema-bound** data stored in relational databases.
- **Static datasets** that fit in memory or warehouses.

However, web data is fundamentally different:
- It is **semi-structured or unstructured** (HTML, free text).
- It is **distributed** across billions of servers worldwide.
- It is **dynamic** — pages change every second.
- It is **massive** in scale (petabytes).
- It is **noisy** with ads, spam, broken links, and bots.

Hence, traditional data mining tools fail, and specialized web mining techniques are required.

**Major Challenges in Web Mining (4 Marks):**

1. **Heterogeneity and Volume:** The web contains text, images, video, structured tables, and links all mixed together. Mining meaningful patterns across such varied formats is extremely difficult at petabyte scale.

2. **Dynamic and Evolving Nature:** Web pages change constantly. A pattern discovered today may be invalid tomorrow. Mining systems must handle concept drift and incremental updates.

3. **Noise and Irrelevant Data:** A significant portion of the web is spam, duplicate content, advertisements, and broken pages. Filtering these from genuine content is non-trivial.

4. **Privacy and Ethical Issues:** Web usage mining involves tracking user behavior via cookies and logs, raising serious concerns about user privacy, consent, and compliance with regulations like GDPR.

5. **Quality and Trustworthiness:** User-generated content (reviews, blogs, social media) is often unreliable, biased, or malicious, making knowledge extraction error-prone.

6. **Legal Issues:** Web scraping, copyright, and data ownership pose ongoing legal grey areas.

7. **Semantic Ambiguity:** The same word may mean different things on different pages (polysemy), and different words may mean the same thing (synonymy), making semantic understanding difficult.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Web Mining with Data Mining:** Students often write a generic data mining answer. Always explicitly tie the answer to **web-specific** data sources (URLs, hyperlinks, server logs, cookies, HTML).
> 2. **Forgetting one of the three categories:** Listing only 2 of {WCM, WSM, WUM} loses 1–2 marks. Always draw the three-way taxonomy diagram.
> 3. **Not providing examples:** Every definition must be backed by at least one real-world example (Google, Amazon, Twitter) to score full marks.
> 4. **Memorizing without understanding:** If the question asks "why is web mining needed?" and the student only writes the definition, marks are forfeited. Always answer the **"why"**.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** In 14-mark questions, a missing block diagram of the web mining process costs 1–2 marks. Always include a labeled diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These:**

- [x] **Definition:** Web Mining = Data Mining techniques + Web data (at least one source is WWW).
- [x] **Three Categories:** **WCM** (content), **WSM** (structure/links), **WUM** (usage/logs).
- [x] **Web Content Mining** = mines what is *inside* the page (text, images, video). Techniques: IR, NLP, text mining.
- [x] **Web Structure Mining** = mines *hyperlinks* and DOM trees. Key algorithm: **PageRank**.
- [x] **Web Usage Mining** = mines *server logs and click-streams*. Key algorithms: Association rule mining (Apriori), clustering.
- [x] **Standard Web Mining Process:** Resource Discovery → Information Extraction/Preprocessing → Pattern Discovery → Pattern Analysis.
- [x] **Differences Table:** Data Mining (structured, static) vs Web Mining (heterogeneous, dynamic, distributed).
- [x] **Real-world examples:** Google PageRank = WSM, Amazon recommendations = WUM, Twitter sentiment = WCM.
- [x] **Challenges:** Volume, heterogeneity, dynamism, noise, privacy (GDPR), semantic ambiguity, legal issues.
- [x] **Golden Mantra for Board Exams:** *Always pair every concept with one real-world example and one application.*
- [x] **High-frequency board question:** "Differentiate Web Mining from Data Mining" — appears in nearly every KTU exam cycle.
- [x] **PageRank equation (intuition):** $PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \rightarrow p} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ — *a page's rank depends on the rank of pages linking to it, divided by their out-degree.*
- [x] **Session timeout threshold** in WUM = **30 minutes** (industry standard, KTU expects this number).
- [x] **Web mining is a SUPERSET** of data mining, text mining, and information retrieval.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Web Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Web Mining** is the application of data mining techniques, machine learning algorithms, and statistical modeling methodologies to automatically discover, extract, analyze, and evaluate useful patterns, latent structures, and actionable knowledge from heterogeneous web documents, web services, hyperlinks, and server logs. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECDT635), Web Mining is formally classified as a sub-discipline of **Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD)** applied over the **World Wide Web (WWW)** ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECDT635 - Module 1):**
> Module 1 establishes the foundational taxonomy of Web Mining. Students must master the **three primary branches** — Web Content Mining (WCM), Web Structure Mining (WSM), and Web Usage Mining (WUM) — along with the challenges of the dynamic, semi-structured, and distributed nature of the web.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine the **World Wide Web** as a colossal, ever-expanding **public library** with **no librarian** and **no cataloging system**. Every day, billions of new "books" (web pages, blogs, videos, transaction logs) are dumped onto the shelves with no standard organization.

- **Data Mining** is like teaching a robot to find patterns in a *single, well-organized database* (e.g., a hospital's patient records).
- **Web Mining** is like teaching that same robot to walk into the chaotic library and still find patterns — across millions of inconsistently written pages, broken hyperlinks, user click trails, and social media chatter.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Web Mining:**
> Traditional search engines only *retrieve* documents based on keyword matching. Web Mining goes a step further — it *understands*, *clusters*, *recommends*, and *predicts* based on patterns hidden in the web's content, structure, and human interaction logs.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Web Mining

| Pillar | Mining Target | Real-World Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Web Content Mining (WCM)** | Text, images, audio, video, structured records inside web pages | Reading every book in the library to extract key insights |
| **Web Structure Mining (WSM)** | Hyperlinks connecting pages (in-links, out-links, PageRank graph) | Mapping the library's *physical layout* and *cross-references* |
| **Web Usage Mining (WUM)** | Server access logs, clickstreams, cookies, user sessions | Analyzing *visitor footprints* to redesign the library for better navigation |

## 1.4 Key Distinctions Students Must Know

- **Web Mining ≠ Information Retrieval (IR):** IR is reactive (user query → documents). Web Mining is proactive (data → patterns → knowledge).
- **Web Mining ≠ Text Mining:** Text Mining operates on *closed* document collections. Web Mining deals with *hypertext, hyperlinks, and logs* in addition to text.
- **Web Mining ≠ Data Mining:** Data Mining assumes clean, structured data. Web Mining must *first* deal with noise, heterogeneity, and semi-structured HTML/XML data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Web Mining

### 2.1.1 Web Content Mining (WCM)
WCM extracts useful information from the *contents* of web pages. The content can be:
- **Unstructured** (free text — news articles, blogs)
- **Semi-structured** (HTML tables, XML, JSON)
- **Structured** (databases exposed via the web — e.g., e-commerce catalogs)

**Sub-categories:**
1. **Agent-Based Approach:** Uses intelligent software agents (e.g., spiders, crawlers, wrappers) to perform automated information filtering and categorization.
2. **Database Approach:** Treats the web as a collection of interconnected databases and uses query languages (e.g., WebSQL, WebOQL) to extract structured data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common WCM Techniques:** Natural Language Processing (NLP), Named Entity Recognition (NER), sentiment analysis, topic modeling (LDA), TF-IDF weighting, and document clustering.

### 2.1.2 Web Structure Mining (WSM)
WSM discovers the *underlying topological structure* of the web by analyzing the hyperlink graph. The web is modeled as a directed graph $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ is the set of pages and $E$ is the set of hyperlinks.

**Two main structural artifacts:**
- **Intra-page Structure:** The HTML/XML tree structure within a single document (DOM tree analysis).
- **Inter-page Structure:** The hyperlink network connecting different pages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hyperlink Graph:** The directed graph $G(V, E)$ where each node $v \in V$ represents a webpage, and each directed edge $(u \rightarrow v) \in E$ represents a hyperlink from page $u$ to page $v$. This graph is the foundation of Google's **PageRank** algorithm.

### 2.1.3 Web Usage Mining (WUM)
WUM analyzes the *behavioral patterns* of users interacting with web servers. The input is typically derived from:
- **Server Access Logs** (Apache/Nginx logs)
- **Cookie Files**
- **User Sessions & Clickstreams**
- **Proxy Server Logs**

**The WUM Pipeline (4 stages):**
1. **Data Collection** — gathering raw logs from servers, proxies, and clients.
2. **Data Preprocessing** — cleaning, user identification, session identification, transaction identification.
3. **Pattern Discovery** — applying statistical analysis, clustering, classification, association rule mining (e.g., Apriori).
4. **Pattern Analysis** — filtering interesting patterns using OLAP tools, visualization, and query mechanisms.

## 2.2 Challenges in Web Mining

The web poses unique challenges not found in traditional data mining:

| Challenge | Description | Mitigation Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scale** | Billions of pages (over **50 billion indexed pages** on Google as of 2024) | Distributed computing (Hadoop, Spark, MapReduce) |
| **Dynamic Nature** | Web content changes continuously (news, prices, tweets) | Periodic re-crawling, incremental indexing |
| **Heterogeneity** | Text, images, videos, multilingual content, varied formats | NLP, multimedia mining, multi-modal learning |
| **Semi-structured Data** | HTML/XML lacks rigid schema | Wrappers, schema mapping, ontology-based extraction |
| **Noise & Redundancy** | Duplicate pages, spam, broken links | Deduplication, PageRank-based quality scoring |
| **Privacy & Ethics** | User logs contain sensitive behavioral data | Anonymization, GDPR compliance, consent-based logging |
| **Deep Web** | Content hidden behind forms, databases (~**96%** of web data) | Focused crawling, form analysis, integration with search APIs |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{N}{df_t}\right)$ | Importance of term $t$ in document $d$; $N$ = total docs, $df_t$ = docs containing $t$ |
| 2 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(A,B) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert\vert A\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert B\vert\vert}$ | Measures document similarity in vector space |
| 3 | **PageRank (Damping)** | $PR(p) = \dfrac{(1-d)}{N} + d \sum_{q \rightarrow p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ | Ranks page $p$; $d$ = damping factor (typically **0.85**), $L(q)$ = out-links of $q$ |
| 4 | **HITS Authority Score** | $a(p) = \sum_{q \rightarrow p} h(q)$ | Authority of page $p$ as sum of hub scores pointing to it |
| 5 | **HITS Hub Score** | $h(p) = \sum_{p \rightarrow q} a(q)$ | Hub value of page $p$ as sum of authorities it links to |
| 6 | **Support (Association Rules)** | $\text{sup}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\vert\{T : X \cup Y \subseteq T\}\vert}{\vert D\vert}$ | Frequency of itemset $X \cup Y$ in transaction DB $D$ |
| 7 | **Confidence** | $\text{conf}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\text{sup}(X \cup Y)}{\text{sup}(X)}$ | Strength of implication rule |
| 8 | **Support Vector Machine (SVM) Margin** | $\text{margin} = \dfrac{2}{\vert\vert w\vert\vert}$ | Maximized in classification; minimizes $\vert\vert w\vert\vert^2$ |
| 9 | **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** | $CTR = \dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100\%$ | Measures ad or recommendation effectiveness in WUM |
| 10 | **Entropy (Information Gain)** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2(p_i)$ | Measures impurity in decision tree splits for WUM classification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of These Formulas:**
> - **PageRank** powers Google's core search ranking.
> - **TF-IDF + Cosine Similarity** drives document clustering, plagiarism detection, and recommendation systems.
> - **Association Rule Mining (Apriori)** is the backbone of e-commerce recommendation engines like Amazon's "Customers who bought X also bought Y."
> - **CTR** and **Entropy** are critical for A/B testing and personalization in WUM.

## 2.4 Relationship Between Web Mining and Adjacent Disciplines

Web Mining sits at the intersection of several fields. The KTU examiner often asks students to differentiate these:

- **Information Retrieval (IR):** IR focuses on document *retrieval* via queries. Web Mining *discovers patterns* autonomously.
- **Information Extraction (IE):** IE extracts structured facts (e.g., entities, relations) from unstructured text — a sub-task of WCM.
- **Machine Learning (ML):** ML provides the algorithmic toolbox (classification, clustering, regression) that Web Mining applies on web-scale data.
- **Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD):** Web Mining is KDD applied to the web.
- **Social Network Analysis (SNA):** WSM overlaps with SNA in analyzing hyperlink graphs and social media networks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: PageRank Algorithm

PageRank is the most frequently tested formula in KTU Web Mining papers. Here is the complete, exhaustive derivation.

### Step 1 — Problem Setup

Consider a directed web graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ is the set of all web pages: $\vert V\vert = N$
- $E$ is the set of directed hyperlinks between pages.

A user (the "random surfer") clicks hyperlinks uniformly at random. We wish to find the **stationary probability distribution** that the surfer is on each page.

### Step 2 — The Basic (Naive) PageRank Equation

If page $u$ has outgoing links to pages $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k$, the surfer picks each with probability $\dfrac{1}{k}$. So the PageRank of page $u$ is:

$$
PR(u) = \sum_{v \rightarrow u} \dfrac{PR(v)}{L(v)}
$$

Where $L(v)$ is the number of outgoing links from page $v$.

> **Logic Row:** Page $u$ receives rank from every page $v$ that links to it, weighted by how *evenly* $v$ distributes its rank across its outgoing links.

### Step 3 — The Dangling Node Problem

If a page $v$ has **no outgoing links** (a "dangling node" or sink), then $L(v) = 0$, causing division by zero. To resolve this, we model the surfer as jumping to a random page when stuck.

### Step 4 — The Random Jump (Teleportation) Fix

We introduce a **damping factor** $d$ (typically **0.85**, chosen empirically by Google founders Brin & Page). With probability $d$, the surfer follows a hyperlink; with probability $(1-d)$, they teleport to a random page:

$$
PR(p) = \dfrac{(1-d)}{N} + d \sum_{q \rightarrow p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$

> **Logic Row:** The term $\dfrac{(1-d)}{N}$ is the "teleportation" probability distributed uniformly over $N$ pages. The second term is the damped rank contribution from linking pages.

### Step 5 — Matrix Formulation

Let $\mathbf{M}$ be the **stochastic transition matrix** of size $N \times N$, where $M_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{L(j)}$ if $j \rightarrow i$, else $0$.

Let $\mathbf{r}$ be the PageRank vector. The PageRank equation becomes:

$$
\mathbf{r} = d \cdot \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{r} + \dfrac{(1-d)}{N} \cdot \mathbf{1}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\mathbf{r} = \left(d \cdot \mathbf{M} + \dfrac{(1-d)}{N} \cdot \mathbf{E}\right) \mathbf{r}
$$

Where $\mathbf{E}$ is the $N \times N$ matrix of all ones.

### Step 6 — Power Iteration Solution

PageRank is computed iteratively until convergence:

$$
\mathbf{r}^{(k+1)} = d \cdot \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{r}^{(k)} + \dfrac{(1-d)}{N} \cdot \mathbf{1}
$$

**Stopping criterion:** $\vert\vert \mathbf{r}^{(k+1)} - \mathbf{r}^{(k)} \vert\vert_1 < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$).

> **Logic Row:** Power iteration converges because the matrix $\left(d\mathbf{M} + \dfrac{(1-d)}{N}\mathbf{E}\right)$ is stochastic, primitive, and irreducible — guaranteeing a unique stationary distribution by the **Perron-Frobenius Theorem**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: TF-IDF Weighting

TF-IDF is essential for WCM. Here's the derivation.

### Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF)

Raw count of term $t$ in document $d$:

$$
tf(t, d) = f_{t,d}
$$

**Normalized form** (used in practice):

$$
tf_{\text{norm}}(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\max_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}
$$

### Step 2 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

IDF measures how *rare* a term is across the corpus:

$$
idf(t) = \log\left(\dfrac{N}{df_t}\right)
$$

Where $N$ = total documents, $df_t$ = number of documents containing $t$.

> **Logic Row:** Common words ("the", "is") appear in nearly every document, so $df_t \approx N$, making $idf \approx 0$. Rare words get high IDF.

### Step 3 — Combined TF-IDF

$$
w(t, d) = tf_{\text{norm}}(t, d) \cdot idf(t)
$$

> **Logic Row:** This product rewards terms that are *frequent* in a specific document but *rare* across the entire corpus — the hallmark of *discriminative* terms.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: WUM Preprocessing Steps (High-Yield for KTU)

| Stage | Task | Input → Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Data Cleaning** | Remove irrelevant entries (robots.txt accesses, failed HTTP status codes $\geq 400$, image/CSS/JS requests) | Raw log $\rightarrow$ Cleaned log |
| **2. User Identification** | Distinguish unique users via IP + Browser + OS combination | Cleaned log $\rightarrow$ User-tagged sessions |
| **3. Session Identification** | Segment each user's access log into sessions (e.g., 30-min inactivity timeout) | User log $\rightarrow$ Sessions |
| **4. Path Completion** | Fill in missing pages via referrer logs and site topology | Session log $\rightarrow$ Complete clickstream |
| **5. Transaction Identification** | Group pages by semantic meaning (e.g., product views, cart actions) | Complete clickstream $\rightarrow$ Transactions |
| **6. Pattern Discovery** | Apply Apriori, clustering, classification on transactions | Transactions $\rightarrow$ Frequent patterns |
| **7. Pattern Analysis** | OLAP queries, visualization, usability insights | Patterns $\rightarrow$ Business rules |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
graph TD
```


> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU Students:**
> The diagrams above map the **flow of data** from raw inputs (crawlers, logs, hyperlink graphs) through layered processing (parsing, feature extraction, session reconstruction) to algorithmic mining (TF-IDF, PageRank, Apriori) and finally to actionable outputs (knowledge bases, recommendation APIs, analytics dashboards). In the KTU exam, students may be asked to "draw the Web Mining architecture" — the layered Block-Level Architecture (Section 4.3) is the recommended template.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Web Mining. List its three main categories. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Web Mining is the process of applying data mining, machine learning, and statistical techniques to extract useful patterns, knowledge, and hidden structures from web documents, hyperlinks, and user interaction logs.

**Three Categories (1 Mark):**
1. **Web Content Mining (WCM)** — extracts knowledge from page contents.
2. **Web Structure Mining (WSM)** — analyzes hyperlink topology.
3. **Web Usage Mining (WUM)** — mines user behavior from logs.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between Information Retrieval (IR) and Web Mining. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Information Retrieval | Web Mining |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Goal** | Retrieve relevant documents for a query | Discover new, hidden patterns and knowledge |
| **Nature** | Reactive (query-driven) | Proactive (data-driven) |
| **Input** | User query + document collection | Web content, structure, usage logs |
| **Output** | Ranked list of documents | Patterns, models, predictions, recommendations |
| **Example** | Google search engine | Amazon recommendation, Google News clustering |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q3 (a). Explain the three categories of Web Mining in detail with suitable examples. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Web Content Mining — WCM (2.5 Marks):**
WCM extracts useful information from the textual and multimedia content of web pages.
- **Text Mining Examples:** News article summarization, sentiment analysis of tweets, topic modeling with LDA.
- **Multimedia Mining Examples:** Image tagging, video recommendation on YouTube, audio fingerprinting.
- **Techniques Used:** TF-IDF, NLP pipelines, Named Entity Recognition, document clustering (K-Means, hierarchical clustering).

**(ii) Web Structure Mining — WSM (2.5 Marks):**
WSM focuses on the hyperlink graph connecting web pages.
- **Hyperlink Analysis:** Models the web as a directed graph $G(V, E)$.
- **Algorithms:** PageRank (used by Google), HITS (Hyperlink-Induced Topic Search).
- **Example:** Determining the most authoritative page on a topic like "diabetes treatment" by analyzing which pages receive the most citations from reputable medical sources.

**(iii) Web Usage Mining — WUM (2 Marks):**
WUM analyzes user interaction logs to understand behavior.
- **Data Sources:** Server access logs, cookies, proxy logs, clickstreams.
- **Applications:** E-commerce personalization (Amazon's "Recommended for You"), website usability optimization, targeted advertising.
- **Example:** Mining shopping cart transactions to find that customers who buy diapers also frequently buy beer (the famous "Beer and Diapers" pattern).

---

#### Q3 (b). Describe the PageRank algorithm with its mathematical formulation. Explain the role of the damping factor $d$. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Conceptual Overview (2 Marks):**
PageRank, developed by Larry Page and Sergey Brin (founders of Google), assigns a numerical weight to each webpage indicating its relative importance within the hyperlink graph.

**Mathematical Formulation (3 Marks):**

$$
PR(p) = \dfrac{(1-d)}{N} + d \sum_{q \rightarrow p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$

Where:
- $PR(p)$ = PageRank score of page $p$
- $d$ = damping factor (typically **0.85**)
- $N$ = total number of pages in the graph
- $q \rightarrow p$ = set of all pages $q$ that link to $p$
- $L(q)$ = number of outgoing links from page $q$

**Role of the Damping Factor (2 Marks):**
1. **Prevents Rank Sinks:** Without damping, pages in a closed loop of links would accumulate all the rank, starving other pages.
2. **Models User Behavior:** Represents the probability that a random surfer clicks a link ($d = 0.85$) versus teleporting to a random page ($1 - d = 0.15$).
3. **Guarantees Convergence:** Ensures the transition matrix is stochastic and primitive, satisfying the **Perron-Frobenius Theorem** and guaranteeing a unique stationary distribution.

**Computation:** PageRank is computed via **power iteration** until the rank vector stabilizes within tolerance $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Q4 (a). With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Web Usage Mining system. List the preprocessing steps in detail. (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture Diagram (2 Marks):**
*(Student should reproduce the WUM pipeline diagram from Section 4.2.)*

**Four Phases of WUM Architecture (5 Marks):**

1. **Data Collection Phase:** Sources include server access logs (Apache, IIS), proxy server logs, client-side cookies, and application-level event logs. Tools: AWStats, Webalizer.

2. **Data Preprocessing Phase:** Critical stage with the following steps:
   - **Data Cleaning:** Remove failed requests, multimedia accesses (images, CSS, JS).
   - **User Identification:** Use combination of IP address + User-Agent.
   - **Session Identification:** Default timeout of **30 minutes** of inactivity.
   - **Path Completion:** Reconstruct missing pages using referrer field.
   - **Transaction Identification:** Group semantically related pages (e.g., "view product" → "add to cart" → "checkout").

3. **Pattern Discovery Phase:** Apply mining algorithms:
   - Statistical analysis (mean, median session duration).
   - Clustering (K-Means on user sessions).
   - Classification (decision trees to predict buyer vs. browser).
   - Association Rule Mining (Apriori algorithm).

4. **Pattern Analysis Phase:** Use OLAP tools, visualization dashboards, and SQL-based queries to extract business insights.

---

#### Q4 (b). Explain the TF-IDF weighting scheme. How is it used in document similarity computation for Web Content Mining? (7 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of TF-IDF (3 Marks):**
TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency) is a statistical measure used to evaluate how important a word is to a document in a collection or corpus.

$$
TF(t, d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\max_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}
$$

$$
IDF(t) = \log\left(\dfrac{N}{df_t}\right)
$$

$$
TF\text{-}IDF(t, d) = TF(t, d) \times IDF(t)
$$

Where $f_{t,d}$ = frequency of term $t$ in document $d$, $N$ = total documents, $df_t$ = number of documents containing $t$.

**Document Similarity Computation (4 Marks):**
1. Each document $d$ is represented as a vector $\vec{d} = (w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n)$ where $w_i$ is the TF-IDF weight of term $i$.
2. Cosine similarity between two documents $A$ and $B$:

$$
\text{sim}(A, B) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert\vert \vec{A}\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert \vec{B}\vert\vert}
$$

> **Logic Row:** Documents with similar TF-IDF vectors (high cosine similarity) are semantically related. This is the foundation of **document clustering**, **plagiarism detection**, and **content-based recommendation** in WCM.

3. **Example Application:** Grouping news articles on the same topic, detecting duplicate web pages, and building personalized news feeds.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Web Mining with Data Mining:** Many students forget to mention the *web-specific* challenges (heterogeneity, hyperlinks, logs). Always mention these explicitly for full marks.
> 2. **PageRank Formula Errors:** Students often write the formula without the damping term $\dfrac{(1-d)}{N}$. This will lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping Preprocessing:** In WUM questions, examiners allocate 3–4 marks purely for preprocessing steps. Skipping these guarantees significant mark loss.
> 4. **Wrong TF-IDF Application:** TF-IDF must be explained with both TF *and* IDF components separately. Writing only the final formula scores partial credit.
> 5. **No Diagrams:** For 7-mark questions, a well-labeled diagram fetches at least 1–2 marks. Always draw the WUM pipeline or Web Mining taxonomy.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Web Mining = KDD applied to the WWW.** It has **three branches**: WCM, WSM, WUM.
- ✅ **WCM** mines *content* (text, images, videos) — uses NLP, TF-IDF, LDA.
- ✅ **WSM** mines *hyperlink structure* — uses PageRank, HITS, graph theory.
- ✅ **WUM** mines *user behavior logs* — uses Apriori, clustering, classification.
- ✅ **PageRank Formula:** $PR(p) = \dfrac{(1-d)}{N} + d \sum_{q \rightarrow p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ — damping factor $d = 0.85$.
- ✅ **HITS** uses two scores: **Authority** (sum of in-linking hubs) and **Hub** (sum of out-linking authorities).
- ✅ **TF-IDF:** High TF + High IDF = highly discriminative term.
- ✅ **Cosine Similarity** is the standard measure for document similarity in WCM.
- ✅ **WUM Pipeline:** Collection → Preprocessing → Pattern Discovery → Pattern Analysis.
- ✅ **WUM Preprocessing Steps:** Cleaning → User ID → Session ID → Path Completion → Transaction ID.
- ✅ **Apriori Algorithm** uses **Support** and **Confidence** metrics for association rule mining.
- ✅ **Web Challenges:** Scale, dynamicity, heterogeneity, semi-structured data, noise, deep web, privacy.
- ✅ **Web Mining ≠ Information Retrieval** — IR retrieves, Web Mining discovers patterns.
- ✅ **Real-World Applications:** Google Search (PageRank), Amazon Recommendations (Association Rules), YouTube (Multimedia Mining), Netflix (Clickstream Mining).
- ✅ **KTU Hot Topics for 2024 Scheme:** PageRank derivation, HITS vs PageRank comparison, WUM preprocessing pipeline, TF-IDF with cosine similarity, challenges of web mining.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
