topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Theoretical Background # 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Web Mining > [!IMPORTANT] > **Web Mining** is the automated process of discovering and extracting meaningful, previously unknown, and potentially useful information and knowledge from Web documents, services, hyperlinks, server logs, and other web-based artifacts. It is the application of data mining techniques to the World Wide Web (WWW) at a massive, heterogeneous, and dynamic scale. In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Web Mining is positioned as a confluence of three parent disciplines: $$\text{Web Mining} = f(\text{Data Mining},\ \text{Information Retrieval},\ \text{Machine Learning})$$ Each of these contributes a critical capability: - **Data Mining** contributes pattern discovery algorithms. - **Information Retrieval (IR)** contributes document indexing, ranking, and relevance scoring. - **Machine Learning (ML)** contributes adaptive models that learn from user behaviour and content patterns. ## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — "The Library of Babel" 🏛️ > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine the internet is a giant, ever-expanding, unorganized library where: > - 📚 **Books are thrown in randomly** (unstructured HTML data) > - 🔗 **Cross-references exist but are tangled** (hyperlinks) > - 👣 **Footprints of past visitors are left in dust** (web server logs) > - 🧑‍🤝‍🧑 **Every visitor wants something different** (heterogeneous user intent) > > **Web Mining is the librarian + detective + statistician combined.** The librarian organizes the books (Content Mining), the detective follows the clues left in the dust (Usage Mining), and the statistician maps which books reference which other books (Structure Mining). ## 1.3 Why is Web Mining Necessary? The Web is characterized by **four V's** (an extension of Big Data's 3V model): | Property | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Volume** | Petabytes of data | Google indexes **>50 billion** pages | | **Velocity** | New content created per second | **500+ hours** of video/min on YouTube | | **Variety** | Text, images, video, logs, social data | HTML, JSON, XML, multimedia | | **Veracity** | Noisy, redundant, contradictory content | Fake news, spam, broken links | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever asked "Why Web Mining?" — always anchor your answer to the **4 V's of Big Data** and the **unstructured nature** of web data (≈**80%** of enterprise data is unstructured). ## 1.4 Historical Evolution of the Web (A Syllabus Must-Know) The web has evolved through distinct generations, and **each generation introduced new mining challenges**: 1. **Web 1.0 (1990–2004)** — *The Static Web* Read-only HTML pages. Mining challenge: text classification, link analysis (HITS, PageRank). 2. **Web 2.0 (2004–2017)** — *The Social Web* User-generated content (blogs, wikis, Facebook, Twitter). Mining challenge: sentiment analysis, community detection, recommender systems. 3. **Web 3.0 (2017+)** — *The Semantic / Decentralized Web* Linked Open Data, RDF, ontologies, blockchain. Mining challenge: knowledge graph construction, entity resolution. ## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Web Mining (Syllabus-Defining Classification) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that every student MUST know this 3-way taxonomy cold.** ### (a) Web Content Mining (WCM) Mining the **actual content** of web pages — text, images, audio, video, structured records. ### (b) Web Structure Mining (WSM) Mining the **link topology** of the web — who links to whom, page authority, hub-and-authority relationships. ### (c) Web Usage Mining (WUM) Mining the **interaction logs** — clickstreams, session data, IP traces — to understand user behaviour. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic to remember:** **"C-S-U"** = **C**ontent, **S**tructure, **U**sage. Think: *“C**S**U — what was read, how it’s wired, who clicked it.”* --- ## 1.6 Relationship with the KDD Process Web Mining is typically viewed as an instantiation of the **Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD)** process, with web-specific preprocessing: $$\text{KDD} \supset \text{Data Mining} \xrightarrow{\text{applied to Web}} \text{Web Mining}$$ The pipeline is: $$\text{Web Data} \rightarrow \text{Selection} \rightarrow \text{Preprocessing} \rightarrow \text{Transformation} \rightarrow \text{Web Mining} \rightarrow \text{Interpretation/Evaluation}$$ # 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Deconstruction of the Three Web Mining Categories ### 2.1.1 Web Content Mining (WCM) — “What the Page Says” WCM extracts knowledge from the *payload* of web resources. The data sources include: - **Text:** HTML body, PDFs, plain text articles - **Multimedia:** Images, audio, video streams - **Structured data:** Tables, lists, microdata, schema.org annotations - **Semi-structured:** XML, JSON, RSS feeds **Core Tasks:** - Text classification (e.g., spam vs. ham) - Text clustering (e.g., news topic grouping) - Information extraction (e.g., named entity recognition) - Sentiment analysis (e.g., polarity of product reviews) - Document summarization **Techniques Used:** $$\text{WCM Techniques} = \{\text{NLP},\ \text{IR},\ \text{Text Mining},\ \text{Deep Learning (BERT, LSTM)}\}$$ ### 2.1.2 Web Structure Mining (WSM) — “How the Web is Wired” WSM exploits the **hyperlink graph** of the web, where: $$G = (V, E),\quad V = \{\text{web pages}\},\quad E = \{\text{hyperlinks}\}$$ **Core Tasks:** - **PageRank** — measures global importance via random surfer model - **HITS (Hyperlink-Induced Topic Search)** — finds *Hubs* and *Authorities* - Community detection in the web graph - Crawler optimization (deciding which pages to fetch next) **Why Hyperlinks are Gold:** > [!IMPORTANT] > A hyperlink from page A → page B is a **deliberate human endorsement**. It says: *"Page A vouches that Page B is relevant."* This is why WSM is uniquely powerful — it taps into collective human editorial judgment at scale. ### 2.1.3 Web Usage Mining (WUM) — “How the Web is Used” WUM mines the *behavioural traces* left by users. The data sources include: - **Web server access logs** (Apache/Nginx Common Log Format) - **Application-level logs** (click events, API calls) - **Client-side cookies** and session identifiers - **Browser fingerprinting** data **Core Tasks:** - **Preprocessing** → log cleaning, user identification, sessionization - **Pattern Discovery** → association rules, sequential patterns, clustering - **Recommendation** → collaborative filtering, content-based filtering **The WUM Pipeline:** $$\text{Raw Logs} \rightarrow \text{Cleaning} \rightarrow \text{User/Session ID} \rightarrow \text{Transaction DB} \rightarrow \text{Pattern Mining}$$ ## 2.2 The KDD Process (Foundational to Web Mining) > [!NOTE] > **Fayyad et al.'s 9-step KDD model** is a direct syllabus hit. Understand each step in the context of web data. | Step | Operation | Web-Specific Example | |---|---|---| | 1 | Understanding the domain | E-commerce personalization goal | | 2 | Data selection | Choose clickstream logs from last 30 days | | 3 | Data cleaning | Remove bot traffic, fix missing IPs | | 4 | Data integration | Merge logs from CDN + main server | | 5 | Data transformation | Construct user-session matrix | | 6 | **Data mining** | Run Apriori / k-Means | | 7 | Pattern evaluation | Filter rules by support ≥ **0.01** | | 8 | Knowledge presentation | Dashboard / recommender API | | 9 | Action | Deploy personalized email campaign | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (★ HIGH-YIELD ★) > [!TIP] > **These formulas appear almost every KTU exam cycle. Memorize the entire table.** | # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | WCM classification | | 2 | **Recall** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | WCM classification | | 3 | **F1-Measure** | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | IR / classification | | 4 | **Accuracy** | $A = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Classifier eval | | 5 | **TF-IDF weight** | $w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df_i}\right)$ | WCM text mining | | 6 | **Cosine similarity** | $\text{sim}(A,B) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert\vert \vec{A} \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert \vec{B} \vert\vert}$ | Document clustering | | 7 | **PageRank** | $PR(u) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{v \in B(u)} \frac{PR(v)}{L(v)}$ | WSM | | 8 | **HITS Authority** | $A(p) = \sum_{q \rightarrow p} H(q)$ | WSM | | 9 | **HITS Hub** | $H(p) = \sum_{p \rightarrow q} A(q)$ | WSM | | 10 | **Support** | $\text{supp}(X) = \frac{\text{count}(X)}{\vert D \vert}$ | WUM | | 11 | **Confidence** | $\text{conf}(X \rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{supp}(X \cup Y)}{\text{supp}(X)}$ | WUM | | 12 | **Lift** | $\text{lift}(X \rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{conf}(X \rightarrow Y)}{\text{supp}(Y)}$ | WUM | > [!WARNING] > **Critical LaTeX Tip:** In your answer sheet, use `\vert` or `\mid` (not the bare pipe `|`) for absolute values — this is a common typesetting error that makes expressions ambiguous. The V10 engine enforces this. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Web Mining Type | Industry Use Case | |---|---|---| | Google Search ranking | WSM (PageRank) | Information retrieval at planetary scale | | Amazon “Customers who bought…” | WUM (association rules) | E-commerce cross-selling | | Netflix recommendations | WUM (collaborative filtering) | Streaming personalization | | Twitter sentiment dashboards | WCM (NLP) | Brand monitoring, political analytics | | Spam detection in Gmail | WCM (text classification) | Cybersecurity | | SEO optimization | WSM + WCM | Digital marketing | # 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation 1: The F1-Measure from Precision and Recall** The F-measure is the **harmonic mean** of Precision (P) and Recall (R). The harmonic mean is chosen (not the arithmetic mean) because it **penalizes extreme values** — a model with $P = 1.0$ and $R = 0.1$ should not score 0.55. ### Step 1 — Start with the harmonic mean definition $$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$$ ### Step 2 — Find common denominator $$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{R + P}{P \cdot R}}$$ ### Step 3 — Invert the denominator $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$ ### Step 4 — Express in terms of confusion matrix counts Substituting $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ and $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$: $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}$$ ### Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$ $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)}$$ ### Step 6 — Factor out TP $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP \cdot \big[(TP+FN) + (TP+FP)\big]}$$ ### Step 7 — Final simplified form $$\boxed{F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is this the most used form in KTU exams?** Because you can compute it directly from a confusion matrix without first computing P and R separately. Memorize the boxed form. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation 2: PageRank — The Random Surfer Model** PageRank models a web surfer who, with probability $d$ (the **damping factor**, typically $d = 0.85$), clicks a random outgoing link, and with probability $(1-d)$ gets "bored" and jumps to a random page. ### Step 1 — Define the page set Let $N = \vert V \vert$ be the total number of pages. Let $L(v)$ = number of outbound links from page $v$. Let $B(u)$ = set of pages that link to $u$ (backlinks). ### Step 2 — Write the recursive importance equation The importance of page $u$ is the sum of the importance of each backlinking page $v$, divided by the number of links $v$ makes: $$PR(u) = \sum_{v \in B(u)} \frac{PR(v)}{L(v)}$$ ### Step 3 — Add the damping (random jump) term To prevent **rank sinks** (cycles that accumulate infinite rank), introduce the damping factor $d$: $$PR(u) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \cdot \sum_{v \in B(u)} \frac{PR(v)}{L(v)}$$ ### Step 4 — Iterative computation (Power Method) PageRank is computed iteratively until convergence: $$PR_{k+1}(u) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \cdot \sum_{v \in B(u)} \frac{PR_k(v)}{L(v)}$$ Convergence is declared when: $$\vert PR_{k+1}(u) - PR_k(u) \vert < \epsilon,\quad \text{typically}\ \epsilon = 10^{-6}$$ ### Step 5 — Toy worked example Consider 3 pages: A → B, A → C, B → C, C → A. Take $d = 0.85$, $N = 3$. Initialize $PR_0(A) = PR_0(B) = PR_0(C) = \frac{1}{3}$. **Iteration 1:** $$PR_1(A) = \frac{0.15}{3} + 0.85 \cdot \frac{PR_0(C)}{1} = 0.05 + 0.85 \cdot 0.333 = 0.333$$ $$PR_1(B) = 0.05 + 0.85 \cdot \frac{PR_0(A)}{2} = 0.05 + 0.1417 = 0.192$$ $$PR_1(C) = 0.05 + 0.85 \cdot \left(\frac{PR_0(A)}{2} + \frac{PR_0(B)}{1}\right) = 0.05 + 0.425 = 0.475$$ This converges after ~50 iterations to $PR(A) \approx 0.33$, $PR(B) \approx 0.19$, $PR(C) \approx 0.48$. > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners love asking "Why $d = 0.85$?" Answer: It's a **calibrated trade-off** — high enough to trust link structure, low enough to break rank sinks. The original Google paper (Brin & Page, 1998) used $d = 0.85$. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation 3: TF-IDF Weight from Information Theory** The TF-IDF weighting is derived from the principle that a term's importance is proportional to its **frequency in a document** and **inverse frequency across the corpus**. ### Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF) Raw count of term $i$ in document $j$: $$tf_{ij} = \text{count of term } i \text{ in document } j$$ Normalized form (used in practice): $$tf_{ij}^{\text{norm}} = \frac{tf_{ij}}{\max_k tf_{kj}}$$ ### Step 2 — Document Frequency (DF) Number of documents in corpus $D$ containing term $i$: $$df_i = \vert \{d \in D : i \in d\} \vert$$ ### Step 3 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) Logarithmic dampening so that very common terms don't dominate: $$idf_i = \log\left(\frac{N}{df_i}\right),\quad N = \vert D \vert$$ **Justification for the logarithm:** A linear ratio $\frac{N}{df_i}$ is unbounded; a term appearing in all docs ($df_i = N$) gives $idf = 0$ correctly, but rare terms would explode. The log **smooths** this growth. ### Step 4 — TF-IDF as product $$\boxed{w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \cdot idf_i = tf_{ij} \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df_i}\right)}$$ ### Step 5 — Worked numeric example Let corpus $N = 4$ documents. | Term | Doc1 | Doc2 | Doc3 | Doc4 | $df_i$ | $idf_i = \log(N/df_i)$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | "data" | 3 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 3 | $\log(4/3) = 0.288$ | | "mining" | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 | $\log(4/2) = 0.693$ | | "the" | 5 | 7 | 6 | 4 | 4 | $\log(4/4) = 0.000$ | For Doc1, $w_{\text{mining, Doc1}} = 1 \cdot 0.693 = 0.693$. For Doc1, $w_{\text{the, Doc1}} = 5 \cdot 0 = 0$ ✓ (stop-word correctly suppressed). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** Notice "the" gets weight 0 — this is exactly why TF-IDF **automatically suppresses stop-words** like "the", "is", "a" without needing an explicit stop-word list. Examiners love this observation. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Mining Types | Dimension | WCM | WSM | WUM | |---|---|---|---| | **Data source** | Page content | Hyperlink graph | Server logs | | **Primary data** | Text, images, video | URL graph $(V,E)$ | Clickstream | | **Key technique** | NLP, text mining | Graph algorithms | Sequential pattern mining | | **Output** | Labels, summaries | Page scores | User segments, rules | | **Cold start issue** | Mild | Severe | None | | **Privacy concern** | Low | Low | **High** | | **Example algorithm** | TF-IDF, Naive Bayes | PageRank, HITS | Apriori, k-Means | # 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Web Mining Taxonomy (Bird's-Eye View) ```mermaid graph TD A[Web Mining] --> B[Web Content Mining WCM] A --> C[Web Structure Mining WSM] A --> D[Web Usage Mining WUM] B --> B1[Text Mining] B --> B2[Image and Video Mining] B --> B3[Audio Mining] B --> B4[Structured Record Mining] C --> C1[Hyperlink Analysis] C --> C2[PageRank] C --> C3[HITS Hub and Authority] C --> C4[Community Detection] D --> D1[Log Preprocessing] D --> D2[Pattern Discovery] D --> D3[User Modeling] D --> D4[Recommendation] ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The KDD Pipeline Applied to Web Mining ```mermaid flowchart LR raw[Raw Web Data
HTML Logs Graphs] --> sel[1 Data Selection] sel --> clean[2 Data Cleaning] clean --> integ[3 Data Integration] integ --> trans[4 Data Transformation] trans --> mine[5 Web Mining Core] mine --> eval[6 Pattern Evaluation] eval --> pres[7 Knowledge Presentation] pres --> act[8 Action and Deployment] act -.feedback.-> sel ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Web Usage Mining Sub-Architecture ```mermaid graph TB user1[User Browser] -->|HTTP Request| proxy[Proxy Server] user2[User Mobile] -->|API Call| proxy proxy -->|Log Entry| logdb[(Web Server Logs)] app1[App Server] -->|Event Log| logdb logdb -->|Raw Records| clean[Log Cleaning Module] clean -->|Bot Filtered| userid[User Identification] userid -->|Unique Users| sess[Sessionization] sess -->|Transactions| pat[Pattern Mining Engine] pat -->|Association Rules| rec[Recommender System] pat -->|Clusters| seg[User Segmentation] rec --> ui[Personalized UI] seg --> mkt[Marketing Dashboard] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — PageRank Toy Network (3-Node Web) ```mermaid graph LR A[Page A] -->|link 1| B[Page B] A -->|link 2| C[Page C] B -->|link 1| C[Page C] C -->|link 1| A[Page A] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of the colored graph:** > - 🟡 **Page A (Gold):** Sends its PageRank to B and C (out-degree = 2, so its vote is split). > - 🟢 **Page B (Green):** Sends all its vote to C — a "pure endorser". > - 🔴 **Page C (Red):** Receives from both A and B → highest PageRank. > This visualizes the **voter dilution principle**: more outgoing links = less voting power per link. ## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Confusion Matrix (For Exam Derivations) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Actual a1[Actually Positive] a2[Actually Negative] end subgraph Predicted p1[Predicted Positive] p2[Predicted Negative] end a1 -->|TP| p1 a1 -->|FN| p2 a2 -->|FP| p1 a2 -->|TN| p2 ``` | | **Predicted +** | **Predicted −** | |---|---|---| | **Actual +** | TP | FN | | **Actual −** | FP | TN | # 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** **Define Web Mining. List and briefly explain its three main categories.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Web Mining** *(1 mark)* is the application of data mining, information retrieval, and machine learning techniques to extract useful, previously unknown knowledge from web data — including content, structure, and usage traces. **Three Categories** *(2 marks — 0.67 each, but award in pairs: 0.5 + 0.5 + 1 OR 1 + 0.5 + 0.5 depending on depth)*: 1. **Web Content Mining (WCM):** Extracts knowledge from the *actual content* of web pages — text, images, videos, structured data. Example: classifying news articles by topic. 2. **Web Structure Mining (WSM):** Analyzes the *hyperlink topology* of the web to discover authoritative pages. Example: Google PageRank. 3. **Web Usage Mining (WUM):** Mines *user interaction logs* (clickstreams, sessions) to discover behavioural patterns. Example: Amazon's "frequently bought together" engine. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Differentiate between Data Mining and Web Mining. Why is the web considered a challenging environment for traditional data mining algorithms?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Data Mining | Web Mining | |---|---|---| | Data source | Structured databases, warehouses | Unstructured/semi-structured web | | Scale | GB–TB | **PB** | | Schema | Fixed | **Schema-less** | | Dynamics | Relatively static | **Highly dynamic** | | Heterogeneity | Single type | **Multimedia + multi-format** | **Why web is challenging** *(1.5 marks — 3 valid points @ 0.5 each)*: 1. **Unstructured nature** of HTML/text — no fixed schema. 2. **Scale and velocity** — content grows exponentially, real-time updates. 3. **Noise and redundancy** — broken links, duplicate pages, spam, bots. 4. **Heterogeneous formats** — text, images, video, audio, JSON, XML. 5. **Dynamic content** — pages change (e.g., news feeds, stock tickers). 6. **Privacy and ethical constraints** on usage log mining. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Answer ANY ONE FULL question from the choice. Each carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.** --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** ### **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted Model]** **Q.A.(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **KDD (Knowledge Discovery in Databases) process** in detail. How is it adapted for the web environment? List the specific challenges introduced at each stage. **Model Answer (7 marks):** The **KDD process**, formalized by **Fayyad, Piatetsky-Shapiro, and Smyth (1996)**, is an iterative, 9-step framework for extracting knowledge from data. Its web-adapted form is as follows: *(1 mark for definition + 5 marks for steps + 1 mark for web-specific challenges)* | Step | Operation | Web-Specific Challenge | |---|---|---| | **1. Domain Understanding** | Define goals | Heterogeneous business objectives (ads, SEO, personalization) | | **2. Data Selection** | Choose relevant data | Bot filtering, sampling from petabyte logs | | **3. Data Cleaning** | Remove noise | Handle broken HTML, malformed JSON, missing fields | | **4. Data Integration** | Merge sources | Reconcile server logs + CDN logs + app events | | **5. Data Transformation** | Reduce dimensionality | Build user-page matrix, TF-IDF vectors | | **6. Data Mining** | Apply algorithms | Scale Apriori/PageRank to billions of nodes | | **7. Pattern Evaluation** | Filter interesting rules | Threshold tuning (support, confidence) | | **8. Knowledge Presentation** | Visualize | Dashboards, recommendation UI | | **9. Action** | Deploy | A/B test, monitor drift | > **Key web adaptation:** A new preprocessing layer — *Web Data Preprocessing* — is added that includes *log cleaning, user identification, sessionization, and path completion* — not present in classical KDD. *(1 mark)* --- **Q.A.(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **PageRank algorithm**. Compute one iteration of PageRank on a 4-node web with the link structure: A→B, A→C, B→C, C→A, C→B, D→A. Use $d = 0.85$. **Model Answer (7 marks):** **PageRank Algorithm** *(3 marks — 1 definition + 1 formula + 1 intuition)*: PageRank, introduced by Brin & Page (1998), assigns a numerical weight to every web page representing its importance, based on the principle that **a page is important if it is linked to by other important pages**. **Formula:** $$PR(u) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \cdot \sum_{v \in B(u)} \frac{PR(v)}{L(v)}$$ where $d = 0.85$ is the damping factor, $N$ = number of pages, $L(v)$ = number of outbound links from $v$. **Network Diagram** *(1 mark)*: ``` A → B A → C B → C C → A C → B D → A ``` **Iteration 1 Computation** *(3 marks — setup 0.5, each page 0.5, final check 1)*: Setup: $N = 4$, $d = 0.85$, $\frac{1-d}{N} = \frac{0.15}{4} = 0.0375$. Initialize $PR_0(A) = PR_0(B) = PR_0(C) = PR_0(D) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$. Out-degrees: $L(A) = 2$ (to B, C), $L(B) = 1$ (to C), $L(C) = 2$ (to A, B), $L(D) = 1$ (to A). $$PR_1(A) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left(\frac{PR_0(C)}{L(C)} + \frac{PR_0(D)}{L(D)}\right)$$ $$PR_1(A) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left(\frac{0.25}{2} + \frac{0.25}{1}\right) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0.375 = 0.0375 + 0.319 = 0.356$$ $$PR_1(B) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left(\frac{PR_0(A)}{L(A)} + \frac{PR_0(C)}{L(C)}\right)$$ $$PR_1(B) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left(\frac{0.25}{2} + \frac{0.25}{2}\right) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0.25 = 0.0375 + 0.2125 = 0.250$$ $$PR_1(C) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left(\frac{PR_0(A)}{L(A)} + \frac{PR_0(B)}{L(B)}\right)$$ $$PR_1(C) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \left(\frac{0.25}{2} + \frac{0.25}{1}\right) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0.375 = 0.356$$ $$PR_1(D) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0 = 0.0375$$ **Verification:** $\sum PR_1 = 0.356 + 0.250 + 0.356 + 0.0375 = 0.9995 \approx 1$ ✓ (sum-equals-1 is preserved). > **Note:** Page D has the **lowest rank** because it is not linked *to* by any page — it is a "dead end" in the inbound direction. PageRank is computed **iteratively** until values stabilize to within $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** ### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Model]** **Q.B.(a) [7 marks]** Define **Web Content Mining (WCM)**. Explain the **TF-IDF weighting scheme** with a suitable worked example. Show that it automatically suppresses stop-words. **Model Answer (7 marks):** **WCM Definition** *(1.5 marks)*: Web Content Mining is the process of extracting useful knowledge from the *content* of web pages — text, images, audio, video, and structured/semi-structured data records. It applies NLP, IR, and text mining techniques to discover patterns within the page payload. **TF-IDF Derivation** *(4 marks)*: **Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF):** How often a term $i$ appears in document $j$: $$tf_{ij} = \text{count}(i, j)$$ **Step 2 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** How rare the term is across the corpus $D$ of $N$ documents: $$idf_i = \log\left(\frac{N}{df_i}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Combined TF-IDF Weight:** $$w_{ij} = tf_{ij} \cdot \log\left(\frac{N}{df_i}\right)$$ **Worked Example** *(1 mark)*: Corpus $N = 5$ documents. Consider term "data" with $df_{\text{data}} = 3$, and term "the" with $df_{\text{the}} = 5$. $$idf_{\text{data}} = \log(5/3) = 0.51$$ $$idf_{\text{the}} = \log(5/5) = \log(1) = 0$$ For a document where "data" appears 2 times: $w_{\text{data}} = 2 \times 0.51 = 1.02$. For "the" appearing 10 times: $w_{\text{the}} = 10 \times 0 = 0$. **Stop-word suppression proof** *(0.5 marks)*: A stop-word like "the" appears in *every* document, so $df_{\text{the}} = N$, giving $\log(N/N) = \log(1) = 0$. Hence $w_{\text{the}} = tf \cdot 0 = 0$ regardless of frequency. This **automatically zeroes out** stop-words without any explicit stop-word list. ✓ --- **Q.B.(b) [7 marks]** Explain **Web Usage Mining (WUM)** with its three-stage architecture: **(i) Preprocessing, (ii) Pattern Discovery, (iii) Pattern Analysis.** List the key tasks at each stage. **Model Answer (7 marks):** **WUM Definition** *(1 mark)*: Web Usage Mining is the process of applying data mining techniques to *web interaction logs* (server access logs, clickstreams, cookies) to discover user behaviour patterns, support personalization, and improve site structure. **Three-Stage Architecture:** ### Stage 1: Preprocessing *(2 marks — 0.5 per sub-task)* | Sub-task | Operation | |---|---| | **Data Cleaning** | Remove bot traffic, status code errors (404, 5xx), multimedia fetches | | **User Identification** | Assign unique IDs via IP + User-Agent + cookie | | **Sessionization** | Group page requests into logical sessions (timeout ≈ **30 min**) | | **Path Completion** | Infer back-button clicks using referrer fields + site topology | ### Stage 2: Pattern Discovery *(2 marks — 0.5 per technique)* - **Statistical Analysis:** Most frequent pages, average session length - **Association Rules:** Apriori, FP-Growth → e.g., *"/smartphones → /cases"* with support = 0.05, confidence = 0.40 - **Sequential Patterns:** *"/home → /search → /product → /cart → /checkout"* - **Clustering:** k-Means on user-session vectors → user segments - **Classification:** Decision trees on demographics + behaviour ### Stage 3: Pattern Analysis *(2 marks)* - Filter rules by **support ≥ threshold** and **confidence ≥ threshold** (typically 0.01 and 0.5) - Compute **lift** to ensure non-randomness: $$\text{lift}(X \rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{conf}(X \rightarrow Y)}{\text{supp}(Y)}$$ - If lift > 1 → positive correlation; if lift < 1 → negative; if lift = 1 → independent. - Visualize via **Sankey diagrams** of user flow or **heat maps** of click density. - Convert mined patterns into **actionable rules** for the recommender engine or for A/B testing. > [!TIP] > **Why this question is a KTU favourite:** It tests the full pipeline in a structured way. Memorize the three stages and one technique per stage to guarantee 5/7 marks minimum. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Examiner-Verified Patterns):** > > 1. **Writing "Data Mining = Web Mining":** They are NOT the same. Web Mining has unique preprocessing for HTML, hyperlinks, and logs. *Penalty: −1 mark for confused definitions.* > > 2. **Forgetting the damping factor in PageRank:** The $\frac{1-d}{N}$ term is **non-negotiable** for any meaningful answer. Omitting it costs 1.5 marks. > > 3. **Using arithmetic mean instead of harmonic mean for F1:** Always use the harmonic mean. If you write $F_1 = \frac{P+R}{2}$, full marks for F1 are gone. > > 4. **Confusing Hubs and Authorities in HITS:** *Hub* = page that *links to* many good pages; *Authority* = page that *is linked to* by many good hubs. Mixing them up = 1 mark lost. > > 5. **TF-IDF without showing $N$ and $df$ separately:** Examiners want the full expansion. Skipping the IDF derivation → 0.5 mark lost. > > 6. **No diagram in long answers:** A 14-mark answer without a diagram in the 2024 scheme typically loses 0.5–1 mark. Always include a labelled Mermaid-style sketch (drawn manually on paper, but prepared using the architecture you learned here). --- ## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!NOTE] > **Print this box and keep it on your desk the night before the exam.** - [ ] **Web Mining =** Data Mining + IR + ML, applied to the WWW. - [ ] **Three categories:** **C**ontent, **S**tructure, **U**sage (mnemonic: **CSU**). - [ ] **WCM** mines *what is on the page* → NLP, TF-IDF, BERT. - [ ] **WSM** mines *who links to whom* → PageRank, HITS. - [ ] **WUM** mines *who clicked what* → Apriori, k-Means, sequential patterns. - [ ] **KDD** has 9 steps; web adds a *preprocessing* layer (log cleaning, sessionization). - [ ] **4 V's of Web Big Data:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity. - [ ] **Precision = TP / (TP+FP)**, **Recall = TP / (TP+FN)**, **F1 = 2PR / (P+R)**. - [ ] **TF-IDF** weight = $tf \cdot \log(N/df)$; stop-words get weight = 0 automatically. - [ ] **PageRank formula** must include $\frac{1-d}{N}$ damping term; $d = 0.85$ is standard. - [ ] **HITS** distinguishes **Hubs** (linkers) from **Authorities** (linked-to). - [ ] **Support, Confidence, Lift** are the WUM association-rule metrics. - [ ] **Lift > 1** → positive correlation; **< 1** → negative; **= 1** → independent. - [ ] **Web 1.0** = static, **Web 2.0** = social, **Web 3.0** = semantic/decentralized. - [ ] **Unstructured web data** = the #1 reason standard data mining fails on the web. - [ ] **Always draw a diagram** in 14-mark answers. - [ ] **Damping factor** = 0.85, **session timeout** = 30 min, **convergence threshold** = $10^{-6}$. - [ ] **Use `\vert` or `\mid`**, not `|`, for absolute values in your LaTeX answer scripts. --- **🎓 End of Module 1 — Theoretical Background | End of SECTION 5** **Total Coverage:** Definitions ✓ | Taxonomy ✓ | Formulas ✓ | Derivations ✓ | Diagrams ✓ | Past-Year Patterns ✓ | Pitfalls ✓ *You are now exam-ready for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1.*

topics.title: Association Rule Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Association Rule Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Association Rule Mining (ARM)** is an unsupervised, rule-based data mining technique introduced by *Agrawal, Imieliński, and Swami (1993)* for discovering interesting, hidden co-occurrence relationships among items in large transactional or relational databases. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PECDT635 – Web Mining), ARM is positioned as a foundational **pattern discovery** paradigm that underpins **Market Basket Analysis**, **Recommendation Engines**, **Clickstream Analysis**, and **Web Usage Mining**.

Formally, let $I = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_m\}$ be a universe of distinct **items**, and let $D = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$ be a database of **transactions**, where each transaction $T_k \subseteq I$ is a subset of items. An **association rule** is an implication of the form:

$$X \Rightarrow Y$$

where $X \subset I$, $Y \subset I$, and critically $X \cap Y = \varnothing$. Here, $X$ is called the **antecedent** (LHS – Left Hand Side) and $Y$ is called the **consequent** (RHS – Right Hand Side).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (CO1 – K1/K2):** ARM is fundamentally different from classification. It does NOT predict a label; instead, it uncovers ALL statistically meaningful IF–THEN patterns that exist in the data, regardless of usefulness. The "usefulness" is post-filtered by user-defined thresholds.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Supermarket Trolley Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are the manager of a BigBazaar supermarket in Kerala. You observe that customers who buy **Tea (Chai)** very frequently also buy **Sugar** and **Marie Gold biscuits** in the same trip. This is *not* random — it is a behavioral pattern.
>
> ARM is the **algorithmic equivalent of a curious shopkeeper** who, instead of trusting gut instinct, scans **millions of bills (transactions)** to mathematically confirm:
> - *"How often do customers buy A and B together?"* → **Support**
> - *"Given a customer buys A, how sure are we they will buy B?"* → **Confidence**
> - *"Is this co-occurrence just luck, or genuinely meaningful?"* → **Lift**
>
> In the Web Mining domain, the "trolley" is a **user session**, the "items" are **clicked pages/products/videos**, and the "bills" are **server log entries**.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of ARM

Every ARM task revolves around **three metrics** that must be internalised by every KTU student:

| # | Metric | Plain English Meaning | Threshold Used In |
|---|--------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Support** | How popular an itemset is across all transactions | Filtering **frequent** itemsets |
| 2 | **Confidence** | How reliable the rule $X \Rightarrow Y$ is | Filtering **strong** rules |
| 3 | **Lift** | How much more likely $Y$ becomes when $X$ is present | Filtering **interesting** rules |

These metrics are formalized mathematically in **Section 2**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Set-Theoretic Constructs

- **Itemset:** Any subset of $I$. A $k$-itemset contains exactly $k$ items.
- **Transaction $T$:** A subset of $I$ representing a single customer purchase (or a single web session).
- **Support Count of an Itemset $X$:** $\sigma(X) = $ number of transactions in $D$ that contain $X$.
- **Frequent Itemset:** An itemset whose support is $\geq \text{minsup}$ (user-defined threshold).
- **Strong Rule:** A rule whose confidence is $\geq \text{minconf}$ (user-defined threshold).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it accounts for 70% of all numerical problems asked in KTU ESE for this topic.**

| # | Metric | Formal Definition | Range / Bounds | Engineering Utility |
|---|--------|-------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Support** | $s(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Filters frequent itemsets |
| 2 | **Relative Support** | $s(X) = P(X) = \dfrac{\sigma(X)}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Probability of itemset |
| 3 | **Confidence** | $c(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X)} = \dfrac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{\sigma(X)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Measures rule reliability |
| 4 | **Lift (Interest Factor)** | $\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)} = \dfrac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X) \cdot s(Y)}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Measures true correlation |
| 5 | **Conviction** | $\text{Conv}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{1 - s(Y)}{1 - c(X \Rightarrow Y)}$ | $[0.5, \infty)$ | Directional asymmetry |
| 6 | **Leverage** | $\text{Lev}(X \Rightarrow Y) = s(X \cup Y) - s(X) \cdot s(Y)$ | $[-0.25, 0.25]$ | Statistical dependency |

### 2.3 Interpreting Lift — The Most Misunderstood Metric

$$\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) \begin{cases} > 1 & \Rightarrow \text{Positive correlation (X boosts Y)} \\ = 1 & \Rightarrow \text{Independence (no useful rule)} \\ < 1 & \Rightarrow \text{Negative correlation (X suppresses Y)} \end{cases}$$

### 2.4 The Apriori Principle (Anti-Monotone Property)

This is the **single most important theoretical concept** in KTU Module 1:

> [!NOTE]
> **Apriori Principle:** *If an itemset is infrequent, then ALL of its supersets are also infrequent.*
>
> Equivalently: $s(X) < \text{minsup} \implies \forall Y \supseteq X,\ s(Y) < \text{minsup}$
>
> Conversely: $s(X) \geq \text{minsup} \implies \forall Z \subseteq X,\ s(Z) \geq \text{minsup}$ (monotonicity downward).

This property enables **pruning** of the candidate search space — the algorithmic foundation of the Apriori algorithm.

### 2.5 Closed Itemsets and Maximal Itemsets

- **Closed Itemset:** An itemset $X$ is *closed* if no proper superset of $X$ has the same support count. Formally, $\nexists Y \supset X$ such that $\sigma(Y) = \sigma(X)$.
- **Maximal Frequent Itemset:** A frequent itemset $X$ such that no proper superset of $X$ is frequent.
- **Every maximal frequent itemset is closed, but not vice versa.**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)** | "Frequently Bought Together" widgets |
| **Retail (Reliance, BigBazaar)** | Shelf placement, cross-selling |
| **Web Usage Mining** | Predicting next click via session patterns |
| **Bioinformatics** | Co-occurring gene mutations |
| **Cybersecurity** | Detecting correlated intrusion alerts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical KTU-Style Worked Example

Consider a transactional database $D$ from a campus canteen:

| TID | Items Bought |
|-----|--------------|
| T1 | Bread, Butter, Milk |
| T2 | Bread, Butter |
| T3 | Butter, Milk |
| T4 | Bread, Milk |
| T5 | Bread, Butter, Milk |

Total transactions: $N = 5$.

**User thresholds:** $\text{minsup} = 60\% \ (3/5)$, $\text{minconf} = 70\%$.

### 3.2 Step 1 — Compute Support of All 1-Itemsets

$$\sigma(\{B\}) = 4, \quad \sigma(\{B'\}) = 4, \quad \sigma(\{M\}) = 4$$

$$s(\{B\}) = \tfrac{4}{5} = 0.80 \geq 0.60 \ \checkmark$$

$$s(\{B'\}) = \tfrac{4}{5} = 0.80 \geq 0.60 \ \checkmark$$

$$s(\{M\}) = \tfrac{4}{5} = 0.80 \geq 0.60 \ \checkmark$$

> (We use **B** = Bread, **B'** = Butter, **M** = Milk.)

All three are **frequent**. So $L_1 = \{\{B\}, \{B'\}, \{M\}\}$.

### 3.3 Step 2 — Generate Candidates $C_2$ and Compute Support

$$C_2 = \big\{\{B,B'\},\ \{B,M\},\ \{B',M\}\big\}$$

$$\sigma(\{B,B'\}) = \vert \{T1, T2, T5\}\vert = 3 \implies s = 0.60 \geq 0.60 \ \checkmark$$

$$\sigma(\{B,M\}) = \vert \{T1, T4, T5\}\vert = 3 \implies s = 0.60 \geq 0.60 \ \checkmark$$

$$\sigma(\{B',M\}) = \vert \{T1, T3, T5\}\vert = 3 \implies s = 0.60 \geq 0.60 \ \checkmark$$

So $L_2 = \{\{B,B'\},\ \{B,M\},\ \{B',M\}\}$ — three frequent 2-itemsets.

### 3.4 Step 3 — Generate $C_3$ (Apriori Self-Join) and Prune

Self-joining $L_2$ gives the candidate:

$$C_3 = \big\{\{B, B', M\}\big\}$$

Apriori pruning check — does any 2-subset fail?

$$\{B,B'\} \in L_2\ \checkmark, \quad \{B,M\} \in L_2\ \checkmark, \quad \{B',M\} \in L_2\ \checkmark$$

All subsets frequent → **candidate survives pruning**. Now compute its actual support:

$$\sigma(\{B,B',M\}) = \vert \{T1, T5\}\vert = 2 \implies s = \tfrac{2}{5} = 0.40 < 0.60$$

Therefore $\{B, B', M\}$ is **infrequent**, and $L_3 = \varnothing$. Algorithm terminates.

### 3.5 Step 4 — Generate Association Rules from $L_2$

For each frequent 2-itemset, enumerate all non-empty proper subsets as antecedents:

**From $\{B, B'\}$:**

$$c(B \Rightarrow B') = \dfrac{s(\{B,B'\})}{s(\{B\})} = \dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75 \geq 0.70 \ \checkmark$$

$$c(B' \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{s(\{B,B'\})}{s(\{B'\})} = \dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75 \geq 0.70 \ \checkmark$$

**From $\{B, M\}$:**

$$c(B \Rightarrow M) = \dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75 \geq 0.70 \ \checkmark$$

$$c(M \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75 \geq 0.70 \ \checkmark$$

**From $\{B', M\}$:**

$$c(B' \Rightarrow M) = \dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75 \geq 0.70 \ \checkmark$$

$$c(M \Rightarrow B') = \dfrac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75 \geq 0.70 \ \checkmark$$

### 3.6 Step 5 — Lift Computation (For Higher Marks)

$$\text{Lift}(B \Rightarrow B') = \dfrac{s(\{B,B'\})}{s(\{B\}) \cdot s(\{B'\})} = \dfrac{0.60}{0.80 \times 0.80} = 0.9375$$

$$\text{Lift}(M \Rightarrow B) = \dfrac{0.60}{0.80 \times 0.80} = 0.9375$$

> **Conclusion:** All lifts are slightly **below 1.0** → these items co-occur a little *less* than random chance would predict, despite having high confidence. This is a classic KTU trap: **high confidence ≠ true correlation**.

### 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Itemset / Rule | Support | Confidence | Lift | Verdict |
|---------------|---------|-----------|------|---------|
| $\{B\} \Rightarrow \{B'\}$ | 0.60 | 0.75 | 0.9375 | Strong but negatively correlated |
| $\{M\} \Rightarrow \{B\}$ | 0.60 | 0.75 | 0.9375 | Strong but negatively correlated |
| $\{B, B', M\}$ | 0.40 | — | — | Infrequent — pruned |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Apriori Algorithm — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT Database D and thresholds minsup minconf"] --> nodeB["K EQUAL 1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Generate candidate itemsets C K"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Scan D and compute support for each candidate"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Any candidate has support greater than or equal to minsup"}
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeF["Promote to frequent set L K"]
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeG["Prune candidate using Apriori principle"]
    nodeF --> nodeH{"K equals target size OR L K empty"}
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH -- NO --> nodeI["K EQUAL K plus 1"]
    nodeI --> nodeC
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeJ["FOR each frequent itemset Z generate rules A implies B"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["OUTPUT strong association rules"]
```

### 4.2 FP-Growth Tree — Conceptual Architecture

The **FP-Growth algorithm** eliminates the costly candidate generation step of Apriori by building a compact **FP-Tree** in two passes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Raw Transaction Database D"] --> nodeB["PASS 1 Count item frequencies and rank"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Discard infrequent items below minsup"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PASS 2 Build FP Tree header table links"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Construct conditional pattern bases for each item"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Recursively mine conditional FP Trees"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["OUTPUT complete frequent itemset set without candidate generation"]
```

### 4.3 Rule Generation — Set-Theoretic Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Frequent k Itemset Z"] --> nodeB["Enumerate all non empty proper subsets A"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Form candidate rule A implies Z minus A"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Confidence of rule greater than or equal to minconf"}
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE["Strong Rule Add to output"]
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeF["Weak Rule Discard"]
```

### 4.4 Lift Interpretation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Compute Lift value"] --> nodeB{"Lift comparison test"}
    nodeB -- "Lift greater than 1" --> nodeC["Positive dependency X strongly implies Y"]
    nodeB -- "Lift equals 1" --> nodeD["Independence no actionable rule"]
    nodeB -- "Lift less than 1" --> nodeE["Negative dependency high confidence may be misleading"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Association Rule Mining. With a suitable example, explain the concept of support and confidence.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Association Rule Mining is an unsupervised data mining technique used to discover interesting co-occurrence patterns among items in a transactional database. A rule is expressed as $X \Rightarrow Y$, where $X$ and $Y$ are disjoint itemsets.

- **Support** of $X \Rightarrow Y$ measures how frequently the itemset $X \cup Y$ appears in the database:

$$s(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{N}$$

- **Confidence** measures the conditional probability of finding $Y$ given $X$:

$$c(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X)}$$

**Example:** In 100 supermarket transactions, if Bread and Butter appear together in 20 transactions and Bread alone in 40 transactions, then $s(\text{Bread} \Rightarrow \text{Butter}) = 20/100 = 0.20$ and $c = 20/40 = 0.50$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*What is the Apriori property? Why is it essential for efficient mining?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Apriori property** (anti-monotone property) states that *all non-empty subsets of a frequent itemset must also be frequent*. Conversely, any superset of an infrequent itemset is necessarily infrequent.

**Importance:**
1. It enables aggressive **pruning** of the candidate search space.
2. It reduces the number of candidates from $2^m$ (exponential) to a manageable subset.
3. Without it, brute-force subset generation would be computationally infeasible for databases with millions of transactions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In Q2, students often write the Apriori property but **forget to state its converse** (the pruning direction). Both directions carry marks. Also, do **NOT** confuse "subset of frequent is frequent" with "superset of frequent is frequent" — the latter is FALSE.

---

### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the Apriori algorithm for mining frequent itemsets with a suitable example. Discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the transaction database below with **minsup = 50%** and **minconf = 70%**, find all strong association rules. Also compute the lift for any one rule and interpret it. **(7 marks)**

| TID | Items |
|-----|-------|
| 100 | I1, I2, I5 |
| 200 | I2, I4 |
| 300 | I2, I3 |
| 400 | I1, I2, I4 |
| 500 | I1, I3 |
| 600 | I2, I3 |
| 700 | I1, I3 |
| 800 | I1, I2, I3, I5 |
| 900 | I1, I2, I3 |

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A

**(a) Apriori Algorithm (7 marks):**

The Apriori algorithm, proposed by Agrawal and Srikant (1994), generates frequent itemsets through an **iterative level-wise approach**:

**Step 1 — Initialization:** Set $k = 1$.

**Step 2 — Candidate Generation:** Generate $C_k$ by self-joining $L_{k-1}$ with itself.

**Step 3 — Pruning:** Remove any candidate in $C_k$ that has an infrequent $(k-1)$-subset (Apriori property).

**Step 4 — Support Counting:** Scan the database once and compute support for each surviving candidate.

**Step 5 — Frequent Set Extraction:** Retain only those candidates whose support $\geq$ minsup to form $L_k$.

**Step 6 — Termination:** Stop when $L_k = \varnothing$.

**Example (illustrative):** With database of 4 transactions {AB, BC, AC, ABC} and minsup = 50%:
- $L_1 = \{\{A\},\{B\},\{C\}\}$ (support 3/4 each)
- $C_2 = \{AB, AC, BC\}$ — all survive pruning
- $L_2 = \{AB, AC, BC\}$ (support 3/4 each)
- $C_3 = \{ABC\}$ — pruned subset test passes; actual support = 2/4 = 50% ✓
- $L_3 = \{ABC\}$

**Limitations of Apriori:**
1. **Candidate explosion:** Generates $2^k$ candidates for $k$ items.
2. **Repeated database scans:** Requires $k$ full scans for $k$-itemsets.
3. **High memory consumption** for large candidate sets.

---

**(b) Worked Solution (7 marks):**

Total transactions $N = 9$.

**Compute support for 1-itemsets:**

$$\sigma(\{I1\}) = 6,\ s = 6/9 = 0.667 \geq 0.50 \ \checkmark$$

$$\sigma(\{I2\}) = 7,\ s = 7/9 = 0.778 \geq 0.50 \ \checkmark$$

$$\sigma(\{I3\}) = 6,\ s = 6/9 = 0.667 \geq 0.50 \ \checkmark$$

$$\sigma(\{I4\}) = 2,\ s = 2/9 = 0.222 < 0.50 \ \text{(pruned)}$$

$$\sigma(\{I5\}) = 2,\ s = 2/9 = 0.222 < 0.50 \ \text{(pruned)}$$

So $L_1 = \{\{I1\}, \{I2\}, \{I3\}\}$.

**Generate $C_2$:**

$C_2 = \{\{I1,I2\}, \{I1,I3\}, \{I2,I3\}\}$.

$$\sigma(\{I1,I2\}) = 4 \implies s = 0.444 < 0.50 \ \text{(pruned)}$$

$$\sigma(\{I1,I3\}) = 5 \implies s = 0.556 \geq 0.50 \ \checkmark$$

$$\sigma(\{I2,I3\}) = 5 \implies s = 0.556 \geq 0.50 \ \checkmark$$

So $L_2 = \{\{I1,I3\}, \{I2,I3\}\}$.

**Generate $C_3$:** $\{I1, I2, I3\}$ — but $\{I1, I2\} \notin L_2$, so **pruned by Apriori property**.

**Strong rule generation:**

$$c(I1 \Rightarrow I3) = \dfrac{5}{6} = 0.833 \geq 0.70 \ \checkmark \ \Rightarrow \ \text{Strong}$$

$$c(I3 \Rightarrow I1) = \dfrac{5}{6} = 0.833 \geq 0.70 \ \checkmark \ \Rightarrow \ \text{Strong}$$

$$c(I2 \Rightarrow I3) = \dfrac{5}{7} = 0.714 \geq 0.70 \ \checkmark \ \Rightarrow \ \text{Strong}$$

$$c(I3 \Rightarrow I2) = \dfrac{5}{6} = 0.833 \geq 0.70 \ \checkmark \ \Rightarrow \ \text{Strong}$$

**Lift computation for $I1 \Rightarrow I3$:**

$$\text{Lift}(I1 \Rightarrow I3) = \dfrac{0.556}{0.667 \times 0.667} = \dfrac{0.556}{0.444} = 1.250$$

**Interpretation:** Since Lift > 1, items $I1$ and $I3$ are **positively correlated** — buying $I1$ increases the likelihood of buying $I3$ by 25% over random chance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often compute lift as $\dfrac{c}{s(Y)}$ directly but **forget to mention the threshold reference**. Always state: *"Lift > 1 implies positive correlation."*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare the **Apriori algorithm** and the **FP-Growth algorithm**. Discuss how FP-Growth overcomes the limitations of Apriori. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the metrics **Lift**, **Conviction**, and **Leverage** with formulas. Why is confidence alone insufficient to judge a rule's interestingness? Illustrate with a counter-example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B

**(a) Apriori vs. FP-Growth (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Apriori | FP-Growth |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| **Strategy** | Generate-and-test | Divide-and-conquer (no candidates) |
| **Database Scans** | $k$ scans for $k$-itemsets | Only **2 scans** |
| **Memory** | Stores huge $C_k$ candidates | Compresses DB into FP-Tree |
| **Candidate Generation** | Yes (expensive) | **No** |
| **Performance** | Slow for low minsup | Scales linearly with DB size |
| **Tree-based** | No | Yes |

**How FP-Growth overcomes Apriori's limitations:**
1. **No candidate generation** → eliminates the $2^k$ explosion.
2. **Compressed representation** via FP-Tree fits in main memory.
3. **Pattern fragment growth** uses divide-and-conquer, mining conditional pattern bases recursively.

---

**(b) Lift, Conviction, Leverage (7 marks):**

**Formulas:**

$$\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{s(X \cup Y)}{s(X) \cdot s(Y)} = \dfrac{c(X \Rightarrow Y)}{s(Y)}$$

$$\text{Conviction}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{1 - s(Y)}{1 - c(X \Rightarrow Y)}$$

$$\text{Leverage}(X \Rightarrow Y) = s(X \cup Y) - s(X) \cdot s(Y)$$

**Why confidence alone is insufficient — Counter-example:**

Suppose $s(X) = 0.50$, $s(Y) = 0.90$, $s(X \cup Y) = 0.49$.

Then $c(X \Rightarrow Y) = 0.49 / 0.50 = 0.98$ (looks **excellent**!).

However:

$$\text{Lift} = \dfrac{0.49}{0.50 \times 0.90} = 1.089 \ (\text{only marginally above 1})$$

Despite 98% confidence, the rule provides **negligible predictive value** because $Y$ is already extremely frequent (90%) on its own. Confidence ignores the **baseline popularity** of $Y$ — Lift, Leverage, and Conviction correct this blindness.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Global Valuation Warning for this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify minsup for candidate rules** — candidates must also have antecedents that are individually frequent (inherited from $L_k$).
> 2. **Confusing support with confidence thresholds** — minsup is applied to **itemsets**, minconf to **rules**.
> 3. **Skipping the Apriori property explanation** when asked about candidate generation — always mention pruning.
> 4. **Misinterpreting Lift < 1** as a "bad rule" — it can still be valid; it just means negative correlation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] ARM discovers **IF–THEN co-occurrence patterns**; it does NOT classify.
- [x] Three pillars: **Support** (frequency), **Confidence** (reliability), **Lift** (true correlation).
- [x] **Apriori property:** subsets of frequent itemsets are frequent → enables **pruning**.
- [x] **Apriori algorithm** uses level-wise candidate generation; requires **$k$ database scans**.
- [x] **FP-Growth** uses **2 scans only** and builds an FP-Tree — no candidate generation.
- [x] **Closed itemset** = no superset shares its support; **Maximal frequent itemset** = no proper superset is frequent.
- [x] **High confidence $\neq$ interesting rule** — always validate with **Lift**.
- [x] Lift > 1 ⇒ positive correlation; = 1 ⇒ independence; < 1 ⇒ negative correlation.
- [x] Typical KTU marks split: **3 marks** for definitions, **7 marks** for Apriori mechanics, **4 marks** for rule generation + lift computation.
- [x] Master the **self-join + pruning** step — it is the most asked 7-mark sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequential Pattern Mining - Information Retrieval and Web Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Sequential Pattern Mining - Information Retrieval and Web Search # Module 1: Sequential Pattern Mining — Information Retrieval and Web Search ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sequential Pattern Mining (SPM)** is formally defined as the process of discovering *frequently occurring ordered subsequences* (called *sequential patterns*) as a pattern of occurrences or transactions in a sequence database, where the support of each pattern (i.e., the fraction of data sequences that contain it) is no less than a user-defined *minimum support threshold* (**`min_sup`**). In the context of **Information Retrieval (IR)** and **Web Search**, Sequential Pattern Mining is the analytical engine that transforms raw, time-stamped user interaction logs (clickstreams, query reformulations, navigation paths) into *ordered, statistically significant behavioural signatures*. It answers the question: > *"Given a sequence database **D**, find the complete set of frequent subsequences satisfying a minimum support threshold."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of a Movie Theatre Concession Stand Analogy:** > Imagine the manager of a multiplex records the *order* in which customers purchase items. Over thousands of transactions, she notices a striking pattern: `Popcorn → Cold Drink → Ice Cream`. This isn't random — it happens in **35%** of all purchases. This is a *sequential pattern*. She now uses it for combo offers. > > Now translate this to the Web: every time you (a) search "best laptops under 50k", (b) click the third result, (c) read reviews, and (d) add a laptop to your cart — you leave behind a *clickstream*. When millions of users repeat similar sequences, **Sequential Pattern Mining** extracts these as structured knowledge for search engines to personalize results, prefetch pages, or recommend products. ### 1.3 Why It Matters in Web Search & IR Web Search is fundamentally an **Information Retrieval** problem. Sequential Pattern Mining contributes to IR in three concrete ways: - **Query Log Mining**: Discovering frequent *query reformulation sequences* (e.g., `iPhone 14 → iPhone 14 review → iPhone 14 vs iPhone 15`) to power **query suggestion** and **auto-completion**. - **Click-Through Analysis**: Mining *URL click sequences* after a search query to learn which result positions receive long-term engagement (used by **Learning to Rank (LTR)** models). - **Session-Based Personalization**: Re-ranking search results in real-time based on the user's current session subsequence matched against historical frequent patterns. ### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary) | Term | Formal Meaning | |---|---| | **Sequence Database (D)** | A set of sequences, where each sequence is an ordered list of itemsets | | **Itemset** | A non-empty set of items that occur simultaneously | | **Element (or Transaction)** | An itemset within a sequence, often tagged with a timestamp | | **Subsequence** | A sequence **S'** contained within another sequence **S** if **S'** can be derived by deleting some items/elements from **S** | | **Support, sup(S)** | The fraction of sequences in **D** that contain **S** as a subsequence | | **Frequent Sequence** | A sequence whose support ≥ **`min_sup`** | | **Sequential Pattern** | A frequent sequence with semantic/utility value beyond frequency | | **min_conf** | The minimum confidence threshold for generating sequential rules | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture: The Three-Stage Pipeline Sequential Pattern Mining in Web Search follows a strict three-stage logical pipeline: ### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition & Preprocessing - **Input Sources**: Web server access logs, query logs, click logs, browser cookies, e-commerce transaction logs. - **Cleaning**: Remove bot traffic, deduplicate entries, handle sessionization (grouping user actions into *sessions* using a **30-minute inactivity timeout** rule). - **Transformation**: Map each session to an ordered sequence of itemsets. ### Stage 2 — Candidate Generation & Support Counting - **Candidate Generation**: Enumerate candidate sequences of length k using the **apriori-gen** join or **pattern-growth** (e.g., **PrefixSpan**) methodology. - **Support Counting**: Scan the database (or projected databases) to count occurrences. - **Pruning**: Eliminate candidates whose support < `min_sup`. ### Stage 3 — Pattern Output & Application - Output the set of all frequent sequences. - Optionally generate **sequential rules** of the form **X → Y** where: - `support(X ∪ Y) ≥ min_sup` - `confidence(X → Y) = support(X ∪ Y) / support(X) ≥ min_conf` ### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Apriori Property for Sequences > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sequential Apriori Property (Anti-Monotonicity):** > *If a sequence S is infrequent, every super-sequence (longer sequence containing S) must also be infrequent.* > > **Why?** Because the support function is anti-monotonic with respect to subsequence containment: any database containing S as a subsequence must also contain every subsequence of S. Hence support can only **decrease or stay equal** as we add items. This single property allows **pruning of the exponential search space** (from $O(2^n \cdot n!)$ candidate space down to a tractable frequent subspace). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | KTU Usage | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Support of a Sequence** | $sup(S) = \dfrac{\vert \{ T \in D : S \subseteq T \} \vert}{\vert D \vert}$ | Decides if a pattern is frequent | | 2 | **Confidence of a Sequential Rule** | $conf(X \rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{sup(X \cup Y)}{sup(X)}$ | Filters strong rules from frequent patterns | | 3 | **Lift of a Sequential Rule** | $lift(X \rightarrow Y) = \dfrac{conf(X \rightarrow Y)}{sup(Y)}$ | Measures rule interestingness beyond independence | | 4 | **Apriori Pruning Bound** | $\forall T \supseteq S, \quad sup(T) \leq sup(S)$ | Justifies candidate elimination | | 5 | **Search Space Bound (Upper)** | $\sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k} \cdot k! \leq \lfloor e \cdot n! \rfloor$ | Worst-case enumeration size for n items | | 6 | **PrefixSpan Projected DB Size** | $\vert DB \vert \geq \sum_{i} \vert DB_i^{proj} \vert$ | Recursive growth bound | | 7 | **TF-IDF (IR Bridge)** | $tf\text{-}idf(t,d) = tf(t,d) \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{N}{df(t)}\right)$ | Links SPM scoring to document relevance | | 8 | **Session Timeout Standard** | $\Delta t = 30 \text{ minutes}$ | Industry default for sessionization | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Sequential patterns extracted from web logs directly feed into **Recommendation Engines** (Amazon, Netflix), **Search Re-ranking** (Google's "People also search for"), **Web Prefetching** (caching pages users will visit next), and **Anomaly Detection** (fraud, intrusion detection). ### 2.4 The Core Algorithms (KTU High-Yield) | Algorithm | Paradigm | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---|---| | **GSP (Generalized Sequential Patterns)** | Apriori-based, candidate generation | Simple, multi-level mining | Multiple DB scans, high I/O | | **PrefixSpan** | Pattern-growth, projected DB | No candidate generation, efficient | Memory-heavy for large DBs | | **SPADE** | Vertical format, lattice-based | Uses join on ID-lists | Memory pressure | | **FreeSpan** | Frequent pattern projection | Early PrefixSpan variant | Sub-pattern overlap | | **ClaSP (CloSpan)** | Closed sequential pattern mining | Compact output | More compute | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: GSP Algorithm > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Setup (KTU Board Style):** > Consider a sequence database **D** of **4 customer sessions** at an e-commerce portal. Each session records the *items bought together* (itemset) in a *temporal order*. The minimum support threshold is **`min_sup = 2`** (i.e., 50%). **Given Database D:** | Sequence ID | Sequence of Itemsets | |---|---| | S1 | `<{Bread, Butter}>` → `<{Milk}>` → `<{Eggs}>` | | S2 | `<{Bread}>` → `<{Milk, Eggs}>` | | S3 | `<{Bread, Butter}>` → `<{Eggs}>` | | S4 | `<{Bread}>` → `<{Butter}>` → `<{Milk}>` | **Mathematically:** $D = \{ S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4 \}$, $|D| = 4$ --- ### Step 1 — First Pass: Find Frequent 1-Sequences (L₁) We count support of every singleton itemset (size-1 itemsets): $$ \text{support}(\{Bread\}) = \frac{\vert \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\} \vert}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.00 $$ $$ \text{support}(\{Butter\}) = \frac{\vert \{S_1, S_3, S_4\} \vert}{4} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 $$ $$ \text{support}(\{Milk\}) = \frac{\vert \{S_1, S_2, S_4\} \vert}{4} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 $$ $$ \text{support}(\{Eggs\}) = \frac{\vert \{S_1, S_2, S_3\} \vert}{4} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 $$ Since `min_sup = 2` (or 50%), **all four items are frequent**. $$ L_1 = \{ \langle Bread \rangle, \langle Butter \rangle, \langle Milk \rangle, \langle Eggs \rangle \} $$ **Explanation:** Every item appears in at least 2 sequences, so none are pruned. They become the seed set for length-2 candidate generation. --- ### Step 2 — Candidate Generation for 2-Sequences (C₂) Using the **apriori-gen join** on L₁ (treating each length-1 sequence as a sortable item), we generate two types of 2-sequences: **Type A — Two consecutive elements of size 1** (e.g., `` → ``): We join L₁ with itself where the join key allows sequence extension. **Type B — One element of size 2** (e.g., `<{Bread, Butter}>`): We combine pairs of frequent items into a single 2-element itemset. Number of candidates: $$ \vert C_2 \vert = \binom{4}{2} \cdot 2 + \binom{4}{2} = 6 \cdot 2 + 6 = 18 \text{ candidates} $$ The first term counts ordered 2-element sequences; the second counts 2-item itemsets. --- ### Step 3 — Support Counting for C₂ (Selected High-Yield Candidates) **Candidate:** `` → `` (two consecutive single-item elements) $$ \text{support}(\langle Bread \rangle \rightarrow \langle Milk \rangle) = \frac{\vert \{S_1, S_2, S_4\} \vert}{4} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 $$ S1 contains `<{Bread, Butter}>` first then `<{Milk}>` — Bread appears before Milk ✓. S2 contains `<{Bread}>` then `<{Milk, Eggs}>` — Bread before Milk ✓. S4 contains `<{Bread}>` then `<{Butter}>` then `<{Milk}>` — Bread before Milk ✓. S3 contains `<{Bread, Butter}>` then `<{Eggs}>` — Bread before Milk? **No**, Milk never appears in S3 ✗. $$ \therefore \text{ support} = 0.75 \quad (\text{Frequent}) $$ **Candidate:** `<{Bread, Butter}>` (single 2-item itemset) $$ \text{support}(\langle \{Bread, Butter\} \rangle) = \frac{\vert \{S_1, S_3, S_4\} \vert}{4} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 $$ **S1**: `<{Bread, Butter}>` ✓ **S3**: `<{Bread, Butter}>` ✓ **S4**: Contains Bread in `<{Bread}>` and Butter in `<{Butter}>` — but **not in the same itemset**. So `<{Bread, Butter}>` is NOT a subsequence of S4 ✗. **Re-evaluation:** $$ \text{support}(\langle \{Bread, Butter\} \rangle) = \frac{\vert \{S_1, S_3\} \vert}{4} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.50 $$ Still meets `min_sup = 2` → **Frequent**. $$ L_2 = \{ \langle Bread \rangle \rightarrow \langle Milk \rangle, \langle Bread \rangle \rightarrow \langle Eggs \rangle, \langle \{Bread, Butter\} \rangle, \ldots \} $$ (The full enumeration yields 11 frequent 2-sequences after pruning.) --- ### Step 4 — Apriori Pruning Verification Verify the **anti-monotonicity principle** with a candidate: **Candidate:** `` → `` → `` Check support: $$ \text{support}(\langle Bread \rangle \rightarrow \langle Milk \rangle \rightarrow \langle Eggs \rangle) = \frac{\vert \{S_1, S_2\} \vert}{4} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.50 $$ **Frequent.** Note: its subsequence `` → `` (skipping Milk) also has support = 0.50. The pruning bound holds. --- ### Step 5 — Sequential Rule Generation For the frequent pattern $\langle Bread \rangle \rightarrow \langle Milk \rangle$ (support = 0.75), generate rule $\langle Bread \rangle \rightarrow \langle Milk \rangle$: $$ \text{confidence}(\langle Bread \rangle \rightarrow \langle Milk \rangle) = \frac{\text{support}(\langle Bread \rangle \rightarrow \langle Milk \rangle)}{\text{support}(\langle Bread \rangle)} = \frac{0.75}{1.00} = 0.75 $$ If `min_conf = 0.6`, this is a **strong rule**. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: IR Techniques vs Sequential Mining | Feature | Classic IR (TF-IDF / BM25) | Sequential Pattern Mining | |---|---|---| | **Input Data** | Static document collection | Time-ordered event log | | **Core Question** | "Which doc matches query Q?" | "What sequence comes after S?" | | **Ordering** | Not inherently ordered | **Order is critical** | | **Output** | Ranked document list | Set of frequent sequences + rules | | **Application** | Web search engines, digital libraries | Click prediction, recommendations | | **Algorithm Type** | Vector space / probabilistic | Apriori / pattern-growth / vertical | | **Scalability Tool** | Inverted index | Projected DB / lattice / FP-Tree | | **Evaluation Metric** | Precision@K, MAP, NDCG | Support, Confidence, Lift | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The SPM-IR Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Web Logs
Server, Query, Click] --> B[Data Cleaning
Bot Filter, Dedup] B --> C[Sessionization
30-min Timeout Rule] C --> D[Sequence Database D
Set of Ordered Itemsets] D --> E[Candidate Generation
apriori-gen or Prefix-Project] E --> F[Support Counting
sup S greater or equal min_sup] F --> G{Pruning Step
Anti-Monotonicity Check} G -->|Frequent| H[Pattern Output
Sequential Rules X implies Y] G -->|Infrequent| X[Discarded Candidate] H --> I[IR Application Layer] I --> J[Query Suggestion] I --> K[Result Re-Ranking] I --> L[Recommendation Engine] I --> M[Web Prefetching Cache] ``` ## 4.2 Apriori-Based GSP Mining Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Input Sequence Database D] --> S2[Scan 1
Find Frequent 1-Sequences L1] S2 --> S3[Generate C2 from L1
apriori-gen join] S3 --> S4[Scan 2
Count Support for C2] S4 --> S5{Prune
support less than min_sup} S5 -->|Keep| S6[Form Frequent L2] S5 -->|Drop| S3 S6 --> S7[Generate C3 from L2] S7 --> S8[Scan 3
Support Count for C3] S8 --> S9{Stop Condition
Lk equals empty set} S9 -->|Yes| S10[Output All Frequent Sequences] S9 -->|No| S7 ``` ## 4.3 Sequential Pattern → IR Application Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Mining Layer P1[Frequent Click Patterns] P2[Query Reformulation Chains] P3[Navigation Path Sequences] end subgraph IR Search Layer Q1[Result Ranking Engine] Q2[Auto-Complete Module] Q3[Personalization Filter] Q4[Cache Prefetcher] end P1 --> Q3 P1 --> Q4 P2 --> Q2 P2 --> Q1 P3 --> Q1 P3 --> Q4 Q1 --> R[Final Ranked Search Results] Q2 --> R Q3 --> R Q4 --> R ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The Mining Layer produces structured knowledge; the IR Search Layer consumes it to enhance user-facing search functionality. Arrows represent data flow, not control flow. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Define Sequential Pattern Mining. List any two applications of it in Web Search.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **(1 Mark)** Sequential Pattern Mining is the data mining task of discovering frequent ordered subsequences in a sequence database whose support exceeds a user-defined minimum support threshold. - **(1 Mark)** Application 1: **Query Log Mining** — extracting frequent query reformulation sequences to power auto-suggest and related-search features. - **(1 Mark)** Application 2: **Click-Stream Analysis for Re-ranking** — using mined URL click patterns as features in Learning-to-Rank models to improve result ordering. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: What is the Apriori property in the context of sequential pattern mining? Why is it important?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **(1 Mark)** The Apriori property (anti-monotonicity) states: *if a sequence S is infrequent, then every super-sequence containing S must also be infrequent.* - **(1 Mark)** Mathematically: $sup(T) \leq sup(S)$ whenever $S \subseteq T$. - **(1 Mark)** **Importance:** It enables aggressive pruning of the candidate search space, reducing complexity from exponential to manageable levels, making algorithms like GSP computationally feasible. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Sequential Pattern Mining system for Web Search. Discuss how mined patterns enhance Information Retrieval. > **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following sequence database **D** with `min_sup = 2`. Apply the **GSP algorithm** step-by-step to find all frequent sequences. | SID | Sequence | |---|---| | 10 | `<{a,b}>` → `<{c}>` → `<{d}>` | | 20 | `<{a}>` → `<{b,c}>` → `<{d}>` | | 30 | `<{a,b}>` → `<{c}>` | | 40 | `<{a}>` → `<{b}>` → `<{c,d}>` | #### **Model Solution (a) [7 Marks]:** **Architecture Diagram Description (3 Marks):** The Sequential Pattern Mining system for Web Search consists of four layers: 1. **Data Source Layer** — Web server logs, query logs, click logs. 2. **Preprocessing Layer** — Sessionization (30-min timeout), noise removal, item encoding. 3. **Mining Engine Layer** — Apriori/Pattern-Growth/Vertical-format miner. 4. **Application Layer** — Query suggestion, re-ranking, recommendation, prefetching. **How Mined Patterns Enhance IR (4 Marks):** - **Query Suggestion:** When a user types `<{laptop}>` → mined pattern `<{laptop}>` → `<{laptop review}>` auto-suggests next query. - **Re-ranking:** If historical pattern shows users who click result #2 after `<{python tutorial}>` eventually buy a course, that result is boosted. - **Personalization:** Session subsequence matched against frequent patterns personalizes SERP (Search Engine Results Page). - **Prefetching:** Web pages appearing later in a frequent sequence are cached in advance, reducing latency. #### **Model Solution (b) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 — Count 1-Sequences (1.5 Marks):** $$ sup(\{a\}) = 4/4 = 1.00 \quad \sup(\{b\}) = 4/4 = 1.00 $$ $$ sup(\{c\}) = 4/4 = 1.00 \quad \sup(\{d\}) = 3/4 = 0.75 $$ All ≥ `min_sup = 2`. So $L_1 = \{a, b, c, d\}$. **Step 2 — Generate C₂ and Count (2.5 Marks):** | Candidate | Occurrences | Support | Frequent? | |---|---|---|---| | `` → `` | S1, S2, S3, S4 | 1.00 | ✓ | | `` → `` | S1, S2, S3, S4 | 1.00 | ✓ | | `` → `` | S1, S2, S4 | 0.75 | ✓ | | `` → `` | S1, S2, S3, S4 | 1.00 | ✓ | | `` → `` | S1, S2, S4 | 0.75 | ✓ | | `` → `` | S1, S2, S4 | 0.75 | ✓ | | `<{a,b}>` | S1, S2, S3 | 0.75 | ✓ | | `<{a,c}>` | S1, S2 | 0.50 | ✓ | | `<{b,c}>` | S2, S3 | 0.50 | ✓ | | `<{c,d}>` | S1, S4 | 0.50 | ✓ | | `<{a,d}>` | S1 | 0.25 | ✗ | | `<{b,d}>` | S1, S4 | 0.50 | ✓ | All 11 candidates except `<{a,d}>` survive pruning. **Step 3 — Generate C₃ (2 Marks):** Join frequent L₂ elements. Key candidates: - `` → `` → ``: support = 3/4 = 0.75 ✓ - `` → ``: support = 2/4 = 0.50 ✓ - `` → ``: support = 2/4 = 0.50 ✓ - `` → `` → ``: support = 2/4 = 0.50 ✓ - `` → `` → ``: support = 2/4 = 0.50 ✓ **Step 4 — Generate C₄ (1 Mark):** - `` → `` → `` → ``: support = 1/4 = 0.25 ✗ (Pruned by Apriori) **Final Frequent Set Output (Free):** $L = \{ a, b, c, d, a \rightarrow b, a \rightarrow c, b \rightarrow c, \{a,b\}, \{b,c\}, \{c,d\}, a \rightarrow b \rightarrow c, a \rightarrow b \rightarrow c \rightarrow d\ (\text{pruned}) \}$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the GSP algorithm and the PrefixSpan algorithm for sequential pattern mining. Discuss at least three points with relevance to Web Mining. > **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** and **Session-based features** in using sequential patterns for personalized web search. Derive the support-confidence framework mathematically. #### **Model Solution (a) [7 Marks]:** **Comparison Table (5 Marks):** | Aspect | GSP | PrefixSpan | |---|---|---| | **Paradigm** | Apriori (candidate generation) | Pattern-growth (divide & conquer) | | **Database Scans** | Multiple (one per length k) | Reduced (via projected DBs) | | **Candidate Set** | Explicit, large | None — grows on projected DB | | **Memory Usage** | Lower (candidates only) | Higher (stores projections) | | **Web Mining Fit** | Good for moderate logs | Better for large-scale click streams | **Three Web-Mining-Relevant Points (2 Marks):** 1. **Scalability:** PrefixSpan avoids candidate explosion, critical when mining billion-scale query logs. 2. **Memory vs Time Trade-off:** GSP is better when memory-constrained; PrefixSpan when time-constrained. 3. **Pattern Length:** PrefixSpan discovers long patterns (long user sessions) more efficiently due to projected growth. #### **Model Solution (b) [7 Marks]:** **CTR-Based Feature Engineering (3 Marks):** - **Click-Through Rate** at position *i* after query *q* measures user engagement. - Sequence mining treats each search session as: `<{q}>` → `<{clicked URL at position 1}>` → `<{clicked URL at position 3}>` → ... - Patterns like `<{q}>` → `<{URL_X}>` → `<{URL_Y, time spent > 30s}>` reveal *satisfied* sessions used for re-ranking. **Support-Confidence Mathematical Derivation (4 Marks):** Given sequence database **D** of N sessions, a sequence **S** is *frequent* if: $$ sup(S) = \frac{\vert \{ T \in D : S \subseteq T \} \vert}{N} \geq \text{min\_sup} $$ For a sequential rule $X \rightarrow Y$ (where X, Y are subsequences and $X \cap Y = \emptyset$): $$ conf(X \rightarrow Y) = \frac{sup(X \cup Y)}{sup(X)} \geq \text{min\_conf} $$ **Lift** quantifies independence violation: $$ \text{lift}(X \rightarrow Y) = \frac{conf(X \rightarrow Y)}{sup(Y)} = \frac{sup(X \cup Y)}{sup(X) \cdot sup(Y)} $$ If `lift > 1`, X positively influences Y; if `lift = 1`, X and Y are statistically independent; if `lift < 1`, X negatively correlates with Y. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing support count vs support fraction:** Always state both — "appears in 2 out of 4 sequences" AND "support = 0.5". > 2. **Itemset vs Sequence confusion:** A single itemset `{Bread, Butter}` is ONE element of a sequence, NOT two elements. > 3. **Subsequence matching mistake:** Order matters strictly — `` → `` is NOT contained in `` → ``. > 4. **Forgetting to apply Apriori pruning:** Marks are deducted when students enumerate all candidates without eliminating based on infrequent subsequences. > 5. **Missing timestamp/session context:** In Web Mining questions, always clarify the *session boundary* (30-min rule) before mining. > 6. **Writing algorithms in code:** KTU theory papers expect **pseudo-code or step-by-step narrative**, not Python/Java implementations. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **SPM Definition:** Frequent ordered subsequences with support ≥ `min_sup`. - ✅ **Three-Stage Pipeline:** Data → Candidate Generation → Support Counting → Output. - ✅ **Apriori Property:** Anti-monotonicity — infrequency propagates to super-sequences. - ✅ **Core Formula Set:** - $sup(S) = \frac{\text{count}(S)}{N}$ - $conf(X \rightarrow Y) = \frac{sup(X \cup Y)}{sup(X)}$ - $lift(X \rightarrow Y) = \frac{sup(X \cup Y)}{sup(X) \cdot sup(Y)}$ - ✅ **Algorithms to Know:** GSP (Apriori-based), PrefixSpan (pattern-growth), SPADE (vertical), CloSpan (closed patterns). - ✅ **Web Search Applications:** Query suggestion, click-through re-ranking, session personalization, web prefetching. - ✅ **Sessionization Rule:** 30-minute inactivity timeout is the industrial default. - ✅ **IR Bridge Concepts:** TF-IDF, BM25, Learning-to-Rank features consume mined sequential patterns. - ✅ **Evaluation Metrics:** Support, Confidence, Lift — all anti-monotonic w.r.t. subsequence inclusion. - ✅ **Memory vs Time Trade-off:** Apriori = less memory / more scans; PrefixSpan = more memory / fewer scans. ---

topics.title: Information Retrieval Models, Relevance Feedback, Text and Web Page Pre-Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
