> The student must be able to explain the **architecture of a search engine**, differentiate **crawling strategies**, construct an **inverted index**, and mathematically derive **ranking algorithms** (PageRank, HITS, TF-IDF).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Web Search

- **Crawl Frontier Size:** typically $10^{10}$ URLs for a major search engine.
- **Average Query Latency Target:** **< 500 ms**.
- **Index Freshness:** major search engines aim for sub-minute updates on breaking news.
- **Average Inverted Index Posting Length:** **50–100 bytes per posting**.
- **PageRank Damping Factor ($d$):** conventionally set to **0.85** (Brin & Page, 1998).

## 1.4 Why Web Search is Hard

The web violates almost every classical IR assumption:

| Classical IR Assumption | Reality on the Web |
|---|---|
| Static corpus | Pages are added/removed constantly |
| Small, curated documents | Billions of heterogeneous pages |
| Trustworthy content | Spam, misinformation, cloaking |
| Authoritative metadata | Sparse, manipulated, or missing metadata |
| Single language | Multilingual, multimodal content |

This is precisely why **Web Mining** (the parent course) exists — to mine structure, content, and usage patterns from this chaotic graph.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Pipeline of a Search Engine

A modern search engine executes the retrieval task through three tightly coupled subsystems:

### Stage 1 — Crawling (Data Acquisition)
A **Web Crawler** (also called *spider*, *bot*, *agent*) systematically traverses the web graph starting from a seed set of URLs.

**Operational Logic (Bulleted):**
- Begin with a **seed list** $S$ of URLs (e.g., popular directories, sitemaps).
- Fetch the HTML of each URL via the **HTTP protocol**, obey `robots.txt`.
- Extract all hyperlinks via **HTML parsing** (anchor tags `<a href="…">`).
- Push newly discovered URLs into a **frontier queue**, often prioritized by:
  - **URL ordering heuristics** (depth-first, breadth-first, or focused).
  - **Page importance** (e.g., PageRank estimation during crawl).
- Apply **politeness policies** (rate-limiting per host) and **freshness scheduling**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Focused Crawling** (Chakrabarti et al., 1999) restricts the crawler to pages relevant to a predefined topic using a **classifier** (e.g., Naive Bayes) that scores candidate URLs before fetching. This is highly testable in KTU exams.

### Stage 2 — Indexing (Data Structuring)
The fetched documents are transformed into an **Inverted Index** — a data structure that maps every term (word) to the list of documents containing it.

**Term-Document Matrix view:**

For a vocabulary $V$ of size $\vert V \vert$ and corpus of $N$ documents, we form a $\vert V \vert \times N$ matrix $M$ where $M_{t,d}$ is the frequency of term $t$ in document $d$. The **inverted index** is the *transpose* of $M$, stored as a postings list.

**Indexing Steps:**
1. **Tokenization** — split text into terms.
2. **Stop-word Removal** — discard frequent words like *the, is, and*.
3. **Stemming / Lemmatization** — reduce to root form (*running → run*).
4. **Posting List Construction** — for each term, store a sorted list of `(docID, term-frequency)`.
5. **Compression** — apply **gap encoding + variable-byte coding** (or **PForDelta**) to fit trillion-posting indices in RAM.

### Stage 3 — Ranking (Retrieval & Scoring)
Given query $q$, compute $score(q, d)$ for each candidate document $d$, then return the top-$k$.

Three families of signals are fused:

| Signal Family | Examples | Weight in Modern Engines |
|---|---|---|
| **Content-based** (text similarity) | TF-IDF, BM25, Embedding cosine | High |
| **Link-based** (graph structure) | PageRank, HITS, SALSA | Medium–High |
| **Behavioral** (click logs) | Click-Through Rate (CTR), dwell time | High |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table.** Every formula below is a guaranteed appearance in KTU University Exam Part B questions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log \left(\dfrac{N}{df_t}\right)$ | **TF-IDF** weight of term $t$ in document $d$ | dimensionless $\geq 0$ |
| 2 | $tf\text{-}idf(t,d) = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{N+1}{df_t+1}\right) + 1$ | **Smoothed TF-IDF** (Lucene classic) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{BM25}(t,d) = \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \dfrac{tf_{t,d} \cdot (k_1+1)}{tf_{t,d} + k_1 \cdot \left(1 - b + b \cdot \dfrac{\vert d \vert}{avgdl}\right)}$ | **BM25** scoring (Robertson–Walker) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{IDF}(t) = \log \dfrac{N - df_t + 0.5}{df_t + 0.5}$ | BM25 inverse document frequency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{sim}(q,d) = \dfrac{\vec{q} \cdot \vec{d}}{\vert \vec{q} \vert \cdot \vert d \vert}$ | **Cosine Similarity** | $[-1, 1]$, usually $[0,1]$ |
| 6 | $PR(u) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{v \in B(u)} \dfrac{PR(v)}{L(v)}$ | **PageRank** of page $u$ | $0$ to $1$ (normalized) |
| 7 | $\mathbf{p} = d \cdot \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{p} + \dfrac{1-d}{N}\mathbf{1}$ | **PageRank Matrix Form** | stationary distribution |
| 8 | $A(p) = \sum_{q: p \to q} H(q)$ and $H(p) = \sum_{q: q \to p} A(q)$ | **HITS Authority & Hub** | iteratively computed |
| 9 | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Fraction of retrieved that are relevant | $[0,1]$ |
| 10 | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Fraction of relevant that are retrieved | $[0,1]$ |
| 11 | $F_\beta = (1+\beta^2) \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | **F-measure** (harmonic mean) | $[0,1]$ |
| 12 | $\text{DCG}_p = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \dfrac{rel_i}{\log_2(i+1)}$ | **Discounted Cumulative Gain** at rank $p$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\text{NDCG}_p = \dfrac{DCG_p}{IDCG_p}$ | **Normalized DCG** | $[0,1]$ |

**Boundary Conditions & Conventions:**
- $d \in (0,1)$, standard PageRank damping **$d = 0.85$**.
- BM25 parameters typically $k_1 \in [1.2,\, 2.0]$, $b \in [0,\, 1]$ (often $b = 0.75$).
- $N$ = total documents in corpus, $df_t$ = document frequency of term $t$, $tf_{t,d}$ = term frequency of $t$ in $d$.
- $B(u)$ = set of pages linking **to** $u$; $L(v)$ = out-degree of $v$.
- $\vert d \vert$ = length of document $d$ in words; $avgdl$ = average document length.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BM25 + PageRank fusion** powers the core ranking of virtually every production search engine.
- **PageRank** is also used in **recommender systems**, **Twitter follower ranking**, **chemical molecule importance**, and **analyzing citation networks**.
- **Cosine similarity on TF-IDF vectors** is foundational to **document clustering**, **near-duplicate detection**, and **plagiarism checkers**.
- **HITS** underlies **Topical PageRank variants** and **community detection** in social graphs.
- **NDCG** is the standard ranking-quality metric used in KTU lab evaluations and in academic competitions like **TREC**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: PageRank

PageRank models a **random surfer** who, at any time step, either:
- With probability $d$, clicks a random outgoing link from the current page.
- With probability $(1-d)$, "teleports" to a uniformly random page on the web.

Let $PR(u)$ denote the stationary probability that the surfer is on page $u$. For every page $u$ that receives links from a set of pages $B(u)$ (the **backlink set**), the probability of arriving at $u$ via a link from any $v \in B(u)$ is the fraction of $v$'s PageRank distributed to each of its outgoing links, i.e., $PR(v)/L(v)$ where $L(v)$ is the out-degree of $v$.

**Step 1 — Write the recurrence relation:**
The total probability flowing into $u$ via random-link-clicks is $\sum_{v \in B(u)} \dfrac{PR(v)}{L(v)}$.

**Step 2 — Add the teleportation contribution:**
With probability $(1-d)$, the surfer teleports uniformly to any of the $N$ pages, giving an additional $\dfrac{(1-d)}{N}$ probability mass to each page.

**Step 3 — Combine to form the PageRank equation:**
$$ PR(u) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{v \in B(u)} \dfrac{PR(v)}{L(v)} $$

**Step 4 — Express in matrix form:**
Let $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$ be the column vector of PageRanks, and $\mathbf{M}$ be the **stochastic transition matrix** where $M_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{L(j)}$ if $j \to i$, else $0$. Then:

$$ \mathbf{p} = d \cdot \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{p} + \dfrac{1-d}{N}\mathbf{1} $$

**Step 5 — Solve via the power-iteration method:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{p}^{(0)} &:= \tfrac{1}{N}\mathbf{1} \\[4pt]
\mathbf{p}^{(k+1)} &:= d \cdot \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{p}^{(k)} + \dfrac{1-d}{N}\mathbf{1} \\[4pt]
\text{Stop when } \Vert \mathbf{p}^{(k+1)} - \mathbf{p}^{(k)} \Vert_1 &< \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why does this converge?** Because $\mathbf{M}$ is *stochastic* (rows of the Google matrix $G = d\mathbf{M} + \tfrac{1-d}{N}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T$ sum to 1) and *primitive* (strictly positive due to the teleportation term), the **Perron–Frobenius theorem** guarantees a unique stationary distribution exists and is reached from any starting vector.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — TF-IDF

**Corpus:** $C = \{d_1, d_2, d_3\}$, $N = 3$
- $d_1$ = *"the cat sat on the mat"*
- $d_2$ = *"the dog sat on the rug"*
- $d_3$ = *"the cat chased the dog"*

**Query:** $q$ = *"cat dog"*

**Step 1 — Build vocabulary $V$:** $\{$*the, cat, sat, on, mat, dog, rug, chased$\}$, $\vert V \vert = 8$.

**Step 2 — Compute document frequencies $df_t$:**

| Term | $df_t$ |
|---|---|
| the | 3 |
| cat | 2 |
| sat | 2 |
| on | 2 |
| mat | 1 |
| dog | 2 |
| rug | 1 |
| chased | 1 |

**Step 3 — Compute TF (raw counts) for query terms in each document:**

| Term | $tf$ in $d_1$ | $tf$ in $d_2$ | $tf$ in $d_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| cat | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| dog | 0 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 4 — Compute IDF using $idf(t) = \log(N / df_t)$ (base 10):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
idf(\text{cat}) &= \log(3/2) = 0.1761 \\
idf(\text{dog}) &= \log(3/2) = 0.1761
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute TF-IDF weights $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot idf(t)$:**

| Term | $w_{t,d_1}$ | $w_{t,d_2}$ | $w_{t,d_3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| cat | $1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761$ | $0$ | $1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761$ |
| dog | $0$ | $1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761$ | $1 \times 0.1761 = 0.1761$ |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Cosine Similarity

Using TF-IDF vectors restricted to query terms $\{$*cat*, *dog*$\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{q} &= (1,\, 1) \quad \text{(query: both terms appear once)} \\
\vec{d_1} &= (0.1761,\, 0) \\
\vec{d_2} &= (0,\, 0.1761) \\
\vec{d_3} &= (0.1761,\, 0.1761)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Similarity with $d_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{q}\cdot \vec{d_1} &= (1)(0.1761) + (1)(0) = 0.1761 \\
\vert \vec{q} \vert &= \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142 \\
\vert \vec{d_1} \vert &= \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0^2} = 0.1761 \\
\cos(q, d_1) &= \dfrac{0.1761}{1.4142 \times 0.1761} = \dfrac{1}{1.4142} \approx 0.7071
\end{aligned}
$$

**Similarity with $d_2$:** $\cos(q, d_2) \approx 0.7071$ (by symmetry).

**Similarity with $d_3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{q}\cdot \vec{d_3} &= (1)(0.1761) + (1)(0.1761) = 0.3522 \\
\vert \vec{d_3} \vert &= \sqrt{0.1761^2 + 0.1761^2} = 0.1761\sqrt{2} \approx 0.2490 \\
\cos(q, d_3) &= \dfrac{0.3522}{1.4142 \times 0.2490} \approx \dfrac{0.3522}{0.3522} = 1.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Ranking:** $d_3 \succ d_1 \sim d_2$ — Document 3 (containing *both* query terms) is ranked highest, which is intuitively correct. ✅

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical Example — PageRank on a 4-Page Mini Web

Let the web graph be:

- $A \to B$, $A \to C$
- $B \to C$
- $C \to A$
- $D \to C$

Out-degrees: $L(A) = 2$, $L(B) = 1$, $L(C) = 1$, $L(D) = 1$.

**Transition matrix $\mathbf{M}$ (column-stochastic, columns sum to 1):**

$$
\mathbf{M} =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
\tfrac{1}{2} & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\tfrac{1}{2} & 1 & 0 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Notice $D$ has no outgoing links (a **dangling node**). Standard fix: replace the dangling column with $\tfrac{1}{N}\mathbf{1}$:

$$
\mathbf{M}' =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1 & \tfrac{1}{4} \\
\tfrac{1}{2} & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{4} \\
\tfrac{1}{2} & 1 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{4} \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{4}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Google matrix $G$ with $d = 0.85$, $N = 4$:**

$$
G = 0.85 \cdot \mathbf{M}' + \dfrac{0.15}{4}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T
$$

**Power iteration from $\mathbf{p}^{(0)} = (0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25)^T$:**

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_A^{(1)} &= 0.85(0 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25 + 1 \cdot 0.25 + 0.25 \cdot 0.25) + 0.0375(1) \\
&= 0.85(0.25 + 0.0625) + 0.0375 = 0.85(0.3125) + 0.0375 = 0.2656 + 0.0375 = 0.3031
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_B^{(1)} &= 0.85(0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25 + 0.25 \cdot 0.25) + 0.0375 \\
&= 0.85(0.125 + 0.0625) + 0.0375 = 0.85(0.1875) + 0.0375 = 0.1594 + 0.0375 = 0.1969
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_C^{(1)} &= 0.85(0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 1 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25 + 0.25 \cdot 0.25) + 0.0375 \\
&= 0.85(0.125 + 0.25 + 0.0625) + 0.0375 = 0.85(0.4375) + 0.0375 = 0.3719 + 0.0375 = 0.4094
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_D^{(1)} &= 0.85(0 + 0 + 0 + 0.25 \cdot 0.25) + 0.0375 = 0.85(0.0625) + 0.0375 = 0.0531 + 0.0375 = 0.0906
\end{aligned}
$$

**After normalization (sum = 1.0):** $\mathbf{p}^{(1)} \approx (0.3031, 0.1969, 0.4094, 0.0906)^T$.

**Interpretation:** Page $C$ is the most authoritative (it is linked from $A$, $B$, and $D$), and Page $D$ is least authoritative (only the dangling node, salvaged only by teleportation).

> [!NOTE]
> In an exam, you need **only 2–3 iterations** to demonstrate convergence behavior. Always show the normalization step.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Search Engine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user1[User / Browser]
    qs[Query Submission Interface]
    qp[Query Processor - tokenize, expand, rewrite]
    ranker[Ranker - BM25 + PageRank + HITS + Click signals]
    idx[(Inverted Index - posting lists)]
    crawl[Crawler - HTTP fetcher, robots.txt aware]
    parser[HTML Parser - DOM tree, anchor extractor]
    front[Crawl Frontier - priority queue]
    indexer[Indexer - tokenizer, stemmer, posting merger]
    storage[(Document Store - raw HTML cache)]
    logs[Click Logs - behavioral signals]
    web[(World Wide Web)]
    user1 --> qs --> qp --> ranker
    ranker -. reads .- idx
    web --> crawl --> parser --> indexer
    parser -. push URLs .- front
    front -. next URL .- crawl
    indexer --> idx
    indexer --> storage
    logs -. feedback .- ranker
```

> **Reading guide for the diagram:** The left side is the **query path** (latency-critical, must respond in milliseconds). The right side is the **crawl path** (throughput-critical, runs continuously). Click logs close the loop by feeding behavioral signals into the ranker.

---

## 4.2 Inverted Index Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawDoc1[Raw Document - HTML/PDF]
    rawDoc2[Raw Document 2]
    rawDoc3[Raw Document N]
    tok[Tokenizer]
    stop[Stop-word Filter]
    stem[Stemmer - Porter algorithm]
    norm[Term Normalization - lowercase]
    post[Posting List Builder]
    sort[Posting Sorter - by docID]
    merge[Posting Merger - across docs]
    compress[Compressor - gap + variable byte]
    disk[(Inverted Index on Disk SSD)]
    rawDoc1 --> tok
    rawDoc2 --> tok
    rawDoc3 --> tok
    tok --> stop --> stem --> norm --> post --> sort --> merge --> compress --> disk
```

---

## 4.3 HITS Algorithm — Authority–Hub Mutual Reinforcement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph baseSet[Step 1 Root Set Construction]
        root[Query Results - top 200 from BM25]
        rootSet[Root Set - R]
    end
    subgraph expand[Step 2 Base Set Expansion]
        rootSet --> addIn[Add pages linking into R - up to 1000]
        rootSet --> addOut[Add pages R links out to - up to 1000]
        baseSet[Base Set - B superset of R]
    end
    subgraph iter[Step 3 Iterative Computation]
        auth[Authority Score - a]
        hub[Hub Score - h]
        updA[Update a equals sum of incoming h]
        updH[Update h equals sum of outgoing a]
        normStep[L2 Normalize a and h]
        conv[Convergence Check]
    end
    baseSet --> auth
    baseSet --> hub
    auth --> updA --> normStep
    hub --> updH --> normStep
    normStep --> conv
    conv -. not converged .-> updA
    conv --> outTop[Top-k Authorities and Hubs]
```

---

## 4.4 PageRank Random-Surfer Markov Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    curr[Current Page u]
    click{Dice roll? - probability d equals 0.85}
    tele[Probability 1 minus d equals 0.15]
    pickLink[Pick random outgoing link from u]
    pickAny[Pick any page uniformly from N]
    next[Next Page]
    curr --> click
    click -- yes --> pickLink --> next
    click -- no --> tele --> pickAny --> next
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of a web crawler. What is a focused crawler and how does it differ from a generic crawler?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **web crawler** is an automated agent that systematically browses the WWW by following hyperlinks, with the goal of downloading web pages for indexing. Its roles include (1) **discovery** of new and updated pages, (2) **fetching** page content via HTTP, and (3) **feeding** the indexer with raw documents.

A **focused crawler** (Chakrabarti et al., 1999) is a *topically-biased* crawler that fetches only pages relevant to a predefined topic. It uses a **classifier** (e.g., Naive Bayes or SVM) trained on seed examples to score the relevance of candidate URLs *before* fetching them, thereby saving bandwidth and storage. Unlike a generic (unfocused) crawler that aims for *breadth*, a focused crawler prioritizes *depth and topical relevance*.

*(Mark split: 1.5 marks for crawler role + 1.5 marks for focused crawler explanation.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
*With a suitable example, explain TF-IDF and state its limitations.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**TF-IDF** (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency) assigns a weight to each term $t$ in a document $d$ as:

$$ w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{N}{df_t}\right) $$

**Example:** In a corpus of $N = 1000$ documents, if the term *"neural"* appears in $d_1$ ten times ($tf = 10$) and in only 5 documents in total ($df = 5$), then $idf(\text{neural}) = \log(1000/5) = 2.30$, giving $w = 10 \times 2.30 = 23.0$.

**Limitations:** (1) It treats words as **independent** (the *bag-of-words* assumption, ignoring word order and semantics). (2) It cannot capture **synonymy** (*car* vs *automobile*). (3) It is **unsupervised** with respect to relevance. (4) It fails on **short queries** due to missing terms.

*(Mark split: 1.5 marks formula + example, 1.5 marks limitations.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common 14-Mark Mistakes:**
> - Students **write the PageRank formula but forget the teleportation term** $\tfrac{1-d}{N}$. This alone costs **2 full marks**.
> - For **HITS**, students confuse **Authority** with **Hub** and define them backwards. Memorize: *Authority = cited; Hub = citing.*
> - In cosine similarity problems, students forget the **denominator** $(\Vert \vec{q}\Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{d}\Vert)$ and only compute the dot product. Always normalize.
> - For numerical PageRank, students **skip the dangling-node fix** when out-degree is zero. The examiner deducts **1 mark** for this.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **architecture of a search engine** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of each component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Construct an **inverted index** for the following three documents. Also compute the **TF-IDF** weight of each term appearing in the query $q$ = *"machine learning"*:
- $d_1$ = *"machine learning is fun"*
- $d_2$ = *"deep learning uses neural networks"*
- $d_3$ = *"machine learning and deep learning"*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture [7 Marks]:**

The architecture consists of three pipelines: **crawl pipeline**, **index pipeline**, and **query pipeline**.

1. **Crawler (1 mark):** Initiates with seed URLs, fetches pages via HTTP, obeys `robots.txt`, extracts hyperlinks. **Focused crawling** uses a classifier to bias toward relevant topics.

2. **Indexer (1 mark):** Receives raw HTML, performs tokenization → stop-word removal → stemming → builds **postings lists** → compresses and stores them in the inverted index on disk.

3. **Document Store (0.5 mark):** A compressed repository of original HTML pages for snippet generation and re-ranking.

4. **Query Processor (1 mark):** Tokenizes the user query, applies the same normalization as indexing, performs **query expansion** (synonyms, spelling correction), and may rewrite using **click-graph data**.

5. **Ranker (1 mark):** Computes $score(q,d)$ combining:
   - **Content score** (BM25 or TF-IDF cosine),
   - **Link-based score** (PageRank or HITS),
   - **Freshness & quality signals** (HTTPS, mobile-friendly, content quality),
   - **Personalization & context** (location, device, search history).

6. **Results Page (0.5 mark):** Generates the SERP (Search Engine Results Page) with **snippets, titles, URLs, and rich features** (knowledge panels, featured snippets).

7. **Click Logs (1 mark):** Capture user clicks and dwell time, which feed back into ranker training (e.g., **learning-to-rank** models like LambdaMART).

*(Mark split as labeled above.)*

---

**Part (b) — Inverted Index + TF-IDF [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary:** $\{$*machine, learning, is, fun, deep, uses, neural, networks, and*$\}$. Remove stop words (*is, and*): vocabulary becomes $\{$*machine, learning, fun, deep, uses, neural, networks*$\}$, $\vert V \vert = 7$.

**Step 2 — Document Frequencies (excluding stop words):**

| Term | $df_t$ | $idf = \log(N/df_t)$ |
|---|---|---|
| machine | 2 | $\log(3/2) = 0.1761$ |
| learning | 3 | $\log(3/3) = 0$ |
| fun | 1 | $\log(3/1) = 0.4771$ |
| deep | 2 | $\log(3/2) = 0.1761$ |
| uses | 1 | $\log(3/1) = 0.4771$ |
| neural | 1 | $\log(3/1) = 0.4771$ |
| networks | 1 | $\log(3/1) = 0.4771$ |

**Step 3 — Inverted Index (postings list) [2 marks]:**

| Term | Posting List (docID, tf) |
|---|---|
| machine | $(d_1, 1) \to (d_3, 1)$ |
| learning | $(d_1, 1) \to (d_2, 1) \to (d_3, 2)$ |
| fun | $(d_1, 1)$ |
| deep | $(d_2, 1) \to (d_3, 1)$ |
| uses | $(d_2, 1)$ |
| neural | $(d_2, 1)$ |
| networks | $(d_2, 1)$ |

**Step 4 — TF-IDF for query terms [3 marks]:**

- For *machine*: $tf_{machine,q} = 1$, $idf = 0.1761$, so $w = 1 \times 0.1761 = \mathbf{0.1761}$.
- For *learning*: $tf_{learning,q} = 1$, $idf = 0$, so $w = 1 \times 0 = \mathbf{0}$.

**Interpretation:** The term *"learning"* receives a TF-IDF weight of **zero** because it appears in *every* document — it carries no discriminative power. Only *"machine"* contributes to ranking. This is precisely why **IDF is essential**: it penalizes ubiquitous terms.

*(Mark split: 1 mark index construction, 1 mark df table, 2 marks idf computation, 3 marks final TF-IDF + interpretation.)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 / Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **PageRank algorithm**. Derive its mathematical formulation starting from the random-surfer model. State clearly the role of the **damping factor $d$** and the **teleportation mechanism**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following web graph with 4 pages. The directed edges are:
$A \to B$, $A \to C$, $B \to D$, $C \to B$, $C \to D$, $D \to A$.
Using $d = 0.85$, perform **two iterations** of PageRank from a uniform initial vector $\mathbf{p}^{(0)} = (0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25)^T$. State the final ranking of pages.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — PageRank Derivation [7 Marks]:**

The **Random Surfer Model** assumes a hypothetical web user who:
- At any page $u$, with probability $d$ clicks one of the outgoing links chosen **uniformly at random**.
- With probability $(1-d)$, becomes "bored" and teleports to a **uniformly random** page on the web.

The **PageRank** of a page $u$, denoted $PR(u)$, is defined as the **stationary probability** that the surfer is on $u$ in the long run.

For every page $v$ that links to $u$, the probability mass reaching $u$ via that link is $PR(v)/L(v)$. Summing over all such $v \in B(u)$ (the set of pages linking *to* $u$, i.e., the **backlink set**):

$$
PR(u) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{v \in B(u)} \dfrac{PR(v)}{L(v)}
$$

**Role of damping factor $d$ (2 marks):**
- $d = 0.85$ (Brin–Page default) ensures the Markov chain is **primitive** and hence the PageRank vector is **unique** by the Perron–Frobenius theorem.
- It models the empirical fact that real web users do **not** follow links forever — they eventually jump.
- Without teleportation, pages in **dead-ends** (no outgoing links) and **spider traps** (cycles) would absorb all probability mass.

**Teleportation mechanism (2 marks):**
The $\tfrac{1-d}{N}$ term guarantees that **every** page receives a non-zero baseline probability, preventing rank sinks and ensuring convergence.

**Matrix form (1 mark):**
$\mathbf{p} = d \cdot \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{p} + \dfrac{1-d}{N}\mathbf{1}$, solved by **power iteration**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical PageRank [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Identify out-degrees:**
$L(A) = 2$, $L(B) = 1$, $L(C) = 2$, $L(D) = 1$. Sum of out-degrees $= 6$.

**Step 2 — Build transition matrix $\mathbf{M}$ (column-stochastic).** For column $j$, $M_{ij} = 1/L(j)$ if $j \to i$, else $0$:

$$
\mathbf{M} =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
1/2 & 0 & 1/2 & 0 \\
1/2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 1/2 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

No dangling nodes, so no column replacement needed.

**Step 3 — Apply Google matrix formula** $G = 0.85 \mathbf{M} + 0.0375 \mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T$ (where $0.0375 = 0.15/4$).

**Step 4 — Iteration 1 from $\mathbf{p}^{(0)} = (0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25)^T$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_A^{(1)} &= 0.85(0 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25 + 1 \cdot 0.25) + 0.0375(1) \\
&= 0.85(0.25) + 0.0375 = 0.2125 + 0.0375 = 0.2500 \\[4pt]
p_B^{(1)} &= 0.85(0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25 + 0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25) + 0.0375 \\
&= 0.85(0.125 + 0.125) + 0.0375 = 0.85(0.25) + 0.0375 = 0.2125 + 0.0375 = 0.2500 \\[4pt]
p_C^{(1)} &= 0.85(0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25) + 0.0375 \\
&= 0.85(0.125) + 0.0375 = 0.1063 + 0.0375 = 0.1438 \\[4pt]
p_D^{(1)} &= 0.85(0 \cdot 0.25 + 1 \cdot 0.25 + 0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 0 \cdot 0.25) + 0.0375 \\
&= 0.85(0.25 + 0.125) + 0.0375 = 0.85(0.375) + 0.0375 = 0.3188 + 0.0375 = 0.3563
\end{aligned}
$$

After **normalization** (sum $\approx 1.0001$, divide by sum): $\mathbf{p}^{(1)} \approx (0.2500, 0.2500, 0.1437, 0.3563)^T$.

**Step 5 — Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_A^{(2)} &= 0.85(0 + 0 + 0 + 1 \cdot 0.3563) + 0.0375 = 0.85(0.3563) + 0.0375 = 0.3029 + 0.0375 = 0.3404 \\[4pt]
p_B^{(2)} &= 0.85(0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 0 + 0.5 \cdot 0.1437 + 0) + 0.0375 \\
&= 0.85(0.125 + 0.0719) + 0.0375 = 0.85(0.1969) + 0.0375 = 0.1673 + 0.0375 = 0.2048 \\[4pt]
p_C^{(2)} &= 0.85(0.5 \cdot 0.25 + 0 + 0 + 0) + 0.0375 = 0.85(0.125) + 0.0375 = 0.1438 \\[4pt]
p_D^{(2)} &= 0.85(0 + 1 \cdot 0.25 + 0.5 \cdot 0.1437 + 0) + 0.0375 \\
&= 0.85(0.25 + 0.0719) + 0.0375 = 0.85(0.3219) + 0.0375 = 0.2736 + 0.0375 = 0.3111
\end{aligned}
$$

After normalization: $\mathbf{p}^{(2)} \approx (0.3404, 0.2048, 0.1438, 0.3111)^T$.

**Step 6 — Final Ranking (after 2 iterations):**

$$
\boxed{D \; (0.3111) \; \succ \; A \; (0.3404) \; \succ \; B \; (0.2048) \; \succ \; C \; (0.1438)}
$$

Wait — checking: numerically $A > D$. Correcting the ordering:

$$
\boxed{A \; (0.3404) \; \succ \; D \; (0.3111) \; \succ \; B \; (0.2048) \; \succ \; C \; (0.1438)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Page $A$ has the highest rank because it is reachable from $D$ (the most-linked-into page), giving it authority propagation. Page $C$ ranks lowest because, although it links out, no page links *into* it.

*(Mark split: 1 mark matrix, 2 marks iteration 1, 2 marks iteration 2, 1 mark normalization, 1 mark final ranking + interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Web Search**

- **Search Engine = Crawler + Indexer + Ranker + UI.** Three pipelines operate asynchronously.
- **Crawler** obeys `robots.txt`, uses a **frontier queue**, and may be **focused** (classifier-driven).
- **Inverted Index** = dictionary of terms → postings list of `(docID, tf)` pairs. Compressed using gap + variable-byte coding.
- **TF-IDF** is **not** a similarity measure; it is a **weighting scheme**. Combine with **cosine similarity** to get a similarity score.
- **PageRank** equation: $\;PR(u) = \tfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{v \in B(u)} PR(v)/L(v)\;$ with $d = 0.85$. **Always include the teleportation term.**
- **Dangling nodes** (out-degree 0) break the stochasticity of $\mathbf{M}$. Fix: replace their column with $\tfrac{1}{N}\mathbf{1}$.
- **HITS = Authority (cited) + Hub (citing).** Compute on the **base set**, not the entire web.
- **BM25** supersedes raw TF-IDF in practice; parameters $k_1 \in [1.2, 2.0]$, $b = 0.75$.
- **Evaluation metrics** must be reported together: **Precision, Recall, F-measure, MAP, NDCG**. Use NDCG when relevance is graded.
- **Power iteration** converges because of **Perron–Frobenius**; typically $O(\log N / \log d)$ iterations suffice.
- **Web search ≠ Document search**: scale, spam, dynamism, and adversarial content make the web graph fundamentally different from classical corpora.
- **Link-based** methods exploit the **graph structure**; **content-based** methods exploit the **text**. Modern engines **fuse** both.
- For KTU exam numerics: **show every iteration**, **normalize after each step**, and **state the final ranking explicitly**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Meta-Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Meta-Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Meta-Search Engine** — A *meta-search engine* (also called a *meta-crawler* or *aggregator search engine*) is a unified information retrieval system that does **not maintain its own web index or crawler infrastructure**. Instead, it simultaneously dispatches a single user query to multiple independent third-party search engines (called *component search engines* or *back-end engines*), retrieves their ranked result lists, and then applies a **result-merging algorithm** to produce a single, unified, re-ranked list presented to the user.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology aligned with **Web Information Retrieval**, a meta-search engine functions as a **middleware layer of the retrieval stack**, sitting logically above independent search engines and providing a *Single Search Interface (SSI)* over heterogeneous retrieval sources.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Component Search Engine** | The individual underlying search engines queried by the meta-searcher (e.g., Google, Bing, Yahoo) |
| **Front-End** | The interface that accepts and normalises the user query |
| **Dispatcher** | Module that forwards the query to component engines |
| **Result Merger** | Module that combines ranked lists |
| **Coverage** | Total unique URLs reachable across all back-end engines |
| **Overlap** | Documents retrieved by more than one component engine |
| **Local Database** | Optional cache used for query rewriting or learning |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Library Analogy:** Imagine you are researching a topic for a project. Instead of going to *one* library, you simultaneously send five research assistants to five different libraries. Each assistant returns a list of the most relevant books (already ranked by their librarian). You then sit at a central desk, **merge** these five lists, **eliminate duplicates**, and **combine the rankings** to give yourself the best single ranked reading list. You never visited any library yourself — you only coordinated. **That central desk is the Meta-Search Engine.**

This analogy captures three cardinal properties of meta-search:
1. **No local index** (you never browsed the stacks yourself).
2. **Parallel dispatch** (all assistants go out at once).
3. **Aggregation/Merging** (you produce one unified list from many).

### 1.4 Why Meta-Search Matters

- The **surface web** indexed by any single engine is estimated to cover only **30%–60%** of publicly indexable pages. By combining engines, meta-search dramatically increases **recall**.
- It provides **engine-independence** — useful when no single engine consistently dominates a domain.
- It is the conceptual ancestor of modern **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** aggregators and federated enterprise search.

### 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics (Highlighted Constants)

- **Coverage (C):** Total unique documents retrievable across all component engines. Expressed as a percentage.
- **Overlap (O):** Documents returned by ≥ 2 component engines. Used as a fusion signal.
- **Response Latency (L):** Bounded by $\max(L_1, L_2, \dots, L_n)$ — the slowest component engine, *not* the sum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike classical federated search, meta-search has **no collection selection problem** — it queries *every* component engine for *every* query. The challenge lies entirely in **result merging**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generic Architecture of a Meta-Search Engine

A meta-search engine decomposes logically into the following functional pipeline:

1. **Query Reception Module** — Accepts the raw user query string.
2. **Query Pre-Processor** — Performs normalisation: stop-word removal, stemming, keyword extraction. Optionally rewrites the query for engines that require special syntax.
3. **Dispatcher (Query Forwarder)** — Sends the normalised query in parallel to *k* component search engines using their public APIs or HTTP endpoints.
4. **Result Collector** — Gathers the top-*N* ranked results from each engine. Typically only the top **10–100** results are fetched due to bandwidth and ToS constraints.
5. **Result Normaliser** — Converts heterogeneous result formats into a uniform internal schema (URL, title, snippet, raw score).
6. **Result Merger / Rank Aggregator** — The *core intellectual module*. Combines multiple ranked lists into one.
7. **Result Presentation Layer** — Renders the final aggregated list to the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Result Merger** is the heart of the meta-search engine. Its quality directly determines the relevance of the final output.

### 2.2 Why a Meta-Search Engine? — Operational Drivers

- **Increased Recall:** Aggregate coverage > any single engine.
- **Engine Bias Reduction:** PageRank bias, freshness bias, demographic bias are smoothed out.
- **Domain Diversity:** Vertical-specific engines (e.g., PubMed for biomedical) can be queried alongside general engines.
- **Privacy:** The query can be anonymised; the user’s IP is not directly exposed to each engine.
- **Robustness:** Failure of one engine degrades but does not collapse the system.

### 2.3 The Core Problem: Result Merging

Given *k* ranked lists $L_1, L_2, \dots, L_k$, each returning documents from set $D_i$, the goal is to produce a single ranked list $L_{final}$ of documents from the union $D = D_1 \cup D_2 \cup \dots \cup D_k$.

The fundamental challenges are:
- **Score Incomparability:** Engine A may score a doc at 0.95 (probability), Engine B at 152 (BM25 raw), Engine C at a rank position 7. These cannot be averaged directly.
- **Overlap Asymmetry:** A document appearing in 8 of 10 engines is almost certainly highly relevant.
- **Local Database Dependency:** Some merging algorithms (e.g., Coral) need historical query logs.

### 2.4 Categories of Result Merging Algorithms

#### A. Score-Based (Weighted) Aggregation
Each engine’s normalised score is weighted and summed:

$$
\text{Score}_{final}(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot s_i(d)
$$

where $s_i(d)$ is the normalised relevance score from engine $i$ (zero if document $d$ is not returned).

#### B. Rank-Based Aggregation
Use only the rank position. The simplest is **Borda Count**:

$$
\text{Score}_{final}(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (N - r_i(d) + 1)
$$

where $r_i(d)$ is the rank of document *d* in engine *i*’s list, and $N$ is the size of that list. The document with the highest total Borda score is ranked first.

#### C. Learning-Based Merging
Train a supervised model (regression or learning-to-rank) on a local database of past (query, merged-results, judged-relevance) triples.

#### D. Bayesian / Probabilistic Fusion
Treat each engine as a noisy classifier and combine posteriors using Bayes’ rule.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | When Used |
|------------------|---------|-----------|
| $C = \vert \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} D_i \vert$ | Coverage (union of doc sets) | Measuring recall boost |
| $O = \vert \{ d : d \in D_i \cap D_j, \; i \neq j \} \vert$ | Overlap between engines | Detecting redundancy |
| $L_{total} = \max(L_1, L_2, \dots, L_k)$ | Latency bound | Performance estimation |
| $w_i = \frac{P(R \mid E_i)}{\sum_j P(R \mid E_j)}$ | Engine weight (reliability-based) | Score-based merging |
| $s_i^{norm}(d) = \frac{s_i(d) - \min(D_i)}{\max(D_i) - \min(D_i)}$ | Min-max normalisation | Before fusion |
| $B(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (N_i - r_i(d) + 1)$ | Borda count for document d | Rank-based merging |
| $R(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{1}{r_i(d)}$ | Reciprocal-rank fusion | Hybrid rank merging |
| $RRF(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{1}{c + r_i(d)}$ | RRF with damping constant c | Robust rank fusion |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Enterprise Search (Intranet):** Federate queries across SharePoint, Confluence, Jira, and email.
- **E-commerce Price Comparison:** Meta-search over Amazon, Flipkart, eBay (e.g., Google Shopping).
- **Travel Aggregators:** Kayak, Skyscanner — meta-search over airline/hotel APIs.
- **Academic Search:** 1Science, MetaLib — federate across IEEE, ACM, Springer.
- **Foundation for RAG Pipelines:** Modern LLM retrieval stacks query multiple vector stores and merge using RRF.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Min-Max Score Normalisation

The objective of normalisation is to map every engine’s raw scores into a common range, typically $[0, 1]$, so they can be combined linearly.

Given engine *i* returning a list with raw scores $\{ s_i(d_1), s_i(d_2), \dots, s_i(d_N) \}$, the **min-max normalisation** is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Identify the bounds of the raw score distribution:**

$$
\min_i = \min_{d \in D_i} s_i(d), \qquad \max_i = \max_{d \in D_i} s_i(d)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the affine linear transformation:**

$$
s_i^{norm}(d) = \frac{s_i(d) - \min_i}{\max_i - \min_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify endpoint values:**

Substituting $s_i(d) = \max_i$ gives numerator $(\max_i - \min_i)$, so $s_i^{norm} = 1$. Substituting $s_i(d) = \min_i$ gives $s_i^{norm} = 0$. Both endpoints are preserved. ✓

**Step 4 — Handle missing documents.** If document $d \notin D_i$, define $s_i^{norm}(d) = 0$. This naturally penalises documents retrieved by fewer engines.

**Step 5 — Aggregation:**

$$
\text{Score}_{final}(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot s_i^{norm}(d)
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Borda Count Rank Aggregation

Given *k* ranked lists, each of length $N_i$, we want to combine them into a single list.

**Step 1 — Assign Borda points per engine.** For a list of length $N_i$, the top-ranked document gets $N_i$ points, the second gets $N_i - 1$, …, the last gets $1$:

$$
b_i(d) = N_i - r_i(d) + 1
$$

where $r_i(d)$ is the rank of $d$ in list $i$. If $d$ is absent, $b_i(d) = 0$.

**Step 2 — Sum across all engines:**

$$
B(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} b_i(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (N_i - r_i(d) + 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Sort all candidate documents by $B(d)$ in descending order.** Ties are broken by the number of engines that returned the document (more is better).

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Borda treats all engines as equally reliable. To incorporate engine quality, a **weighted Borda count** is used:

$$
B_w(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot (N_i - r_i(d) + 1)
$$

where $w_i$ is the reliability weight of engine $i$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)

RRF is widely used in modern systems because it requires **no score normalisation** — only ranks.

**Step 1 — Define the reciprocal contribution of document $d$ from engine $i$:**

$$
c_i(d) = \frac{1}{c + r_i(d)}
$$

where $c$ is a damping constant (commonly $c = 60$).

**Step 2 — Sum across all engines:**

$$
RRF(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c_i(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{1}{c + r_i(d)}
$$

**Step 3 — Sort by $RRF(d)$.**

**Step 4 — Why $c$?** It dampens the contribution of very low ranks. Without it ($c=0$), a document at rank 1 contributes $1$ and one at rank 100 contributes $0.01$, making the scale unstable. With $c=60$, contributions become $\approx 0.016$ and $\approx 0.0062$, a much smoother range.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Three search engines return top-5 results for query *“web mining tutorial”*.

| Rank | Engine 1 | Engine 2 | Engine 3 |
|:----:|:---------|:---------|:---------|
| 1    | A        | C        | A        |
| 2    | B        | A        | D        |
| 3    | C        | E        | B        |
| 4    | D        | B        | E        |
| 5    | E        | —        | —        |

Documents: A, B, C, D, E.

**Solution using Borda Count (assume $N_i = 5$ for all engines):**

For document **A**: $b_1 = 5 - 1 + 1 = 5$; $b_2 = 5 - 2 + 1 = 4$; $b_3 = 5 - 1 + 1 = 5$. Total = $5 + 4 + 5 = 14$.

For document **B**: $b_1 = 5 - 2 + 1 = 4$; $b_2 = 5 - 4 + 1 = 2$; $b_3 = 5 - 3 + 1 = 3$. Total = $4 + 2 + 3 = 9$.

For document **C**: $b_1 = 5 - 3 + 1 = 3$; $b_2 = 5 - 1 + 1 = 5$; $b_3 = 0$. Total = $3 + 5 + 0 = 8$.

For document **D**: $b_1 = 5 - 4 + 1 = 2$; $b_2 = 0$; $b_3 = 5 - 2 + 1 = 4$. Total = $2 + 0 + 4 = 6$.

For document **E**: $b_1 = 5 - 5 + 1 = 1$; $b_2 = 5 - 3 + 1 = 3$; $b_3 = 5 - 4 + 1 = 2$. Total = $1 + 3 + 2 = 6$.

**Final Merged Order:** A (14) > B (9) > C (8) > D (6) ≈ E (6). Tie between D and E broken by # engines that returned them — D appears in 2, E in 3, so **E > D**.

Final ranking: **A, B, C, E, D**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Merging Strategies

| Strategy | Score Required? | Overlap-Aware? | Tunable? | Robust to Score Scale? |
|----------|:---------------:|:--------------:|:--------:|:----------------------:|
| Raw Score Sum | Yes | Partially | Yes (weights) | No |
| Min-Max + Weighted Sum | Yes | Partially | Yes | Yes |
| Borda Count | No | Partially | Limited (weights) | Yes |
| Weighted Borda | No | Partially | Yes | Yes |
| RRF | No | Yes (via summation) | Yes (c) | Yes |
| Bayesian Fusion | Yes (probs) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Learning-to-Rank | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Meta-Search Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User] --> Q[Query Reception Module]
    Q --> P[Query Pre-Processor]
    P --> D[Dispatcher Parallel Fan-out]
    D --> E1[Engine 1 Google]
    D --> E2[Engine 2 Bing]
    D --> E3[Engine 3 Yahoo]
    D --> E4[Engine 4 DuckDuckGo]
    E1 --> C[Result Collector]
    E2 --> C
    E3 --> C
    E4 --> C
    C --> N[Result Normaliser]
    N --> M[Result Merger Rank Aggregator]
    M --> R[Final Ranked List]
    R --> U
    M -.uses.-> DB[(Local Cache optional)]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram of the Result Merger

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[List from Engine 1] --> NM1[Score Normaliser 1]
    L2[List from Engine 2] --> NM2[Score Normaliser 2]
    L3[List from Engine 3] --> NM3[Score Normaliser 3]
    L4[List from Engine 4] --> NM4[Score Normaliser 4]
    NM1 --> AGG{Aggregation Strategy}
    NM2 --> AGG
    NM3 --> AGG
    NM4 --> AGG
    AGG --> BOR[Borda Rank]
    AGG --> WGT[Weighted Sum]
    AGG --> RRF[Reciprocal Rank Fusion]
    BOR --> SORT[Sort and De-duplicate]
    WGT --> SORT
    RRF --> SORT
    SORT --> OUT[Final Merged List]
```

### 4.3 Information Flow During a Single Query

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant M as Meta-Search Front-End
    participant E1 as Engine A
    participant E2 as Engine B
    participant E3 as Engine C
    U->>M: Submit query q
    M->>M: Normalise q
    par Parallel Dispatch
        M->>E1: Query q
        M->>E2: Query q
        M->>E3: Query q
    end
    E1-->>M: Ranked list L1
    E2-->>M: Ranked list L2
    E3-->>M: Ranked list L3
    M->>M: Merge L1, L2, L3
    M-->>U: Unified ranked list L_final
```

### 4.4 Classification of Merging Strategies (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Merging Strategies] --> S1[Score Based]
    ROOT --> S2[Rank Based]
    ROOT --> S3[Learning Based]
    ROOT --> S4[Probabilistic]
    S1 --> S1A[Min Max Normalised Sum]
    S1 --> S1B[Weighted Score Fusion]
    S2 --> S2A[Borda Count]
    S2 --> S2B[Reciprocal Rank Fusion]
    S2 --> S2C[Condorcet Method]
    S3 --> S3A[Supervised Regression]
    S3 --> S3B[Learning to Rank LambdaMART]
    S4 --> S4A[Bayesian Inference Fusion]
    S4 --> S4B[CombMNZ]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a meta-search engine. List any two advantages over a single search engine.*

**Model Answer:**
A meta-search engine is an information retrieval system that does not maintain its own web index. Instead, it dispatches a user query in parallel to multiple independent component search engines, retrieves their ranked result lists, and merges them using a rank-aggregation algorithm to present a single unified list.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Higher Recall:** Combines coverage of multiple engines, retrieving documents that no single engine indexed.
2. **Reduced Engine Bias:** Aggregating multiple engines smooths out individual ranking biases (PageRank bias, freshness bias, demographic bias).
3. **Robustness:** Failure of one component engine only partially degrades the result quality.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the role of a result merger in a meta-search engine? Name any two merging techniques.*

**Model Answer:**
The **result merger** is the core module of a meta-search engine responsible for combining the heterogeneous ranked lists returned by the component search engines into a single, unified, relevance-ordered list. It must handle score incomparability, document overlap, and varying list lengths.

**Two merging techniques:**
1. **Borda Count Rank Aggregation** — Each document receives points equal to its rank; total points determine final order.
2. **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)** — Each document’s score is $\sum_i \frac{1}{c + r_i(d)}$, where $r_i(d)$ is its rank in engine *i*’s list.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly lose marks by **confusing meta-search with federated search**. Meta-search queries *all* engines for *all* queries; federated search performs **collection selection** and queries only a subset.
> - When asked to explain a merging algorithm, students often **skip the normalisation step**. Score-based fusion without normalisation is mathematically invalid — always explain normalisation first.
> - In numerical problems, students forget to assign **zero score** to documents absent from a particular engine. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> - Always state the **assumption** that engines return top-N only and that scores are not directly comparable.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a meta-search engine with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The architecture of a meta-search engine comprises the following sequential modules:

1. **Query Reception Module:** Accepts the raw text query from the user through a web form or API. Performs initial validation.

2. **Query Pre-Processor:** Normalises the query through:
   - Tokenisation (splitting on whitespace and punctuation),
   - Stop-word removal (filtering “the”, “is”, “of”),
   - Stemming / Lemmatisation (reducing “running” → “run”),
   - Optional query rewriting for engine-specific syntax.

3. **Dispatcher:** Sends the normalised query to *k* component engines in **parallel** using their APIs. Parallelism is essential because the latency is bounded by $\max(L_i)$ rather than $\sum(L_i)$.

4. **Result Collector:** Receives the top-*N* ranked results from each engine, typically as JSON or XML payloads. Filters out malformed responses.

5. **Result Normaliser:** Maps heterogeneous result records to a uniform internal schema with fields: $\{$URL, Title, Snippet, Engine ID, Raw Score, Rank$\}$.

6. **Result Merger:** Applies the chosen fusion algorithm (Borda, RRF, Weighted Sum, etc.) to produce one unified ranking. This is the intellectual core.

7. **Presentation Layer:** Renders the final list with snippets, thumbnails, and source-engine attribution for user transparency.

**(Block diagram should be drawn — refer to Section 4.1.)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **Borda Count** rank aggregation algorithm for merging results in a meta-search engine. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Borda Count** is a positional, rank-based fusion method that does not require score normalisation — only rank positions.

**Algorithm:**
1. For each component engine *i* returning a list of length $N_i$, assign Borda points to document $d$ as:
   
   $$
   b_i(d) = N_i - r_i(d) + 1
   $$
   
   where $r_i(d)$ is the rank of $d$. If $d$ is absent from engine *i*’s list, $b_i(d) = 0$.

2. Sum across all *k* engines:
   
   $$
   B(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} b_i(d)
   $$

3. Sort all candidate documents by $B(d)$ in descending order.

4. Break ties using the number of engines that returned the document (more is better), and if still tied, by the highest individual rank.

**Worked Example:**
Three engines return top-5 for query “*data mining*”:

| Rank | Engine 1 | Engine 2 | Engine 3 |
|:----:|:---------|:---------|:---------|
| 1    | P1       | P3       | P1       |
| 2    | P2       | P1       | P4       |
| 3    | P3       | P4       | P2       |
| 4    | P4       | P2       | P5       |
| 5    | P5       | —        | —        |

Borda scores:
- $P_1$: $5 + 4 + 5 = 14$
- $P_2$: $4 + 2 + 3 = 9$
- $P_3$: $3 + 5 + 0 = 8$
- $P_4$: $2 + 0 + 4 = 6$
- $P_5$: $1 + 0 + 0 = 1$

**Final ranking:** P1, P2, P3, P4, P5.

**Limitations:** Treats all engines as equally reliable; can be overcome by **Weighted Borda** with engine-reliability weights $w_i$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]* — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What are the challenges in result merging for a meta-search engine? Explain any two merging techniques in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Key Challenges:**

1. **Score Incompatibility:** Each engine uses a different scoring function (BM25, PageRank, TF-IDF variants). Raw scores cannot be averaged directly without normalisation.
2. **Document Overlap Variability:** A document in 8 out of 10 lists is more relevant than one in 1 of 10. The merger must exploit this signal.
3. **Heterogeneous Result Formats:** JSON, XML, RSS — must be parsed and normalised.
4. **Coverage and Latency Trade-off:** More engines increase recall but also increase response time and failure probability.
5. **Absence of Ground Truth:** There is no local index to verify relevance, so the merger operates blindly.

**Technique 1: Min-Max Normalised Weighted Sum**
- Normalise each engine’s scores to $[0,1]$ using min-max scaling.
- Multiply by engine weight $w_i$ (engine reliability).
- Sum across engines:

  $$
  \text{Score}_{final}(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot \frac{s_i(d) - \min_i}{\max_i - \min_i}
  $$

**Technique 2: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)**
- For each engine, contribute $\frac{1}{c + r_i(d)}$ where $c$ is the damping constant (typically $c = 60$).
- Sum across all engines and sort descending.
- RRF is **robust, score-free, and empirically strong**.

**(b)** Compare meta-search engines with traditional (single) search engines. Discuss the limitations of meta-search. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Single Search Engine | Meta-Search Engine |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Index | Maintains its own | Does not maintain |
| Crawler | Yes | No |
| Coverage | Limited to own crawl | Aggregated, higher |
| Latency | Single-engine bound | $\max(L_i)$ of components |
| Result Quality | Engine-specific | Aggregated, often more diverse |
| Bias | High (one algorithm) | Reduced |
| Resource Cost | Very high (crawling, indexing) | Low (mostly compute + bandwidth) |

**Limitations of Meta-Search:**

1. **Cannot use deep page content:** Component engines return only top-N titles, snippets, and URLs — the meta-searcher cannot re-rank using full content.
2. **API / ToS Restrictions:** Most engines limit the number of queries per day, the number of results returned, and prohibit automated bulk querying.
3. **No Personalisation:** The meta-searcher lacks the query history of a single engine like Google, so personalised ranking is impossible.
4. **Latency Bottleneck:** The slowest component engine determines total response time.
5. **Vulnerability to Engine Failure:** If a major component engine goes down or rate-limits the meta-searcher, result quality degrades sharply.
6. **Snippet Limitations:** Cannot access deep-web content not indexed by any component engine.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Meta-Search = Parallel Dispatch + Aggregation.** No local index, no crawler.
- **The two cardinal challenges** are *score incomparability* and *overlap exploitation*.
- **Result merging strategies** fall into four families: score-based, rank-based (Borda, RRF), learning-based, probabilistic.
- **RRF with $c = 60$** is the modern de-facto standard for rank fusion in production systems.
- **Latency** is bounded by the slowest component engine, not the sum of all engines — parallelism is essential.
- **Coverage** is the union of document sets; **Overlap** is the intersection across engines.
- **Engine weights** can be learned from click logs or set heuristically based on domain relevance.
- **Meta-search is NOT federated search** — federated search performs collection selection; meta-search does not.
- **Local database (cache)** can be used for query rewriting, learning engine weights, and storing past query-result triples for supervised merging.
- **Modern relevance:** Meta-search is the conceptual ancestor of RRF-based retrieval in RAG pipelines and federated enterprise search systems.
- **KTU 2024 high-yield keywords:** *Component search engine, Dispatcher, Result merger, Borda count, RRF, Min-Max normalisation, Coverage, Overlap, Latency bound, Local database.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Spamming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Web Spamming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Web Spamming — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Web Spamming** is the deliberate manipulation of web page content, hyperlink structure, or user-facing presentation with the malicious intent of artificially inflating a page's rank in Information Retrieval (IR) systems, particularly search engines, irrespective of the page's actual relevance or authority. It exploits the algorithmic assumptions of crawling, indexing, and ranking pipelines used in Web Mining.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> "Web Spamming refers to all those deliberate actions that bring a web page to an undeserved high ranking position in the search engine results, thereby degrading the quality of search results and the user experience."

The phenomenon is formally studied under the umbrella of **Adversarial Information Retrieval (AIR)**. The mathematical basis of most ranking algorithms (e.g., PageRank, HITS) assumes that a link from page A to page B represents an editorial endorsement. Spammers violate this assumption to game the system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Library Analogy:** Imagine a public library where any book can be "recommended" by the librarian. A spammer would:
> - Stuff their own book with **hidden text** (writing the word "medicine" 1,000 times in microscopic invisible ink to game the catalog's keyword search).
> - **Buy fake referrals** (paying thousands of people to insert "This book is the best!" cards in 50,000 other random books — the book-cross equivalent of a link farm).
> - **Cloak** the catalog entry (showing the librarian a sophisticated book on "quantum physics" while the actual book is a cookbook).
>
> The librarian's job (the search engine) is to detect these manipulative patterns. This is precisely what **TrustRank, Anti-TrustRank, and Spam Mass** do.

## 1.3 Why Does Web Spamming Matter?

| Impact Area | Consequence of Spamming |
|---|---|
| **Search Quality** | Relevant results get buried under low-quality, manipulated pages. |
| **Economic Loss** | Users click on irrelevant pages, costing time and ad revenue. |
| **User Trust** | Erosion of faith in search engine results. |
| **Index Bloat** | Crawler bandwidth wasted on spam pages. |
| **Adversarial Loop** | Forces search engines into a constant arms race with spammers. |

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Spam (High-Level Taxonomy)

1. **Content Spamming (Term Spamming)** — Manipulating the textual/visual content of the page.
2. **Link Spamming** — Manipulating the hyperlink structure across the web graph.
3. **Cloaking & Redirection** — Serving different content to crawlers vs. human users.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Cue:** Whenever you see a question on "types of web spamming," always structure your answer using the **Content / Link / Cloaking** trichotomy. Examiners award full marks only when this classification is explicit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Web Spamming Techniques

### 2.1.1 Content Spamming (Term Spamming)
Content spamming targets the on-page features used by indexing pipelines (TF-IDF, BM25, embeddings).

- **Keyword Stuffing:** Repetitive insertion of popular search terms (often unrelated) to artificially boost TF-IDF scores. Example: A real-estate page repeating "cheap flights to Delhi" 200 times.
- **Hidden/ Invisible Text:** Using CSS (`color: white; font-size: 0;`) or `display:none` to render keywords invisible to humans but readable to crawlers.
- **Meta-Tag Stuffing:** Cramming the `<meta name="keywords">` tag with hundreds of high-traffic terms, including competitor brands.
- **Doorway / Gateway Pages:** Low-quality pages heavily optimized for one specific query, which redirect the user to a different target page upon click.
- **Page Hijacking (301 Hijack):** Exploiting expired domains with high PageRank by serving a 301 redirect to spam content.
- **Scraping & Spinning:** Auto-rewriting legitimate content using synonym substitution to evade duplicate-content detection.

### 2.1.2 Link Spamming
Link spamming exploits the assumption that **a link is a vote of confidence**.

- **Link Farms:** A dense, mutually-linked cluster of pages (e.g., 10,000 pages all linking to each other) created solely to inflate PageRank.
- **Link Exchange / Reciprocal Linking:** "You link to me, I'll link to you" networks.
- **Blog/ Forum Comment Spam:** Automated bots posting links in comment fields (e.g., WordPress blogs, guestbooks).
- **Wiki Spam:** Editing wiki pages (Wikipedia, etc.) to insert promotional external links.
- **Paid Links:** Buying links from authoritative sites purely for PageRank transfer (a direct violation of Google's quality guidelines).
- **Honey Pot:** Creating an attractive free resource (e.g., "Free iPhone 15") to harvest organic backlinks.

### 2.1.3 Cloaking & Redirection
- **Cloaking:** The server detects the User-Agent. If the visitor is a search engine crawler (e.g., Googlebot), it returns an optimized, content-rich HTML page. If the visitor is a human, it returns a commercial/irrelevant page. The IP and UA detection are done via server-side scripts.
- **Redirect Spam:** Using `301`, `302`, or JavaScript-based refreshes to funnel PageRank to an unrelated target page.
- **User-Generated Spam:** Spam injected into a legitimate site via compromised forms, comments, or forums.

## 2.2 Spam Detection Strategies

The defense against web spam operates at **two levels**:

### Level 1 — Pre-Ranking (Link-Based Analysis)
Used *before* the final ranking is computed. Operates on the web graph.

- **TrustRank (Gyongyi, Garcia-Molina, Pedersen, 2004)**
- **Anti-TrustRank (Krishnan & Raj, 2006)**
- **Spam Mass (Gyongyi et al., 2006)**
- **Bad-PageRank / Bad-Rank**

### Level 2 — Post-Ranking (Content & Behavior Analysis)
Used *after* ranking, on the candidate set of top results.

- Content-based classifiers (n-gram analysis, language models).
- Click-through anomaly detection.
- Honeypot traps (pages that only a bot would find and follow).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these four equations — they account for **~70% of the marks** in any numerical or derivation question on Web Spamming.

### Equation 1 — Standard PageRank (Baseline)

$$PR(u) = \frac{1-d}{N} \;+\; d \sum_{v \in B(u)} \frac{PR(v)}{L(v)}$$

- $d$ = **damping factor** (empirically set to **0.85**)
- $N$ = total number of web pages in the indexed graph
- $B(u)$ = set of pages that contain a hyperlink to $u$
- $L(v)$ = number of outgoing hyperlinks from page $v$
- $\frac{1-d}{N}$ is the teleportation term (random surfer resets)

### Equation 2 — TrustRank (Biased PageRank)

$$TR(u) = d \sum_{v \in B(u)} \frac{TR(v)}{L(v)} \;+\; (1-d) \cdot \frac{s(u)}{\sum_{w \in S} s(w)}$$

- $S$ = a small **manually selected seed set** of trusted pages (e.g., 200 hand-picked domains).
- $s(u)$ = a binary or weight vector with $s(u) = 1$ if $u \in S$, else $0$.
- The teleportation is **biased** toward the seed set, so trust propagates outward.

### Equation 3 — Anti-TrustRank (Inverted Propagation)

$$ATR(u) = d \sum_{v \in B(u)} \frac{ATR(v)}{L(v)} \;+\; (1-d) \cdot \frac{a(u)}{\sum_{w \in A} a(w)}$$

- $A$ = the **known spam set** (anti-seed).
- Anti-trust propagates along the *same direction as a regular link* (i.e., from a spam page to whatever it links to, in contrast to TrustRank which propagates *incoming* trust).
- A high $ATR$ value means a page is likely a spam supporter.

### Equation 4 — Spam Mass (Per-Page Spam Likelihood)

$$SpamMass(p) = \frac{PR(p) - TR(p)}{PR(p)} = 1 - \frac{TR(p)}{PR(p)}$$

- $PR(p)$ = regular PageRank score of page $p$.
- $TR(p)$ = TrustRank score of the same page $p$.
- Interpretation:
  - $SpamMass(p) \approx 1$ → page is almost certainly spam.
  - $SpamMass(p) \approx 0$ → page is almost certainly legitimate.
  - A common **threshold of 0.65** is used to flag pages for demotion or removal.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Search Engines** | Filtering billions of pages before SERP generation. |
| **Ad-Tech** | Preventing ad-fraud via fake publishers. |
| **Cybersecurity** | Detecting phishing link structures. |
| **Academic Citation Analysis** | Detecting self-citation cartels. |
| **Social Network Analysis** | Detecting fake followers / bot networks. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the PageRank Formula (Foundation for TrustRank)

The PageRank of a page $u$ is defined as the **stationary probability** that a random surfer — who follows links with probability $d$ and teleports to a random page with probability $1-d$ — lands on $u$.

**Step 1 — Define the random-surfer transition.**
With probability $d$, the surfer clicks one of the $L(v)$ outgoing links of the current page $v$ uniformly at random. The probability of moving from $v$ to $u$ in a single click is therefore $\frac{1}{L(v)}$.

**Step 2 — Sum over all pages linking into $u$.**
If $B(u)$ is the set of pages linking to $u$, the total probability of arriving at $u$ via a click is:

$$P_{\text{click}}(u) = \sum_{v \in B(u)} \frac{PR(v)}{L(v)}$$

**Step 3 — Add the teleportation term.**
With probability $1-d$, the surfer abandons link-following and teleports uniformly to **any** of the $N$ pages:

$$P_{\text{teleport}}(u) = \frac{1-d}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Combine both terms (law of total probability).**

$$PR(u) = (1-d) \cdot \frac{1}{N} + d \cdot \sum_{v \in B(u)} \frac{PR(v)}{L(v)}$$

> This is the **canonical PageRank equation**. It is the foundation on which TrustRank and Anti-TrustRank are mathematically constructed.

## 3.2 Derivation of the TrustRank Formula

TrustRank modifies the teleportation distribution of standard PageRank. Instead of teleporting uniformly to all $N$ pages, the surfer teleports **only** to the trusted seed set $S$.

**Step 1 — Replace the uniform teleportation with a biased one.**
Standard PageRank teleports with weight $\frac{1}{N}$ to every page. TrustRank assigns teleportation weight $1 \div \vert S \vert$ to each seed page and $0$ to all others.

$$P_{\text{biased teleport}}(u) = \frac{s(u)}{\sum_{w \in S} s(w)}$$

where $s(u) = 1$ if $u \in S$, else $0$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the PageRank template.**

$$TR(u) = d \sum_{v \in B(u)} \frac{TR(v)}{L(v)} + (1-d) \cdot \frac{s(u)}{\sum_{w \in S} s(w)}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the bias.**
The random surfer is now "anchored" to the trusted seed. Trust flows outward through hyperlinks: a page linked by many trusted pages accumulates high TrustRank; a page only linked by unknown pages receives very little.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Invariant of TrustRank:** The sum of all TrustRank scores equals **1.0** (it remains a valid probability distribution), but the mass is heavily concentrated around the seed set.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Spam Mass Equation

Spam Mass quantifies how much of a page's PageRank is "unexplained" by TrustRank.

**Step 1 — Decompose PageRank into trusted and untrusted components.**

$$PR(p) = TR(p) + \text{UntrustedPR}(p)$$

The untrusted component is the portion of $p$'s PageRank that flowed in from **non-trusted** (or unvetted) ancestors in the web graph.

**Step 2 — Isolate the untrusted component.**

$$\text{UntrustedPR}(p) = PR(p) - TR(p)$$

**Step 3 — Normalize by total PageRank to get a [0, 1] ratio.**

$$SpamMass(p) = \frac{\text{UntrustedPR}(p)}{PR(p)} = \frac{PR(p) - TR(p)}{PR(p)} = 1 - \frac{TR(p)}{PR(p)}$$

**Step 4 — Threshold and classify.**

$$ \text{Label}(p) = \begin{cases} \text{Spam}, & \text{if } SpamMass(p) > \tau \\ \text{Ham (Legitimate)}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

Empirically, $\tau \in [0.5, 0.7]$ is the operational range used by commercial search engines.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Spam Mass

Consider a 4-node graph: $A \to B \to C \to D$, plus a spam link $S \to C$.

**Step 1 — Compute standard PageRank (assume $d=0.85$, $N=4$).**

For node $C$, two pages link in: $B$ and $S$.

$$PR(C) = \frac{0.15}{4} + 0.85 \cdot \left( \frac{PR(B)}{L(B)} + \frac{PR(S)}{L(S)} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute TrustRank (assume $S = \{A\}$ is the trusted seed).**

Since $S$ does not link to $C$ (and $A$ does not either), the spam contribution from $S$ contributes **zero** to TrustRank of $C$:

$$TR(C) = \frac{0.15 \cdot 0}{1} + 0.85 \cdot \frac{TR(B)}{L(B)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Spam Mass.**

$$SpamMass(C) = \frac{PR(C) - TR(C)}{PR(C)}$$

Because $PR(C)$ contains a non-trivial spam contribution from $S$ but $TR(C)$ does not, $SpamMass(C) > 0$. If this value exceeds $\tau = 0.65$, $C$ is demoted.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favorite)

> [!TIP]
> Examiners frequently ask: *"Differentiate between TrustRank and Anti-TrustRank"* or *"Compare content and link spamming."* This table is your ready-made answer.

| Feature | TrustRank | Anti-TrustRank | Spam Mass |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Purpose** | Measure legitimacy | Measure spamminess | Quantify spam impact |
| **Seed Set** | Trusted pages ($S$) | Known spam pages ($A$) | N/A (it is a derived metric) |
| **Propagation Direction** | Inbound (toward a page) | Outbound (away from spam) | None — computed per page |
| **Mathematical Form** | Biased PageRank | Inverted PageRank | Ratio: $1 - TR/PR$ |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(kN)$ | $O(kN)$ | $O(N)$ after PR and TR |
| **Output Range** | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, 1]$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Decision Threshold** | Score percentile | Score percentile | $\tau \approx 0.65$ |
| **Key Limitation** | Seed selection bias | Misses link-based spam farms | Heuristic, not absolute |
| **Year Proposed** | 2004 (Stanford) | 2006 (IIT/Wiki) | 2006 (Yahoo! Research) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
