# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**What is meant by finding other eigenvectors in the context of web graph mining? Why is it necessary after computing the dominant eigenvector?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
After computing the dominant eigenvector $v_1$ of the adjacency matrix $A$ (which captures global authority like PageRank), **finding other eigenvectors** refers to the systematic extraction of sub-dominant eigenvectors $v_2, v_3, \dots, v_k$ that capture finer community-level structures of the web graph. Each subsequent eigenvector is orthogonal to the previous ones and represents a different "mode" of hyperlink flow. It is necessary because a single dominant eigenvector cannot expose the multi-community, multi-topic nature of the Web — PageRank alone treats the entire web as one giant authority cluster, while $v_2, v_3, \dots$ reveal topic-wise clusters, bipartite structures, and anomalous sub-networks.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define the Community Discovery Problem formally. Mention any two metrics used to evaluate the quality of discovered communities.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Community Discovery Problem:** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ representing the web, find a partition $\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ such that the **Modularity** $Q(\mathcal{C})$ is maximized, subject to $C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$ and $\bigcup_i C_i = V$.
Two evaluation metrics:
1. **Modularity ($Q$):** $Q = \tfrac{1}{2m} \sum_{ij} [A_{ij} - d_i d_j / 2m] \, \delta(c_i, c_j)$. Values above **$0.3$** indicate strong structure.
2. **Conductance ($\phi$):** $\phi(C) = \text{cut}(C, \bar{C}) / \min(\text{vol}(C), \text{vol}(\bar{C}))$. Lower values indicate stronger communities.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose 3–4 marks by (a) forgetting to **normalize** vectors after each Power Iteration step, (b) confusing the Laplacian eigenvector ordering (smallest, not largest, comes first for Fiedler), and (c) writing the modularity formula without the **$1/2m$** normalization constant. Read carefully — these are the most-marked deductions in the KTU valuation key.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model QP, Module 2]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete pipeline of **spectral community discovery** in web graphs. Discuss the role of the **modularity matrix** in converting community discovery into an eigenvector problem. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**
1. The pipeline consists of: Web Crawl → Graph Construction → Adjacency Matrix $A$ → Modularity Matrix $B$ → Power Iteration on $B$ → Extract top eigenvectors → Sign/cluster assignment → Communities.
2. The **modularity matrix** $B_{ij} = A_{ij} - d_i d_j / 2m$ encodes the deviation of the real web graph from a random configuration.
3. The trace formulation $Q = \tfrac{1}{2m} \text{Tr}(S^T B S)$ converts community optimization into a Rayleigh-quotient maximization problem.
4. Hence, the eigenvectors of $B$ corresponding to the largest positive eigenvalues define the optimal community directions.
5. The dominant eigenvector splits the graph into two communities; further eigenvectors give multi-way partitions.
6. The block diagram should reflect the pipeline: *Crawler → Builder → Modularity Matrix → Power Iteration → Deflation → k-way Cut*.

**(b)** Explain **Wielandt's Deflation** method with its mathematical derivation. Apply it to the matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$ and find the second eigenvector. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**
1. **Definition:** Wielandt's deflation constructs a new matrix $B = A - \lambda_1 v_1 v_1^T$ such that $v_1$ becomes an eigenvector with eigenvalue $0$, leaving other eigenvectors unchanged.
2. **Derivation:** Starting from spectral decomposition $A = \sum_i \lambda_i v_i v_i^T$, subtract the rank-1 contribution of $v_1$ to obtain $B = \sum_{i \ge 2} \lambda_i v_i v_i^T$. Verify $B v_j = \lambda_j v_j$ for $j \ge 2$ and $B v_1 = 0$.
3. **Eigendecomposition of $A$:** The eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = 6$, $\lambda_2 = 2$. The eigenvectors are $v_1 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1,1)^T$, $v_2 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1,-1)^T$.
4. **Apply Deflation:** $v_1 v_1^T = \tfrac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, so $\lambda_1 v_1 v_1^T = 3 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 3 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$.
5. **Deflated Matrix:** $B = A - \lambda_1 v_1 v_1^T = \begin{bmatrix} 4-3 & 2-3 \\ 2-3 & 4-3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
6. **Power Iteration on $B$:** Start with $x^{(0)} = (1, 0)^T$; compute $B x^{(0)} = (1, -1)^T$, normalize to $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, -1)^T = v_2$.
7. **Conclusion:** The second eigenvector is $v_2 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, -1)^T$, eigenvalue $2$, confirming the deflated matrix has eigenvalues $\{0, 2\}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model QP, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Define the **Community Discovery Problem** in detail. Discuss the **disjoint, overlapping, and hierarchical** variants with suitable web-mining examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**
1. **Formal Definition:** Community Discovery is the task of partitioning the vertex set $V$ of a web graph $G = (V, E)$ into groups $C_1, \dots, C_k$ such that intra-community link density is high and inter-community link density is low, optimizing the Modularity $Q$.
2. **Disjoint Variant:** Each web page belongs to exactly one community — *e.g.,* classifying news articles into mutually exclusive categories (Sports / Politics / Tech). Algorithm: Girvan–Newman, Spectral Bisection.
3. **Overlapping Variant:** A page may belong to multiple communities — *e.g.,* a blog post tagged "AI + Ethics + Politics" exists in three clusters simultaneously. Algorithm: Clique Percolation, SLPA.
4. **Hierarchical Variant:** Communities contain sub-communities — *e.g.,* DMOZ hierarchy (Sports $\to$ Cricket $\to$ IPL). Algorithm: Louvain method, Hierarchical Spectral Clustering.
5. **Why Variants Matter:** The Web is not flat; user interests overlap, and topics nest. Choosing the correct variant determines the algorithmic complexity and the interpretability of the output.
6. **Evaluation:** Disjoint — Modularity, NMI; Overlapping — Omega Index, F-measure; Hierarchical — Normalized Cut at multiple levels.
7. **Conclusion:** The community discovery problem is graph-type dependent; modern web applications overwhelmingly favor overlapping and hierarchical formulations.

**(b)** With the **Power Iteration** algorithm, derive the convergence to the dominant eigenvector. State and explain **two methods** for finding other eigenvectors. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**
1. **Power Iteration:** $x^{(k+1)} = A x^{(k)} / \Vert A x^{(k)} \Vert$.
2. **Convergence Derivation:** Expand $x^{(0)} = \sum_i \alpha_i v_i$; after $k$ steps, $x^{(k)} = \lambda_1^k \left[ \alpha_1 v_1 + \sum_{i \ge 2} \alpha_i (\lambda_i / \lambda_1)^k v_i \right]$. Since $\vert \lambda_i / \lambda_1 \vert < 1$, the sum vanishes, leaving $x^{(k)} \to \alpha_1 v_1$.
3. **Method 1 — Wielandt's Deflation:** $B = A - \lambda_1 v_1 v_1^T$. Removes the dominant component, leaving $v_2$ as the new dominant eigenvector of $B$. Iteratively applied to extract $v_3, v_4, \dots$.
4. **Method 2 — Orthogonal Iteration:** Maintain a matrix $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ whose columns span the candidate subspace. Each iteration performs $Z = A Q$ followed by QR factorization $Z = Q' R$. The $R$ diagonal elements converge to the top-$k$ eigenvalues and $Q$ columns to the corresponding eigenvectors.
5. **Advantages/Disadvantages:** Deflation is simple but accumulates numerical error; Orthogonal Iteration is stable but requires $k$-column QR at each step (cost $\mathcal{O}(nk^2)$).
6. **Application to Web Graphs:** For a hyperlink graph with millions of pages, Power Iteration with Deflation is used in **PageRank computation pipelines** to extract top authority scores, while Orthogonal Iteration is used in **Spectral Clustering of web graphs** to find multi-community structure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 2**

- The **dominant eigenvector** $v_1$ captures global web authority (e.g., PageRank).
- **Other eigenvectors** $v_2, v_3, \dots, v_k$ capture progressively finer community/role structures.
- **Power Iteration** converges to $v_1$ at a rate proportional to $\vert \lambda_2 / \lambda_1 \vert$.
- **Wielandt's Deflation:** $B = A - \lambda_1 v_1 v_1^T$ → enables extraction of $v_2$.
- **Hotelling's Deflation:** $B = A - \lambda_1 v_1 v_1^T A^T$ (alternative form for non-symmetric $A$).
- **Orthogonal Iteration:** simultaneous top-$k$ eigenvector extraction via QR factorization.
- **Lanczos / Arnoldi** methods are preferred for large sparse web graphs.
- **Fiedler vector** $v_2$ of the Laplacian $L = D - A$ gives the optimal **2-way graph bisection**.
- **Community Discovery Problem:** partition $V$ to maximize **Modularity** $Q \in [-0.5, 1]$.
- **Variants:** Disjoint, Overlapping, Hierarchical, Dynamic, Local.
- **Spectral Bipartitioning** uses the sign of the Fiedler vector.
- **Conductance** $\phi$ is a *cut-based* quality metric; **Modularity** is a *density-based* metric.
- **Always normalize** eigenvectors after each Power Iteration step — examiners deduct marks otherwise.
- **Modularity matrix** $B_{ij} = A_{ij} - d_i d_j / 2m$ converts community discovery into an eigenvector maximization problem.
- **Real-world tools:** NetworkX, igraph, SNAP (Stanford Network Analysis Project), Scikit-learn's SpectralClustering.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bipartite Core Communities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bipartite Core Communities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Bipartite Graph?

A **bipartite graph** is a graph whose vertex set can be partitioned into two disjoint, independent sets $U$ and $V$ such that every edge in the graph connects a vertex in $U$ to a vertex in $V$. No edge exists between two vertices belonging to the same partition.

Formally, a bipartite graph is defined as:

$$G = (U, V, E)$$

where:
- $U \cap V = \emptyset$ (disjoint vertex sets)
- $E \subseteq U \times V$ (every edge joins one vertex from each side)
- $n = \vert U \vert + \vert V \vert$ (total vertex count)
- $m = \vert E \vert$ (total edge count)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bipartite graphs matter in Web Mining:** Many real-world web structures are inherently bipartite. Examples include User–Item networks (Amazon, Netflix), Document–Term matrices (text mining), Author–Conference networks (bibliographic mining), and Customer–Product graphs (e-commerce recommendation engines). Modeling such data as bipartite graphs lets us exploit symmetry and dual-side patterns.

## 1.2 What is a Core in a Bipartite Graph?

The classical **$k$-core** of a graph is the maximal subgraph in which every vertex has degree at least $k$. For bipartite graphs, this single-parameter definition is insufficient because the two partitions play structurally different roles.

This leads to the **$(k, \ell)$-core** (also called the **bipartite core**):

$$\text{Core}_{k,\ell}(G) = \text{maximal subgraph } H = (U_H, V_H, E_H) \subseteq G$$

such that:
- For every $u \in U_H$ : $\deg_{H}(u) \geq k$
- For every $v \in V_H$ : $\deg_{H}(v) \geq \ell$

Here $k$ and $\ell$ are **positive integers** that act as independent degree thresholds for the two partitions. The resulting $(k, \ell)$-core is the largest possible subgraph satisfying both constraints simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Recall:** A $(k, \ell)$-core in a bipartite graph $G = (U, V, E)$ is the maximal induced subgraph in which every vertex in $U$ retains at least $k$ neighbors in $V$ and every vertex in $V$ retains at least $\ell$ neighbors in $U$.

## 1.3 What is a Bipartite Core Community?

A **Bipartite Core Community** is the connected (or dense) component extracted from a bipartite graph using the $(k, \ell)$-core decomposition. It represents a tightly knit sub-network of entities on both sides (e.g., a set of users and a set of items/products) where every entity is engaged with multiple entities on the opposite side.

The community has three defining properties:

1. **Density guarantee** — Every entity (user, document, etc.) on each side is engaged above a minimum threshold.
2. **Maximality** — You cannot add any extra vertex or edge from the original graph without violating the threshold.
3. **Nestedness** — $\text{Core}_{k+1, \ell+1} \subseteq \text{Core}_{k, \ell}$ — higher cores are strict subsets of lower cores.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Movie Club":**
> Imagine a cinema hall with two groups of people — **Critics** ($U$) and **Movies** ($V$). An edge exists when a critic has reviewed a particular movie. A $(k, \ell)$-core is the largest sub-hall where **every critic has reviewed at least $k$ movies** AND **every movie has been reviewed by at least $\ell$ critics**.
>
> - If we ask for a $(3, 5)$-core, we want critics who watched at least 3 movies and movies that attracted at least 5 critics.
> - Increase $k$ and $\ell$ and the community shrinks to its most devoted, active participants.
> - This is exactly what e-commerce platforms, recommender systems, and bibliometric services exploit to find "power user × popular item" cores.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Size of left partition | $\vert U \vert$ | $n_U$ |
| Size of right partition | $\vert V \vert$ | $n_V$ |
| Number of edges | $\vert E \vert$ | $m$ |
| Minimum left degree | $k$ | $\geq 1$ |
| Minimum right degree | $\ell$ | $\geq 1$ |
| Core density | $\rho_{k,\ell}$ | $\frac{2 \cdot \vert E_H \vert}{\vert U_H \vert \cdot \vert V_H \vert}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundations

A bipartite graph $G = (U, V, E)$ can be represented by its **bi-adjacency matrix** $B \in \{0, 1\}^{|U| \times |V|}$ where:

$$B_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (u_i, v_j) \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The degree of a vertex is given by row/column sums:

$$\deg_U(u_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{|V|} B_{ij}$$

$$\deg_V(v_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{|U|} B_{ij}$$

## 2.2 The (k, ℓ)-Core Decomposition — Step-by-Step Logic

The core is found by a **peeling (iterative pruning) algorithm**. Here is the structural logic broken into clear phases:

### Phase 1 — Initialisation
- Compute $\deg_U(u)$ for every $u \in U$
- Compute $\deg_V(v)$ for every $v \in V$
- Initialise the candidate set $H = G$

### Phase 2 — Threshold Filtering
- Mark every $u \in U$ with $\deg_H(u) < k$ as **pruned**
- Mark every $v \in V$ with $\deg_H(v) < \ell$ as **pruned**

### Phase 3 — Cascade Update
- Remove pruned vertices and their incident edges from $H$
- Re-compute degrees of remaining vertices because neighbours were lost
- If any vertex falls below its threshold, mark it as pruned

### Phase 4 — Termination
- Continue Phase 2 and 3 until no vertex violates the threshold
- The resulting $H$ is the $(k, \ell)$-core

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why peeling works:** The threshold condition is monotonic — once a vertex is removed, it can never return. So the algorithm converges in at most $n_U + n_V$ rounds, but a well-implemented queue-based version runs in **linear time**.

## 2.3 Properties of (k, ℓ)-Cores

| Property | Statement |
|---|---|
| **Existence** | A $(k, \ell)$-core may be empty (especially for high $k, \ell$). |
| **Uniqueness** | The $(k, \ell)$-core of a given $G$ is unique. |
| **Nestedness** | $\text{Core}_{k',\ell'}(G) \subseteq \text{Core}_{k,\ell}(G)$ whenever $k' \geq k$ AND $\ell' \geq \ell$. |
| **Maximality** | No supergraph of the core satisfies the same degree constraints. |
| **Induced subgraph** | The core is always a vertex-induced subgraph of $G$. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises every key formula required for KTU board examinations on this topic. **Critical note:** Avoid the bare pipe symbol inside table cells — the `\vert` and `\mid` rendering is used to protect markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bipartite graph notation | $G = (U, V, E)$ | $U \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \subseteq U \times V$ |
| 2 | Bi-adjacency matrix | $B \in \{0,1\}^{\vert U \vert \times \vert V \vert}$ | $B_{ij} = 1 \iff (u_i,v_j) \in E$ |
| 3 | Left degree | $\deg_U(u) = \sum_{j} B_{uj}$ | Counts edges from $u$ into $V$ |
| 4 | Right degree | $\deg_V(v) = \sum_{i} B_{iv}$ | Counts edges from $v$ into $U$ |
| 5 | Core threshold | $\deg_H(u) \geq k,\ \deg_H(v) \geq \ell$ | For all $u \in U_H, v \in V_H$ |
| 6 | Core nestedness | $\text{Core}_{k',\ell'} \subseteq \text{Core}_{k,\ell}$ | If $k' \geq k$ AND $\ell' \geq \ell$ |
| 7 | Core density | $\rho_{k,\ell} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert E_H \vert}{\vert U_H \vert \cdot \vert V_H \vert}$ | Ranges in $[0, 1]$ |
| 8 | Average left degree | $\bar{d}_U = \dfrac{2 \vert E_H \vert}{\vert U_H \vert}$ | After core extraction |
| 9 | Average right degree | $\bar{d}_V = \dfrac{2 \vert E_H \vert}{\vert V_H \vert}$ | After core extraction |
| 10 | Peeling complexity | $O(\vert E \vert)$ time, $O(\vert V \vert)$ memory | Queue-based implementation |
| 11 | Number of cores | $O(k_{\max} \cdot \ell_{\max})$ | Across all valid $(k,\ell)$ pairs |
| 12 | Core number of $u$ | $\kappa_U(u) = \max\{k : u \in \text{Core}_{k,\ell}\}$ | Fixed $\ell$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Web Mining

| Web-Mining Application | Bipartite Setting | $(k, \ell)$-Core Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Recommendation Systems** | Users $\leftrightarrow$ Products | Identify customers who buy many items + popular items with many buyers |
| **Text Mining (Document Clustering)** | Documents $\leftrightarrow$ Terms | Find documents sharing many terms + terms common across many documents |
| **Social Tagging Networks** | Users $\leftrightarrow$ Tags / Resources | Discover tag communities with engaged users and rich resources |
| **Citation Networks** | Authors $\leftrightarrow$ Papers | Find prolific authors and heavily cited papers simultaneously |
| **Web Graph Hyperlink Mining** | Domains $\leftrightarrow$ Domains (host-level) | Dense inter-domain link communities |
| **Ad Targeting** | Users $\leftrightarrow$ Ads | Core user-base whose engagement justifies high-CPC ad slots |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Peeling Algorithm on a 6-Vertex Bipartite Graph

Let us trace the entire $(k, \ell)$-core extraction with $k = 2, \ell = 2$.

### Initial Graph

Let $G = (U, V, E)$ with:

$$U = \{u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4\}$$

$$V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3\}$$

$$E = \{(u_1,v_1), (u_1,v_2), (u_2,v_1), (u_2,v_2), (u_2,v_3), (u_3,v_2), (u_3,v_3), (u_4,v_3)\}$$

### Step 1 — Compute Initial Degrees

For each vertex in $U$:

$$\deg_U(u_1) = \vert \{v_1, v_2\} \vert = 2$$

$$\deg_U(u_2) = \vert \{v_1, v_2, v_3\} \vert = 3$$

$$\deg_U(u_3) = \vert \{v_2, v_3\} \vert = 2$$

$$\deg_U(u_4) = \vert \{v_3\} \vert = 1$$

For each vertex in $V$:

$$\deg_V(v_1) = \vert \{u_1, u_2\} \vert = 2$$

$$\deg_V(v_2) = \vert \{u_1, u_2, u_3\} \vert = 3$$

$$\deg_V(v_3) = \vert \{u_2, u_3, u_4\} \vert = 3$$

### Step 2 — Apply Threshold $k=2,\ \ell=2$

Check each vertex against its threshold:

| Vertex | Partition | Degree | Required | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $u_1$ | $U$ | 2 | $\geq 2$ | Keep |
| $u_2$ | $U$ | 3 | $\geq 2$ | Keep |
| $u_3$ | $U$ | 2 | $\geq 2$ | Keep |
| $u_4$ | $U$ | 1 | $\geq 2$ | **PRUNE** |
| $v_1$ | $V$ | 2 | $\geq 2$ | Keep |
| $v_2$ | $V$ | 3 | $\geq 2$ | Keep |
| $v_3$ | $V$ | 3 | $\geq 2$ | Keep |

### Step 3 — Remove Pruned Vertex $u_4$ and Cascade

Remove $u_4$ and its edge $(u_4, v_3)$. Update degrees:

$$\deg_V(v_3) \leftarrow 3 - 1 = 2$$

Check $v_3$ again: $2 \geq 2$ ✓ — keep $v_3$.

No further vertex violates the threshold. The peeling process halts.

### Step 4 — Resultant $(2, 2)$-Core

$$H = (\{u_1, u_2, u_3\},\ \{v_1, v_2, v_3\},\ E_H)$$

$$E_H = \{(u_1,v_1),\ (u_1,v_2),\ (u_2,v_1),\ (u_2,v_2),\ (u_2,v_3),\ (u_3,v_2),\ (u_3,v_3)\}$$

### Step 5 — Verify Final Degrees

$$\deg_H(u_1) = 2 \geq 2 \quad ✓$$

$$\deg_H(u_2) = 3 \geq 2 \quad ✓$$

$$\deg_H(u_3) = 2 \geq 2 \quad ✓$$

$$\deg_H(v_1) = 2 \geq 2 \quad ✓$$

$$\deg_H(v_2) = 3 \geq 2 \quad ✓$$

$$\deg_H(v_3) = 2 \geq 2 \quad ✓$$

All constraints satisfied → $H$ is the valid $(2, 2)$-core.

### Step 6 — Compute Core Density

$$\rho_{2,2} = \frac{2 \cdot \vert E_H \vert}{\vert U_H \vert \cdot \vert V_H \vert}$$

$$\rho_{2,2} = \frac{2 \times 7}{3 \times 3} = \frac{14}{9} \approx 1.555$$

The density exceeds 1, which simply reflects the asymmetric edge distribution in bipartite graphs (density is not capped at 1 when partitions have different sizes — formal normalized density is reported instead).

> [!NOTE]
> **Normalized density** uses $\min(\vert U_H \vert, \vert V_H \vert)$ as denominator: $\rho^{norm}_{2,2} = \frac{\vert E_H \vert}{\vert U_H \vert \cdot \vert V_H \vert} = \frac{7}{9} \approx 0.778$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Multiple Cores of the Same Graph

Continuing with the same graph, let us compute multiple cores:

| Core $(k,\ell)$ | $\vert U_H \vert$ | $\vert V_H \vert$ | $\vert E_H \vert$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,1)$ | 4 | 3 | 8 | Entire graph — every vertex has degree ≥ 1 |
| $(2,2)$ | 3 | 3 | 7 | $u_4$ pruned |
| $(2,3)$ | 2 | 2 | 4 | Only $u_1,u_2$ paired with $v_1,v_2$ survive |
| $(3,3)$ | 1 | 1 | 1 | Only $(u_2, v_2)$ satisfies both ≥ 3 |

### Proof of Nestedness

$$\text{Core}_{3,3} \subseteq \text{Core}_{2,3} \subseteq \text{Core}_{2,2} \subseteq \text{Core}_{1,1}$$

This satisfies the nestedness rule because $(3 \geq 2 \geq 2 \geq 1)$ AND $(3 \geq 3 \geq 2 \geq 1)$.

## 3.3 Formal Correctness Argument

The peeling algorithm terminates with the correct $(k, \ell)$-core because:

1. **Termination** — Each iteration removes at least one vertex, so at most $\vert U \vert + \vert V \vert$ iterations occur.
2. **Correctness of constraint** — At termination, every remaining vertex has degree $\geq$ its threshold by definition.
3. **Maximality** — Suppose vertex $w$ was removed. It was removed because its degree dropped below the threshold due to prior removals. Adding $w$ back to $H$ would still leave its degree $\lt$ threshold → no vertex can be restored without violation.
4. **Uniqueness** — Suppose two cores $H_1, H_2$ both satisfy the constraint. Their union also satisfies it (degrees only increase with more vertices), contradicting maximality of both. Hence $H_1 = H_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time complexity derivation:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{peel}} &= O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \\ &= O(\vert E \vert)\ \text{since}\ \vert E \vert \geq \vert V \vert / 2\ \text{in any meaningful bipartite core} \end{aligned}$$
>
> This is **linear** in input size — the same complexity as the classical $k$-core peeling (Matula–Beck algorithm).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Initial Bipartite Graph (Worked Example)

```mermaid
graph LR
    u1((u1)) --- v1((v1))
    u1((u1)) --- v2((v2))
    u2((u2)) --- v1((v1))
    u2((u2)) --- v2((v2))
    u2((u2)) --- v3((v3))
    u3((u3)) --- v2((v2))
    u3((u3)) --- v3((v3))
    u4((u4)) --- v3((v3))
```

> **Legend:** Circles on the **left** = $U$ partition (u1–u4). Circles on the **right** = $V$ partition (v1–v3). Edges represent links between partitions.

## 4.2 Resultant (2,2)-Core After Pruning u4

```mermaid
graph LR
    u1((u1)) --- v1((v1))
    u1((u1)) --- v2((v2))
    u2((u2)) --- v1((v1))
    u2((u2)) --- v2((v2))
    u2((u2)) --- v3((v3))
    u3((u3)) --- v2((v2))
    u3((u3)) --- v3((v3))
```

> **Observation:** Vertex $u_4$ has been removed along with edge $(u_4, v_3)$. All remaining vertices satisfy $\deg \geq 2$ on their respective sides. This is the **bipartite core community**.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Peeling Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Bipartite Graph G equals U V E] --> B[Build Bi-Adjacency Matrix B]
    B --> C[Compute Initial Degrees degU and degV]
    C --> D[Load Vertices into Queue Q]
    D --> E{Pop Vertex w from Q}
    E --> F{w belongs to U?}
    F -->|Yes| G{degU of w less than k?}
    F -->|No| H{degV of w less than l?}
    G -->|Yes| I[Mark w as Pruned and Remove]
    G -->|No| J[Skip Keep Vertex]
    H -->|Yes| I
    H -->|No| J
    I --> K[Update Degrees of Neighbors]
    K --> L{Neighbor Below Threshold?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Push Neighbor to Q]
    L -->|No| N[Continue]
    M --> E
    N --> E
    J --> E
    E --> O{Q Empty?}
    O -->|No| E
    O -->|Yes| P[Output: k l Core as H]
    P --> Q[Compute Core Density and Statistics]
    Q --> R[Return Bipartite Core Community]
```

> **Reading guide:** The flowchart maps the iterative peeling mechanism — pop a vertex, check its side-specific threshold, prune if violated, and propagate the degree update to its neighbours until the queue empties.

## 4.4 Nested Core Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Core 1 1 - Entire Graph 4 left, 3 right] --> L2[Core 2 2 - 3 left, 3 right]
    L2 --> L3[Core 2 3 - 2 left, 2 right]
    L3 --> L4[Core 3 3 - 1 left, 1 right]
    L4 --> L5[Core 4 4 - Empty]
```

> **Reading guide:** Each successive core is strictly contained in the previous one. The diagram illustrates the **nested property** that is foundational to the hierarchical decomposition of bipartite graphs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Define the $(k, \ell)$-core of a bipartite graph. Explain why two parameters are needed instead of one.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A $(k, \ell)$-core of a bipartite graph $G = (U, V, E)$ is the maximal induced subgraph $H$ in which every vertex in $U$ has degree at least $k$ and every vertex in $V$ has degree at least $\ell$.

Two parameters are needed because the two partitions play structurally different roles in bipartite graphs — they often have different sizes, densities, and semantic meaning. For example, in a user-item network, users and items are not interchangeable; a community of "active users and popular items" requires separate thresholds. A single parameter would either over-prune the smaller side or under-prune the larger side.

[Mark allocation: 1 mark — correct definition; 1 mark — bi-partition distinction; 1 mark — example/justification.]

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**State any three properties of $(k, \ell)$-cores.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Uniqueness** — The $(k, \ell)$-core of a bipartite graph $G$ is unique.
2. **Nestedness** — If $k' \geq k$ AND $\ell' \geq \ell$, then $\text{Core}_{k',\ell'}(G) \subseteq \text{Core}_{k,\ell}(G)$.
3. **Maximality** — No vertex or edge from $G$ can be added to the core without violating at least one degree constraint.

[1 mark per correctly stated property.]

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Internal Choice Option]

**(a)** Explain the $(k, \ell)$-core decomposition algorithm for a bipartite graph with a suitable example. Show all steps of the peeling procedure and verify the result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how bipartite core communities are applied in (i) recommender systems and (ii) document clustering. Explain what the parameters $k$ and $\ell$ represent in each application. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Algorithm + Worked Example (7 marks)

**Algorithm Outline (3 marks):**

The $(k, \ell)$-core decomposition uses an iterative **peeling** strategy:

1. Compute $\deg_U(u)$ for every $u \in U$ and $\deg_V(v)$ for every $v \in V$.
2. Initialise a queue $Q$ with all vertices that violate the threshold $\deg_U(u) < k$ or $\deg_V(v) < \ell$.
3. While $Q$ is non-empty:
   - Pop vertex $w$.
   - For each neighbour $x$ of $w$:
     - Decrement $\deg(x)$ by 1.
     - If $\deg(x)$ drops below its threshold, push $x$ into $Q$.
   - Remove $w$ and its incident edges.
4. Return the remaining subgraph as the $(k, \ell)$-core.

**Complexity:** $O(\vert E \vert)$ time and $O(\vert V \vert)$ memory.

**Worked Example (4 marks):**

Consider the bipartite graph:

$$U = \{u_1, u_2, u_3\}, \quad V = \{v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\}$$

$$E = \{(u_1,v_1), (u_1,v_2), (u_1,v_3), (u_2,v_2), (u_2,v_3), (u_3,v_3), (u_3,v_4)\}$$

Compute initial degrees:

$$\deg_U(u_1) = 3,\ \deg_U(u_2) = 2,\ \deg_U(u_3) = 2$$

$$\deg_V(v_1) = 1,\ \deg_V(v_2) = 2,\ \deg_V(v_3) = 3,\ \deg_V(v_4) = 1$$

**Find the $(2, 2)$-core:**

| Vertex | Degree | Required | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $u_1$ | 3 | $\geq 2$ | Keep |
| $u_2$ | 2 | $\geq 2$ | Keep |
| $u_3$ | 2 | $\geq 2$ | Keep |
| $v_1$ | 1 | $\geq 2$ | **Prune** |
| $v_2$ | 2 | $\geq 2$ | Keep |
| $v_3$ | 3 | $\geq 2$ | Keep |
| $v_4$ | 1 | $\geq 2$ | **Prune** |

After removing $v_1$ and $v_4$:

$$\deg_U(u_1) \leftarrow 3 - 1 = 2 \quad ✓$$

$$\deg_U(u_3) \leftarrow 2 - 1 = 1 \quad \text{NOW BELOW THRESHOLD} \Rightarrow \text{Prune } u_3$$

After removing $u_3$:

$$\deg_V(v_3) \leftarrow 3 - 1 = 2 \quad ✓$$

No further violations. **Final $(2, 2)$-core:**

$$H = (\{u_1, u_2\},\ \{v_2, v_3\},\ \{(u_1,v_2),(u_1,v_3),(u_2,v_2),(u_2,v_3)\})$$

Core density:

$$\rho_{2,2} = \frac{2 \times 4}{2 \times 2} = 2.0$$

Normalized density = $\frac{4}{4} = 1.0$ — a perfectly dense bipartite subgraph (a complete bipartite $K_{2,2}$).

#### Part (b) — Applications (7 marks)

**(i) Recommender Systems (3.5 marks):**

In e-commerce or streaming platforms, the bipartite graph is built as **Users $\leftrightarrow$ Items** (movies, songs, products). A $(k, \ell)$-core extracts a community where:

- $k$ = minimum number of items each user has purchased/rated in this community.
- $\ell$ = minimum number of users each item has been purchased/rated by.

This identifies a high-engagement micro-segment useful for **collaborative filtering**, **cross-selling**, and **targeted promotions**.

**(ii) Document Clustering (3.5 marks):**

In text mining, documents and terms form a bipartite graph via a document-term matrix (presence/absence or TF-IDF weighted). A $(k, \ell)$-core extracts clusters where:

- $k$ = minimum number of shared core terms each document contains.
- $\ell$ = minimum number of documents each term appears in.

This produces **tight topical clusters** that are robust against noise words and one-off documents, improving precision of web search and topic modelling.

---

### Question B-B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Internal Choice Option]

**(a)** Prove that the $(k, \ell)$-core of a bipartite graph is unique. State and justify the nestedness property. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the $(k, \ell)$-core with the classical $k$-core for general graphs. Explain with a diagram why a single-parameter $k$-core is insufficient for bipartite settings. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Proof of Uniqueness and Nestedness (7 marks)

**Uniqueness Proof (4 marks):**

**Statement:** For any bipartite graph $G = (U, V, E)$ and thresholds $(k, \ell)$, the $(k, \ell)$-core of $G$ is unique.

**Proof by contradiction:**

Assume two distinct cores $H_1$ and $H_2$ both satisfy the $(k, \ell)$-threshold. Since they are vertex-induced subgraphs of $G$, define:

$$H^+ = (U_{H_1} \cup U_{H_2},\ V_{H_1} \cup V_{H_2},\ E_{H_1} \cup E_{H_2})$$

For any $u \in U_{H_1} \cup U_{H_2}$:

$$\deg_{H^+}(u) \geq \deg_{H_1}(u) \geq k \quad \text{or} \quad \deg_{H^+}(u) \geq \deg_{H_2}(u) \geq k$$

Similarly for $v \in V$. Therefore $H^+$ also satisfies the threshold. But this contradicts the **maximality** of both $H_1$ and $H_2$, which assert that no further vertex or edge can be added.

Hence $H_1 = H_2$, proving uniqueness. ∎

**Nestedness Property (3 marks):**

**Statement:** If $k' \geq k$ AND $\ell' \geq \ell$, then $\text{Core}_{k',\ell'}(G) \subseteq \text{Core}_{k,\ell}(G)$.

**Justification:** Every vertex in $\text{Core}_{k',\ell'}$ has degree $\geq k' \geq k$ on its side, and $\geq \ell' \geq \ell$ on the other. Hence it qualifies for $\text{Core}_{k,\ell}$ as well. The set inclusion follows by definition of the maximal subgraph.

#### Part (b) — Comparison with Classical k-Core (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Classical $k$-core | $(k, \ell)$-core |
|---|---|---|
| Graph type | Any graph | Bipartite graphs only |
| Parameters | One ($k$) | Two ($k, \ell$) |
| Threshold | Uniform across all vertices | Side-specific |
| Computational complexity | $O(\vert E \vert)$ | $O(\vert E \vert)$ |
| Useful for bipartite data | Weak — ignores side distinction | Strong — models dual semantics |
| Nestedness | $\text{Core}_{k+1} \subseteq \text{Core}_k$ | Two-dimensional nesting |

**Why single $k$ is insufficient (3 marks):**

Imagine a User-Item graph with 1000 users and 10 items. A single $k$-core with $k = 10$ would extract only users with degree $\geq 10$, but in such a dense small-item scenario, almost no user has degree 10 — the core would be empty.

A $(2, 5)$-core, however, asks for users with $\geq 2$ items and items with $\geq 5$ users — a more meaningful structure that respects the asymmetry between the partitions.

**Diagram (block-level):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Item Bipartite Graph] --> B{Single k Core}
    A --> C{Two Parameter k l Core}
    B --> D[Uniform Threshold Problem]
    D --> E[Empty or Biased Core]
    C --> F[Side Aware Threshold]
    F --> G[Balanced Dense Community]
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting partition distinction** — Students often write a single threshold for both $U$ and $V$. Always state **two separate parameters** $k$ and $\ell$.
> 2. **Confusing degree directions** — In a bipartite graph, the degree of a $U$-vertex counts only its $V$-neighbours and vice versa. Do not count same-side edges (there are none).
> 3. **Missing the cascade step** — In the peeling algorithm, after removing a vertex you **must** update the degrees of all its neighbours. Many students forget this and stop after the first round, producing an invalid core.
> 4. **Mixing up nestedness direction** — Higher $(k, \ell)$ → **smaller** core. The set inclusion arrow points from the higher core to the lower core, not the other way around.
> 5. **Ignoring maximality** — A core is not just any subgraph satisfying the threshold; it must be the **largest** such subgraph.
> 6. **Not citing complexity** — Board answers almost always lose marks if they omit the $O(\vert E \vert)$ time complexity of peeling.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bipartite graph** $G = (U, V, E)$ has two disjoint vertex sets with edges crossing between them only.
- **$(k, \ell)$-core** is the maximal induced subgraph where every $u \in U$ has degree $\geq k$ and every $v \in V$ has degree $\geq \ell$.
- **Core ≠ Community** — A core is a structural guarantee; a community often refers to a connected, dense, or semantically meaningful component of the core.
- **Peeling algorithm** removes vertices that violate the threshold in iterative waves until stable.
- **Time complexity** of peeling is linear: $O(\vert E \vert)$ time and $O(\vert V \vert)$ memory.
- **Nestedness**: $\text{Core}_{k',\ell'} \subseteq \text{Core}_{k,\ell}$ when $k' \geq k$ AND $\ell' \geq \ell$.
- **Uniqueness** is guaranteed for every valid $(k, \ell)$ pair.
- **Maximality** ensures the core cannot be enlarged without violating a constraint.
- **Real-world mappings**:
  - Users–Items → E-commerce / Recommender systems
  - Documents–Terms → Text mining / Clustering
  - Authors–Papers → Bibliometrics
  - Customers–Products → Targeted advertising
- **Normalized density** $\rho^{norm} = \frac{\vert E_H \vert}{\vert U_H \vert \cdot \vert V_H \vert}$ is the preferred density metric for bipartite cores.
- **Always update neighbour degrees** during peeling — cascade propagation is the heart of the algorithm.
- **Use two parameters**, never one, when working with bipartite graphs — single-threshold $k$-cores ignore the structural asymmetry that defines bipartite networks.
- **Mermaid note**: Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are kept plain — no markdown formatting inside quoted labels, per KTU diagram safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Web Crawling** is the automated, systematic process of traversing the World Wide Web by downloading web pages and following hyperlinks embedded within them, typically performed by a software agent known as a **Web Crawler**, **Spider**, or **Bot**. The crawler's primary objective is to collect (or update) an index of web pages maintained by a search engine.

A **Basic Crawler Algorithm** is the foundational, single-threaded or multi-threaded procedural logic that defines how a crawler initializes its seed set, fetches pages, extracts hyperlinks, and adds them to a queue for subsequent fetching.

A **Breadth-First Crawler (BFS Crawler)** is a specific implementation of a crawler that traverses the web graph in **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** order — meaning it visits all the URLs at the current depth (or distance from the seed) before moving on to URLs at the next depth level. In a BFS crawler, the URL frontier behaves as a **First-In-First-Out (FIFO)** queue, ensuring that pages closer to the seed URL are fetched and processed before pages that are "deeper" in the web graph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Crawling algorithms form the *data acquisition backbone* of every search engine and web mining pipeline. Mastering BFS crawler mechanics is essential to understand search engine indexing, focused crawling, and PageRank-style graph mining that follows in later modules.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a **vast library where books reference other books** in footnotes. Your task is to read every important book and build a catalogue.

You begin with **one starting book** (the *seed URL*). You read it, and write down the titles of every book it references. Now:

- A **Depth-First Crawler (DFS)** behaves like an obsessive reader: it picks the *first* referenced book, dives into it, then the *first* reference from that book, and so on — going deep into a single thread of references.
- A **Breadth-First Crawler (BFS)** behaves like a disciplined librarian: it reads the starting book, lists *all* referenced books, then visits **each of those books in order** before peeking inside the books they reference. It expands level by level, like ripples spreading outward on a pond.

In technical terms, BFS uses a **queue** (think: a line at a canteen — first one in is the first one served), while DFS uses a **stack** (think: a stack of plates — last one placed is the first one taken).

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference Box:**
> - **Seed URL(s):** Initial starting web page(s) given to the crawler.
> - **URL Frontier:** The waiting list (queue) of URLs scheduled to be fetched.
> - **Crawled Set (Visited Set):** The set of URLs already fetched — used to prevent revisits.
> - **FIFO Queue:** First-In-First-Out structure that defines BFS ordering.
> - **Web Graph:** Directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = pages and $E$ = hyperlinks.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Web Graph Model

The web is formally modeled as a **directed graph**:

$$G = (V, E)$$

where:
- $V$ = set of web pages (vertices / nodes)
- $E$ = set of hyperlinks (directed edges)

If page $p$ contains a hyperlink pointing to page $q$, then there exists a directed edge $(p, q) \in E$.

A BFS crawler performs a **level-order traversal** of this graph starting from one or more seed vertices.

### 2.2 Operational Steps of a Basic BFS Crawler Algorithm

A basic BFS crawler follows this structured sequence at every iteration:

1. **Initialization Phase**
   - Begin with a set of seed URLs: $S = \{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_k\}$.
   - Insert all seeds into an empty FIFO queue called the **URL Frontier** $F$.
   - Initialize an empty **Hash Table / Bloom Filter** called the *Seen Set* $C$ (Crawled set).
   - For each seed $u_i$, add it to $C$ to mark it as already "seen."

2. **Main Loop (Repeats until $F$ is empty or termination criteria met)**
   - **Dequeue** the URL $u$ at the head of $F$.
   - **Fetch** the document $D$ corresponding to $u$ using HTTP GET request.
   - **Resolve DNS** for $u$ to get IP address (if not cached).
   - **Download** $D$ and store it in a local repository / pass to indexing pipeline.
   - **Parse** $D$ to extract:
     - Text content (for indexing)
     - All outgoing hyperlinks: $H(u) = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_m\}$
   - For each extracted URL $v \in H(u)$:
     - Normalize $v$ (resolve relative URLs, strip fragments, lowercase host, etc.).
     - Check if $v \in C$ (already seen).
     - If **not** in $C$, add $v$ to $C$ and **enqueue** $v$ into $F$.

3. **Termination**
   - Stop when $F$ is empty, a page count limit is hit, or a time budget expires.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Property Sheet

| Concept / Property | Symbolic Form | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Web Graph | $G = (V, E)$ | Directed graph of web pages and hyperlinks |
| Out-degree of a page | $\deg^{+}(p) = \vert H(p) \vert$ | Number of hyperlinks on page $p$ — average is **~50** for static web |
| BFS Frontier (Queue) | $F = [u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_n]$ | FIFO structure; head dequeued first |
| Crawled / Seen Set | $C \subseteq V$ | Set of URLs already fetched — prevents loops |
| BFS Level of a page | $\text{level}(v)$ | Shortest-path hop distance from any seed |
| Time complexity (theoretical) | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Standard BFS complexity on a graph |
| Space complexity | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | To store visited set + frontier |
| Crawl rate | $R = \dfrac{\vert V_{\text{fetched}} \vert}{T}$ | Pages crawled per second — target $\sim$**1000 pages/sec** for production crawlers |
| Duplicate URL ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{\vert V_{\text{duplicate}} \vert}{\vert V_{\text{seen}} \vert}$ | Fraction of URLs that are duplicates — BFS tends to reduce this |
| Frontier size bound | $\vert F \vert \leq \sum_{i=0}^{d} N_i$ | Where $N_i$ = pages at BFS level $i$, $d$ = current depth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of BFS Crawlers:**
> BFS is favored in **early-stage / focused crawlers** because it:
> 1. Distributes requests across many hosts early (better politeness, parallelism).
> 2. Reduces revisit of the same host in quick succession.
> 3. Naturally yields a "broad" snapshot of the web — ideal for building a *general* search index.
> 4. Has a predictable, memory-bounded frontier size for small depths.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind BFS for Web Crawling

The BFS strategy is not chosen arbitrarily. The web has unique properties:
- **Power-law in-degree distribution** (a few hubs like Wikipedia, Reddit have millions of in-links).
- **Nepotistic linking** — pages often link to other pages on the same host.

A pure DFS would hammer a single host repeatedly (bad for politeness). BFS naturally **rotates across hosts** at each level, distributing load — making it the canonical choice for the *basic* and *universal* crawler in the Heydon-Najork seminal framework.

### 2.5 Components of a Production BFS Crawler

Even a "basic" BFS crawler has these subsystems:

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| **URL Frontier** | Priority/FIFO queue of URLs awaiting fetch |
| **Fetcher (Worker Threads)** | Issues HTTP requests, downloads pages |
| **DNS Resolver** | Cached hostname-to-IP lookup |
| **Parser** | Extracts text + links from HTML |
| **Duplicate Detector** | Hash/Bloom filter for seen URLs |
| **Politeness Module** | Enforces per-host delay $\Delta t_{\text{host}}$ |
| **Storage** | Compressed page repository (e.g., Disk-based archive) |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive BFS Crawler Algorithm (Pseudocode)

Below is the **complete, step-by-step pseudocode** of a Basic Breadth-First Crawler. This is the canonical reference figure often expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Algorithm: BasicBreadthFirstCrawler} \\
&\textbf{Input:} \text{ A set of seed URLs } S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k\} \\
&\textbf{Output:} \text{ A repository of crawled pages} \\
& \\
&\text{1.  Initialize empty FIFO queue } F \leftarrow \emptyset \\
&\text{2.  Initialize empty hash set } C \leftarrow \emptyset \quad \text{(Crawled / Seen URLs)} \\
&\text{3.  For each seed } s_i \in S: \\
&\text{4.  \qquad ENQUEUE}(F, s_i) \\
&\text{5.  \qquad ADD}(C, \text{NORMALIZE}(s_i)) \\
& \\
&\text{6.  While } F \neq \emptyset \textbf{ do} \\
&\text{7.  \qquad } u \leftarrow \text{DEQUEUE}(F) \\
&\text{8.  \qquad } \text{page} \leftarrow \text{FETCH}(u) \quad \text{(HTTP GET + DNS resolve)} \\
&\text{9.  \qquad \textbf{if} } \text{page} = \text{NULL} \textbf{ then continue} \\
&\text{10. \qquad STORE}(\text{page}, u) \\
&\text{11. \qquad } H(u) \leftarrow \text{EXTRACT\_LINKS}(\text{page}) \\
&\text{12. \qquad For each raw link } v \in H(u): \\
&\text{13. \qquad \qquad } v' \leftarrow \text{NORMALIZE}(v) \quad \text{(strip fragment, lowercase host, absolutize)} \\
&\text{14. \qquad \qquad \textbf{if} } v' \notin C \textbf{ then} \\
&\text{15. \qquad \qquad \qquad ADD}(C, v') \\
&\text{16. \qquad \qquad \qquad ENQUEUE}(F, v') \\
&\text{17. \qquad \textbf{end if} \\
&\text{18. \qquad End For} \\
&\text{19. End While} \\
&\text{20. \textbf{Return} repository}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — BFS Traversal of a Mini Web

**Problem Setup:** Consider a tiny web graph with 6 pages. The seed URL is $A$. Each page links to the pages shown below. Trace the BFS crawl order, the URL frontier state, and the visited set.

**Link structure:**

| Page | Outgoing Links |
|------|----------------|
| A    | B, C, D         |
| B    | E               |
| C    | D, F            |
| D    | (none)          |
| E    | F               |
| F    | (none)          |

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Iteration | Dequeue (Head) | Frontier $F$ (after pop) | Visit (Fetch) | Newly Discovered | Visited Set $C$ | BFS Level |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|-----------|
| Init      | —              | $[A]$                    | —             | A                | $\{A\}$          | 0         |
| 1         | $A$            | $[\,]$                   | A             | B, C, D          | $\{A, B, C, D\}$ | 0         |
| 2         | $B$            | $[C, D]$                 | B             | E                | $\{A,B,C,D,E\}$  | 1         |
| 3         | $C$            | $[D, E]$                 | C             | F (D already seen)| $\{A,B,C,D,E,F\}$| 1         |
| 4         | $D$            | $[E, F]$                 | D             | (none)           | $\{\ldots\}$     | 1         |
| 5         | $E$            | $[F]$                    | E             | (F already seen) | $\{\ldots\}$     | 2         |
| 6         | $F$            | $[\,]$                   | F             | (none)           | $\{\ldots\}$     | 2         |
| 7         | (queue empty)  | STOP                     | —             | —                | —                | —         |

**Crawl Order:** $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F$

**Key Observations from the Trace:**

1. **Crawl order = BFS order of the web graph** — every page at level 1 ($\{B, C, D\}$) is fetched before any page at level 2 ($\{E, F\}$).
2. **Duplicate suppression at line 14** is what makes BFS efficient — without it, $D$ would be re-enqueued when $C$ is processed, and $F$ would be re-enqueued when $E$ is processed.
3. **Frontier size peaks at 2** in this example; in a real crawl it can reach **hundreds of millions** of URLs.

### 3.3 Derivation of BFS Frontier Memory Bound

For a BFS crawler crawling up to depth $d$ in a web graph with average out-degree $\bar{d}$ and branching factor $b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } b & = \text{ mean unique out-links per page:} \\
\text{then page count at level } i: \quad N_i &\leq b \cdot N_{i-1} \\
N_i &\leq b^i \cdot N_0 \quad \text{where } N_0 = \vert S \vert = \text{seeds}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the **frontier size at depth $d$** (worst case, no duplicates) is bounded by:

$$
\vert F_d \vert \leq \sum_{i=0}^{d} N_i \leq \vert S \vert \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{d} b^i = \vert S \vert \cdot \dfrac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}
$$

For $b = 50$ (a typical static-web average), $d = 5$, $\vert S \vert = 1$:

$$
\vert F_5 \vert \leq \dfrac{50^6 - 1}{49} \approx 2.6 \times 10^8 \text{ URLs}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This geometric growth is why **pure BFS does not scale to the entire web** in a single frontier — production crawlers use **distributed shards**, **host partitioning**, and **priority queues** instead of a naive single FIFO. This motivates the more advanced architectures (Mercator, UbiCrawler, IRLbot) studied later in the module.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison: BFS vs DFS vs Focused Crawler

| Property | BFS Crawler | DFS Crawler | Focused Crawler |
|---|---|---|---|
| Underlying data structure | FIFO Queue | Stack | Priority Queue (Best-First) |
| Traversal order | Level-by-level | Branch-deep | Topic-similarity-weighted |
| Host load distribution | Good (rotates) | Poor (piles on one host) | Depends on classifier |
| Politeness compliance | Naturally better | Often violates | Tunable |
| Page quality | Mixed (broad) | Mixed (deep, narrow) | High (topic-relevant) |
| Memory needed | $O(\vert V \vert)$ frontier | $O(\text{path length})$ | $O(\vert V \vert)$ frontier |
| Use case | Universal search index | Mirror a single site | Vertical / domain mining |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a BFS Crawler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    seedBlock[Seed URLs] --> frontierQueue[URL Frontier FIFO Queue]
    frontierQueue --> dequeueOp[Dequeue Head URL]
    dequeueOp --> dnsRes[DNS Resolver Cache]
    dnsRes --> httpFetch[HTTP Fetcher Worker Thread]
    httpFetch --> parseModule[HTML Parser]
    parseModule --> storeRepo[(Page Repository)]
    parseModule --> linkExtract[Hyperlink Extractor]
    linkExtract --> normalizeStep[URL Normalizer]
    normalizeStep --> dupCheck{Already in Seen Set}
    dupCheck -- No --> addSeen[Add to Seen Set]
    addSeen --> frontierQueue
    dupCheck -- Yes --> discardOp[Discard URL]
    storeRepo --> indexPipe[Indexing Pipeline]
```

### 4.2 BFS Traversal Flow on the Web Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Page A - Level 0"] --> nodeB["Page B - Level 1"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Page C - Level 1"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Page D - Level 1"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Page E - Level 2"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Page F - Level 2"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeE --> nodeF
    seed["Start"] --> nodeA
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows represent hyperlinks. BFS traversal visits **A → {B, C, D} → {E, F}** in that exact order.

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — One Crawl Iteration

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Q as URL Frontier Queue
    participant F as Fetcher
    participant P as Parser
    participant S as Seen Set Filter
    Q->>F: DEQUEUE next URL u
    F->>F: HTTP GET u
    F->>P: raw HTML content
    P->>P: EXTRACT_LINKS(content) = H_u
    loop for each v in H_u
        P->>S: contains(NORMALIZE(v))?
        alt v not in Seen Set
            S-->>P: false
            P->>Q: ENQUEUE NORMALIZE(v)
            P->>S: add NORMALIZE(v)
        else v already seen
            S-->>P: true
            P->>P: discard v
        end
    end
```

### 4.4 Front Queue / Back Queue Architecture (Heydon-Najork Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Front_Queue
        fq1[Priority FQ per host]
    end
    subgraph Back_Queue
        bq1[Biased BQ]
    end
    fetcher1[Fetcher 1] --> bq1
    fetcher2[Fetcher 2] --> bq1
    bq1 --> fq1
    fq1 --> net((Internet))
    net --> fetcher1
    net --> fetcher2
```

> The **Front Queue** enforces *politeness* (per-host delay), while the **Back Queue** maintains *BFS-like breadth* across hosts. This is the architectural bridge between the "basic" BFS crawler and the "Mercator" scalable crawler.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define a **Web Crawler**. With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **Breadth-First Crawler**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Web Crawler** (also called a spider or bot) is a software program that automatically traverses the World Wide Web by downloading web pages and recursively following hyperlinks to discover and fetch new pages, typically for building a search engine index.

A **Breadth-First Crawler** uses a **FIFO queue** as its URL frontier. It starts with one or more **seed URLs**, fetches them, extracts all hyperlinks from each fetched page, and adds *all newly discovered* URLs to the **end** of the queue. The crawler then fetches the URL at the **head** of the queue next. This ensures all pages at BFS-level 1 are fetched before any page at level 2.

> **Mark Distribution (as per KTU key):**
> - Definition of Web Crawler: **1 mark**
> - BFS FIFO queue concept: **1 mark**
> - Diagram / traversal example: **1 mark**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2.** List and briefly explain the **main components of a basic web crawler**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The main components of a basic web crawler are:

1. **Seed URLs** — the initial set of starting web addresses.
2. **URL Frontier (FIFO Queue)** — the waiting list of URLs scheduled to be fetched.
3. **Fetcher / Worker Threads** — issue HTTP GET requests and download page content.
4. **DNS Resolver** — translates hostnames to IP addresses (usually with caching).
5. **Parser** — extracts hyperlinks and text content from HTML.
6. **Seen Set / Duplicate Filter** — a hash set or Bloom filter that records already-seen URLs to prevent revisits and infinite loops.
7. **Storage / Repository** — where fetched pages are stored for later indexing.

> **Mark Distribution:** Each component 0.5 mark × 6 = 3 marks (any 6).

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **basic algorithm of a Breadth-First Crawler** with a neat flowchart and pseudocode. Mention the role of the **URL Frontier** and the **Seen Set**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BFS crawler starts from seed $S_1$ and $S_2$. The link structure of the discovered web graph is as follows:

| Page | Links to |
|------|----------|
| $S_1$ | A, B |
| $S_2$ | C, A |
| A    | D |
| B    | D, C |
| C    | D |
| D    | (none) |

**Trace the BFS crawl order**, show the state of the frontier and the seen set at each step, and **explain how duplicate URLs are handled**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Algorithm and Architecture (7 marks):**

A Basic Breadth-First Crawler is built on a **FIFO queue** (URL Frontier) and a **Hash Set** (Seen Set). The flow is:

1. **Initialize:** Add all seed URLs to the frontier and the seen set.
2. **Loop while frontier is not empty:**
   - Dequeue the head URL $u$.
   - Fetch the page using HTTP.
   - Parse the page to extract hyperlinks $H(u)$.
   - For each link $v$:
     - Normalize $v$ (e.g., strip fragment `#`, lowercase host, absolutize relative URLs).
     - **If $v$ is not in the Seen Set**, add it to the Seen Set and enqueue it to the frontier.
3. **Stop** when the frontier is empty or a termination criterion (page count / time / depth) is met.

**Roles:**
- **URL Frontier (FIFO Queue):** Maintains the BFS order — pages closer to the seed are fetched first. Enforces level-order traversal.
- **Seen Set:** Prevents the crawler from re-fetching the same URL — critical because the web contains many duplicate or near-duplicate URLs and cycles in the link graph.

**Pseudocode** *(refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the exhaustive version)*.

**Flowchart:** *(refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1)*.

**Mark split:** Algorithm narrative (3) + Pseudocode (2) + Frontier/Seen Set roles (2).

---

**(b) Trace (7 marks):**

We assume both seeds are enqueued in the order $S_1, S_2$ (i.e., $S_1$ first).

| Step | Dequeue | Frontier After Pop | Fetch | Newly Found | Visited (Seen) Set After Step |
|------|---------|--------------------|-------|-------------|------------------------------|
| Init | —       | $[S_1, S_2]$       | —     | $S_1, S_2$  | $\{S_1, S_2\}$               |
| 1    | $S_1$   | $[S_2]$            | $S_1$ | A, B        | $\{S_1, S_2, A, B\}$         |
| 2    | $S_2$   | $[A, B]$           | $S_2$ | C (A is duplicate) | $\{S_1, S_2, A, B, C\}$ |
| 3    | $A$     | $[B, C]$           | $A$   | D           | $\{\ldots, D\}$              |
| 4    | $B$     | $[C, D]$           | $B$   | (D dup, C dup) | $\{\ldots\}$              |
| 5    | $C$     | $[D]$              | $C$   | (D dup)     | $\{\ldots\}$                  |
| 6    | $D$     | $[\,]$             | $D$   | (none)      | $\{\ldots\}$                  |
| 7    | (empty) | STOP               | —     | —           | —                            |

**Crawl Order:** $S_1 \rightarrow S_2 \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D$

**Duplicate URL Handling:**
- When $S_2$ is fetched, link $A$ is discovered but is already in the Seen Set — so it is **discarded, not re-enqueued**.
- When $B$ is fetched, both $D$ and $C$ are already in the Seen Set — discarded.
- When $C$ is fetched, $D$ is again a duplicate — discarded.
- This **Seen Set lookup** (a hash lookup, $O(1)$) is what keeps the frontier from exploding in size on real-world web graphs where duplicate URLs are extremely common (often 20–30% of all discovered URLs).

**Mark split:** Trace table (3) + crawl order (1) + duplicate handling explanation (2) + conclusion (1).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Breadth-First Crawlers** and **Depth-First Crawlers**. Discuss, with justification, which is **more suitable for indexing the live web**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BFS crawler begins with a single seed $A$. Page $A$ links to $\{B_1, B_2, B_3\}$. Each $B_i$ links to three new $C$-pages, and each $C$-page links to two new $D$-pages. **Calculate the maximum number of pages the crawler can fetch up to BFS level 3** if no duplicates exist. If the average page-fetch time is $0.5$ seconds, estimate the **total crawl time** to complete all three levels. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | BFS Crawler | DFS Crawler |
|---|---|---|
| Data structure | FIFO Queue | Stack (or recursion) |
| Traversal pattern | Level-by-level (wide) | Branch-deep (narrow) |
| Host load distribution | Even (rotates across hosts) | Concentrated on one path's hosts |
| Politeness | Naturally better | Often violates per-host delay |
| Memory | Stores full frontier $O(b^d)$ | Stores current path $O(d)$ |
| Suited for | Broad, general indexing | Site mirroring, deep scraping |
| Robustness to cycles | Good (with Seen Set) | Risk of deep loops without backtracking rules |

**Justification for live-web indexing:**
BFS is **more suitable for indexing the live web** because:
1. The web is a **shallow-but-wide** graph — most pages are within 4–5 hops of a seed. BFS captures this efficiently.
2. BFS naturally rotates across **different hosts** at each level, respecting **politeness policies** and avoiding overloading any single web server.
3. Early web studies (Cho et al., 1998) showed BFS yields a **higher PageRank-biased** sample in early crawl windows — useful for seeding a high-quality index.
4. DFS would keep hammering the same host/server along a path, triggering **anti-crawl defenses** and wasting bandwidth.

Hence, BFS is the **canonical choice for a basic, universal web crawler**.

**Mark split:** Comparison table (3) + explanation (2) + justification (2).

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

Given the tree-like structure with no duplicates:

- **Level 0:** $A$ → 1 page
- **Level 1:** $A$ has 3 children → **3 pages** ($B_1, B_2, B_3$)
- **Level 2:** Each of 3 $B$-pages has 3 children → $3 \times 3 =$ **9 pages** ($C_1 \ldots C_9$)
- **Level 3:** Each of 9 $C$-pages has 2 children → $9 \times 2 =$ **18 pages** ($D_1 \ldots D_{18}$)

**Total pages fetched up to and including level 3:**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 1 + 3 + 9 + 18 = 31 \text{ pages}
$$

**Total crawl time at 0.5 sec per page:**

$$
T_{\text{crawl}} = 31 \times 0.5 = 15.5 \text{ seconds}
$$

> **Note:** In a real-world BFS crawler, fetching is parallelized — using $k$ worker threads, the effective wall-clock time would be approximately $T_{\text{crawl}} / k$ (ignoring politeness delays).

**Mark split:** Level 1 count (1) + Level 2 count (1) + Level 3 count (1) + Sum (1) + Time calculation (2) + Comment on parallelism (1).

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in BFS Crawler Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Queue with Stack** — stating BFS uses a stack. BFS = queue, DFS = stack. Marks deducted.
> 2. **Forgetting the Seen Set** — without it, your algorithm is incorrect for any real web (which has cycles).
> 3. **Skipping URL Normalization** — failing to mention stripping fragments (`#section`), lowercasing hosts, or absolutizing relative URLs. KTU key gives a separate mark for this.
> 4. **No diagram in 7-mark answers** — KTU explicitly awards 1–2 marks for a "neat flowchart/diagram."
> 5. **Trace table without showing duplicates** — always demonstrate a duplicate-suppression step to earn full marks on trace questions.
> 6. **Confusing BFS with PageRank / Focused Crawling** — these are *not* BFS; BFS uses simple FIFO ordering, not priority or relevance ranking.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Web Crawler:** Automated agent that traverses the web graph by following hyperlinks.
- **Basic BFS Crawler:** Uses a **FIFO queue** as URL frontier; level-by-level traversal.
- **Three pillars:** Seed URLs → URL Frontier (queue) → Seen Set (hash/Bloom filter).
- **Algorithm:** `Enqueue seeds → Loop { Dequeue → Fetch → Parse → Extract Links → Enqueue unseen }`.
- **Web graph** is a **directed graph** $G = (V, E)$ — vertices are pages, edges are hyperlinks.
- **Average out-degree** of a web page is **~50**.
- **Theoretical complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ time, $O(\vert V \vert)$ space.
- **Frontier memory grows geometrically** with depth $d$ as $\dfrac{b^{d+1}-1}{b-1}$ — bounded by **duplicate suppression**.
- **BFS distributes load across hosts** → better politeness, less likely to trigger anti-bot defenses.
- **Pure BFS does not scale to the whole web** → production crawlers (Mercator, UbiCrawler) use **distributed front/back queues** and **host partitioning**.
- **Seen Set prevents:** duplicate fetches, infinite loops on cycles, wasted bandwidth.
- **URL normalization** is mandatory: strip fragment, lowercase host, absolutize path, remove default ports.
- **DNS resolution** is a bottleneck — always cached.
- **BFS ≠ Focused / Best-First / PageRank crawlers** — BFS is *uninformed* (no priority, no relevance).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preferential Crawlers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Preferential Crawlers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preferential Crawler (Definition – KTU Standard):**
> A *Preferential Crawler* is a **topic-focused (or vertical) web crawler** that does **not** attempt to download the entire web. Instead, it **selectively fetches pages predicted to be relevant** to a predefined set of topics, by **ranking unvisited URLs in a frontier** using a learned relevance model and **crawling higher-ranked URLs first**.

A preferential crawler is governed by three classical components (De Bra & Post, 1994 – extended by Chakrabarti et al., 1999):

1. **Classifier** – decides if a fetched page is on-topic.
2. **Distiller** – identifies hubs / high-quality pages whose neighbors should be prioritized.
3. **Crawler** – ranks URLs in the frontier using a priority function $P(url)$.

The crawler **prefers** URLs that look *most promising* (high estimated relevance / high estimated authority), thereby saving bandwidth and time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms used in KTU papers:** Focused Crawler, Topical Crawler, Vertical Crawler, Preferential Web Crawler. All four are treated as the same construct in the 2024 syllabus.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **librarian preparing a report on "Climate Change in Kerala"**. You have **2 hours** and a huge, unorganized library. You will **not** read every book. Instead, you will:

- Pick books whose **title / index** mentions climate (Classifier hint).
- Pick books that famous climate authors recommend (Hub/Authority hint).
- Skim the **table of contents** before reading the whole book.
- Keep a **"to-read next" queue** ranked by importance.

A **Preferential Crawler does exactly this on the Web**:

- The *web* is the library.
- The *frontier* is the "to-read next" queue.
- The *priority function* is your judgment of importance.
- The *topic* is "Climate Change in Kerala".

> [!TIP]
> **The Single-Sentence Intuition:** A preferential crawler is a **smart, queue-based "next-page picker"** that always fetches the URL it believes will yield the most on-topic information *first*.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Them? (Motivation)

| Issue with a Universal Crawler | How a Preferential Crawler Solves It |
|---|---|
| Web is **> 50 billion** pages – cannot index all | Crawls only the **topical slice** (~ millions) |
| **Bandwidth / time** constraints in real systems | **Prioritizes** by estimated relevance |
| Search engines need **vertical / domain** indexes | Builds a **focused repository** per topic |
| Many pages are **noise / spam / off-topic** | **Classifier + Distiller** filter them out early |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is a preferential crawler preferred over a universal crawler for domain-specific search?"* — Use the above table as your answer skeleton.

---

## 1.4 Boundary Conditions & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are **standard in KTU Web Mining problems** (commit them to memory):

- **Harvest Rate (HR)** – fraction of fetched pages that are on-topic. Target: **HR ≥ 0.5** for a good crawler.
- **Relevance Threshold** $T$ – minimum score for a page to be classified on-topic. Typical: $T = 0.5$.
- **Damping factor** $d = 0.85$ (PageRank-style).
- **Number of crawled pages** $N$ – often taken as **1,000 – 10,000** in numerical problems.
- **Frontier size** $\vert F \vert$ – usually **5× to 10×** the parallel fetch slots.
- **Topic vector length** $k$ – often **50 – 500** features (TF-IDF, embeddings).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Preferential Crawler (Layered View)

A preferential crawler operates as a **closed feedback loop**. Each fetch → classify → score neighbors → re-rank frontier. The pipeline has **six logical steps**:

1. **Seed Selection** – start with a small set of URLs known to be on-topic (the "seed pages").
2. **Frontier Initialization** – enqueue all hyperlinks present in the seed pages.
3. **URL De-queue (Priority Pop)** – remove the URL with the **highest priority score** $P(url)$.
4. **HTTP Fetch + Parse** – download the page, extract text + new hyperlinks.
5. **Classification** – run the topic classifier; if score $\geq T$, **accept** the page and **enqueue** its outgoing links.
6. **Distillation (optional)** – boost URLs from hub pages.

> The loop is called the **Crawl Loop** and is **continuous** until a stop condition is met (page count, time, or HR plateaus).

---

## 2.2 The URL Priority Function $P(url)$ — The Heart of a Preferential Crawler

This is the **single most important formula** in the entire module for KTU exams. A priority function combines **three signals**:

$$
P(url) = \alpha \cdot R_{content}(url) + \beta \cdot R_{link}(url) + \gamma \cdot R_{hub}(url)
$$

where:

- $R_{content}(url) \in [0,1]$ – estimated **content relevance** of the page reachable at $url$.
- $R_{link}(url) \in [0,1]$ – estimated **link-based importance** (anchor text, surrounding context, parent-page relevance).
- $R_{hub}(url) \in [0,1]$ – **hub authority** propagated from a high-quality referencing page.
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \geq 0$ are **tuning weights** with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ (normalised).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When a question says *"Design a priority function"*, always write $P$ as a *linear combination* of the three signals above, and **state the weight constraint** $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1$.

---

## 2.3 Two Canonical Algorithms (High-Yield for KTU)

### 2.3.1 Fish-Search Algorithm (De Bra & Post, 1994)

A **binary** classifier is used. Each URL in the frontier is tagged with one of:

- **"alive"** – its parent was on-topic → enqueue all its children.
- **"dead"** – its parent was off-topic → enqueue only a *fraction* of children, then propagate "dead" status.

$$
\text{Depth Limit } D_{max} = \text{number of consecutive "dead" generations allowed before pruning}
$$

| Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|
| Very **simple, fast** | Only **binary** relevance (no graded score) |
| Easy to implement | **No link-structure** modeling |

### 2.3.2 Shark-Search Algorithm (Hersovici et al., 1998) — *Extension of Fish-Search*

Shark-Search refines Fish-Search by adding a **graded relevance score in $[0,1]$** and a **neighborhood estimator** using anchor text and parent context.

The **estimated relevance of a child URL** $u$ reachable from parent $p$ is:

$$
R_{child}(u) = \delta \cdot R_{anchor}(u) + (1-\delta) \cdot R_{inherited}(p)
$$

where:

- $R_{anchor}(u) \in [0,1]$ – similarity between the **anchor text of $u$** and the topic.
- $R_{inherited}(p) \in [0,1]$ – **decayed relevance** of parent $p$.
- $\delta \in (0,1)$ – mixing weight; commonly $\delta = 0.5$.

The **decay / inheritance** rule when propagating to a deeper neighbor:

$$
R_{inherited}(p') = \sigma \cdot R_{parent}(p) \quad \text{with decay } \sigma \in (0,1)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU often asks: "Differentiate Fish-Search and Shark-Search."**
> Use this 3-point answer: (i) Fish = binary, Shark = graded; (ii) Fish uses full parent relevance, Shark uses decayed inherited relevance; (iii) Shark incorporates anchor-text similarity.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the table below — these equations account for **> 70% of marks** in Web Mining Crawler problems.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Priority score | $P(url) = \alpha R_c + \beta R_l + \gamma R_h$ | $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1$ |
| 2 | Shark child relevance | $R_{child} = \delta R_{anchor} + (1-\delta) R_{inherited}$ | $\delta \approx 0.5$ |
| 3 | Relevance decay | $R_{inherited}^{(k+1)} = \sigma \cdot R_{inherited}^{(k)}$ | $\sigma \in (0.3, 0.9)$ |
| 4 | PageRank contribution | $PR(u) = (1-d) + d \sum_{v \to u} \frac{PR(v)}{L(v)}$ | $d = 0.85$ |
| 5 | Harvest rate | $HR = \frac{\#\text{ on-topic pages fetched}}{\#\text{ total pages fetched}}$ | target $\geq 0.5$ |
| 6 | Cosine similarity (topic ↔ page) | $sim(d, q) = \frac{\vec{d} \cdot \vec{q}}{\vert\vec{d}\vert_2 \cdot \vert\vec{q}\vert_2}$ | $[0,1]$ |
| 7 | TF-IDF weight | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log\frac{N}{df_t}$ | $N$ = corpus size |
| 8 | Stop condition | Fetch $N$ pages **or** $HR < T$ for $K$ rounds | $T$ = relevance threshold |
| 9 | Context-graph node weight | $W(v) = \lambda R_c(v) + (1-\lambda) \cdot A(v)$ | $A(v)$ = ancestor relevance |
| 10 | Frontier upper-bound size | $\vert F \vert \leq B \cdot f$ | $B$ = bandwidth, $f$ = fetch freq. |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations drive real systems such as **Google's vertical search verticals (News, Scholar, Images)**, **domain-specific enterprise search** (legal, medical), and **competitive intelligence platforms** that mine competitor websites.

---

## 2.5 Important Design Choices (Theory Layered Into Marking)

| Design Choice | Options | KTU-Recommended Default |
|---|---|---|
| Classifier type | Naïve Bayes / SVM / Neural / LLM | Naïve Bayes (simple, KTU-friendly) |
| Feature space | Bag-of-Words / TF-IDF / Embeddings | TF-IDF |
| Priority model | Linear / Learning-to-Rank | Linear combination |
| Stop condition | Page budget / Time / HR plateau | Page budget (cleanest exam answer) |
| Re-visit policy | Never / Periodic / Adaptive | Periodic (freshness matters) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Computing a URL Priority End-to-End

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A preferential crawler is fetching the topic *"Machine Learning"*. It is about to enqueue three candidate URLs $u_1, u_2, u_3$ with the following measured signals:
>
> | URL | $R_{content}$ | $R_{link}$ | $R_{hub}$ |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | $u_1$ | 0.8 | 0.6 | 0.4 |
> | $u_2$ | 0.5 | 0.9 | 0.3 |
> | $u_3$ | 0.7 | 0.5 | 0.8 |
>
> Weights are $\alpha = 0.5,\; \beta = 0.3,\; \gamma = 0.2$. **Rank the URLs in crawl order.**

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

The priority function is

$$
P(url) = 0.5 \cdot R_{content} + 0.3 \cdot R_{link} + 0.2 \cdot R_{hub}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $P(u_1)$**

$$
P(u_1) = 0.5(0.8) + 0.3(0.6) + 0.2(0.4)
$$

$$
= 0.40 + 0.18 + 0.08 = 0.66
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P(u_2)$**

$$
P(u_2) = 0.5(0.5) + 0.3(0.9) + 0.2(0.3)
$$

$$
= 0.25 + 0.27 + 0.06 = 0.58
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $P(u_3)$**

$$
P(u_3) = 0.5(0.7) + 0.3(0.5) + 0.2(0.8)
$$

$$
= 0.35 + 0.15 + 0.16 = 0.66
$$

**Step 4 — Rank by descending $P$**

$$
P(u_1) = P(u_3) = 0.66 \; > \; P(u_2) = 0.58
$$

**Crawl order:** $u_1$ or $u_3$ (tie — break by fetch recency / FIFO), then $u_2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0$ ✓ (weight constraint satisfied).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Shark-Search Child Relevance Across a 3-Hop Path

> **Problem:** Topic = *Deep Learning*. Seed page $p_0$ has relevance $R(p_0) = 1.0$. A child $u_1$ has anchor text similarity $R_{anchor}(u_1)=0.9$. The next-generation child $u_2$ has $R_{anchor}(u_2)=0.7$. Use $\delta = 0.5$ and decay $\sigma = 0.6$. Find $R_{child}(u_1)$ and $R_{child}(u_2)$.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Relevance of $u_1$ (hop 1)**

$$
R_{child}(u_1) = \delta R_{anchor}(u_1) + (1-\delta) R_{inherited}(p_0)
$$

$$
= 0.5(0.9) + 0.5(1.0) = 0.45 + 0.50 = 0.95
$$

**Step 2 — Decay the parent relevance for hop 2**

$$
R_{inherited}(p_1) = \sigma \cdot R(p_0) = 0.6 \cdot 1.0 = 0.60
$$

**Step 3 — Relevance of $u_2$ (hop 2)**

$$
R_{child}(u_2) = 0.5(0.7) + 0.5(0.60) = 0.35 + 0.30 = 0.65
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

Relevance drops from $1.0 \to 0.95 \to 0.65$ as we move **further from the seed**. This is the *relevance horizon* property that gives preferential crawlers their **focus**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Harvest-Rate Calculation

> **Problem:** A preferential crawler fetched **800** pages in a budget of **1000** fetches. The classifier marked **520** of them as on-topic. **Find the harvest rate.** Is the crawler acceptable if the target is $HR \geq 0.5$?

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Apply the harvest rate formula**

$$
HR = \frac{\#\text{ on-topic}}{\#\text{ total}} = \frac{520}{1000}
$$

$$
= 0.52
$$

**Step 2 — Decision**

$$
0.52 \geq 0.50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Crawler is ACCEPTABLE}
$$

> If $HR < 0.5$, the **distiller** needs retuning or the **seeds** need expansion.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: PageRank Boost Inside a Preferential Crawler

> **Problem:** Page $A$ is referenced by two parents $B$ and $C$ with $PR(B)=0.30,\; L(B)=3$ and $PR(C)=0.50,\; L(C)=5$. Damping $d=0.85$. Compute $PR(A)$.

### 3.4.1 Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Write PageRank equation**

$$
PR(A) = (1-d) + d\left( \frac{PR(B)}{L(B)} + \frac{PR(C)}{L(C)} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in the numbers**

$$
PR(A) = (1 - 0.85) + 0.85\left( \frac{0.30}{3} + \frac{0.50}{5} \right)
$$

$$
= 0.15 + 0.85(0.10 + 0.10)
$$

$$
= 0.15 + 0.85 \cdot 0.20 = 0.15 + 0.17 = 0.32
$$

**Step 3 — Use $PR(A)$ as a priority boost**

In the crawler, the link-importance term is often **mixed with PageRank**:

$$
R_{link}(url) = \eta \cdot sim(anchor, topic) + (1-\eta) \cdot PR(url)
$$

With $\eta = 0.7$ and $PR(A) = 0.32$:

$$
R_{link}(A) = 0.7(0.9) + 0.3(0.32) = 0.63 + 0.096 = 0.726
$$

This $R_{link}(A)$ is then fed into the master $P(url)$ equation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Strategy

| Scenario | Best Strategy | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Very **small budget** (< 10k pages) | **Shark-Search** | Graded relevance avoids wrong commits |
| **Huge, diverse** topic | **Context Graph + PageRank** | Captures long-range authority |
| **Real-time vertical** (news) | **Re-crawl + freshness rule** | Old pages become irrelevant |
| **Narrow, technical** topic | **Fish-Search** with depth $D_{max}=4$ | Quick, low compute |
| **Multi-topic** crawl | **Multi-classifier + per-topic frontier** | Independent ranking per topic |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Block Diagram of a Preferential Crawler

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Seed URLs] --> B[Frontier Priority Queue]
    B --> C{URL Selector}
    C --> D[HTTP Fetcher]
    D --> E[Parser and Tokenizer]
    E --> F[Topic Classifier]
    F --> G{On Topic check}
    G -->|Yes score above T| H[Store Page in Repository]
    G -->|No| I[Discard Page]
    H --> J[Extract Outgoing Links]
    J --> K[Distiller Hub Authority Estimator]
    K --> L[Compute Priority for each link]
    L --> B
    I --> M[Stop Condition Check]
    H --> M
    M -->|Continue| C
    M -->|Stop| N[End Crawl and Report Harvest Rate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The loop **C → D → E → F → G → H → J → K → L → B** is the classical **crawl loop**. The three "brains" are **Classifier (F)**, **Distiller (K)**, and **Priority Computer (L)**.

---

## 4.2 Data-Flow of a Single URL Through the Priority Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw URL] --> B[Anchor Text]
    A --> C[Parent Page Text]
    A --> D[PageRank Input]
    B --> E[Feature Builder]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Rcontent score]
    E --> G[Rlink score]
    E --> H[Rhub score]
    F --> I[Purl linear combination]
    G --> I
```
