    H --> I
    I --> J[Insert into Frontier]
```

---

## 4.3 State Machine of a URL in the Frontier

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Scored : compute Purl
    Scored --> Visited : fetched
    Scored --> Pruned : priority below threshold
    Visited --> OnTopic : classifier score above T
    Visited --> OffTopic : classifier score below T
    OnTopic --> [*] : stored and children enqueued
    OffTopic --> Pruned : children may still enqueue
    Pruned --> [*]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture: Shark-Search Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Topic Profile Vector] --> B[Cosine Similarity Module]
    C[Anchor Text] --> B
    B --> D[Ranchor score]
    E[Parent Relevance] --> F[Decay Module sigma]
    F --> G[Rinherited score]
    D --> H[Mixer delta]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Rchild score]
    I --> J[Insert into Frontier with Rchild as Purl]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a preferential crawler. How does it differ from a universal crawler?**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**
A *preferential crawler* is a topic-focused web crawler that selectively fetches pages predicted to be on-topic by ranking unvisited URLs in a frontier using a learned priority function. Unlike a *universal crawler* (which aims to download the entire web broadly, e.g., Googlebot), a preferential crawler is restricted to a **vertical / domain slice**, has a **relevance classifier + distiller**, and uses **far less bandwidth**. It optimizes for **harvest rate** rather than **coverage**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List the three main components of a preferential crawler and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 60 words):**
1. **Classifier** – assigns a topic score to a fetched page; decides on-topic / off-topic.
2. **Distiller** – identifies *hub* pages and propagates authority to their neighbors.
3. **Crawler (frontier manager)** – ranks the unvisited URL queue by priority $P(url) = \alpha R_c + \beta R_l + \gamma R_h$ and fetches the top-ranked URL next.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Fish-Search algorithm** for preferential crawling. Discuss its **strengths and limitations**.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Origin & Idea (2 marks):**
Fish-Search was proposed by **De Bra and Post (1994)**. It models URLs as *fish swimming upstream* in a stream of on-topic content. Each potential URL is tagged as **"alive"** or **"dead"** based on the relevance of its parent page. A depth limit $D_{max}$ controls how many consecutive "dead" generations are tolerated before a branch is discarded.

**2. Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. Start with a seed set of on-topic URLs.
2. For each seed, **fetch** the page and run a binary classifier.
3. If **on-topic** → mark URL as **alive**; enqueue all outgoing links as **alive** with depth 0.
4. If **off-topic** → mark URL as **dead**; enqueue a *fraction* $w$ of its outgoing links as **dead** with depth 1.
5. If a **dead** URL's children are themselves off-topic, increment their depth; when depth reaches $D_{max}$, prune the branch.
6. Continue until page budget / time is exhausted.

**3. Strengths (1 mark):**
- Extremely **simple** to implement.
- **Memory-efficient** (only binary tags).
- **Fast**, suitable for narrow vertical searches.

**4. Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Binary** relevance — cannot distinguish *strongly* on-topic from *marginally* on-topic.
- **Ignores** link structure / PageRank / anchor text nuance.
- Performance **degrades** when the topic is sparsely connected on the web.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive and explain the **Shark-Search** priority formula. Show how relevance **decays** with crawl depth.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Motivation (1 mark):** Shark-Search, by **Hersovici et al. (1998)**, extends Fish-Search by using *graded* relevance in $[0,1]$ and by incorporating **anchor-text similarity** to estimate the relevance of an *unfetched* URL — a key innovation.

**2. Derivation (3 marks):**
For a child URL $u$ reachable from parent page $p$:

$$
R_{child}(u) = \delta \cdot R_{anchor}(u) + (1-\delta) \cdot R_{inherited}(p)
$$

where:

- $R_{anchor}(u) = sim(anchor\_text(u), topic)$ — measured via cosine similarity or TF-IDF.
- $R_{inherited}(p)$ = decayed relevance of parent $p$ from the previous hop.
- $\delta \in (0,1)$ is a mixing weight (commonly $\delta=0.5$).

**3. Decay with depth (2 marks):**
If a child becomes a parent of a deeper URL $v$, its relevance is **decayed**:

$$
R_{inherited}^{(k+1)} = \sigma \cdot R_{inherited}^{(k)}
$$

with **decay factor** $\sigma \in (0,1)$, commonly $\sigma=0.6$. So relevance **decreases geometrically** with depth:

$$
R^{(k)} = \sigma^{k} \cdot R^{(0)}
$$

This models the *relevance horizon*: pages far from a seed are less likely on-topic.

**4. Why it improves Fish-Search (1 mark):** Graded scoring avoids premature pruning, anchor-text cues allow smarter pre-fetch estimation, and decay keeps the crawler **topically anchored**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **architecture of a preferential crawler**. Explain the role of the **classifier**, **distiller**, and the **frontier priority function**.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Block Diagram (2 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.1 diagram above]. Show the crawl loop: *Seeds → Frontier → Fetcher → Parser → Classifier → Repository → Link Extractor → Distiller → Priority Computer → back to Frontier*.

**2. Classifier (2 marks):** A *supervised* model trained on positive (on-topic) and negative (off-topic) examples. Common choices: **Naïve Bayes**, **SVM**, or modern **transformer-based** zero-shot classifiers. Outputs a probability $P(topic \mid page) \in [0,1]$. If $\geq T$ (threshold, typically 0.5), the page is accepted; else discarded.

**3. Distiller (2 marks):** A *link-structure-aware* module that boosts URLs reachable from authoritative hub pages. Implements PageRank / HITS or simple in-degree heuristics. Helps the crawler escape *local topical bubbles* by following *high-quality off-topic pages* that nonetheless link to relevant content.

**4. Frontier Priority Function (1 mark):**

$$
P(url) = \alpha R_{content} + \beta R_{link} + \gamma R_{hub}, \quad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1
$$

The URL with the **highest** $P$ is fetched next. The frontier is implemented as a **max-heap** for $O(\log n)$ insert / pop.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A preferential crawler is configured with weights $\alpha=0.6,\; \beta=0.3,\; \gamma=0.1$. Compute the priority of four candidate URLs and rank them.

| URL | $R_{content}$ | $R_{link}$ | $R_{hub}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 0.9 | 0.5 | 0.2 |
| $B$ | 0.6 | 0.8 | 0.7 |
| $C$ | 0.7 | 0.4 | 0.9 |
| $D$ | 0.4 | 0.6 | 0.3 |

**Model Answer:**

Using $P = 0.6 R_c + 0.3 R_l + 0.1 R_h$:

**For $A$:**

$$
P(A) = 0.6(0.9) + 0.3(0.5) + 0.1(0.2) = 0.54 + 0.15 + 0.02 = 0.71
$$

**For $B$:**

$$
P(B) = 0.6(0.6) + 0.3(0.8) + 0.1(0.7) = 0.36 + 0.24 + 0.07 = 0.67
$$

**For $C$:**

$$
P(C) = 0.6(0.7) + 0.3(0.4) + 0.1(0.9) = 0.42 + 0.12 + 0.09 = 0.63
$$

**For $D$:**

$$
P(D) = 0.6(0.4) + 0.3(0.6) + 0.1(0.3) = 0.24 + 0.18 + 0.03 = 0.45
$$

**Crawl order (descending):** $A\;(0.71) \;\to\; B\;(0.67) \;\to\; C\;(0.63) \;\to\; D\;(0.45)$.

**Interpretation:** URL $A$ is fetched first because content relevance dominates ($\alpha=0.6$). URL $D$ is fetched last because it is **weak on all three signals**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Preferential Crawler Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the weight constraint** $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1$. Examiners deduct **1 mark** silently if your linear combination does not sum to 1.
> 2. **Confusing Fish-Search with Shark-Search.** Fish = *binary*; Shark = *graded with anchor text*. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 3. **Not showing decay formula** $\sigma^{k} R^{(0)}$ when asked "how relevance changes with depth". KTU specifically tests this.
> 4. **Skipping the harvest-rate computation** in design questions. Always end your answer with a *quantitative* evaluation (e.g., $HR = 0.52$).
> 5. **Omitting the stop condition** in architecture answers. Always specify: *page budget / time / HR plateau*.
> 6. **Writing PageRank as "PR" without damping factor $d$**. Always state $d=0.85$ explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preferential Crawlers — Rapid-Fire Revision Bullets:**
>
> - A **preferential crawler** is a *topic-focused* crawler that ranks URLs by a priority function and fetches the **top-ranked** URL first.
> - **Three classical components:** Classifier, Distiller, Crawler (frontier).
> - **Master priority equation:**
>   $$P(url) = \alpha R_{content} + \beta R_{link} + \gamma R_{hub}, \quad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1$$
> - **Fish-Search** = binary relevance ("alive"/"dead"), simple, no anchor cues.
> - **Shark-Search** = graded relevance in $[0,1]$ + anchor text + decay.
> - **Shark child relevance:**
>   $$R_{child}(u) = \delta R_{anchor}(u) + (1-\delta) R_{inherited}(p)$$
> - **Relevance decay with depth:**
>   $$R^{(k)} = \sigma^{k} R^{(0)}, \quad \sigma \in (0,1)$$
> - **Harvest Rate (HR):**
>   $$HR = \frac{\#\text{ on-topic fetched}}{\#\text{ total fetched}}, \quad \text{target} \geq 0.5$$
> - **PageRank boost** (when used):
>   $$PR(u) = (1-d) + d \sum_{v \to u} \frac{PR(v)}{L(v)}, \quad d=0.85$$
> - **Cosine similarity** (for $R_{anchor}$):
>   $$sim(d, q) = \frac{\vec{d} \cdot \vec{q}}{\vert\vec{d}\vert_2 \cdot \vert\vec{q}\vert_2}$$
> - **Frontier** is a **max-heap** keyed on $P(url)$.
> - **Stop conditions:** page budget exhausted **or** HR plateau detected for $K$ rounds.
> - **Architecture loop:** Seed → Frontier → Fetch → Parse → Classify → Store → Extract links → Score → Reinsert → repeat.
> - **KTU buzzwords to sprinkle in answers:** *vertical search, topical crawler, focused crawler, hub authority, relevance horizon, harvest rate, distiller, classifier, frontier, priority function, learning-to-rank, anchor-text similarity, decay factor, damping factor.*
> - **One-line definition for any 2-mark question:** *"A preferential crawler is a topic-focused web crawler that selectively downloads pages relevant to a predefined topic by ranking unvisited URLs in a frontier using a learned priority function combining content, link, and hub signals."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Universal Crawlers, Focused Crawlers and Topical Crawlers
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Universal Crawlers, Focused Crawlers and Topical Crawlers # MODULE 2 — UNIVERSAL CRAWLERS, FOCUSED CRAWLERS AND TOPICAL CRAWLERS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A **Web Crawler** (also known as a *robot*, *spider*, *bot*, or *worm*) is an automated software program that systematically browses the World Wide Web in a methodical, automated manner. It downloads web pages, parses their content, extracts hyperlinks, and recursively follows them to discover and fetch new pages. ### 1.1 The Three Canonical Crawler Paradigms Under KTU's PECDT635 syllabus, web crawlers are classified into **three fundamental architectural paradigms**, each designed to address a different scale, scope, and intent of web traversal. > [!NOTE] > **Universal Crawler (Breadth Crawler):** Aims to download *as much of the Web as possible* without restriction to any specific subject matter. Operates as the backbone of general-purpose search engines. > > **Focused Crawler (Topical Crawler — Soft Form):** Targets a *predefined set of topics* and attempts to retrieve only pages that are relevant to those topics, while avoiding irrelevant branches of the Web. > > **Topical Crawler (Ontology-based Crawler):** A more refined variant that uses a *formal ontology or taxonomy* to model the topic, employing semantic understanding to guide the crawl frontier intelligently. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Metaphor Imagine a vast, ever-expanding library with **billions of unorganized books** scattered across a near-infinite number of interconnected rooms: - A **Universal Crawler** is like a librarian who decides to visit *every single room* in the library, collect *every book*, and index it — without caring what the books are about. This is incredibly thorough but enormously expensive. - A **Focused Crawler** is like a *biologist* sent to the library who only collects books related to "genetics." When they enter a room full of cookbooks, they quickly leave and never return. - A **Topical Crawler** is a *biology professor* who not only collects genetics books, but understands that *molecular biology* and *biochemistry* are related, and uses a *subject tree* (an ontology) to navigate intelligently toward neighboring but related subjects. ### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bold Definitions) - **Scalability** — Ability of the crawler to handle **billions of pages**. - **Freshness** — How quickly the crawler revisits and updates already-crawled pages. - **Politeness** — Adherence to the `robots.txt` protocol and avoidance of server overload. - **Coverage** — Fraction of the Web actually retrieved relative to the total Web. - **Throughput** — Number of pages downloaded per unit time (typically **hundreds to thousands of pages/second**). - **Duplicate Elimination** — Avoiding storage of the same page (or near-duplicate) multiple times. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Insight:** A *universal crawler* sacrifices *precision* for *coverage*. A *focused crawler* sacrifices *coverage* for *precision*. A *topical crawler* attempts to balance both using semantic context. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Universal Crawler — Operational Breakdown A universal crawler (e.g., **Googlebot**, **Bingbot**, **Yahoo Slurp**) operates on the following architectural components: 1. **URL Seed List (Starting Points):** A set of high-quality seed URLs (e.g., dmoz.org categories, university homepages). 2. **URL Frontier (Priority Queue):** A managed structure holding URLs awaiting fetch. It uses: - A **Front Queue** for politeness (rate-limiting per host). - A **Back Queue** for priority-based selection. 3. **Fetcher (HTTP Worker):** Performs the actual `GET` request and downloads the page. 4. **Parser / Extractor:** Tokenizes HTML, extracts text, identifies links via `` tags. 5. **Duplicate Eliminator:** Uses a **hash function** (e.g., **SimHash**, **MD5**, or **SHA-1**) on each URL and content fingerprint to drop near-duplicates. 6. **DNS Resolver:** Translates hostnames to IP addresses efficiently via caching. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Traversal Strategy:** Universal crawlers use **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** in the early frontier layers (to maximize coverage) and then blend with **PageRank-prioritized** ordering for the deeper layers. ### 2.2 Focused Crawler — Operational Breakdown A focused crawler (introduced by **Chakrabarti, van den Berg, and Dom, 1999**) is governed by a **relevance classifier** that scores each candidate URL before it enters the frontier. 1. **Topic Definition:** User provides a set of *positive examples* (relevant documents) and possibly *negative examples* (irrelevant documents). 2. **Classifier Training:** A supervised model (e.g., **Naive Bayes**, **SVM**, **Neural Network**) is trained to compute $P(\text{relevant} \mid \text{page})$. 3. **Crawler fetches** the top-scoring URL from the frontier. 4. **Page parsed** → links extracted → each link is *provisionally scored* based on: - The textual context surrounding the link (anchor text). - The parent page's relevance score. - URL token similarity to topic keywords. 5. **Back-links are pruned** if their predicted relevance falls below a threshold $T$. > [!NOTE] > **Key Algorithms in Focused Crawling:** > - **Fish-Search (De Bra & Post, 1994):** Treats the crawl as a school of fish; when a "fish" finds an irrelevant page, it "dies" after a fixed number of attempts. > - **Shark-Search (Hersovici et al., 1998):** Refines Fish-Search using a continuous **fuzzy relevance score** in the range $[0, 1]$ inherited by child URLs from parent pages. ### 2.3 Topical Crawler — Operational Breakdown A topical crawler is a *semantically aware* focused crawler. It differs in that it uses a **formal ontology** (e.g., the **Open Directory Project / DMOZ hierarchy** or a **domain-specific taxonomy**). - The ontology provides a **concept graph** where each node is a topic. - The crawler maintains a **belief vector** over the ontology, indicating the current probability that each topic is relevant to the query. - Links are scored using a **semantic similarity** measure (e.g., **Resnik similarity**, **Lin similarity**, or **cosine similarity** on **WordNet** concept vectors). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Range / Unit** | |---|---|---|---| | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(A, B) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{B} \vert}$ | Measures topical closeness between two TF-IDF vectors $A$ and $B$ | $[0, 1]$ (unitless) | | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log\dfrac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | Term importance of $t$ in doc $d$; $N$ = total docs, $\text{df}$ = document frequency | $\geq 0$ | | **PageRank Score** | $PR(p) = \dfrac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ | $d$ = damping factor (**0.85**); $L(q)$ = out-links of $q$ | $[0, 1]$ | | **Shark-Search Child Score** | $\text{score}(u) = \gamma \cdot \text{score}(\text{parent}) + (1-\gamma) \cdot \text{sim}(\text{anchor}, \text{topic})$ | Inherited relevance of URL $u$; $\gamma$ = decay (**0.5–0.8**) | $[0, 1]$ | | **Fish-Search Depth Limit** | $D = D_{\max}$ | Maximum allowed depth before "fish dies" | Integer depth | | **Crawl Frontier Size** | $\vert F \vert = \sum_{h=1}^{H} f_h$ | $H$ = number of hosts; $f_h$ = pages allowed per host | $\leq$ **millions** | | **BFS Time Complexity** | $T_{\text{BFS}} = O(V + E)$ | $V$ = pages, $E$ = links | Operations | | **DNS Lookup Cost** | $T_{\text{DNS}} \approx 10$–**$100$ ms** | Bottleneck in distributed crawlers | Milliseconds | > [!WARNING] > **Board Pitfall:** In tables, the absolute value of a vector is written as $\vert \vec{A} \vert$ using the `\vert` LaTeX command — *never* the bare pipe `|` character, which breaks the markdown table renderer. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | **Crawler Type** | **Industry Use Case** | |---|---| | Universal Crawler | Google Search Index, Bing Index, Baidu Spider | | Focused Crawler | Vertical search engines (job portals, real-estate aggregators) | | Topical Crawler | Scientific literature mining (PubMed), e-commerce product discovery | | Hybrid (Universal + Focused) | News aggregators (Google News), competitive intelligence platforms | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Cosine Similarity for Topical Relevance The cosine similarity between two documents is derived from the dot-product formula. Let $\vec{A} = (a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n)$ and $\vec{B} = (b_1, b_2, \dots, b_n)$ be the TF-IDF vectors of two documents in an $n$-dimensional term space. **Step 1 — Define the dot product:** $$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \cdot b_i$$ **Step 2 — Define the Euclidean norm of each vector:** $$\vert \vec{A} \vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i^2}, \quad \vert \vec{B} \vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i^2}$$ **Step 3 — The dot product is also expressible as:** $$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \vert \vec{A} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{B} \vert \cdot \cos(\theta)$$ where $\theta$ is the angle between the two vectors in the term space. **Step 4 — Solve for $\cos(\theta)$:** $$\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{B} \vert}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute Step 1 and Step 2:** $$\text{sim}(A, B) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \cdot b_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i^2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Two documents are *topically identical* if their TF-IDF vectors point in the *exact same direction* ($\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \cos = 1$). They are *unrelated* if orthogonal ($\theta = 90° \Rightarrow \cos = 0$). This is the foundational relevance metric in topical crawlers. ### 3.2 Derivation — Shark-Search Score Inheritance The Shark-Search algorithm refines the Fish-Search binary logic into a continuous score propagation model. **Step 1 — Define the initial relevance of a seed URL:** $$\text{score}(u_0) = 1.0 \quad \text{(seed assumed fully relevant)}$$ **Step 2 — For a child URL $u$ extracted from parent page $p$:** $$\text{score}(u) = \gamma \cdot \text{score}(p) + (1 - \gamma) \cdot \text{sim}_{\text{anchor}}(u)$$ where: - $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ is the **inheritance decay** (typically $0.5$ to $0.8$). - $\text{sim}_{\text{anchor}}(u)$ is the cosine similarity between the *anchor text vector* of link $u$ and the *topic query vector*. **Step 3 — The frontier is a priority queue sorted by $\text{score}(u)$ descending.** **Step 4 — A page is fetched only if $\text{score}(u) \geq T$**, where $T$ is the relevance threshold (typically $0.2$ to $0.4$). **Step 5 — For pages below $T$, links are still extracted but the children inherit:** $$\text{score}_{\text{child}} = \gamma^{k+1} \cdot \text{score}(p) \cdot \text{sim}_{\text{anchor}}(u)$$ where $k$ is the *depth below threshold* — deeper generations decay exponentially. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Property** | **Universal Crawler** | **Focused Crawler** | **Topical Crawler** | |---|---|---|---| | **Scope** | Entire Web | Specific topic | Specific topic with ontology | | **Coverage Goal** | Maximum | Selective | Selective + Semantically Expanded | | **Precision** | Low | High | Very High | | **Recall** | High | Medium | Medium-High | | **Classifier Used** | None (or PageRank) | Binary/Multi-class | Ontology + Semantic Similarity | | **Topic Representation** | None | Keyword vector / Example set | Concept hierarchy (taxonomy) | | **Typical Page Volume** | **Billions** | **Millions** | **Millions** | | **Storage Cost** | **Petabytes** | **Terabytes** | **Terabytes** | | **Politeness** | Strict (robots.txt) | Strict | Strict | | **Update Frequency** | Weeks–Months | Days–Weeks | Days–Weeks | | **Example** | Googlebot | Heritrix + classifier | Academic focused crawler | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Universal Crawler — Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD seed1["Seed URL List"] --> frontierA["URL Frontier Front Queue"] frontierA --> resolver1["DNS Resolver with Cache"] resolver1 --> fetchA["HTTP Fetcher"] fetchA --> parseA["HTML Parser and Link Extractor"] parseA --> dedupA["Duplicate Eliminator SimHash or MD5"] dedupA --> storeA["Page Repository"] parseA --> frontierB["URL Frontier Back Queue Priority"] frontierB --> frontierA storeA --> indexerA["Search Index Builder"] ``` ### 4.2 Focused Crawler — Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD topic1["User Topic Definition Positive and Negative Examples"] --> train1["Train Classifier Naive Bayes or SVM"] train1 --> score1["Relevance Scorer Module"] seed2["Seed URLs"] --> frontierC["Crawl Frontier"] score1 --> frontierC frontierC --> fetchB["HTTP Fetcher Politeness Controller"] fetchB --> parseB["HTML Parser"] parseB --> class1["Classify Page via Trained Model"] class1 --> filter1["Threshold Filter T equals 0.3"] filter1 --> accept1["Accept and Index Page"] filter1 --> reject1["Reject and Prune Subtree"] parseB --> anchScore1["Score Child URLs via Anchor Text Similarity"] anchScore1 --> score1 ``` ### 4.3 Topical Crawler — Ontology-Driven Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD query1["User Query"] onto1["Ontology Knowledge Base DMOZ or WordNet"] onto1 --> belief1["Belief Vector over Concepts"] query1 --> belief1 belief1 --> rankLinks1["Semantic Link Ranker Resnik or Lin Similarity"] seed3["Seed URLs"] --> frontierD["Crawl Frontier"] rankLinks1 --> frontierD frontierD --> fetchC["Fetcher with robots.txt Compliance"] fetchC --> parseC["Parser and Concept Extractor"] parseC --> updateBelief1["Update Belief Vector via Bayes Rule"] updateBelief1 --> belief1 parseC --> scoreChildren1["Score Children via Cosine Similarity to Concept Vector"] scoreChildren1 --> rankLinks1 ``` ### 4.4 Crawler Comparison Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start1["Start Decision: Choose Crawler Type"] start1 --> q1{"Goal is Complete Web Indexing?"} q1 --> yes1["Use Universal Crawler"] q1 --> no1["Goal is Topic Specific Search?"} no1 --> q2{"Do you have a Formal Ontology?"} q2 --> yes2["Use Topical Crawler"] q2 --> no2["Use Focused Crawler with Example based Classifier"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]** > > **Q1.** *Differentiate between a Universal Crawler and a Focused Crawler in terms of scope, precision, and storage requirement.* > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Aspect | Universal Crawler | Focused Crawler | > |---|---|---| > | Scope | Entire Web | Specific topic/domain | > | Precision | Low (collects all pages) | High (collects only relevant) | > | Storage | Petabytes | Terabytes | > | Classifier | Not used | Used (Naive Bayes/SVM) | > > *(1 mark for scope, 1 mark for precision, 1 mark for storage distinction)* > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]** > > **Q2.** *List and explain the four main components of a Universal Crawler's URL frontier.* > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > 1. **Front Queue** — Holds URLs ready to be fetched; enforces *politeness* via per-host rate limiting. *(0.75 mark)* > 2. **Back Queue** — Holds newly discovered URLs awaiting prioritization. *(0.75 mark)* > 3. **Prioritizer** — Ranks URLs using **PageRank**, **BFS layer**, or **freshness score**. *(0.75 mark)* > 4. **Politeness Manager** — Maintains a per-host delay table (typically **1–10 seconds**). *(0.75 mark)* --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Choose either Question A or Question B.** #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **(a)** Explain the architecture of a **Universal Web Crawler** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the URL frontier, duplicate eliminator, and DNS resolver in detail. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With suitable examples, explain the **PageRank algorithm** used by universal crawlers for URL prioritization. Show its mathematical formulation and discuss why a damping factor of **0.85** is used. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Universal Crawler Architecture (7 marks):** A Universal Web Crawler is a distributed system designed to fetch and index the entire Web. Its architecture consists of the following modules: **1. Seed URL Generator (1 mark):** - Provides initial high-quality URLs. - Example seeds: `dmoz.org`, `wikipedia.org`, university `.edu` homepages. - These seeds are chosen for *high PageRank* and *diverse topical coverage*. **2. URL Frontier (1.5 marks):** - The most critical component — a managed priority queue. - **Front Queue:** Implements *politeness* by ensuring no more than **1 fetch per host per second**. - **Back Queue:** Holds URLs in priority order. Priority is determined by PageRank, freshness, and BFS layer depth. - It is partitioned across multiple machines using **consistent hashing**. **3. Fetcher (1 mark):** - Issues **HTTP GET** requests using libraries such as `libcurl` or `Apache HttpClient`. - Supports **HTTP/1.1 keep-alive** to amortize TCP handshake cost. - Enforces `robots.txt` directives — disallowed paths are dropped. **4. Parser and Link Extractor (0.5 mark):** - Tokenizes HTML using a parser like **LexHTML** or **jsoup**. - Extracts URLs from ``, ``, `

topics.title: Wrapper Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Wrapper Generation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Web Mining (PECDT635), **Wrapper Generation** is defined as the systematic process of constructing a software module (called a *wrapper*) that automatically extracts structured data from semi-structured or unstructured web documents, typically by exploiting the underlying HTML/XML structure, formatting delimiters, or recurring visual templates of web pages. A **wrapper** is formally defined as a procedural construct — usually expressed as a set of extraction rules, context patterns, or finite-state transducers — that maps a target web page (input) to a tuple of relational records (output), such that the schema of the output matches a pre-defined template. The mathematical abstraction is often expressed as: $$W : D_{web} \rightarrow R$$ where **W** is the wrapper, **D_web** is the input web document, and **R** represents the extracted relational record set. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Wrapper Generation sits at the intersection of *Information Extraction (IE)* and *Web Mining*. It is the foundational technique for converting free-form web content into queryable, machine-readable form for downstream tasks such as web integration, vertical search, and competitive intelligence. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **specialized factory worker** standing beside a conveyor belt carrying unsorted mail from hundreds of different countries. Each envelope has different layouts, fonts, languages, and shapes, but each contains the same core information: a name, an address, a tracking number. A **wrapper** is the worker's mental checklist: 1. *Look for the small box on the top-right corner — that's the tracking number.* 2. *Read the bold line beginning with "To:" — that's the recipient.* 3. *The block right after the dashed line is the address.* The worker doesn't need to understand the language or design of every envelope; the wrapper tells them **"what landmark to find, and what to do once found."** If the envelopes change their layout (e.g., the company redesigns their website), the worker (or the factory) must be *re-trained* — this re-training is what we call **Wrapper Generation / Re-Generation**. ## 1.3 Wrapper vs. Crawler vs. Parser — Distinguishing the Roles | Component | Role | Input | Output | |---|---|---|---| | **Crawler (Spider)** | Fetches pages across the web | Seed URLs | Raw HTML pages | | **Parser (Tokenizer)** | Converts HTML into a DOM tree | Raw HTML | Parsed DOM / token stream | | **Wrapper** | Extracts target data records from parsed pages | Parsed pages | Structured tuples | | **Integrator** | Merges and normalizes records from many wrappers | Multiple result sets | Unified database | > [!NOTE] > **Core Takeaway:** A crawler *finds*, a parser *understands*, and a wrapper *extracts*. Wrapper Generation is therefore the construction of the "extraction intelligence" that turns noise into knowledge. ## 1.4 Why Wrapper Generation is Non-Trivial A robust wrapper must be: - **Resilient to layout drift** — sites redesign without notice. - **Tolerant of missing data** — some fields may be optional. - **Scalable** — capable of handling millions of pages from a template family. - **Domain-adaptive** — reusable across structurally similar sites. This motivates the three classical families of wrapper generation: **Hand-Coded**, **Supervised (Induction)**, and **Unsupervised / Automatic**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Wrapper Architecture (Logical Layers) A modern wrapper is best understood as a four-layer pipeline: 1. **Pre-processing Layer** — tokenizes the input HTML, resolves encoding, repairs malformed tags, and normalizes whitespace. 2. **Pattern/Landmark Layer** — locates *anchor regions* in the document (delimiters, tags, path expressions, or visual cues). 3. **Extraction Layer** — applies extraction rules to slice out field values. 4. **Post-processing Layer** — type-casts, formats, validates, and aligns results to a target schema. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Favored Terminology:** Always describe the extraction step in terms of *rules* (e.g., "rule R_i fires when delimiter D_j is matched"). This is the language used in standard texts and is expected in board answers. ## 2.2 Classification of Wrapper Generation Approaches | Family | Learning | Human Effort | Robustness | Example Systems | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Hand-Coded Wrappers** | None | Very High (per site) | Low (fragile) | TSIMMIS, WebOQL | | **Supervised / Wrapper Induction** | Yes (labeled examples) | Medium (per template) | Medium | WIEN, SoftMealy, Stalker, DEByE | | **Semi-Supervised** | Partial labels + heuristics | Low | Medium | Thresher, IEPAD | | **Unsupervised / Automatic** | None | Minimal | High across templates | RoadRunner, EXALG, DEPTA | | **Visual / Tree-Based** | DOM or vision cues | Medium | High (DR-tolerant) | VIDE, ViPER | ## 2.3 The Wrapper Induction Paradigm (Supervised) Wrapper Induction treats wrapper construction as a **concept-learning problem**: 1. The user provides a small set of *labeled pages* — pages where the fields of interest are highlighted. 2. An induction algorithm generalizes the user's annotations into a set of extraction rules. 3. The rules are applied to unseen pages drawn from the same template. The induced rules are typically expressed as: - **Delimiters** — characters or strings that surround the data. - **Context patterns** — text appearing immediately before/after a field. - **Path expressions** — XPath-like traversals in the DOM tree. ## 2.4 Key Sub-Families of Induction Algorithms ### (a) **Delimiter-Based (LR-style) — WIEN family** The earliest and simplest approach. The wrapper is a sequence of *left-context* and *right-context* delimiters per field. - **WIEN** — supports Nested, Head, Adjacency, Optional, Repeated, Hierarchical, Disjunctive patterns. - **SoftMealy** — uses finite-state transducers; more expressive than WIEN. ### (b) **Tree-Based — Stalker / XWrap** The page is modeled as a **DOM tree** (or a tag tree). Extraction rules traverse the tree using **landmark automata** (Stalker) or **XPath expressions** (XWrap). ### (c) **Bilingual / Schema-Driven — IEF (Internet Extraction Forge)** A *bilingual report* pairs a formatted page with its corresponding structured form. The algorithm aligns the two to learn translation rules. ### (d) **Probabilistic — HMM-based wrappers** Hidden Markov Models are trained on tokenized pages; field boundaries are the *hidden states* and tokens are the observations. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Symbolic Form | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Wrapper Mapping | $W: D_{web} \to R$ | Maps documents to record sets | | Extraction Rule | $R_i = (L_i, C_i, R_i)$ | Tuple of Left-context, Center field, Right-context | | Rule Confidence | $C(R) = \frac{\text{Supp}(R)}{\text{Cover}(R)}$ | Fraction of pages where rule fires correctly | | Edit Distance (DR metric) | $D_{edit}(P, Q) = \min \; \text{ops to transform } P \to Q$ | Used to measure layout drift between page versions | | Pattern Support | $\text{Supp}(p) = \frac{\vert\{d \in D : p \in d\}\vert}{\vert D \vert}$ | Frequency of pattern *p* in corpus *D* | | Induction Objective | $\max_{R} \; \Pr(R \mid E) \propto \Pr(E \mid R) \cdot \Pr(R)$ | Bayesian learning of best rule set R from examples E | | Wrapper Complexity | $O(n \cdot k)$ | *n* = number of pages, *k* = number of landmarks per page | | HMM Likelihood | $P(O \mid \lambda) = \sum_{S} P(O, S \mid \lambda)$ | Likelihood of observations under wrapper HMM *λ* | | Token Emission | $b_j(o_t) = P(o_t \mid q_j)$ | Probability of emitting token *o_t* in state *q_j* | | Viterbi Recursion | $\delta_t(j) = \max_i \left[\delta_{t-1}(i) a_{ij}\right] b_j(o_t)$ | Best-path field boundary decoding | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** For the KTU board exam, when asked to "explain wrapper generation," always cite at least one named system (e.g., WIEN, Stalker, RoadRunner) and briefly describe its rule format. Examiners explicitly reward this as evidence of *beyond-the-syllabus reading*. ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **E-commerce intelligence** — competitor price monitoring across thousands of product pages. - **Job aggregators** — extracting postings from corporate career sites. - **Real-estate portals** — merging listings from heterogeneous broker websites. - **Academic crawlers** — building citation graphs from publisher pages. - **Government transparency tools** — scraping public-records portals. - **AI training data** — large-scale structured corpora for LLMs and knowledge graphs. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a Delimiter-Based Wrapper **Scenario.** A book-selling site lists titles in the following recurring HTML pattern: ```html
  • Title: AI Foundations
    Author: Jane Doe
    Price: 499 INR
    • ``` **Goal.** Extract *Title*, *Author*, and *Price* from every `
  • ` block. ### Step 1 — Identify the *Landmarks* We scan the page for stable substrings that bracket the data. These are our **delimiters**. | Field | Left Delimiter | Right Delimiter | |---|---|---| | Title | `Title:` | `
    ` | | Author | `Author:` | `
    ` | | Price | `Price:` | `INR
    • ` | > [!NOTE] > **Why this works:** These substrings are *template artifacts* of the underlying site generator. As long as the template does not change, the delimiters remain stable across millions of pages. ### Step 2 — Formulate the Extraction Rule Each rule is the triple $(L_i, C_i, R_i)$ where $C_i$ is the value to be extracted. For the *Title* field: $$R_{title} = (\; L_{title} = \texttt{Title:}\;,\; C_{title} = \texttt{extracted text}\;,\; R_{title} = \texttt{
    }\;)$$ The same formulation extends to *Author* and *Price* by simply replacing the delimiter triple. ### Step 3 — Apply the Wrapper to a New Page Given a new page block: ```html
  • Title: Deep Learning
    Author: Alan Turing
    Price: 799 INR
    • ``` The wrapper executes the following logical transitions: 1. Search the document for the first occurrence of $L_{title} = \texttt{Title:}$. 2. Skip past the delimiter, then collect every character until $R_{title} = \texttt{
    }$ is encountered. 3. Store the collected substring as the *Title* record. 4. Repeat for $L_{author}$, $L_{price}$ in the order they appear. Mathematically, if the *i*-th block in a page is denoted $B_i$, the wrapper output is: $$W(B_i) = \left( \;\text{extract}(B_i, L_{title}, R_{title}),\; \text{extract}(B_i, L_{author}, R_{author}),\; \text{extract}(B_i, L_{price}, R_{price}) \;\right)$$ This is the canonical **delimiter-based wrapper** taught in WIEN-style systems. ### Step 4 — Confidence Scoring of an Induced Rule Suppose we tested the rule on a validation set of 1,000 pages and it fired correctly on 920 of them. The **Rule Confidence** is computed as: $$\begin{aligned} C(R_{title}) &= \frac{\text{Supp}(R_{title})}{\text{Cover}(R_{title})} \\ &= \frac{920}{1{,}000} \\ &= 0.92 \end{aligned}$$ A confidence of **0.92** is excellent for a production wrapper; KTU examiners typically expect you to comment on whether a wrapper is "production-ready" based on this metric. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the empty-line rule matters for LaTeX:** Display equations in KTU answer scripts must be visually separated from surrounding text. Always leave a blank line before and after a `$$` block to avoid merging it with the prose. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Major Approaches | Criterion | Hand-Coded | Wrapper Induction | Automatic (RoadRunner) | |---|---|---|---| | **Required input** | Site code + page samples | Labeled pages | 2-3 sample pages of a template | | **Rule representation** | Program code | Delimiters, FSAs, paths | Regular expression union from alignment | | **Drift handling** | Manual re-coding | Partial (re-train) | Re-run on fresh samples | | **Strength** | Maximum precision | Human-in-the-loop accuracy | High automation, scales | | **Weakness** | High cost | Requires labeled corpus | May over-fit to two samples | | **KTU exam relevance** | Define + example | Most asked | Mention as auto approach | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — WIEN Rule Classes | Pattern Type | Structure Captured | Example | |---|---|---| | **Simple** | A single field with two delimiters | `Title: X
    ` | | **Head** | Field that appears at the top of a block | Title in a product page | | **Adjacency** | Two fields glued together | FirstNameLastName | | **Nested** | Records within records | Orders containing items | | **Optional** | Field that may be absent | Discount price | | **Repeated** | Field that recurs | Multiple author names | | **Disjunctive** | Alternative delimiters per field | Pages with two layouts | | **Hierarchical** | Multi-level records | Categories → products | > [!TIP] > **Board Strategy:** If a 14-mark question asks you to "describe wrapper induction with an example," structure the answer as: (1) define the paradigm, (2) name WIEN, (3) show the rule $R_i = (L_i, C_i, R_i)$, (4) work through the worked example above, (5) mention the confidence formula, (6) close with limitations. This is the **canonical 14-mark template**. ## 3.4 The Bayesian View of Rule Learning When the induction algorithm must select the *best* rule $R^*$ from a hypothesis space $\mathcal{H}$ given labeled examples $E$: $$R^* = \arg\max_{R \in \mathcal{H}} \; \Pr(R \mid E)$$ By Bayes' theorem: $$\begin{aligned} \Pr(R \mid E) &= \frac{\Pr(E \mid R) \cdot \Pr(R)}{\Pr(E)} \\ &\propto \Pr(E \mid R) \cdot \Pr(R) \end{aligned}$$ Since $\Pr(E)$ is a constant over the hypothesis space, maximizing $\Pr(R \mid E)$ reduces to maximizing the product of **likelihood** $\Pr(E \mid R)$ and **prior** $\Pr(R)$. The likelihood is typically estimated by the rule's accuracy on the labeled set, and the prior penalizes overly complex rules (Occam's razor) — this is the formal origin of *simplicity-biased* wrapper induction. ## 3.5 HMM-Based Wrapper (Outline of Viterbi Decoding) In probabilistic wrappers, the page is tokenized and the wrapper is a Hidden Markov Model $\lambda = (A, B, \pi)$ where states correspond to field labels. To find the *most likely* field labeling $S^* = (s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_T)$ given the observed token stream $O = (o_1, \ldots, o_T)$: $$\begin{aligned} \delta_1(j) &= \pi_j \, b_j(o_1) \\ \delta_t(j) &= \max_{i} \left[\delta_{t-1}(i) \, a_{ij}\right] \, b_j(o_t) \quad t = 2, \ldots, T \\ S^* &= \arg\max_{s_1, \ldots, s_T} \; P(s_1, \ldots, s_T, o_1, \ldots, o_T \mid \lambda) \end{aligned}$$ This is the **Viterbi algorithm** applied to wrapper field-segmentation — a high-yield item for KTU Part A. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Wrapper Generation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sample Web Pages] --> B[Pre-processing] B --> C[Tokenization and DOM Parsing] C --> D{Generation Mode} D -- Hand-Coded --> E[Manual Rule Authoring] D -- Supervised --> F[Wrapper Induction Algorithm] D -- Unsupervised --> G[Alignment and Pattern Discovery] E --> H[Rule Set Repository] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Wrapper Application Engine] J[New Unseen Web Pages] --> I I --> K[Structured Records] K --> L[Database or Knowledge Base] ``` ## 4.2 Wrapper Induction Internal Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Labeled Page 1] --> M[Example Consolidator] P2[Labeled Page 2] --> M P3[Labeled Page N] --> M M --> I[Rule Inducer WIEN or Stalker] I --> R1[Rule R1] I --> R2[Rule R2] I --> R3[Rule Rn] R1 --> V[Validator] R2 --> V R3 --> V V --> S[Scored Rule Set] S --> A[Wrapper Executor] N[New Page] --> A A --> O[Extracted Tuples] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Wrapper System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer] I1[HTTP Fetcher] I2[HTML Normalizer] end subgraph AnalysisLayer[Analysis Layer] A1[DOM Tree Builder] A2[Landmark Detector] A3[Token Sequencer] end subgraph RuleLayer[Rule Layer] R1[Delimiter Rules] R2[Tree Path Rules] R3[HMM Models] R4[Learned Regex] end subgraph ExecutionLayer[Execution Layer] E1[Rule Engine] E2[Field Extractor] E3[Type Caster] end subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer] O1[Record Assembler] O2[Schema Aligner] O3[Quality Validator] end I1 --> I2 I2 --> A1 I2 --> A3 A1 --> A2 A2 --> R2 A3 --> R1 A3 --> R3 R1 --> E1 R2 --> E1 R3 --> E1 R4 --> E1 E1 --> E2 E2 --> E3 E3 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O2 --> O3 ``` ## 4.4 Wrapper Drift Lifecycle (Visual Cues) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Site v1 Template] --> W1[Wrapper W1 Trained] W1 --> D1[Drift Detected via Edit Distance] D1 -- Below Threshold --> C1[Continue with W1] D1 -- Above Threshold --> REG[Re-Induction Triggered] S2[Site v2 Template] --> REG REG --> W2[Wrapper W2 Trained] W2 --> M[Monitor Drift Continuously] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU answers:** Examiners award marks for showing that you understand wrappers are *living* artifacts that must be regenerated. The drift loop above is a high-yield diagram to include in any 7-mark sub-part asking about wrapper maintenance. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define a *wrapper* in the context of web information extraction. Differentiate between a wrapper and a parser.** **Model Answer:** A *wrapper* is a software module that extracts structured data records from semi-structured or unstructured web pages by exploiting the underlying HTML structure, formatting delimiters, or visual templates. Formally, it is a mapping $W : D_{web} \to R$ from a web document to a relational record set. **Wrapper vs. Parser:** | Aspect | Wrapper | Parser | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Extracts *target data* | Builds a syntactic tree | | Output | Field values / records | DOM tree / token stream | | Trigger | Applied to selected pages | Applied to *every* page | | Schema | Page-specific | Language-specific (HTML/XML) | --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] List any three challenges faced during wrapper generation.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Layout drift** — when a website redesigns its pages, old wrapper rules break. 2. **Heterogeneity** — different sites use different templates, requiring multiple wrappers. 3. **Nested and optional data** — many pages contain records inside records or fields that may be missing. 4. **Dynamic content** — JavaScript-rendered content is invisible to traditional HTML scraping. 5. **Scale** — manually maintaining wrappers for thousands of sites is infeasible. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** > *"Explain the Wrapper Generation process in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of a wrapper induction system. Differentiate supervised and unsupervised wrapper generation."* #### (a) **Wrapper Generation Process — 7 Marks** Wrapper Generation is the procedure of constructing a wrapper $W$ that transforms a raw web page into structured records. The canonical steps are: 1. **Sample collection** — fetch multiple pages from the same template family. 2. **Pre-processing** — clean HTML, remove scripts, normalize whitespace. 3. **Tokenization / DOM construction** — break the page into tokens or a tree. 4. **Rule learning** — induce extraction rules from examples (or hand-code them). 5. **Validation** — compute confidence $C(R) = \text{Supp}(R) / \text{Cover}(R)$ on a held-out set. 6. **Deployment** — apply the wrapper at scale, with periodic drift checks. #### (b) **Architecture of a Wrapper Induction System — 4 Marks** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.2. The system comprises: - An **Example Consolidator** that merges user-labeled pages. - A **Rule Inducer** (e.g., WIEN, Stalker) that generalizes the labels into rules. - A **Validator** that scores each rule on a hold-out set. - A **Wrapper Executor** that applies the chosen rules to new pages. #### (c) **Supervised vs Unsupervised Wrapper Generation — 3 Marks** | Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | |---|---|---| | Training data | Labeled pages needed | Pages only, no labels | | Human effort | Moderate (labeling) | Minimal | | Algorithms | WIEN, Stalker, SoftMealy | RoadRunner, EXALG, DEPTA | | Accuracy | High (within template) | Slightly lower, but scalable | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** > *"With a suitable example, describe delimiter-based wrapper induction. Explain how rule confidence is computed and how a wrapper handles layout drift."* #### (a) **Delimiter-Based Wrapper Induction with Example — 7 Marks** Consider a product page that repeats the following block: ```html
    Name: Laptop X Price: 55000 INR
    ``` A delimiter-based wrapper defines for each field a triple $(L, C, R)$: $$\begin{aligned} R_{name} &= (\,\text{`Name:`},\; C_{name},\; \text{`Price:`}\,) \\ R_{price} &= (\,\text{`Price:`},\; C_{price},\; \text{`INR`}\,) \end{aligned}$$ The algorithm **induces** these delimiters by: 1. Aligning the user-labeled examples side by side. 2. Finding the **left-most and right-most stable strings** that flank each labeled field. 3. Generalizing these strings into the rule representation above. This is the WIEN-style induction process — simple, fast, and explainable. #### (b) **Rule Confidence Computation — 4 Marks** For a rule $R_i$ applied to a validation corpus $D$: $$C(R_i) = \frac{\text{Supp}(R_i)}{\text{Cover}(R_i)} = \frac{\text{Number of pages where } R_i \text{ extracts the correct field}}{\text{Number of pages where } R_i \text{ fires}}$$ If a wrapper has 5 rules and each achieves $C \geq 0.9$, the overall wrapper confidence is approximately the product: $$C(W) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{5} C(R_i) = 0.9^5 \approx 0.59$$ This is the **AND-combination** rule taught in WIEN evaluations. #### (c) **Handling Layout Drift — 3 Marks** Layout drift occurs when a website updates its template. The wrapper system handles it by: 1. **Edit-distance monitoring** — computing $D_{edit}(P_{old}, P_{new})$ between historical and new pages. 2. **Threshold trigger** — if $D_{edit}$ exceeds a threshold (say 0.2), re-induction is scheduled. 3. **Automatic re-training** — RoadRunner-class systems re-run induction on the fresh sample set. 4. **Continuous quality validation** — ongoing confidence scoring flags degrading rules. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Students commonly **omit the diagram** in 14-mark questions, losing up to **2 marks**. > - Do **not confuse** "wrapper" with "crawler" or "parser" — examiners deduct marks for this mix-up. > - When discussing *confidence*, always show the **formula**, not just a verbal description. > - For *layout drift*, avoid vague answers like "we manually fix it" — credit is awarded for **algorithmic handling** (edit-distance threshold, re-induction). > - In *unsupervised* approaches, do **not claim** that the algorithm uses labeled data; this is a frequent error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Wrapper** = a procedure that extracts structured records from semi-structured web pages; formally $W: D_{web} \to R$. - **Wrapper Generation** is the construction of such a procedure, classified into **hand-coded**, **supervised (induction)**, **semi-supervised**, and **unsupervised** families. - **WIEN** pioneered delimiter-based induction with pattern classes: Simple, Head, Adjacency, Nested, Optional, Repeated, Disjunctive, Hierarchical. - **Stalker** uses landmark automata over the tree representation; rules are hierarchical in nature. - **SoftMealy** uses finite-state transducers and is more expressive than pure delimiters. - **RoadRunner / EXALG** are automatic (unsupervised) systems that *align* multiple sample pages to infer the underlying template via regular-expression union. - **Rule confidence** is $C(R) = \text{Supp}(R) / \text{Cover}(R)$, and wrapper-level confidence is the product over rules. - **Layout drift** is measured via **edit distance** between page versions; mitigation strategies include threshold-based re-induction, visual cues, and DOM-tree paths. - **HMM-based wrappers** formalize field segmentation as a decoding problem solved by the **Viterbi algorithm**. - For KTU board answers, always include: (1) a definition, (2) a named system, (3) a rule representation, (4) a worked example, and (5) a limitation discussion. - The **XPath / DOM-tree view** of wrappers is the most drift-resilient representation and is increasingly the default in modern systems.

topics.title: Preliminaries, Wrapper Induction, Instance Based Wrapper Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Preliminaries, Wrapper Induction, Instance Based Wrapper Learning ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Preliminaries of Wrapper Induction **Wrapper** (in the context of Web Mining) is a software procedure that automatically extracts data (records/fields) from semi-structured web sources (typically HTML pages) and transforms them into a structured format such as a relational database, XML, or CSV. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > A *wrapper* is a program that converts implicit information buried in web sources into explicit, relational form. *Wrapper Induction (WI)* refers to the supervised machine learning approach where a wrapper is **automatically learned** from a set of manually labeled training pages, rather than being hand-coded. #### Sub-areas that form the Preliminaries 1. **Information Extraction (IE)** – the broader field of extracting structured data from unstructured/semi-structured text. 2. **Semi-structured data** – data that does not conform to a rigid schema (like relational tables) but has tags/markers (like HTML tags) that hint at structure. 3. **Web record extraction** – locating and segmenting multiple records listed on a single result page. 4. **Tokenization & delimiters** – identifying the HTML tags/strings that separate one record from another. > [!NOTE] > **Why is wrapper learning needed?** > Hand-coded wrappers are brittle – they break the moment a website changes its template. Wrapper induction builds *adaptive* extractors that can be retrained quickly with new examples. --- ### 1.2 Wrapper Induction – Definition **Wrapper Induction** is a *supervised* learning paradigm in which the learner is given: - A **set of labeled training pages** (manually annotated with the records/fields to be extracted). - A **page representation model** (token sequences with HTML structure). - A **hypothesis space** (set of candidate wrapper patterns). The induction algorithm then searches the hypothesis space and outputs a wrapper that, with high probability, will correctly extract the desired data from new, unseen pages generated by the *same template*. --- ### 1.3 Instance Based Wrapper Learning – Definition **Instance-Based Learning (IBL)** is a *lazy* learning paradigm: instead of building an explicit generalized model at training time, the learner simply stores the training instances and performs generalization at *query time* by comparing the new page to the stored instances. In the wrapper context, **Instance-Based Wrapper Learning** stores labeled example pages (or labeled regions) and, for a new page, finds the most similar stored page(s) and re-uses (or adapts) the wrapper that worked on them. --- ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy – "The Form-Filling Assistant":** > > Imagine a new tax form is released every year. The form is *semi-structured* (has boxes, labels, layout cues) but the wording/format changes annually. > > - **Hand-coded wrapper** = You learn the exact form by heart and write specific instructions. If the form changes, you must rewrite everything. > - **Wrapper Induction** = You are given 3-4 filled-out sample forms and a tutor points out which text is "Income", "Tax", "Name". You study the *patterns* (e.g., "Income is always the number appearing after the word 'Income:' and before a colon") and write a *general* rule. > - **Instance-Based Wrapper Learning** = Instead of deriving a general rule, you keep the 3-4 sample forms on your desk. When a new form arrives, you *compare* it visually to your stored samples, find the closest one, and copy the corresponding fields. No general rule was ever created. **In one line:** - *Wrapper Induction* → learn **rules**. - *Instance-Based Wrapper Learning* → learn by **remembering examples**. --- ### 1.5 Formal Symbols Used Throughout the Module | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | $P$ | A web page (sequence of tokens) | | $T = \{P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n\}$ | Training set of labeled pages | | $r_i$ | A record on page $P$ | | $f_j$ | A field within a record | | $L_k$ | A delimiter/tuple separating consecutive records | | $H$ | Hypothesis space of wrappers | | $h^* \in H$ | Best wrapper chosen by the learner | | $\text{sim}(P, P')$ | Similarity function between two pages | | $\alpha$ | Significance / confidence threshold | > [!IMPORTANT] > These symbols are used consistently in the KTU board exam. Memorize them before attempting derivations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Wrapper Induction Pipeline (End-to-End) The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to draw and explain this pipeline. It has **five canonical stages**: 1. **Input Stage** – Collect web pages from the same source/template. 2. **Pre-processing** – HTML parsing, tokenization into a linear sequence of *open tags*, *close tags*, and *text tokens*. 3. **Labeling / Annotation** – Human annotator marks the *begin* and *end* of each record and each field on training pages. 4. **Induction (Learning)** – Algorithm searches hypothesis space $H$ and returns a wrapper $h^*$. 5. **Application (Extraction)** – $h^*$ is applied to new pages; records and fields are output as relational tuples. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The hypothesis space $H$ is what *distinguishes* one wrapper induction algorithm from another. For example: > - $H$ = set of regular expressions → **regular-expression wrappers**. > - $H$ = set of context-free grammars → **grammar wrappers**. > - $H$ = set of landmark automata → **landmark wrappers** (Kushmerick). > - $H$ = set of stored pages → **Instance-Based wrappers**. --- ### 2.2 The Page Representation Model (Preliminaries) A web page is first converted into a **token sequence**. Three token classes are standard: - **Open tag** $O_t$ – e.g., ``, ``. - **Close tag** $C_t$ – e.g., ``, ``. - **Text token** $T_k$ – actual content string. A page $P$ is therefore a sequence: $$P = \langle t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots, t_m \rangle$$ When the labeled *begin* and *end* positions of records are known, the wrapper's job is to predict these positions on a new page. --- ### 2.3 The Standard Wrapper Induction Framework (Kushmerick, 1997 – Board-Standard Reference) Kushmerick formalized the wrapper induction problem and identified **six delimiter discovery sub-tasks** that every induction algorithm must solve. The KTU examiner loves asking the *names* and *purpose* of these. | # | Sub-task | Symbol | What it learns | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Start of record** | $\text{Start}(R)$ | Pattern marking the beginning of a new record | | 2 | **End of record** | $\text{End}(R)$ | Pattern marking the end of a record | | 3 | **Empty record** | $\emptyset$ | Pattern identifying skipped (empty) records | | 4 | **Start of field $f_j$** | $\text{Start}(f_j)$ | Pattern marking the beginning of field $j$ | | 5 | **End of field $f_j$** | $\text{End}(f_j)$ | Pattern marking the end of field $j$ | | 6 | **Empty field $f_j$** | $\emptyset(f_j)$ | Pattern identifying a missing field value | **Key Insight:** Once all six pattern types are learned, extraction is deterministic – the wrapper simply *scans* the page looking for the next matching start/end pair. --- ### 2.4 Hypothesis Space – the Six Wrapper Families (Kushmerick Taxonomy) KTU frequently tests the *taxonomy* of wrapper classes. The six classes are obtained by combining three binary choices: | Wrapper Class | Start/End format | Field-format | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | **LR** | Left-Right delimiters | None – whole record is one field | Simplest | | **HLRT** | Head-Left Right-Tail | 2 fields | Adds head & tail | | **OCLR** | Open-Close-Left-Right | multi-field | Uses matching tag pairs | | **HOCLR** | Head-Open-Close-Left-Right | multi-field + head | Most expressive *non-nested* | | **N-LR** | Nested LR | multi-field (nested) | Allows nesting | | **N-HOCLR** | Nested HOCLR | most expressive | Full nesting + head/tail | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Most-tested line in KTU:** *"HOCLR is the union of HLRT and OCLR and is the most expressive non-nested wrapper class."* --- ### 2.5 The General Inducer (GI) Algorithm – Intuition The **General Inducer** is a generic search algorithm used to find a wrapper $h^*$ in any of the above classes: 1. Initialize $h$ with the most specific wrapper (one that matches *nothing*). 2. For each training page $P_i$: - Identify the smallest *generalization* of $h$ that still correctly labels $P_i$. 3. The final $h^*$ is the *most specific wrapper* that is consistent with **all** training pages. This is essentially a *specific-to-general* search (a dual of FOIL) and is the conceptual ancestor of the famous **Stalker** and **WIEN** algorithms. --- ### 2.6 Instance-Based Wrapper Learning – Detailed Theory #### 2.6.1 Why IBL is Used - Web templates change *gradually* – the new page is often a small edit of an old one. - Generalization across many templates is risky; memorizing examples is safe. - No explicit training phase – fast to update (just add a new labeled page to memory). #### 2.6.2 Two Core Concepts (Stalker / IBL wrapper literature) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stalk vs. Header Patterns** – these are the *primitives* of instance-based wrappers. > > - **Stalk** = a *short* (a few tokens) recurring pattern that appears in front of (or after) the field of interest, on **every** page. > - **Header** = a *longer* pattern that *sometimes* appears in front of the field (less reliable). > - The learner generalizes from header to stalk as more pages are seen. #### 2.6.3 The IBL Procedure For a new page $P$ and a target field $f$: 1. **Tokenize** $P$ into a sequence of tokens $t_1 \dots t_m$. 2. **Compute similarity** $\text{sim}(P, P_i)$ between $P$ and every stored labeled page $P_i$. 3. **Select** the $k$ most similar stored pages – this is the *neighborhood*. 4. **Re-use / vote** – extract the field using the wrapper(s) of the neighborhood, possibly with a confidence-weighted vote. 5. **Optional update** – if the user corrects the result, store $(P, \text{correct wrapper})$ as a new instance. #### 2.6.4 Similarity Function A common choice is normalized edit distance (Levenshtein) between two token sequences: $$\text{sim}(P, P') = 1 - \frac{\text{LD}(P, P')}{\max(\vert P \vert, \vert P' \vert)}$$ where $\text{LD}$ is the Levenshtein distance and $\vert P \vert$ is the number of tokens in $P$. > [!NOTE] > **Why normalized?** To keep the value in $[0, 1]$ regardless of page length, so the same threshold can be applied across websites. --- ### 2.7 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat-Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | $P = \langle t_1, \dots, t_m \rangle$ | Page as a token sequence | | 2 | $H$ | Hypothesis space (set of candidate wrappers) | | 3 | $h^* = \arg\max_{h \in H} \; \text{Accuracy}(h, T)$ | Best wrapper is the one with highest accuracy on training set $T$ | | 4 | $\text{Accuracy}(h, T) = \frac{1}{\vert T \vert} \sum_{i=1}^{\vert T \vert} \mathbb{1}[\,h(P_i) = \text{label}_i\,]$ | Fraction of pages correctly extracted | | 5 | $\text{sim}(P, P') = 1 - \dfrac{\text{LD}(P, P')}{\max(\vert P \vert, \vert P' \vert)}$ | Normalized similarity (IBL) | | 6 | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Fraction of extracted tuples that are correct | | 7 | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of correct tuples that were extracted | | 8 | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R (board favourite) | | 9 | Specific-to-general: $h \xrightarrow{\text{min. generalize}} h'$ | General Inducer step | | 10 | Stalk $\subseteq$ Header | Stalks are *reliable* patterns; headers are *unreliable* patterns | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Tip:** Always quote $F_1$ alongside Precision and Recall in any numerical wrapper-evaluation question. The $F_1$ value ranges from **0** (worst) to **1** (best). --- ### 2.8 Real-World Utility - **E-commerce price comparison** portals (e.g., comparing the price of a phone across Amazon, Flipkart, Snapdeal) – each portal is a wrapper. - **News aggregation** – extracting headlines and dates from multiple news sites. - **Job-board scraping** – extracting job title, company, location, salary. - **Financial data feeds** – extracting stock quotes from exchange websites. - **Research** – building academic-paper corpora from publisher sites (DBLP, Google Scholar). Wrapper induction techniques form the foundation of modern *Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)* pipelines where structured facts must be lifted off web pages and fed to LLMs. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the General Inducer's Generalization Rule **Problem:** Given a wrapper $h$ that is *too specific* (matches zero pages in $T$), find the *smallest* generalization $h'$ that correctly labels one more page. **Setup.** Each wrapper in class LR is a pair of delimiters $(L, R)$ where $L$ and $R$ are *Landmark* patterns. A landmark is a 5-tuple: $$\text{landmark} = (\text{type}, \text{prefix}, \text{tag}, \text{separator}, \text{text})$$ - `type` $\in \{$ *open*, *close*, *text* $\}$ - `prefix` = number of opening/closing tags appearing before the landmark - `tag` = the specific tag name (or `*` for "any tag") - `separator` = the literal string appearing right after the tag (or `*`) - `text` = the literal string the landmark must contain (or `*`) A *more general* landmark is one that allows *more* matches (e.g., `*` instead of a fixed string). **Derivation of the "minimum generalization" step:** Let $L = (\text{type}, p, g, s, x)$ be the current (most specific) start-of-record landmark, and let $P$ be a new training page where the true record starts at token $t_k$. The induced landmark $L'$ must: - Match position $k$ in $P$ **(coverage)** - Match the previously covered positions in the other training pages **(consistency)** The *minimum* generalization $L'$ is obtained by relaxing, **in order**, the most constraining fields first: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & L' = (\text{type}, p, g, s, *) & \text{[relax text]} \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & L' = (\text{type}, p, g, *, *) & \text{[relax separator]} \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & L' = (\text{type}, p, *, *, *) & \text{[relax tag]} \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & L' = (\text{type}, *, *, *, *) & \text{[relax prefix]} \\ \text{Step 5:} \quad & L' = (*, *, *, *, *) & \text{[relax type – last resort]} \end{aligned} $$ We pick the **first** (most specific) $L'$ in this ladder that satisfies coverage on the new page *and* consistency on all previously learned pages. > [!NOTE] > **Why relax in this order?** Because earlier fields (type, prefix) are *more semantic* – relaxing them last preserves the structural meaning. This gives the *smallest* generalization in the partial order, which is the most *specific* wrapper consistent with the data – the principle of **Maximum Specific Generalization** (a special case of the MDL principle). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the IBL Similarity Function **Problem:** Compute $\text{sim}(P, P')$ for two tokenized pages. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } P &= \langle t_1, t_2, \dots, t_m \rangle \\ \text{Let } P' &= \langle t'_1, t'_2, \dots, t'_n \rangle \\ \text{Step 1: } \quad \text{LD}(P, P') &= \text{minimum number of single-token insertions, deletions, or substitutions} \\ &\quad \text{to transform } P \text{ into } P' \\ \text{Step 2: } \quad \vert P \vert &= m, \quad \vert P' \vert = n \\ \text{Step 3: } \quad \text{Normalization} \quad \text{sim}(P, P') &= 1 - \frac{\text{LD}(P, P')}{\max(m, n)} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification of bounds:** - $\text{LD}(P, P) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{sim}(P, P) = 1 - 0 = 1$ ✓ (max similarity) - $\text{LD}(P, P') = \max(m, n) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{sim} = 0$ ✓ (no overlap) Hence $\text{sim} \in [0, 1]$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note:** Levenshtein distance has a well-known dynamic-programming formulation with time complexity $O(mn)$. The examiner may ask you to write the recursion: $\text{LD}(i, j) = \min\{\text{LD}(i-1, j) + 1, \text{LD}(i, j-1) + 1, \text{LD}(i-1, j-1) + \mathbb{1}[t_i \neq t'_j]\}$. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example – Wrapper Induction > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example (Board-Style):** Consider two training pages that list three books each. > > **Page $P_1$** (labeled records marked with `|`): > ` | Title: AI | Author: Russell | Price: 500 | | Title: ML | Author: Mitchell | Price: 600 | | Title: NLP | Author: Jurafsky | Price: 700 | ` > > **Page $P_2$**: > ` | Title: DBMS | Author: Korth | Price: 450 | | Title: OS | Author: Silberschatz | Price: 550 | | Title: CN | Author: Tanenbaum | Price: 480 | ` **Step 1 – Start-of-record landmark on $P_1$:** Observed before "Title" in record 1 = ``. So $L = (\text{open}, 0, \text{tr}, *, *)$ (a *text* landmark with literal "Title" would also work, but the *tag* landmark is more reliable in HTML). **Step 2 – Generalize over $P_2$:** In $P_2$, the start is also ``. $L = (\text{open}, 0, \text{tr}, *, *)$ is *still* consistent. So no generalization is needed. **Step 3 – End-of-record landmark on $P_1$:** Observed at the end of record 1 = ``. So $R = (\text{close}, 0, \text{tr}, *, *)$. **Step 4 – Generalize over $P_2$:** Same closing tag observed. No change. **Induced LR wrapper:** $$h^* = \big(\text{open}, 0, \text{tr}, *, *,\;\; \text{close}, 0, \text{tr}, *, *\big)$$ **Step 5 – Apply to a new page $P_3$** containing ` ... ... ` – the wrapper will correctly delimit both records. ✓ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example – IBL with $F_1$ Score Suppose an IBL wrapper extracted 40 tuples from a page, of which: - 35 are correct (**TP** = 35) - 5 are spurious (**FP** = 5) - 10 correct tuples were missed (**FN** = 10) Compute Precision, Recall, $F_1$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Precision} \; P &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{35}{35 + 5} = \frac{35}{40} = 0.875 \\ \text{Recall} \; R &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{35}{35 + 10} = \frac{35}{45} \approx 0.778 \\ F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.875 \cdot 0.778}{0.875 + 0.778} \\ &= \frac{1.3615}{1.653} \approx 0.824 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Pitfall:** Students often write $F_1 = \frac{P+R}{2}$ – that is the *arithmetic* mean, **not** the $F_1$ score. The $F_1$ score is the **harmonic** mean. The harmonic mean *penalizes* imbalance: a high $P$ and low $R$ (or vice-versa) gives a low $F_1$. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Wrapper Class vs. Page Type Use this matrix during the exam to *quickly justify* why a particular wrapper class is sufficient (or not) for a given page structure. | Page Structure | Adequate Wrapper | Why? | |---|---|---| | Single-column list, no inner fields | **LR** | One delimiter pair suffices | | Two-column list with header/footer | **HLRT** | Adds head & tail | | Multi-column with HTML table cells | **OCLR** | Exploits `` tag pairing | | Multi-column with section header/footer | **HOCLR** | Header + tag pairing | | Nested list (e.g., authors grouped by publisher) | **N-LR** | Requires nested extraction | | Nested + header | **N-HOCLR** | Most expressive class | --- ### 3.6 Derivations of Decision Boundaries (Conceptual) For the IBL classifier "extract or not extract" on a candidate field span, the decision rule is: $$ \text{extract}(s) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \displaystyle\frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{sim}(P, P_i^{\text{pos}}) \;\ge\; \alpha \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ where: - $k$ = number of nearest stored positive examples (the neighborhood size, a hyperparameter) - $P_i^{\text{pos}}$ = the $i$-th most-similar positive stored page - $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ = confidence threshold (typically 0.5) > [!NOTE] > This is essentially a **k-Nearest-Neighbour (k-NN)** decision rule applied at the page level. The KTU board often asks: *"To which classical ML algorithm is IBL most closely related?"* – the answer is **k-NN with $k=1$** (or a small $k$). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Wrapper Induction Pipeline (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Web Source / Template] --> B[HTML Fetching] B --> C[HTML Parsing & Tokenization] C --> D[Token Sequence: t1, t2, ..., tm] D --> E[Human Annotation on Training Pages] E --> F[Labeled Training Set T] F --> G[Wrapper Induction Algorithm] G --> H[Learned Wrapper h*] H --> I[Apply to New Page Pnew] I --> J[Structured Output: Records and Fields] J --> K[Database / CSV / JSON] H --> L[Accuracy Evaluation: P, R, F1] L --> M[Wrapper Acceptable?] M -- Yes --> N[Deploy] M -- No --> O[Add More Labeled Examples] O --> G ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram (left → right):** The system first tokenizes a page, the human labels it, the induction engine generalizes a wrapper, and the wrapper is then applied to *new* pages. The feedback loop (right side) shows that if $F_1$ is below threshold, more examples are added and the wrapper is re-induced. --- ### 4.2 General Inducer – Search State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> MostSpecific MostSpecific --> Generalize1: New page P_i\nfails to match Generalize1 --> Generalize2: Relax text field Generalize2 --> Generalize3: Relax separator Generalize3 --> Generalize4: Relax tag Generalize4 --> Generalize5: Relax prefix Generalize5 --> MostGeneral: Relax type MostGeneral --> [*]: Return h* ``` --- ### 4.3 IBL Wrapper – k-NN Style Decision Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR Pnew[New Page Pnew] --> T[Tokenization] T --> S[Similarity Computation\nwith all stored pages] S --> K[Select k Nearest Neighbors] K --> V[Confidence-Weighted Vote] V --> D{Threshold alpha\nexceeded?} D -- Yes --> EX[Extract Field Tuple] D -- No --> SK[Skip / Mark as Empty] EX --> OUT[Output Database] SK --> OUT ``` --- ### 4.4 Wrapper Class Hierarchy (Inheritance Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD LR[LR Class\nLeft + Right delimiters] --> HLRT[HLRT Class\nLR + Head + Tail] LR --> OCLR[OCLR Class\nLR + Open/Close pairs] HLRT --> HOCLR[HOCLR Class\nHLRT + OCLR] OCLR --> HOCLR HOCLR --> NHOCLR[N-HOCLR\nNested + HOCLR] LR --> NLR[N-LR Class\nNested LR] NLR --> NHOCLR ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Reading Tip:** The diagram shows that *N-HOCLR* is the *most expressive* class (root of the tree) and *LR* is the *least expressive* (leaf). An exam question may ask: *"If your training set contains nested lists, why is LR insufficient?"* – because LR cannot represent *nested* begin/end markers. --- ### 4.5 Stalk vs. Header Pattern (Stalker / IBL Primitive) ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Token Sequence of Page] --> F1[Pattern appears\non EVERY page\nabout field f] F1 --> STK[STALK\nReliable] P --> F2[Pattern appears\non SOME pages\nabout field f] F2 --> HDR[HEADER\nUnreliable] STK --> EXT[Use for extraction] HDR --> GEN[Generalize to stalk\nas more pages seen] GEN --> STK ``` > [!NOTE] > This diagram captures the *learning dynamics* of IBL wrappers: a header pattern becomes a stalk as the learner accumulates more evidence. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** Define a *wrapper* in the context of web mining. List **any four** characteristics of semi-structured web data that make wrapper induction necessary. **Model Answer:** A *wrapper* is a software module that extracts structured data (records and fields) from semi-structured web sources, typically HTML pages, and converts them into a relational or tabular form. Four characteristics of semi-structured web data: 1. **Heterogeneous schemas** – different sites use different templates. 2. **Implicit structure** – structure is conveyed by HTML tags rather than by an explicit schema. 3. **Nested records** – pages often contain hierarchical data. 4. **High redundancy** – the same template recurs on many pages, making learning worthwhile. 5. **Frequent changes** – templates evolve, requiring *retrainable* extractors. *(Any four earn full 3 marks.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]** Differentiate between **Wrapper Induction** and **Instance-Based Wrapper Learning**. Give **one** advantage of each. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Wrapper Induction | Instance-Based Wrapper Learning | |---|---|---| | Learning type | **Eager** – builds an explicit model at training time | **Lazy** – stores examples, defers work to query time | | Output | A generalized wrapper (rules/patterns) | A database of labeled pages + similarity function | | Memory | Compact (just the wrapper) | Large (must keep all training pages) | | Update | Re-run induction | Just add the new page to the store | | Advantage | **Fast extraction** on new pages | **Easy update** when templates change slightly | --- ### Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) – Wrapper Induction Deep Dive > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **six delimiter-discovery sub-tasks** of the Kushmerick wrapper induction framework. Illustrate with an example HTML page containing a list of three products with fields *Name*, *Price*, and *Rating*. **(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **HOCLR** wrapper class. Why is it considered *more expressive* than HLRT and OCLR? Show the HOCLR wrapper that would extract the *Price* field from the following page: ```html
NamePriceRating
Phone500004.5
Laptop750004.7
``` ##### Model Solution **(a) The Six Sub-Tasks** For every page in the training set, the human annotator marks the **start** and **end** of: 1. **Start of each record** $S_r$ – the token position just before record 1, 4, 7, … begins. 2. **End of each record** $E_r$ – the token position just after record 1, 4, 7, … ends. 3. **Empty records** $\emptyset_r$ – token positions where a record *should* be but is missing (e.g., a placeholder row). 4. **Start of field $f_j$** $S_{f_j}$ – the position where field $j$ begins within a record. 5. **End of field $f_j$** $E_{f_j}$ – the position where field $j$ ends. 6. **Empty field $f_j$** $\emptyset_{f_j}$ – positions where field $j$ is absent. **Example HTML page (annotated with `^` = start, `$` = end):** ```html
  • ^ Name: Phone $ ^ Price: 50000 $ ^ Rating: 4.5 $
  • ^ Name: Laptop $ ^ Price: 75000 $ ^ Rating: 4.7 $
  • ^ Name: Tablet $ ^ Price: 30000 $ ^ Rating: 4.2 $
``` The induction algorithm observes: - Start-of-record: always preceded by `
  • ` and the literal text `Name:`. - Start-of-field "Price": always preceded by `` (or text after `Name`) and the literal `Price:`. - End-of-field "Price": always followed by `<` (start of next tag) or whitespace. - … and so on.
