It then generalizes these to the **most specific pattern** that is consistent with all training pages. **(b) HOCLR Class and the Wrapper for "Price"** **HOCLR = HLRT ∪ OCLR**: - It uses **Head** & **Tail** patterns (from HLRT) to find a unique *region* of the page (skipping header/footer). - It then uses **Open** & **Close** tag patterns (from OCLR) to delimit individual fields *within* that region. It is more expressive than HLRT (which cannot use tag pairing) **and** more expressive than OCLR (which cannot skip non-relevant header/footer regions). Hence it handles real-world pages with both. **The HOCLR wrapper for the *Price* field:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Head} &= \langle \text{open}, 0, \text{tr}, *, \text{``Name"} \rangle \\ \text{Open}_{price} &= \langle \text{open}, 0, \text{td}, *, \text{``Price"} \rangle \\ \text{Close}_{price} &= \langle \text{close}, 0, \text{td}, *, * \rangle \\ \text{Tail} &= \langle \text{close}, 0, \text{tr}, *, * \rangle \end{aligned} $$ **Extraction procedure:** 1. Find `Head` – jump to the row containing "Name" header. 2. Within that region, find every `Open`ₚᵣᵢcₑ – the `` containing "Price". 3. Capture the text from the `Open` to the next `Close`ₚᵣᵢcₑ ``. 4. Continue until `Tail` is encountered. Applied to the sample page, this yields `["50000", "75000"]` – two correctly extracted price values. ✓ --- #### Question B (14 Marks) – Instance-Based Wrapper Learning > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Define **Instance-Based Wrapper Learning**. Explain the role of **stalks** and **headers** in this paradigm. Why are *stalks* considered more reliable? **(b)** [7 Marks] Consider a new page $P$ of length 8 and a stored labeled page $P'$ of length 10. The Levenshtein distance $\text{LD}(P, P') = 3$. Compute the similarity $\text{sim}(P, P')$. If the threshold $\alpha = 0.7$, will the IBL wrapper re-use $P'$'s wrapper on $P$? Justify. Then describe the **k-NN** extraction procedure in **five steps** for extracting a target field $f$ from $P$. ##### Model Solution **(a) Instance-Based Wrapper Learning – Definition + Stalks/Headers** **Definition:** *Instance-Based Wrapper Learning* is a lazy-learning approach to information extraction in which the system **stores a set of labeled training pages** rather than inducing an explicit generalized wrapper. When a new page arrives, the system identifies the *most similar* stored page(s) and re-uses (or adapts) the wrapper that worked on them. **Stalks vs. Headers:** | Aspect | Stalk | Header | |---|---|---| | Frequency | Appears in front of field $f$ on **every** training page | Appears in front of field $f$ on **some** training pages only | | Reliability | **High** – can be trusted as a delimiter | **Low** – may be coincidental | | Role | Used directly for extraction | Used to *seed* a stalk candidate; promoted to stalk once evidence accumulates | **Why are stalks more reliable?** A pattern that occurs on *every* page is statistically unlikely to be coincidental; it almost certainly reflects the template's design. A header pattern may be coincidental or template-specific, so the algorithm waits for more evidence before trusting it. **(b) Numerical Computation of Similarity + k-NN Procedure** **Step 1 – Apply the formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{sim}(P, P') &= 1 - \frac{\text{LD}(P, P')}{\max(\vert P \vert, \vert P' \vert)} \\ &= 1 - \frac{3}{\max(8, 10)} \\ &= 1 - \frac{3}{10} \\ &= 0.7 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 – Compare with threshold:** $$ \text{sim}(P, P') = 0.7 \;\ge\; \alpha = 0.7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{RE-USE the wrapper of } P'. $$ The wrapper of $P'$ *will* be re-applied to $P$ (boundary case – the decision rule is non-strict $\ge$). **Step 3 – The k-NN Extraction Procedure (five steps):** 1. **Tokenize** $P$ into a sequence of tokens $\langle t_1, \dots, t_m \rangle$. 2. **Compute** $\text{sim}(P, P_i)$ for every stored labeled page $P_i$. 3. **Select** the top-$k$ pages with the highest similarity – the *neighborhood* $\mathcal{N}_k(P)$. 4. **Vote / Weight**: For each candidate span $s$ on $P$ that could contain field $f$, compute the average similarity-weighted confidence from $\mathcal{N}_k(P)$: $$ \text{score}(s) = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{P_i \in \mathcal{N}_k(P)} \text{sim}(P, P_i) \cdot \mathbb{1}[\,s \text{ matches field } f \text{ on } P_i\,] $$ 5. **Output** the span $s^*$ with the highest score, provided $\text{score}(s^*) \ge \alpha$; otherwise mark the field as *empty*. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. Forgetting to **normalize** the Levenshtein distance by `max(|P|, |P'|)` – this gives a *raw* similarity that is not in $[0,1]$. > 2. Confusing **header** with **stalk** in definition questions (header is *unreliable*, stalk is *reliable*). > 3. Using the **arithmetic mean** $(P+R)/2$ instead of the **harmonic mean** for $F_1$. > 4. In Kushmerick's six sub-task questions, students often list only *start* and *end* and forget the **empty-record** and **empty-field** sub-tasks. > 5. For IBL extraction, students forget to *weight* the vote by similarity – an unweighted majority vote is wrong. --- ### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Wrapper** = a procedure that lifts structured data out of semi-structured web pages. - **Wrapper Induction** is a *supervised, eager* learning approach: it generalizes **delimiter patterns** from labeled training pages. The **General Inducer (GI)** does a *specific-to-general* search in the wrapper's hypothesis space. - The **six delimiter sub-tasks** (Kushmerick) are: start-of-record, end-of-record, empty-record, start-of-field, end-of-field, empty-field. - **Six wrapper classes** – LR, HLRT, OCLR, HOCLR, N-LR, N-HOCLR – in order of increasing expressiveness. - **Instance-Based Wrapper Learning** is a *lazy* learning approach: it stores examples and uses **similarity** (e.g., normalized Levenshtein distance) to decide which stored wrapper to apply to a new page. - **Stalks** = patterns appearing on *every* page (reliable); **Headers** = patterns appearing on *some* pages (unreliable, may be promoted to stalks as more pages accumulate). - **Evaluation metrics**: Precision = $TP / (TP + FP)$, Recall = $TP / (TP + FN)$, $F_1 = 2PR / (P + R)$. - **IBL is essentially k-NN** at the page/region level. - **Why bother?** Wrapper induction is the foundation of price-comparison portals, news aggregators, job boards, financial data feeds, and modern RAG pipelines. - **Board Exam Memory Hooks**: 1. Memorize the **6 sub-tasks** and the **6 wrapper classes**. 2. Memorize the **5-step ladder** of landmark generalization (text → separator → tag → prefix → type). 3. Memorize the **normalized Levenshtein similarity formula**. 4. Memorize the **$F_1$ formula** and remember it is a *harmonic* mean. 5. Be ready to draw **two diagrams**: (i) the pipeline, and (ii) the wrapper class hierarchy.

topics.title: Automatic Wrapper Generation: Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Automatic Wrapper Generation: Problems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Automatic Wrapper Generation** refers to the semi-supervised or fully automated process of inducing *extraction rules* (wrappers) from a set of training web pages, enabling the system to extract structured records from unseen, similarly templated web documents. The **Problems** subtopic in KTU Module 3 enumerates the theoretical and operational bottlenecks that make this task *NP-hard* in the general case, including disjunctive data regions, missing attributes, inconsistent labeling, schema heterogeneity, and page-versus-site-level complexity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECDT635, Module 3):** > The student is expected to identify, classify, and reason about the *types of heterogeneity* encountered during wrapper induction and to map each problem type to its corresponding detection/heuristic strategy. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Form Filler" Intuition Imagine you are a bank clerk given a stack of one hundred application forms. Every form has roughly the same fields — *Name, Account Number, IFSC, Branch* — but: - Some forms are in **English**, others in **Malayalam** (labeling heterogeneity). - Some forms have a *missing "Middle Name"* box (optional attributes). - A few forms have *two applicants on a single page* (multi-record / disjunction). - A few fields are *left blank* (missing values). - The *font, alignment, and exact wording of labels* differ wildly across forms. Your task is to **write a single universal rule-book** that, given any new form, fills a spreadsheet with the right values. The *rule-book* is the **wrapper**, and the act of writing it is **wrapper induction**. The "problems" we study are precisely the structural irregularities that make writing one rule-book impossible without sophisticated heuristics. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in Web Mining:** > Over **60%** of business data on the modern web lives inside HTML templates. Until a wrapper can robustly handle heterogeneity, automated knowledge-base construction, e-commerce comparison engines, and price aggregators cannot scale. ### 1.3 Taxonomy of Wrapper Generation Problems — Quick Map | Layer | Problem Class | Symptom | |------|---------------|---------| | Page | **Disjunction** | Multiple optional regions in one DOM subtree | | Page | **Optional / Missing Attributes** | Some pages omit a field | | Page | **Inconsistent Labeling** | Same concept, different word | | Page | **Type Heterogeneity** | Same label, different value formats | | Site | **Multi-Page Records** | One logical record spans N pages | | Site | **Template Variation** | Two pages, different HTML structure | | Operational | **Dynamic / AJAX content** | Page content not in initial HTML | | Operational | **Nested / Hierarchical Data** | Records inside records | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Walk-Through of the Wrapper Pipeline A wrapper is a function $\mathcal{W}$ that maps a web document $D$ to a set of structured records $R$: $$\mathcal{W} : D \longmapsto R = \{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_k\}$$ where each record $r_i$ is an $n$-tuple of attribute values $(a_{i1}, a_{i2}, \ldots, a_{in})$. The **problems** arise when the *induction* of $\mathcal{W}$ from a labelled sample $\mathcal{S} = \{(D_j, R_j)\}_{j=1}^{m}$ is undermined by irregularities. The pipeline can be decomposed into four logical stages, each of which has its own failure mode: 1. **Page segmentation** — partitioning the DOM tree into *data regions* and *non-data regions*. 2. **Data-record detection** — identifying repeated record blocks (often via tree-edit distance or tag-string similarity). 3. **Attribute alignment** — labelling the *k-th* field across records. 4. **Value extraction & normalization** — cleaning the raw text. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" behind the four stages:** > Wrappers fail *rarely* at extraction itself and *frequently* at *segmentation* and *alignment*. This is the reason the KTU syllabus lists problems before solutions. ### 2.2 The Eight Canonical Problem Types (Deep Analysis) #### 2.2.1 Disjunction of Data Regions A *disjunctive* data region is one in which not all child sub-trees belong to the same record type. The generic HTML pattern is: ```html ... record A ... ... record B (different schema) ... ... record A ...
``` A naive wrapper that assumes a uniform schema will hallucinate fields. The **detection heuristic** relies on computing a *similarity threshold* between consecutive siblings in the DOM tree. If $\text{Sim}(T_i, T_{i+1}) < \tau$ (a tunable threshold), the boundary is declared a **disjunction point**. #### 2.2.2 Missing / Optional Attributes A page may legitimately omit a field. Naïve extraction fills blanks with `null`, but downstream consumers may misinterpret this as a *parse error* rather than a *legitimate absence*. The challenge is therefore **distinguishing** between: - *Genuine missing value* (the page simply has no data). - *Wrapper failure* (the field is present but the rule missed it). #### 2.2.3 Inconsistent Labeling The same semantic field may be labelled as `"Price"`, `"Cost"`, `"MRP"`, or `"Our Price"`. A purely syntactic wrapper cannot resolve this without an **ontology** or a **synonym dictionary** (e.g., WordNet, domain-specific lexicons). #### 2.2.4 Type / Schema Heterogeneity Even when labels are consistent, *value types* differ: one page lists `"4.5 / 5"`, another lists `"★★★★½"`, and a third lists `"Very Good"`. Aligning these requires **type-aware normalization**. #### 2.2.5 Multi-Page Records A single logical record (e.g., a product) may be split across `description.html`, `specs.html`, and `reviews.html`. The wrapper must perform **cross-page joining** keyed by an entity identifier (often a URL slug or SKU). #### 2.2.6 Template Variation Even within a single site, the same data field may appear under two different page templates (e.g., a *search-results template* vs a *detail-page template*). A site-level wrapper must be a **union** of per-template rules. #### 2.2.7 Dynamic / Scripted Content JavaScript-rendered data is absent from the static HTML fetched by `curl` or `wget`. Detection requires a *headless browser* (Puppeteer, Playwright, Selenium). The wrapper must therefore be **two-stage**: a *renderer* and an *extractor*. #### 2.2.8 Nested / Hierarchical Data Lists within lists, e.g., *"a book has multiple authors, each author has multiple affiliations"*. Flat record extraction loses this structure; **tree-pattern wrappers** preserve it. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table collects every formula a student may be asked to derive or apply in the ESE / Module test for this topic. Symbols use `\vert` (not `|`) to remain safe inside markdown. | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes | |---|---------|----------------------|-------| | 1 | Wrapper mapping | $\mathcal{W}: D \rightarrow R$ | $D$ = document, $R$ = record set | | 2 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Measures *exactness* of extracted fields | | 3 | Recall | $R_{\text{recall}} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Measures *completeness* | | 4 | F-measure | $F_\beta = \dfrac{(1+\beta^2)\,P\,R_{\text{recall}}}{\beta^2 P + R_{\text{recall}}}$ | Harmonic mean; $\beta=1$ is standard | | 5 | Tree-edit distance (lower bound) | $\delta(T_1, T_2) = \sum_{v \in V} \mathbb{1}\!\left[\text{label}(v)\right]$ | Used in MDR-style alignment | | 6 | Cosine similarity (tag-vectors) | $\text{sim}(u,v) = \dfrac{u \cdot v}{\Vert u \Vert_2 \;\Vert v \Vert_2}$ | Used for sibling similarity | | 7 | Disjunction threshold | $\text{Sim}(T_i, T_{i+1}) < \tau$ | Detects *mixed-schema* siblings | | 8 | Attribute coverage | $\text{Cov}(a) = \dfrac{\#\text{records with }a}{\#\text{total records}}$ | $0 \le \text{Cov} \le 1$ | | 9 | Wrapper induction cost | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot \log n)$ for DOM sort | $n$ = number of DOM nodes | | 10 | XPath cardinality | $\text{count}(//\text{record}) = k$ | Returns record count $k$ | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **E-commerce price intelligence** (e.g., Flipkart, Amazon) — wrappers must tolerate the seven problem types above daily. - **Knowledge-graph construction** (Google Knowledge Graph, Wikidata import) — millions of wrappers feed the entity registry. - **Financial compliance** — regulators scrape filings; missing-attribute handling is a *legal* requirement, not just an engineering nicety. - **News aggregation** — multi-page records (article + comments + author bio) are the norm. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Disjunction Threshold from First Principles **Goal.** Show that the optimal similarity threshold $\tau^*$ for declaring a disjunction in a sibling sequence of DOM sub-trees $\{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_m\}$ is the *minimum* of the within-cluster similarities. **Step 1 — Define the similarity matrix.** Compute a symmetric matrix $S \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ where $$S_{ij} = \text{sim}(T_i, T_j) = \frac{\langle \phi(T_i), \phi(T_j) \rangle}{\Vert \phi(T_i) \Vert_2 \, \Vert \phi(T_j) \Vert_2}$$ and $\phi(T)$ is a feature vector (e.g., tag-path histogram) extracted from sub-tree $T$. **Step 2 — Assume clustering.** Suppose the $m$ sub-trees are partitioned into $c$ homogeneous clusters $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_c$ where every sub-tree within a cluster has the *same schema*. Then the *intra-cluster* similarity satisfies $$S_{ij} \geq \tau^* \quad \forall\, i,j \in C_p,\; p=1,\ldots,c$$ and the *inter-cluster* similarity satisfies $$S_{ij} < \tau^* \quad \forall\, i \in C_p,\; j \in C_q,\; p \neq q$$ **Step 3 — Bound the threshold.** Taking the minimum over all intra-cluster pairs and the maximum over all inter-cluster pairs: $$\max_{p \neq q}\;\max_{\substack{i \in C_p \\ j \in C_q}} S_{ij} \;<\; \tau^* \;\leq\; \min_{p}\;\min_{\substack{i,j \in C_p}} S_{ij}$$ **Step 4 — Practical estimator.** In practice, because the true clustering is unknown, the wrapper induction algorithm estimates $\tau^*$ as the *largest gap* in the sorted sibling-similarity sequence. Formally: $$\tau^* \approx \arg\max_{1 \le k < m} \bigl[ S_{(k)}^{\text{intra}} - S_{(k)}^{\text{inter}} \bigr]$$ where $S_{(k)}^{\text{intra}}$ is the $k$-th largest intra-cluster similarity estimate and $S_{(k)}^{\text{inter}}$ is its inter-cluster counterpart. **Step 5 — Decision rule.** A boundary between $T_i$ and $T_{i+1}$ is therefore declared a *disjunction point* iff $$\text{sim}(T_i, T_{i+1}) < \tau^*$$ This is the operational rule implemented in classical systems such as **MDR (Mining Data Records)** by Liu, Zhai and Zhang. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** the threshold is *not* a global constant; it is **data-dependent**. A wrapper tuned for an e-commerce site at $\tau^*=0.85$ may completely fail on a news portal where the same boundary sits at $\tau^*=0.60$. ### 3.2 Worked Example: F-measure under Missing Attributes Suppose a wrapper is asked to extract three fields: `title`, `price`, `rating` from 100 product pages. Of these, **15 pages legitimately omit `rating`**. | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Total pages | $N = 100$ | | Records with `rating` | $n_r = 85$ | | Records without `rating` (genuine miss) | $n_0 = 15$ | | True Positives (correctly extracted) | $TP = 80$ | | False Negatives (failed to find an existing rating) | $FN = 5$ | | False Positives (hallucinated a rating) | $FP = 0$ | **Step 1 — Compute precision.** $$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 0} = 1.000$$ **Step 2 — Compute recall.** Recall ignores *legitimate* missing values, so the denominator is $n_r$, not $N$: $$R_{\text{recall}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 5} = 0.9412$$ **Step 3 — Compute $F_1$.** $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R_{\text{recall}}}{P + R_{\text{recall}}} = \frac{2 \times 1.000 \times 0.9412}{1.000 + 0.9412} = 0.9699$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** A recall of **94.12%** with **100%** precision indicates the wrapper is conservative — it never hallucinates data, but it *missed* 5 legitimate ratings. A more *aggressive* threshold might trade precision for higher recall. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Problem → Detection → Mitigation | # | Problem | Detection Signal | Mitigation Strategy | |---|---------|------------------|---------------------| | 1 | Disjunction | Drop in sibling similarity $S_{i,i+1}$ | Cluster siblings, split region | | 2 | Optional attribute | Coverage $\text{Cov}(a) < 1$ | Mark as nullable in schema | | 3 | Inconsistent label | Token-set divergence | Synonym dictionary / ontology | | 4 | Type heterogeneity | Value-format regex mismatch | Normalizer module | | 5 | Multi-page record | Pagination links, shared key | Cross-page join | | 6 | Template variation | URL pattern + DOM fingerprint | Multi-template union wrapper | | 7 | Dynamic content | Empty initial HTML, populated DOM | Headless browser | | 8 | Nested data | Recursive DOM sub-trees | Tree-pattern wrapper | ### 3.4 Worked Derivation: Tree-Edit Distance for Alignment **Goal.** Show that the *alignment* problem across records of the same data region can be reduced to *minimum-cost matching* on a bipartite graph. **Step 1 — Flatten each record to a token sequence.** For record $r_i$, let $\mathbf{t}_i = (t_{i1}, t_{i2}, \ldots, t_{iL_i})$ be the leaf-text tokens in document order. **Step 2 — Define the cost matrix.** $$C_{ij} = 1 - \text{sim}\!\bigl(\text{context}(t_i),\; \text{context}(t_j)\bigr)$$ **Step 3 — Hungarian assignment.** The optimal alignment is the minimum-cost perfect matching on the bipartite graph $G = (U \cup V, E, C)$ where $U = \mathbf{t}_1$, $V = \mathbf{t}_2$. Its cost is $$C^* = \min_{\pi} \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{i,\pi(i)}$$ **Step 4 — Termination condition.** If $C^* > \theta_{\text{align}}$ (an alignment threshold), the two records are considered *non-alignable* and the wrapper flags a **disjunction** or a **missing attribute** for that record. > [!NOTE] > The Hungarian algorithm runs in $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$, which is acceptable because the data region typically contains $n \le 50$ candidate fields. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Wrapper Generation Pipeline with Failure Points ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw HTML Document D] --> B[DOM Tree Construction] B --> C[Page Segmentation] C --> D{Disjunction Check} D -- Yes --> E[Split Data Region] D -- No --> F[Data Record Detection] E --> F F --> G[Attribute Alignment] G --> H{Optional Attrs?} H -- Yes --> I[Mark Nullable Schema] H -- No --> J[Value Extraction] I --> J J --> K{Type Check} K -- Pass --> L[Structured Record R] K -- Fail --> M[Normalizer Module] M --> L L --> N{Site-Level Check} N -- Multi-Page --> O[Cross-Page Join] N -- Single-Page --> P[Final Output] O --> P ``` ### 4.2 Problem Taxonomy Map ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Wrapper Generation Problems] P --> PG[Page Level] P --> SL[Site Level] P --> OP[Operational] PG --> PG1[Disjunction] PG --> PG2[Optional Attribute] PG --> PG3[Inconsistent Label] PG --> PG4[Type Heterogeneity] SL --> SL1[Multi-Page Record] SL --> SL2[Template Variation] SL --> SL3[Site Schema Drift] OP --> OP1[Dynamic Content] OP --> OP2[Nested Hierarchy] OP --> OP3[Anti-Scrape Defenses] ``` ### 4.3 Disjunction Detection — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Input: Sibling Sequence T1...Tm] --> S2[Compute Pairwise Similarity Matrix S] S2 --> S3[Sort Similarity Values] S3 --> S4[Find Largest Gap] S4 --> S5[Set Threshold tau-star] S5 --> S6{Pair Similarity < tau-star?} S6 -- Yes --> S7[Declare Disjunction Boundary] S6 -- No --> S8[Continue Homogeneous Region] ``` ### 4.4 Multi-Page Record Resolution ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[Page A: Title and Price] --> K[Shared Key: SKU] R2[Page B: Specs] --> K R3[Page C: Reviews] --> K K --> J[Join Engine] J --> MR[Unified Multi-Page Record] ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robust Wrapper ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FrontendLayer F1[HTTP Fetcher] F2[Headless Renderer] F1 --> F3[HTML Cleaner] F2 --> F3 end subgraph AnalysisLayer F3 --> A1[DOM Parser] A1 --> A2[Segmentation Engine] A2 --> A3[Disjunction Detector] A3 --> A4[Record Aligner] A4 --> A5[Type Normalizer] end subgraph BackendLayer A5 --> B1[Schema Registry] A5 --> B2[Output Formatter JSON or CSV] B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3[Downstream Database] end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]** > *Define the term "disjunctive data region" in the context of automatic wrapper generation. Give one example from a real-world web page.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A *disjunctive data region* is a sub-tree in the DOM that contains records belonging to **two or more distinct schemas** interleaved with one another. Classical examples include the *sponsored-ad slot* on an e-commerce search results page, where a *product card* and a *textual ad* are siblings under the same `
` parent. A naïve wrapper that assumes a uniform schema for all siblings will hallucinate fields or fail to extract anything. (3 marks: definition 2, example 1) --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > *Differentiate between "optional attribute" and "wrapper-induced missing value". Why is this distinction important?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An *optional attribute* is a field that is **legitimately absent** from a page (e.g., a product with no customer rating). A *wrapper-induced missing value* is a field that **is present in the page** but the wrapper failed to extract it. The distinction is critical because conflating the two inflates the false-negative count, **depressing recall** spuriously, and misleads downstream consumers into treating correctable failures as semantic absences. (3 marks: definitions 2, importance 1) --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions > **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]** **(a)** *Enumerate and explain the major page-level problems encountered during automatic wrapper generation. Discuss how each is detected. (7 marks)* **(b)** *With a suitable block diagram, describe the architecture of a robust automatic wrapper generation system. Explain how it handles disjunction, optional attributes, and dynamic content. (7 marks)* --- **Model Answer for Q.A(a):** The four major page-level problems are: 1. **Disjunction of data regions (2 marks).** A single sub-tree in the DOM contains records of *different schemas*. *Detection:* compute pairwise cosine similarity between consecutive sibling sub-trees; declare a disjunction boundary wherever $\text{sim}(T_i, T_{i+1}) < \tau^*$, with $\tau^*$ chosen as the largest gap in the sorted similarity sequence. 2. **Optional / missing attributes (2 marks).** Some pages legitimately omit a field. *Detection:* compute the attribute coverage $\text{Cov}(a)$ over a training set; if $\text{Cov}(a) < 1$ but the page contains *no error markers* (e.g., `N/A`), mark the field as nullable in the schema rather than treating it as a wrapper failure. 3. **Inconsistent labeling (2 marks).** The same concept appears under different textual labels (`"Price"` vs `"MRP"`). *Detection:* tokenize the candidate labels and consult a synonym dictionary or domain ontology; the labels that map to the **same ontological node** are unified. 4. **Type / schema heterogeneity (1 mark).** The same field is rendered in different value formats (numeric `"4.5"` vs string `"Very Good"`). *Detection:* apply a *type-classification regex* to each extracted value and route it through a normalizer. **(7 marks distributed as 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for the four sub-parts.)** --- **Model Answer for Q.A(b):** A robust wrapper generation system consists of three layers as shown in Section 4.5: - **Frontend Layer (2 marks).** A *fetch* module that can operate in two modes — a *lightweight HTTP fetcher* for static HTML and a *headless renderer* (Puppeteer, Playwright) for JavaScript-driven pages. The latter handles **dynamic content** (problem 7). - **Analysis Layer (3 marks).** A pipeline of *(i)* DOM parser, *(ii)* segmentation engine, *(iii)* disjunction detector (using the threshold $\tau^*$ derived in Section 3.1), *(iv)* record aligner, and *(v)* type normalizer. The disjunction detector splits a mixed region into homogeneous sub-regions; the aligner uses a *Hungarian-style* bipartite matching (Section 3.4) to assign fields across records; the normalizer converts heterogeneous value formats to a canonical schema. - **Backend Layer (2 marks).** A *schema registry* that records nullable attributes, synonyms, and template fingerprints; an *output formatter* that emits JSON or CSV; and a *downstream consumer* (database, knowledge graph, or analytics dashboard). **(7 marks distributed as 2 + 3 + 2 across the three layers.)** --- > **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** *Discuss the site-level problems in automatic wrapper generation. How does multi-page record resolution work? (7 marks)* **(b)** *Explain the evaluation metrics used to assess wrapper quality. A wrapper extracts `price` from 200 product pages. There are 180 pages that genuinely contain a price, of which 165 are correctly extracted. The wrapper also produces 5 spurious "price" fields on pages that have none. Compute Precision, Recall, and $F_1$. (7 marks)* --- **Model Answer for Q.B(a):** The two principal site-level problems are: - **Multi-page records (3 marks).** A single logical entity (e.g., a product) is fragmented across multiple URLs — a *listing page*, a *specification page*, a *reviews page*. The wrapper must therefore perform a *join* keyed on a unique entity identifier such as the URL slug, the SKU, or an in-page anchor link. The architecture in Section 4.4 illustrates this: pages A, B, C funnel into a *shared-key join engine* that emits a unified record. - **Template variation (3 marks).** A single domain may host *N* different page templates (e.g., search results vs detail pages vs category landing pages). A site-level wrapper must be a *union* of per-template wrappers, each with its own DOM signature. Templates are clustered by *URL pattern* and by *DOM-tree fingerprint*; pages are routed to the correct template wrapper by a *classifier*. - **Schema drift over time (1 mark).** The site redesigns itself; the wrapper must therefore support *re-induction* and *versioning* of templates. **(7 marks distributed as 3 + 3 + 1.)** --- **Model Answer for Q.B(b):** **Step 1 — Identify the entries of the confusion matrix (3 marks).** - $TP = 165$ (correctly extracted prices) - $FP = 5$ (spurious extractions) - $FN = 180 - 165 = 15$ (genuine prices the wrapper missed) - $TN = 200 - 180 - 5 = 15$ (pages with no price, none extracted) **Step 2 — Precision (1 mark).** $$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{165}{165 + 5} = \frac{165}{170} = 0.9706$$ **Step 3 — Recall (1 mark).** $$R_{\text{recall}} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{165}{165 + 15} = \frac{165}{180} = 0.9167$$ **Step 4 — $F_1$ (2 marks).** $$F_1 = \frac{2 P R_{\text{recall}}}{P + R_{\text{recall}}} = \frac{2 \times 0.9706 \times 0.9167}{0.9706 + 0.9167} = \frac{1.7797}{1.8873} = 0.9431$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation.** The wrapper has very high *precision* (97%) but moderate *recall* (92%). The $F_1$ of **94.31%** indicates a well-balanced, production-grade extractor. **(7 marks distributed as 3 + 1 + 1 + 2.)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks in this topic:** > 1. **Confusing "optional" with "missing".** Examiners award the mark *only* if you explicitly state that an *optional* attribute is a schema-level decision, not a wrapper failure. > 2. **Skipping the threshold derivation.** The disjunction threshold $\tau^*$ is *not* a constant — students who state "use a threshold of 0.7" without derivation lose 2 marks. > 3. **Forgetting the denominator in recall.** Recall is computed over the *truly present* fields, not the *total pages*. Mismatched denominators cost 1 mark. > 4. **Ignoring dynamic content.** A wrapper architecture diagram that does not include a *headless-renderer* branch will be marked incomplete for 2 marks. > 5. **Single-template assumption.** If a question asks for *site-level* extraction, the examiner expects **multi-template union** — a single-template answer is capped at 60% of the marks for that sub-part. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **wrapper** maps an HTML document to a set of structured records: $\mathcal{W}: D \rightarrow R$. - **Eight canonical problem types** must be memorised: disjunction, optional attribute, inconsistent label, type heterogeneity, multi-page record, template variation, dynamic content, nested data. - The **disjunction threshold** $\tau^*$ is *data-dependent* and is estimated as the *largest gap* in sorted sibling-similarity. - **Precision, Recall, $F_1$** are the three evaluation metrics; recall is computed over *truly present* fields, not over the total page set. - **Optional attributes** must be marked as *nullable* in the schema; never treat them as wrapper failures. - **Dynamic content** requires a *headless renderer*; static `curl` is insufficient. - **Multi-page records** are resolved by a *join* on a shared key (SKU, URL slug). - **Template variation** demands a *union* of per-template wrappers routed by a classifier. - The **Hungarian algorithm** ($\mathcal{O}(n^3)$) is the classical tool for record alignment. - **MDR (Liu, Zhai, Zhang)** is the seminal algorithm for disjunction-aware data-record mining.

topics.title: String Matching and Tree Matching - Multiple Alignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** String Matching and Tree Matching - Multiple Alignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 String Matching — Formal Definition

**String Matching** is the problem of finding all (or some) occurrences of a pattern string $P$ of length $m$ within a larger text string $T$ of length $n$, where typically $m \ll n$. Formally, we seek every position $i$ in $T$ such that $T[i : i + m - 1] = P[0 : m - 1]$.

In Web Mining, string matching is the foundational operation behind:
- Web crawlers detecting duplicate or near-duplicate pages.
- Plagiarism detection across documents.
- Indexing engines (inverted index construction).
- Pattern-driven information extraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Flavours of String Matching (KTU Syllabus Focus)**
> 1. **Exact Matching** — every character of the pattern must match the substring (e.g., `find`, `KMP`, `Boyer-Moore`, `Rabin-Karp`).
> 2. **Approximate (Fuzzy) Matching** — up to $k$ mismatches, insertions, or deletions are tolerated (e.g., Edit Distance / Levenshtein Distance).

## 1.2 Tree Matching — Formal Definition

**Tree Matching** generalises string matching to hierarchical structures. Given two ordered, labelled trees $T_1$ and $T_2$, the task is to find a correspondence (a matching) between their nodes that preserves the ancestor-descendant relationship as much as possible. Formally, a **tree matching** is a bijection $M \subseteq V(T_1) \times V(T_2)$ such that for any two pairs $(u_1, v_1)$ and $(u_2, v_2) \in M$, the ancestor relation in $T_1$ between $u_1$ and $u_2$ is the same as that between $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $T_2$.

In Web Mining, tree matching is used to:
- Compare **DOM trees** of HTML pages to detect templated regions.
- Identify **data records** in result pages (e.g., product lists) via the **MDR algorithm**.
- Perform wrapper induction for structured data extraction.

## 1.3 Multiple Alignment — Formal Definition

**Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA)** extends pairwise alignment to $k \geq 3$ sequences. Given $k$ strings $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k$, the goal is to insert gaps (`-`) into each string so that the resulting strings $S'_1, S'_2, \ldots, S'_k$ all have the same length $L$, maximising an overall similarity score under a chosen scoring scheme (commonly the **Sum-of-Pairs** score).

In Web Mining, multiple alignment is used to:
- Align multiple **wrapper-induction** examples that extract the same field.
- Cluster templated web pages by aligning their **HTML tag sequences**.
- Bio-informatics-inspired web sequence analysis (e.g., URL clustering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Module-3 syllabus explicitly binds these three topics together because they form the *algorithmic backbone* of any web-pattern discovery pipeline. Mastering them is mandatory for the **14-mark ESE question**.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogies

| Concept | Plain-English Analogy | Core Insight |
|---|---|---|
| **String Matching** | Searching for the word **"sale"** in a long newspaper page by sliding your finger across each column. | A brute-force linear scan, accelerated by smart skipping (KMP failure function). |
| **Approximate String Matching** | Spotting **"SALE"** even when misspelt as **"sael"** or **"sa1e"** in a noisy OCR document. | Allow a *budget* of mismatches — quantified by edit distance. |
| **Tree Matching** | Comparing the **family trees** of two cousins to find shared ancestral branches. | The *shape* of the hierarchy matters as much as the labels. |
| **Multiple Alignment** | Lining up 5 friends' **DNA samples** side-by-side to find the common mutation column. | One global alignment must satisfy **all** sequences simultaneously. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Exact String Matching — The Brute Force Logic

**Operational Logic (step-by-step):**

1. Align pattern $P$ at position $i = 0$ of text $T$.
2. Compare $P[0], P[1], \ldots, P[m-1]$ against $T[i], T[i+1], \ldots, T[i+m-1]$ **left-to-right**.
3. If any comparison fails, slide $P$ one position right to $i \leftarrow i + 1$.
4. If all $m$ characters match, **report** match at position $i$.
5. Terminate when $i > n - m$.

**Time Complexity:** $O(m \cdot n)$ — *worst case* (e.g., $P =$ "aaaa", $T =$ "aaaaaaaaaab"). **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$.

## 2.2 The Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) Algorithm — Why It Wins

KMP improves over brute force by **never re-comparing** characters already known to match. It does this using a **failure function** $\pi[j]$ that tells us the longest proper prefix of $P[0..j]$ that is also a suffix.

$$
\pi[j] = \max \{ k : 0 \le k < j \text{ and } P[0..k-1] = P[j-k+1..j] \}
$$

**Operational Logic:**

1. Pre-compute the failure function $\pi$ over the pattern in $O(m)$ time.
2. Scan $T$ once. On mismatch at position $j$, instead of sliding $P$ by 1, jump $j \leftarrow \pi[j-1]$.
3. Continue until end of $T$.

**Time Complexity:** $O(m + n)$ — *guaranteed linear*. **Space Complexity:** $O(m)$.

## 2.3 The Rabin-Karp Algorithm — Hashing Approach

Rabin-Karp uses **rolling hash** to compare substrings in $O(1)$ expected time.

$$
h(T[i..i+m-1]) = \left( \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} T[i+j] \cdot d^{\,m-1-j} \right) \bmod q
$$

On slide: $h_{i+1} = \left( d \cdot (h_i - T[i] \cdot d^{m-1}) + T[i+m] \right) \bmod q$.

**Time Complexity:** $O(m + n)$ expected, $O(mn)$ worst case.

## 2.4 Approximate Matching — Edit Distance (Levenshtein)

The **edit distance** $D[i,j]$ between prefixes $T[0..i]$ and $P[0..j]$ is computed via dynamic programming:

$$
D[i,j] = \begin{cases}
i & \text{if } j = 0 \\
j & \text{if } i = 0 \\
D[i-1, j-1] & \text{if } T[i] = P[j] \\
1 + \min\bigl( D[i-1,j],\; D[i,j-1],\; D[i-1,j-1] \bigr) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The three options correspond to **deletion**, **insertion**, and **substitution** respectively.

**Time & Space Complexity:** $O(m \cdot n)$.

## 2.5 Tree Matching — Tree Edit Distance (Zhang-Shasha)

Tree edit distance generalises string edit distance. Three elementary operations:
- **Delete** a node $v$ (its children become children of $v$'s parent).
- **Insert** a node $v$.
- **Relabel** a node $v$ from label $a$ to label $b$ (cost $c(a,b)$).

The Zhang-Shasha algorithm computes **forest edit distance** using post-order traversal with a **LR-key decomposition** (leftmost leaf descendant + rightmost leaf descendant). **Time Complexity:** $O(\vert T_1 \vert \cdot \vert T_2 \vert \cdot \min(\text{depth}_1, \text{depth}_2)^2)$.

## 2.6 Multiple Alignment — Sum-of-Pairs (SP) Scoring

Given $k$ aligned sequences of length $L$, the **sum-of-pairs score** is:

$$
SP = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{k} \sum_{l=1}^{L} s\bigl( S'_i[l],\, S'_j[l] \bigr)
$$

where $s(x,y)$ is a column-wise substitution cost (e.g., BLOSUM, PAM, or simple match/mismatch) with $s(x,-) = s(-,y) = \text{gap penalty } g$.

The objective is to find alignment $(S'_1, \ldots, S'_k)$ that **minimises** SP (or equivalently, **maximises** a positive similarity). **Exact optimisation** is NP-hard for $k \ge 3$; **progressive heuristics** (e.g., ClustalW-style star alignment) provide $O(k^2 \cdot L^2)$ practical solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Star Alignment Heuristic (KTU Favourite):**
> 1. Compute all $k(k-1)/2$ pairwise alignments.
> 2. Choose the **centre sequence** $S_c$ — the one with maximum average similarity to all others.
> 3. Align every other sequence to $S_c$ and merge into the multiple alignment.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-room ready**. Every formula has appeared (or is likely to appear) in a KTU Web Mining question paper. Pipes `|` in mathematical expressions are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Failure Function (KMP) | $\pi[j] = \max \vert k : P[0..k-1] = P[j-k+1..j] \vert$ | Longest border of $P[0..j]$ | $O(m)$ |
| 2 | Rolling Hash (Rabin-Karp) | $h_{i+1} = \bigl( d \cdot h_i - T[i] \cdot d^{m} + T[i+m] \bigr) \bmod q$ | Slide hash in $O(1)$ | $O(m+n)$ expected |
| 3 | Edit Distance Recurrence | $D[i,j] = 1 + \min(D[i-1,j], D[i,j-1], D[i-1,j-1])$ for mismatch | Dynamic programming | $O(mn)$ |
| 4 | Gap Penalty Model (Linear) | $\gamma(g) = g \cdot d$ where $g$ = gap length, $d$ = per-gap cost | Simple linear gap | $O(mn)$ |
| 5 | Gap Penalty Model (Affine) | $\gamma(g) = o + (g-1) \cdot e$ | Open cost $o$, extend cost $e$ | $O(mn)$ |
| 6 | Tree Edit Distance (Zhang-Shasha) | $F(T_1[i..i'], T_2[j..j'])$ via LR-key DP | Forest DP | $O(\vert T_1 \vert \cdot \vert T_2 \vert \cdot d^2)$ |
| 7 | Sum-of-Pairs Score (MSA) | $SP = \sum_{i<j} \sum_{l=1}^{L} s(S'_i[l], S'_j[l])$ | Quality of multiple alignment | NP-hard (exact) |
| 8 | Needleman-Wunsch Global | $F[i,j] = \max(F[i-1,j-1] + s, F[i-1,j] + g, F[i,j-1] + g)$ | Global pairwise alignment | $O(mn)$ |
| 9 | Smith-Waterman Local | $F[i,j] = \max(0, F[i-1,j-1]+s, F[i-1,j]+g, F[i,j-1]+g)$ | Local alignment | $O(mn)$ |
| 10 | MDR Confidence | $\text{Confidence}(D) = \frac{\text{freq}(D)}{\text{freq}(\text{siblings})}$ | Tree-pattern confidence | $O(n)$ |
| 11 | Hamming Distance | $H(P,Q) = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} [P[i] \neq Q[i]]$ | Mismatches only, equal length | $O(m)$ |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Search Engines (Google/Bing):** KMP and suffix-tree-based string matchers index the entire web.
- **Bioinformatics Pipelines (BLAST, ClustalW):** Direct application of MSA to DNA — the inspiration for *web sequence alignment*.
- **Web Data Extraction (Wrappers):** Tree matching on DOM trees powers tools like **RoadRunner** and **Lixto**.
- **Cybersecurity:** Rabin-Karp-style hashing detects malware signatures in network traffic.
- **Plagiarism Detection (Turnitin):** Approximate string matching with edit distance underpins similarity scoring.
- **DNA-inspired Web Mining:** Aligning URL strings across mirror sites to detect syndicated content.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — KMP Failure Function Construction

**Problem:** Compute the failure function $\pi$ for the pattern $P = \text{"ababaca"}$ of length $m = 7$.

### Step 1: Initialise

$$
\pi[0] = 0 \quad \text{(by definition, the first character has no proper border)}
$$

### Step 2: Compute $\pi[1]$

Compare $P[1] = \text{"b"}$ with $P[0] = \text{"a"}$. They differ. No proper prefix equals a suffix.

$$
\pi[1] = 0
$$

### Step 3: Compute $\pi[2]$ (Pattern character "a")

Now compare $P[2] = \text{"a"}$ with $P[0] = \text{"a"}$. They match.

$$
\pi[2] = \pi[1] + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1
$$

### Step 4: Compute $\pi[3]$ (Pattern character "b")

Compare $P[3] = \text{"b"}$ with $P[1] = \text{"b"}$. They match.

$$
\pi[3] = \pi[2] + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

### Step 5: Compute $\pi[4]$ (Pattern character "a")

Compare $P[4] = \text{"a"}$ with $P[2] = \text{"a"}$. They match.

$$
\pi[4] = \pi[3] + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

### Step 6: Compute $\pi[5]$ (Pattern character "c")

Compare $P[5] = \text{"c"}$ with $P[3] = \text{"b"}$. They differ. Fall back via $\pi[3] = 2$; compare $P[5]$ with $P[2] = \text{"a"}$. Still differ. Fall back via $\pi[2] = 1$; compare $P[5]$ with $P[1] = \text{"b"}$. Differ. Fall back via $\pi[1] = 0$; compare $P[5]$ with $P[0] = \text{"a"}$. Differ.

$$
\pi[5] = 0
$$

### Step 7: Compute $\pi[6]$ (Pattern character "a")

Compare $P[6] = \text{"a"}$ with $P[0] = \text{"a"}$. Match.

$$
\pi[6] = \pi[5] + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1
$$

### Final Failure Function Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi &= [\, 0,\ 0,\ 1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 0,\ 1\,] \\
i   &= [\, 0,\ 1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6\,]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love asking "construct $\pi$ for this pattern." Memorise the fallback rule — on mismatch, $k \leftarrow \pi[k-1]$ until $k = 0$ or a match is found.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Edit Distance (Levenshtein DP)

**Problem:** Compute the edit distance between $T = \text{"kitten"}$ and $P = \text{"sitting"}$. Use unit costs for insertion, deletion, substitution.

### Step 1: Build the DP Matrix Skeleton

Dimensions: $(n+1) \times (m+1) = 7 \times 7$. Initialise:

$$
D[i,0] = i \quad \text{and} \quad D[0,j] = j
$$

### Step 2: Fill Row $i=1$ (character $T[1]=\text{"k"}$)

$$
D[1,1]: T[1]=\text{"k"},\ P[1]=\text{"s"} \Rightarrow \text{mismatch}. \quad D[1,1] = 1 + \min(0,1,1) = 1
$$

$$
D[1,2]: T[1]=\text{"k"},\ P[2]=\text{"i"} \Rightarrow \text{mismatch}. \quad D[1,2] = 1 + \min(1,2,1) = 2
$$

(Continue similarly for all cells.)

### Step 3: Complete the Filled Matrix

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccccccc}
 & \epsilon & s & i & t & t & i & n & g \\
\hline
\epsilon & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 7 \\
k & 1 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & 7 \\
i & 2 & 2 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
t & 3 & 3 & 2 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\
t & 4 & 4 & 3 & 2 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\
e & 5 & 5 & 4 & 3 & 2 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\
n & 6 & 6 & 5 & 4 & 3 & 3 & 2 & 3
\end{array}
$$

### Step 4: Read Answer

$$
D[\text{"kitten"},\ \text{"sitting"}] = D[6,7] = 3
$$

This matches the classical result: substitute **k→s**, substitute **e→i**, insert **g** at the end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trace-back rule:** From $D[i,j]$, arrow points back to whichever neighbour gave the minimum. Substitutions and matches go diagonally; insertions go **up**; deletions go **left**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Multiple Alignment by Star Alignment

**Problem:** Align the three web URL sequences (treated as strings):

$$
S_1 = \text{"abracadabra"},\quad S_2 = \text{"abracababra"},\quad S_3 = \text{"abracadabara"}
$$

Use match score $= +2$, mismatch $= -1$, gap $= -2$.

### Step 1: Compute Pairwise Scores

Using Needleman-Wunsch on all three pairs (or trusting instructor-given values):

$$
\text{score}(S_1, S_2) = 18,\quad \text{score}(S_1, S_3) = 19,\quad \text{score}(S_2, S_3) = 16
$$

### Step 2: Choose Centre Sequence

Average scores for each:

$$
\text{avg}(S_1) = \frac{18 + 19}{2} = 18.5, \quad \text{avg}(S_2) = \frac{17}{2} = 17, \quad \text{avg}(S_3) = \frac{17.5}{2} = 17.5
$$

Centre $S_c = S_1 = \text{"abracadabra"}$.

### Step 3: Align Each Sequence to $S_1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 & : \text{a b r a c a d a b r a} \\
S_2 & : \text{a b r a c a - b r a -} \\
S_3 & : \text{a b r a c a d a b a r a}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Merge Alignments into Profile

Insert gaps consistently across all rows based on $S_1$'s column positions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 & : \text{a b r a c a d a b r a} \\
S_2 & : \text{a b r a c a - b r a -} \\
S_3 & : \text{a b r a c a d a b a r a}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Sum-of-Pairs Score

Only the inserted gap columns and any mismatches count:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP} &= \sum_{i<j} \sum_{l} s(S'_i[l], S'_j[l]) \\
&= \text{matches}(20) \cdot 2 + \text{mismatches}(1) \cdot (-1) + \text{gap pairs}(2) \cdot (-2) \\
&= 40 - 1 - 4 = 35
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This heuristic **does not guarantee** the globally optimal MSA, but it scales to thousands of sequences and is the basis of tools like ClustalW.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Simple Tree Matching on DOM Subtrees

**Problem:** Match the two DOM sub-trees (representing a product listing block from two templated e-commerce sites):

```
Tree T1:                  Tree T2:
    div                      section
   / | \                    /  |  \
  h1 ul span              h1  ul  aside
    /  \                  / \  \
   li   li               li  li span
   |    |
   "A"  "B"              "A"  "B"  "ad"
```

A valid tree matching pairs nodes preserving ancestor order:

$$
M = \{(div, section),\ (h1, h1),\ (ul, ul),\ (li, li),\ (li, li)\}
$$

Tree edit distance between $T_1$ and $T_2$ is **2** (delete `span` from $T_1$, delete `aside` from $T_2$).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Criterion | Brute Force | KMP | Rabin-Karp | Edit Distance (Approx.) | Tree Edit (Zhang-Shasha) | Star MSA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Exact / Approx.** | Exact | Exact | Exact | Approximate | Approximate | Approximate |
| **Time** | $O(mn)$ | $O(m+n)$ | $O(m+n)$ avg | $O(mn)$ | $O(\vert T_1\vert \vert T_2\vert d^2)$ | $O(k^2 L^2)$ |
| **Space** | $O(1)$ | $O(m)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(mn)$ | $O(\vert T_1\vert \vert T_2\vert)$ | $O(kL^2)$ |
| **Pre-processing** | None | $O(m)$ failure | $O(m)$ hash | None | None | $O(k^2)$ pairwise |
| **Best Use Case** | Short patterns | Long patterns in long text | Multi-pattern / plagiarism | Noisy OCR, fuzzy search | HTML wrapper comparison | Multiple web template alignment |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Deterministic | Probabilistic (expected) | Deterministic | Deterministic | Heuristic |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 KMP Failure Function Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: j = 0, k = 0] --> B[j less than m?]
    B -- No --> Z[Return pi table]
    B -- Yes --> C{k less than j and P k not equal P j}
    C -- Yes --> D[k = pi k minus 1]
    D --> C
    C -- No --> E{P k equals P j}
    E -- Yes --> F[k = k + 1]
    F --> G[pi j = k]
    E -- No --> H[pi j = 0]
    G --> I[j = j + 1]
    H --> I
    I --> B
```

## 4.2 Edit Distance DP Matrix — Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Start: i = 0, j = 0]) --> Init[Initialise row 0 and column 0]
    Init --> Loop1[For i from 1 to n]
    Loop1 --> Loop2[For j from 1 to m]
    Loop2 --> Check{Ti equals Tj?}
    Check -- Yes --> Diag[D i,j = D i-1, j-1]
    Check -- No --> MinCalc[D i,j = 1 + min of 3 neighbours]
    Diag --> Cont
    MinCalc --> Cont[Continue]
    Cont --> Loop2
    Loop2 --> Done[Return D n,m as edit distance]
    Done --> End([End])
```

## 4.3 Star Multiple Alignment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph StageA[Stage 1: Pairwise Scoring]
        S1[S1] --- P12[Pairwise NW score S1 S2]
        S1 --- P13[Pairwise NW score S1 S3]
        S2 --- P23[Pairwise NW score S2 S3]
        S3[Sn] --- P1n[Pairwise NW score S1 Sn]
        S3 --- P3n[Pairwise NW score Sn S3]
    end
    StageA --> Centre[Stage 2: Pick Centre Sequence Sc]
    Centre --> Merge[Stage 3: Merge all sequences to Sc]
    Merge --> Output[Stage 4: Multiple Alignment output]
```

## 4.4 Tree Edit Distance Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputStage[Input]
        T1Input[Tree T1]
        T2Input[Tree T2]
    end
    InputStage --> LRKey[LR Key Computation Post order Traversal]
    LRKey --> ForestDP[Forest Edit DP F arrays]
    ForestDP --> Decompose[Decompose T1 into forests]
    ForestDP --> Decompose2[Decompose T2 into forests]
    Decompose --> CostMatrix[Cost Matrix F i to i prime j to j prime]
    Decompose2 --> CostMatrix
    CostMatrix --> Traceback[Traceback for edit script]
    Traceback --> Result[Tree Edit Distance Result]
```

## 4.5 High-Level Matching Pipeline in Web Mining

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Page[Web Page HTML] --> Parse[DOM Parser]
    Parse --> Tree[DOM Tree]
    Tree --> TreeMatch[Tree Matcher Zhang Shasha]
    TreeMatch --> Compare{Similar to Template?}
    Compare -- Yes --> Extract[Extract Data Record]
    Compare -- No --> MSA[Multiple Sequence Alignment on URL Strings]
    MSA --> Cluster[Cluster Duplicate Pages]
    Cluster --> Index[Search Engine Index]
    Extract --> DB[(Structured Database)]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the failure function used in the Knuth-Morris-Pratt (KMP) algorithm with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The failure function $\pi[j]$ of a pattern $P$ is the length of the **longest proper prefix** of $P[0..j]$ that is also a **suffix** of $P[0..j]$.

$$
\pi[j] = \max \, \{ \, k \mid 0 \le k < j,\ P[0..k-1] = P[j-k+1..j] \, \}
$$

It allows KMP to skip previously matched characters on a mismatch instead of re-comparing them.

**Example:** For $P = \text{"ababaca"}$, $\pi = [\,0,\ 0,\ 1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 0,\ 1\,]$.

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for example.)

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define edit distance. How is it used for approximate string matching in web mining?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Edit distance** (or Levenshtein distance) between two strings $S_1$ and $S_2$ is the **minimum number** of single-character insertions, deletions, or substitutions required to transform $S_1$ into $S_2$.

It is computed via the recurrence relation:

$$
D[i,j] = \begin{cases} i, & j = 0 \\ j, & i = 0 \\ D[i-1,j-1], & S_1[i] = S_2[j] \\ 1 + \min(D[i-1,j],\, D[i,j-1],\, D[i-1,j-1]), & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

In web mining, edit distance is used for **fuzzy URL matching**, **near-duplicate page detection**, and **plagiarism analysis**, where exact matches are rare due to dynamic content.

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for recurrence, 1 mark for web-mining application.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always **draw the DP matrix** — even a partial one earns 2 marks.
> - When computing $\pi$, **show the fallback step** explicitly; skipping the fallback loses a full mark.
> - For multiple alignment, **define the scoring function** before computing the answer.
> - Many students confuse **tree edit distance** with **subtree isomorphism** — they are different problems.
> - Always state **time and space complexity** in any algorithmic answer — KTU awards a separate mark for this.

---

### Question B-A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Compute the **edit distance** between $T = \text{"WEB"}$ and $P = \text{"MINING"}$ using dynamic programming. Show the full DP matrix and trace-back the optimal alignment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Sum-of-Pairs (SP) score** for multiple sequence alignment. Given three aligned sequences of length $L = 4$, determine the SP score when match $= +1$, mismatch $= -1$, and gap penalty $g = -2$:

```
S1: A T - G
S2: A - C G
S3: T T C G
```
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-A (a)

**Step 1 — Initialise the DP matrix of size $(n+1) \times (m+1) = 4 \times 7$:**

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
 & \epsilon & M & I & N & I & N & G \\
\hline
\epsilon & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
W & 1 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
E & 2 & 2 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
B & 3 & 3 & 3 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6
\end{array}
$$

**Step 2 — Explain filling rule:**

For each cell $D[i,j]$:
- If $T[i] = P[j]$: $D[i,j] = D[i-1, j-1]$ (diagonal copy).
- Else: $D[i,j] = 1 + \min(D[i-1,j],\ D[i,j-1],\ D[i-1,j-1])$.

**Step 3 — Read answer:**

$$
D[3,6] = 6
$$

**Step 4 — Trace-back the optimal alignment:**

```
W E B - - - -
- - - M I N G
```

The **edit distance is 6** — this requires deleting W, E, B (3 deletions) and inserting M, I, N, I, N, G (but since we're aligning different lengths, the optimal uses substitutions and gaps).

**Time Complexity:** $O(mn) = O(21)$. **Space Complexity:** $O(mn)$.

---

#### Model Solution to B-A (b)

**Sum-of-Pairs (SP) Score — Definition (3 Marks):**

The SP score sums the **pairwise column scores** over all pairs of aligned sequences:

$$
SP = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{k} \sum_{l=1}^{L} s(S'_i[l],\, S'_j[l])
$$

**Pairwise combinations (3 pairs, so 3 sums):**

**Step 1 — Pair (S1, S2):**

Column-by-column scoring (match $= +1$, mismatch $= -1$, gap $= -2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Col 1 (A, A)} &: +1 \\
\text{Col 2 (T, -)} &: -2 \\
\text{Col 3 (-, C)} &: -2 \\
\text{Col 4 (G, G)} &: +1 \\
\hline
\text{SP}(S_1, S_2) &= +1 - 2 - 2 + 1 = -2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Pair (S1, S3):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Col 1 (A, T)} &: -1 \\
\text{Col 2 (T, T)} &: +1 \\
\text{Col 3 (-, C)} &: -2 \\
\text{Col 4 (G, G)} &: +1 \\
\hline
\text{SP}(S_1, S_3) &= -1 + 1 - 2 + 1 = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pair (S2, S3):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Col 1 (A, T)} &: -1 \\
\text{Col 2 (-, T)} &: -2 \\
\text{Col 3 (C, C)} &: +1 \\
\text{Col 4 (G, G)} &: +1 \\
\hline
\text{SP}(S_2, S_3) &= -1 - 2 + 1 + 1 = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total SP Score:**

$$
\boxed{SP = -2 + (-1) + (-1) = -4}
$$

This score is **negative** because of the heavy gap penalties, indicating a low-quality alignment.

---

### Question B-B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **Rabin-Karp string matching algorithm** using rolling hash. State its time complexity and list two advantages over brute force. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Tree Matching** in the context of web data extraction. How does the **MDR (Mining Data Records)** algorithm use tree matching to identify data regions in a web page? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B-B (a)

**Step 1 — Concept (2 Marks):**

Rabin-Karp uses **hashing** to compare the pattern $P$ of length $m$ with every substring of $T$ of length $m$. Instead of comparing characters one-by-one (as in brute force), it computes a **hash value** for the pattern and a rolling hash for each text window, comparing only the integers.

**Step 2 — Hash Function (2 Marks):**

Treating characters as digits in base $d$:

$$
h(T[i..i+m-1]) = \left( \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} T[i+j] \cdot d^{\,m-1-j} \right) \bmod q
$$

where $q$ is a large prime. The **rolling update** is:

$$
h_{i+1} = \bigl( d \cdot (h_i - T[i] \cdot d^{m-1}) + T[i+m] \bigr) \bmod q
$$

This update runs in **$O(1)$** time per slide.

**Step 3 — Algorithm Steps (2 Marks):**

1. Compute $h(P) \bmod q$ once — $O(m)$.
2. Compute hash of first window $T[0..m-1]$ — $O(m)$.
3. For each slide $i = 0$ to $n-m$:
   - If $h(P) = h(T[i..i+m-1])$, perform **explicit verification** ($O(m)$) to avoid hash collisions.
   - Else, slide and update hash in $O(1)$.

**Step 4 — Complexity and Advantages (1 Mark):**

- **Time complexity:** $O(m + n)$ expected, $O(mn)$ worst case (many spurious hash matches).
- **Space complexity:** $O(1)$.
- **Advantages:** (i) Excellent for **multi-pattern search** (compute hash of every pattern, scan text once); (ii) Easily generalises to **2D pattern matching** (e.g., image plagiarism).

---

#### Model Solution to B-B (b)

**Step 1 — Tree Matching Concept (2 Marks):**

A web page can be represented as a **DOM (Document Object Model) tree**, where HTML tags are internal nodes and text/attributes are leaves. **Tree matching** finds structurally similar subtrees across pages — the foundation of template detection and data extraction.

**Step 2 — Tree Edit Distance (2 Marks):**

Given two trees $T_1, T_2$, the **tree edit distance** is the minimum cost sequence of **node insertions, deletions, and relabellings** that transforms $T_1$ into $T_2$. The Zhang-Shasha algorithm computes this in $O(\vert T_1 \vert \cdot \vert T_2 \vert \cdot \min(\text{depth}_1, \text{depth}_2)^2)$.

**Step 3 — MDR Algorithm Overview (2 Marks):**

The **Mining Data Records (MDR)** algorithm by Liu, Grossman, and Zhai (2003) extracts data records from a single result page:

1. **Build the DOM tree** of the page.
2. Identify **contiguous, similar subtrees** under a common parent — these are candidate data records.
3. Use a **string matching** technique to compare sibling subtrees and a tree-matching threshold to group them.
4. Extract text/links from each record in the group.

**Step 4 — Role of Tree Matching (1 Mark):**

Tree matching quantifies the **structural similarity** between sibling subtrees. A high similarity (low edit distance) implies the siblings represent **repeated records** (e.g., a list of products), which the algorithm then aligns and extracts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorise Before Exam**

- **Exact String Matching** aims to find a pattern $P$ in text $T$ character-by-character — complexity $O(mn)$ for brute force.
- **KMP** uses the **failure function $\pi$** to skip redundant comparisons — guaranteed $O(m + n)$.
- **Rabin-Karp** uses **rolling hash** with modulus $q$ — best for multi-pattern search.
- **Boyer-Moore** skips characters using the **bad-character** and **good-suffix** heuristics — sublinear average case.
- **Edit distance** $D[i,j]$ uses three operations: insertion, deletion, substitution — solved via 2D dynamic programming in $O(mn)$.
- **Affine gap penalties** model biologically realistic gaps: open cost $o$, extend cost $e$, formula $\gamma(g) = o + (g-1)e$.
- **Tree Edit Distance (Zhang-Shasha)** uses **post-order LR-key decomposition** — applied to DOM trees for wrapper extraction.
- **Tree matching ≠ subtree isomorphism** — the former maps structurally similar trees; the latter asks if $T_1$ appears exactly inside $T_2$.
- **Multiple Alignment (MSA)** scores use **Sum-of-Pairs (SP)** — exact optimisation is NP-hard for $k \ge 3$.
- **Star Alignment** heuristic: pick the sequence with **maximum average similarity** as the centre; align all others to it.
- **Progressive alignment** (ClustalW) builds a **guide tree** and progressively aligns profiles.
- **Hamming distance** applies only to strings of **equal length** — useful for fixed-format URL checks.
- **Web Mining Applications:** duplicate-page detection, wrapper induction, MDR data-record mining, URL clustering.
- **Always quote time and space complexity** in algorithmic answers — KTU awards dedicated marks.
- **For 14-mark questions**, draw the **DP matrix** or **tree diagram** even if partial — visual aids earn easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building DOM Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Building DOM Trees # 📘 Module 3: Building DOM Trees — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuition ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Document Object Model (DOM) Tree** is a **W3C-standardized**, platform- and language-independent application programming interface (API) that represents any **HTML** or **XML** document as a hierarchical **tree structure** where each node corresponds to a part of the document (element, attribute, text, comment, document, etc.). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECDT635 – Web Mining, Module 3):** > A *DOM Tree* is a parsed, in-memory, object-oriented representation of a web page in which the root node denotes the **Document** object, internal nodes represent **HTML elements (tags)**, and leaf nodes represent **text content, attributes, and comments*. It is the foundational data structure used in **Web Content Mining** to extract features, apply wrappers, perform tree-based matching (Tree Edit Distance, tree kernels), and execute **XPath/XQuery**-based information extraction. Formally, a DOM Tree $T$ can be defined as a rooted, ordered, labeled tree: $$ T = (V, E, \Sigma, L, v_{root}) $$ Where: - $V$ = set of vertices (nodes) - $E \subseteq V \times V$ = set of parent–child edges - $\Sigma$ = alphabet of valid tag/attribute/text labels - $L : V \rightarrow \Sigma$ = labeling function - $v_{root}$ = the document (root) node The tree is **ordered** because the sequence of children of any node preserves the document's source order — a property critical for web mining tasks such as data record extraction. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree" of a Web Page Imagine a web page as a **multi-generation family photograph**: - The **patriarch** at the top is the `` element (the Document root). - His **children** are `` and `` (the major sections). - **Grandchildren** are ``, `<div>`, `<p>`, `<table>`, etc. - **Great-grandchildren** are the text, links, and images nested inside. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** > Building a DOM tree is exactly like building a *genealogical family tree* from a paragraph of text. A **parser** reads the HTML source code line by line. Every time it sees an **opening tag** like `<div>`, it *creates a new branch* under the current parent. Every time it sees a **closing tag** like `</div>`, it *climbs back up* to the parent. **Text between tags** becomes a *leaf* hanging from the current branch. This hierarchical representation is what allows web miners to: 1. **Locate** data records on a page (e.g., product listings). 2. **Compare** the structure of two web pages to detect templates. 3. **Extract** values via XPath expressions such as `/html/body/div[2]/table/tr[3]/td[1]`. --- ## 1.3 Why DOM Trees Matter in Web Mining (KTU 2024 Context) | Web Mining Sub-Area | Role of DOM Tree | |---|---| | **Web Content Mining** | Structural source for feature extraction, wrapper induction | | **Web Structure Mining** | Comparison of page templates via tree-edit distance | | **Web Usage Mining** | DOM events serve as clickstream signals | | **Information Extraction** | XPath, XSLT, and CSS-selector queries operate on the tree | > [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway for KTU Exams:** Whenever a question asks *"How does a crawler or wrapper understand a web page?"*, the correct KTU-level answer is: **"By parsing the raw HTML into a DOM Tree and traversing it."** <!-- SECTION_1_END --> <!-- SECTION_2_START --> # 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of DOM Tree Construction The construction of a DOM tree from raw HTML proceeds through **four sequential phases**: ### Phase 1 — **Tokenization (Lexical Analysis)** The raw HTML byte stream is split into discrete **tokens**: - **Start-tag tokens** — e.g., `<div class="row">` - **End-tag tokens** — e.g., `</div>` - **Self-closing tags** — e.g., `<br/>`, `<img .../>` - **Text tokens** — character data between tags - **Comment tokens** — `<!-- ... -->` - **DOCTYPE / Document-type tokens** ### Phase 2 — **Tree Construction (Syntactic Analysis)** A state machine consumes tokens and emits DOM nodes. The critical states are: 1. **Initial State** — before any tag is seen. 2. **Before HTML State** — DOCTYPE and `<html>` handling. 3. **In Head / In Body / In Table** — context-specific insertion rules. 4. **After Body State** — terminating markers like `</html>`. ### Phase 3 — **Error Recovery & Implicit Tag Insertion** Unlike XML (strict), HTML allows missing closing tags. The parser applies **adoption agency algorithm** and **Foster Parenting** rules to recover gracefully and produce a well-formed DOM. ### Phase 4 — **Script Execution & Mutation** JavaScript embedded in `<script>` tags may modify the live DOM (relevant for dynamic content mining). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" of the Stack-Based Insertion:** > The HTML parser uses an **explicit stack** called the *open elements stack*. Every start-tag pushes its corresponding element node onto the stack as a child of the current top. Every end-tag pops nodes until the matching one is found. This is the **"Why"** behind correct nesting. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Since DOM Trees are an *information-structure* topic, the "formulas" are **algorithmic properties and complexity bounds** rather than physical equations. These are the high-yield items KTU examiners test. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Time to build DOM} &\;\approx\; O(n) \quad \text{(linear in document size } n \text{ bytes)} \\[4pt] \text{Time to traverse DOM (DFS)} &\;=\; O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \quad \text{(visits every node and edge once)} \\[4pt] \text{Depth of an element } v &\;=\; \text{number of ancestors of } v \text{ up to } v_{root} \\[4pt] \text{Subtree size } \vert T_v \vert &\;=\; 1 + \sum_{c \in \text{children}(v)} \vert T_c \vert \\[4pt] \text{Path from root to node } v &\;=\; \langle v_{root}, v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k = v \rangle \end{aligned} $$ ### 📊 Master Properties Table (High-Yield for KTU) | Property | Symbol / Expression | Meaning / Engineering Utility | |---|---|---| | Root Node | $v_{root}$ | `<html>` element; the entry-point of any XPath query | | Children of $v$ | $children(v)$ | Ordered list preserving source sequence | | Parent of $v$ | $parent(v)$ | Used for backtracking tree-edit distance | | Ancestors of $v$ | $anc(v)$ | Set of nodes on the path to $v_{root}$ | | Siblings of $v$ | $sib(v)$ | Used in **vision-based data records extraction** | | Node Label | $L(v)$ | Tag name + attributes + text content | | Tree Height | $h(T) = \max_v depth(v)$ | Predicts XPath path-length features | | Node Degree | $deg(v) = \vert children(v) \vert$ | Useful in detecting list-style templates | > [!TIP] > **Critical Pitfall to Avoid:** > Never confuse a *DOM Tree* with an *HTML string*. A string is **linear**; a tree is **hierarchical and navigable**. KTU questions often ask this as a 3-mark conceptual. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Web Wrappers (Wrapper Induction)** — algorithms like **WIEN**, **STALKER**, and **SoftMealy** operate by inducing rules over DOM tree paths. 2. **Tree Edit Distance (TED)** — used to detect near-duplicate pages and template regions: $$ TED(T_1, T_2) = \min \{ \text{cost of sequence of edit operations transforming } T_1 \text{ into } T_2 \} $$ 3. **Vision-based Page Segmentation (VIPS)** — leverages DOM depth and visual cues to segment a page into semantic blocks. 4. **Search Engine Indexing** — Google, Bing, and KTU-research prototypes use DOM-based feature extraction to identify headings, articles, and navigation. > [!NOTE] > The edit operations in TED are: **insertion**, **deletion**, and **substitution** of nodes. The Zhang–Shasha algorithm computes TED in $O(\vert V_1 \vert \cdot \vert V_2 \vert \cdot (\deg_{max})^2)$ time. <!-- SECTION_2_END --> <!-- SECTION_3_START --> # 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Parsing Logic & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a DOM Tree from Raw HTML **Given HTML Document:** ```html <html> <head> <title>CSE Dept

Welcome

Hello KTU students

``` ### Step-by-Step Tokenization Output | # | Token Type | Token Value | |---|---|---| | 1 | Start-tag | `` | | 2 | Start-tag | `` | | 3 | Start-tag | `` | | 4 | Text | `CSE Dept` | | 5 | End-tag | `` | | 6 | End-tag | `` | | 7 | Start-tag | `` | | 8 | Start-tag | `

` | | 9 | Text | `Welcome` | | 10 | End-tag | `

` | | 11 | Start-tag | `

` | | 12 | Text | `Hello ` | | 13 | Start-tag | `` | | 14 | Text | `KTU` | | 15 | End-tag | `` | | 16 | Text | ` students` | | 17 | End-tag | `

` | | 18 | End-tag | `` | | 19 | End-tag | `` | ### Step-by-Step Tree Construction (with Open-Elements Stack) We maintain a **stack $S$** where $top(S)$ is the *current parent*. The algorithm: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{On Start-tag } t \;&:\;\; \text{create node } v = \text{Element}(t),\;\; parent(v) \leftarrow top(S),\;\; \text{push } v \text{ onto } S \\[4pt] \text{On Text } t \;&:\;\; \text{create node } v = \text{TextNode}(t),\;\; parent(v) \leftarrow top(S) \\[4pt] \text{On End-tag } t \;&:\;\; \text{while } L(top(S)) \neq t : \text{pop } S,\;\; \text{pop } S \end{aligned} $$ #### Trace Execution | Step | Token | Stack State (top → bottom) | Action | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `` | `[html]` | Push `` | | 2 | `` | `[head, html]` | Push `` | | 3 | `` | `[title, head, html]` | Push `<title>` | | 4 | `CSE Dept` | `[title, head, html]` | Attach text child to `<title>` | | 5 | `` | `[head, html]` | Pop `title` | | 6 | `` | `[html]` | Pop `head` | | 7 | `` | `[body, html]` | Push `` | | 8 | `

` | `[h1, body, html]` | Push `

` | | 9 | `Welcome` | `[h1, body, html]` | Attach text child to `

` | | 10 | `

` | `[body, html]` | Pop `h1` | | 11 | `

` | `[p, body, html]` | Push `

` with attribute `id="main"` | | 12 | `Hello ` | `[p, body, html]` | Attach text to `

` | | 13 | `` | `[b, p, body, html]` | Push `` | | 14 | `KTU` | `[b, p, body, html]` | Attach text to `` | | 15 | `` | `[p, body, html]` | Pop `b` | | 16 | ` students` | `[p, body, html]` | Attach text to `

` | | 17 | `

` | `[body, html]` | Pop `p` | | 18 | `` | `[html]` | Pop `body` | | 19 | `` | `[ ]` | Pop `html`. Done. | ### Final DOM Tree (in nested notation) $$ \begin{aligned} T \;=\; \text{html} \big(\; & \text{head}\big(\; \text{title}\big(\text{"CSE Dept"}\big) \;\big), \\[2pt] & \text{body}\big(\; \text{h1}\big(\text{"Welcome"}\big),\; \text{p}\big(\; \text{"Hello "},\; \text{b}\big(\text{"KTU"}\big),\; \text{" students"} \;\big) \;\big) \;\big) \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Tree-Edit Distance Derivation To compute similarity between two DOM trees $T_1$ and $T_2$ (e.g., two product pages), KTU examiners expect knowledge of the **recursive decomposition**: $$ \begin{aligned} TED(T_1[\,i\,],\, T_2[\,j\,]) \;=\; \min \Big\{ & TED(T_1[\,i-1\,],\, T_2[\,j\,]) + \text{del-cost}\big(L_1(v_i)\big), \\[4pt] & TED(T_1[\,i\,],\, T_2[\,j-1\,]) + \text{ins-cost}\big(L_2(v_j)\big), \\[4pt] & TED(T_1[\,i-1\,],\, T_2[\,j-1\,]) \;+\; \delta\big(L_1(v_i), L_2(v_j)\big) \Big\} \end{aligned} $$ Where the substitution cost is: $$ \delta(a, b) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } a = b \quad (\text{labels match}) \\[4pt] 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Interpretation:** - **Line 1** — *Delete* node $v_i$ from $T_1$. - **Line 2** — *Insert* node $v_j$ into $T_2$. - **Line 3** — *Substitute* $v_i$ with $v_j$ (free if labels match). This dynamic-programming formulation is a **standard KTU 14-mark question** in web mining modules that combine structure mining with content extraction. --- ## 3.3 XPath Query Resolution on the DOM Tree A KTU-favourite question is: *"Given this DOM tree, evaluate the XPath."* **Example XPath:** `/html/body/p[@id="main"]/b/text()` **Step-by-step resolution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Root } \rightarrow \text{html} \\[2pt] \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{html} \rightarrow \text{body (only child element)} \\[2pt] \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{body} \rightarrow \text{p with attribute } id = "main" \\[2pt] \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{p} \rightarrow \text{b} \\[2pt] \text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{b} \rightarrow \text{text()} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{returns the text node "KTU"} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Final Answer:** The XPath `/html/body/p[@id="main"]/b/text()` evaluates to the string literal **"KTU"**. # 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Final DOM Tree from Worked Example ```mermaid graph TD n_html["html"] n_head["head"] n_title["title"] n_body["body"] n_h1["h1"] n_p["p id main"] n_b["b"] n_t1["Text CSE Dept"] n_t2["Text Welcome"] n_t3["Text Hello"] n_t4["Text KTU"] n_t5["Text students"] n_html --> n_head n_html --> n_body n_head --> n_title n_title --> n_t1 n_body --> n_h1 n_body --> n_p n_h1 --> n_t2 n_p --> n_t3 n_p --> n_b n_p --> n_t5 n_b --> n_t4 ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — End-to-End DOM Construction Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Raw HTML Source"] --> B["Tokenizer Lexical Analysis"] B --> C["Token Stream Start End Text Comment"] C --> D["Tree Constructor State Machine"] D --> E["Open Elements Stack"] D --> F["Adoption Agency Algorithm"] E --> G["DOM Tree In Memory"] F --> G G --> H["Attribute and Style Resolution"] H --> I["Script Execution if any"] I --> J["Live DOM Tree"] J --> K["XPath CSS Selector Queries"] J --> L["Tree Edit Distance Wrapper Induction"] J --> M["Web Mining Feature Extraction"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Web Mining Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR F1["Web Crawler Fetches URL"] --> F2["HTTP Response HTML"] F2 --> F3["HTML Parser Builds DOM Tree"] F3 --> F4["DOM Tree In Memory Representation"] F4 --> F5["Tree Based Mining Algorithms"] F5 --> F6["Wrapper Induction and XPath Extraction"] F5 --> F7["Tree Edit Distance Comparison"] F5 --> F8["Vision Based Segmentation VIPS"] F6 --> F9["Structured Data Records"] F7 --> F10["Template Detection"] F8 --> F11["Semantic Page Blocks"] F9 --> F12["Knowledge Base or Data Warehouse"] F10 --> F12 F11 --> F12 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Architecture:** The DOM Tree is the **central pivot** of nearly every web content and structure mining pipeline. KTU examiners love diagrams like these; a labelled flowchart alone can secure 3–4 marks in a 14-mark question. # 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define a DOM Tree. Why is it preferred over a linear string representation in web mining?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **DOM (Document Object Model) Tree** is a hierarchical, object-oriented, in-memory representation of an HTML/XML document standardized by the W3C. Each node represents a part of the document — elements, attributes, text, or comments — connected by parent–child relationships rooted at the Document node. It is preferred over a linear string in web mining because: 1. It preserves **structural and semantic relationships** between elements. 2. It enables **XPath, CSS selectors, and tree-traversal algorithms** (DFS/BFS) for targeted extraction. 3. It supports **tree-based similarity measures** (Tree Edit Distance) for template detection and near-duplicate identification. *(1 mark each)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **List any six types of nodes that can appear in a DOM Tree.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The six common node types are: 1. **Document Node** — the root, representing the entire document. 2. **Element Node** — represents HTML tags (e.g., `
`, `

`). 3. **Attribute Node** — represents attributes of elements (e.g., `class="row"`). 4. **Text Node** — contains the character data inside elements. 5. **Comment Node** — contains `` content. 6. **DocumentType Node** — represents the `` declaration. *(½ mark each)* --- ## 📝 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]** **(a)** Explain the step-by-step process of building a DOM Tree from raw HTML source. Describe the role of the *Open Elements Stack* and the *Tokenizer*. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given the HTML snippet below, draw the resulting DOM Tree and evaluate the XPath query: `/html/body/div[1]/ul/li[2]/a/@href` **(7 marks)** ```html

  • Home
  • Courses
  • Contact
``` #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** The DOM construction process involves **four sequential stages** *(2 marks for listing, 5 marks for explanation)*: **Stage 1 — Tokenization (Lexical Analysis):** The raw HTML byte stream is fed to a tokenizer that emits a stream of tokens. Tokens are categorized as: *Start-tag, End-tag, Self-closing tag, Character (text), Comment, DOCTYPE*. Each start-tag token carries the tag name and an ordered list of attribute name-value pairs. *(1 mark)* **Stage 2 — Tree Construction (Syntactic Analysis):** The token stream is consumed by a state machine that builds the DOM incrementally. The state machine maintains context (e.g., "in body", "in table") to apply HTML5-specific insertion rules. *(1 mark)* **Stage 3 — Open Elements Stack:** A crucial LIFO stack holds currently open elements. *top(S)* denotes the **current insertion location** (the parent for the next node).
