The algorithm follows these rules: *(2 marks)* $$ \begin{aligned} \text{On Start-tag } t \;&:\;\; \text{create node } v, parent(v) \leftarrow top(S), push(v, S) \\[4pt] \text{On End-tag } t \;&:\;\; \text{while } L(top(S)) \neq t : pop(S);\; pop(S) \\[4pt] \text{On Text } t \;&:\;\; \text{create TextNode } v, parent(v) \leftarrow top(S) \end{aligned} $$ **Stage 4 — Error Recovery:** HTML5 parsers use the *Adoption Agency Algorithm* and *Foster Parenting* to handle mismatched tags gracefully, producing a well-formed DOM even from malformed input — important for mining noisy real-world pages. *(1 mark)* #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Tokenize and build the tree** *(3 marks for the diagram)* The DOM Tree structure is: - `html` (root) - `body` - `div class="menu"` - `ul` - `li` → `a href="home.html"` → TextNode "Home" - `li` → `a href="courses.html"` → TextNode "Courses" - `li` → `a href="contact.html"` → TextNode "Contact" **Step 2 — Evaluate the XPath** *(2 marks for stepwise evaluation)* $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Path so far} &\;:\;\; \text{html} \rightarrow \text{body} \rightarrow \text{div}[1] \rightarrow \text{ul} \rightarrow \text{li}[2] \rightarrow \text{a} \rightarrow \text{@href} \end{aligned} $$ - `/html` → root element - `/body` → only child of html - `/div[1]` → first (and only) `
` child of body - `/ul` → only child of div - `/li[2]` → **second** `
  • ` (1-indexed) under ul - `/a` → the `` element inside that `
  • ` - `/@href` → the `href` **attribute node** of that `` **Step 3 — Final Answer** *(2 marks)* $$ \text{Result of XPath} \;=\; \boxed{"courses.html"} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** Discuss the role of DOM Trees in **Wrapper Induction** and **Tree Edit Distance-based similarity detection** for web content mining. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two web pages $P_1$ and $P_2$ have the following DOM Tree structures. Compute the **Tree Edit Distance** using the Zhang–Shasha algorithm (or equivalent DP). Assume insertion = deletion = substitution = 1. **(7 marks)** ``` T1: html (body (div (p "Hello"), p "World")) T2: html (body (div (p "Hello"), span "World")) ``` #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Wrapper Induction (3 marks):** Wrappers are programs that extract structured data from semi-structured web pages. **Wrapper Induction** automatically learns extraction rules by generalizing from labelled examples. DOM Trees provide the structural skeleton over which induction algorithms operate: - **STALKER** uses hierarchical landmark trees to extract nested data. - **WIEN** induces k-grams over tree paths. - **SoftMealy** uses finite-state transducers over DOM paths. **Tree Edit Distance (TED) (3 marks):** TED measures the minimum-cost sequence of edit operations (insert, delete, substitute) needed to transform $T_1$ into $T_2$. It is central to: - **Template detection** in deep-web crawlers. - **Near-duplicate page detection** for search engines. - **Change detection** between successive crawls of a website. **Mathematical Formulation (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} TED(T_1, T_2) = \min \big\{ & TED(T_1 - v, T_2) + \text{del}(v), \\ & TED(T_1, T_2 - w) + \text{ins}(w), \\ & TED(T_1 - v, T_2 - w) + \delta(L(v), L(w)) \big\} \end{aligned} $$ #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Identify the differing sub-trees** *(2 marks)* In $T_1$ the second leaf is `p "World"`. In $T_2$ the second leaf is `span "World"`. The parent context is identical. **Step 2 — Build the DP table** *(3 marks)* Let $A = $ subtree rooted at the differing branch in $T_1$ = `(p "World")` and $B = $ subtree rooted at differing branch in $T_2$ = `(span "World")`. Post-order traversals give node sequences: - $A$: $[p, W1]$ - $B$: $[span, W2]$ Build $2 \times 2$ DP matrix $D[i][j]$: $$ D[0][0] = 0, \quad D[i][0] = i \cdot 1, \quad D[0][j] = j \cdot 1 $$ $$ D[1][1] = \min\big\{ D[0][1] + 1,\; D[1][0] + 1,\; D[0][0] + \delta(p, span) \big\} $$ Since $L(p) \neq L(span)$, $\delta = 1$, so $D[1][1] = \min\{2, 2, 1\} = 1$. **Step 3 — Extend to the text leaves** *(1 mark)* Since both leaves contain `"World"`, their substitution cost is $0$. So $D[2][2] = D[1][1] + 0 = 1$. **Step 4 — Final Answer** *(1 mark)* $$ TED(T_1, T_2) = \boxed{1} $$ *Interpretation:* Only **one substitution** (p → span) is required, with text labels matching for free. The two pages share a high structural similarity, useful for template clustering. --- > [!WARNING] > 🚨 **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the Stack Rule:** Many students write that on a closing tag, the parser "goes to the parent." This is incomplete — the parser must **pop until the matching tag is found**, otherwise nested mismatches are scored wrong. > 2. **XPath Indexing Mistake:** XPath uses **1-based indexing** (`li[2]` is the second child, not the third). Off-by-one errors are the most common 1-mark deduction. > 3. **Tree Edit Distance Definition:** Students often quote TED as a single number without specifying the **edit operation costs**. KTU 14-mark answers always require the cost vector to be stated. > 4. **Conflating DOM with HTML:** Writing "DOM is the HTML code" costs 1 mark. The DOM is the **parsed tree representation** of HTML, not the source code itself. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density, Exam-Ready):** - ✅ **DOM Tree** = hierarchical, in-memory, W3C-standard representation of a web page. - ✅ Root node is the **Document**; structural root is ``. - ✅ Construction pipeline: **Tokenize → Tree-build via Open-Elements Stack → Error Recovery → Live DOM**. - ✅ The **Open-Elements Stack** is the key data structure; **push on start-tag, pop on end-tag**. - ✅ Node types: **Document, Element, Attribute, Text, Comment, DocumentType** (6 total). - ✅ XPath axes (`/`, `//`, `[@attr]`, `[n]`, `text()`, `@attr`) are evaluated **left-to-right** on the DOM. - ✅ XPath indexing is **1-based**, not 0-based. - ✅ **Tree Edit Distance (TED)** = min-cost sequence of insert/delete/substitute to map $T_1 \to T_2$. - ✅ Cost vector for KTU defaults: **insert = delete = substitute = 1**; label-match substitution = 0. - ✅ DOM Tree enables **Wrapper Induction (STALKER, WIEN, SoftMealy)** and **Template Detection**. - ✅ Difference between **DOM and HTML**: HTML = source string (linear); DOM = parsed tree (hierarchical, navigable). - ✅ Standard complexity: Build = $O(n)$; DFS Traverse = $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. - ✅ **Foster Parenting** and **Adoption Agency Algorithm** are HTML5-specific error-recovery mechanisms. - ✅ Sample XPath: `/html/body/p[@id="main"]/b/text()` returns text of `` inside the `

    `. - ✅ Sample TED for p-vs-span substitution: $TED = 1$. > 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** Always draw a small ASCII / Mermaid DOM tree in your answer sheet. Diagrams earn *easy* partial marks even when the textual explanation is incomplete.

topics.title: Extraction Based on a Single List Page and Multiple Pages - Introduction to Schema Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extraction Based on a Single List Page and Multiple Pages - Introduction to Schema Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Extraction Based on a Single List Page

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Single List Page Extraction* is the process of automatically identifying and harvesting a **set of structurally similar records** that are co-located on one HTML document (a "list page" or "result page"). The records are usually generated by a common server-side template, so they share repeating DOM sub-trees, repeating CSS classes, and repeating visual patterns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A list page is **NOT** the same as a detail page. A detail page carries data for **one** entity. A list page carries the *index* of many entities at once (e.g., 25 product cards on a single Amazon search page).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Classifieds

Imagine you buy a Sunday newspaper and you only want the *Used-Car Listings* on page 14. Every listing on that page looks almost identical — a **box** with a *photo on the left*, *title at the top*, *price at the bottom-right*, and *mileage in small italics*. A human learns the pattern after reading two or three boxes and then *fluidly extracts* the same four fields from every remaining box.

A list-page extractor works the same way:

1. It looks for **repeated structural blocks** (the "boxes").
2. It generalises a *wrapper* — a pattern that says, *"Inside every repeated block, take the text in the H3 tag (title), the div with class 'price' (cost), and the span with class 'mileage' (km)."*
3. It sweeps across the page and emits a *tabular record* per block.

The three canonical techniques for a single list page are:

- **String-pattern wrappers** — detect fixed prefix/separator/suffix tokens (classical *WIEN*, *SoftMealy*).
- **Tree-pattern wrappers** — align DOM sub-trees and pick the common sub-structure (*RoadRunner*, *EXALG*).
- **Visual / render-tree wrappers** — use font size, colour, pixel position (Cai-Ni's *ViPER*, *ViNTs*).

---

## 1.2 Extraction Based on Multiple Pages

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Multi-Page Extraction* extends the list-page paradigm by first **discovering the pagination surface** of a website, then *iteratively applying* a list-page (or detail-page) extractor to every reachable page, and finally **merging** the records into a single homogeneous dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a real-world crawler, *multi-page extraction* is what enables coverage of the **deep web** — sites where the data is split across thousands of pages behind a single search form.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Library Card Catalogue

A library card catalogue (the *old wooden drawer kind*) has:

- A **main index card** that says *"Computer Science — Section 004 to 678"*.
- Multiple **drawer cards**, each pointing to a *sub-section*.
- A **book card** at the bottom giving the full bibliographic record.

A multi-page extractor mimics this navigation:

1. It detects the **pagination links** ("Next 1 2 3 ... 47") and builds a *URL frontier*.
2. For each URL in the frontier, it either (a) finds a list of items to drill into, or (b) finds the actual record.
3. It de-duplicates, normalises, and persists every record into one table.

The four primary multi-page strategies are:

| Strategy | How it finds more pages | Best suited for |
|---|---|---|
| **URL-template inference** | Learns `?page=$1$` from observed URLs | Search results, blogs |
| **Sitemap / robots.txt mining** | Reads `sitemap.xml` | News portals, e-commerce |
| **Link-following (BFS/DFS)** | Crawls `<a href>` edges | Wikis, forums, directories |
| **Form-submission / query generation** | Submits POST/GET forms | The deep web, databases |

---

## 1.3 Introduction to Schema Matching

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Schema Matching* is the semi-automatic process of **identifying semantic correspondences** between elements of two (or more) heterogeneous database schemas, XML Schemas, ontologies, or HTML wrapper outputs. The output is a set of *match mappings* of the form `(e_1, e_2, \rho, \sigma)`, where `e_1` and `e_2` are elements, `\rho` is a relation (e.g., `equivalence`, `overlap`, `subsumes`), and `\sigma \in [0,1]` is a *confidence score*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU syllabus, schema matching is the **bridge** that allows web-mined data from two differently-designed sites to be *fused* into a single warehouse. Without schema matching, every record from site A would live in a different "column" than the same concept from site B.

### Conceptual Analogy — The International Plug Adapter

Imagine you travel from India to the US. Both your phone charger and the wall socket deliver *electricity*, but the **shapes differ** (round-pin vs. flat-blade). A *travel adapter* does **not generate electricity**; it only *recognises the equivalence* ("3.3 V is still 3.3 V") and *maps* the contacts.

Schema matching is the **adapter of the data world**:

- Site A says `<price currency="INR">45000</price>`.
- Site B says `<cost>$499</cost>`.
- The matcher says: *"price ≈ cost, with 0.86 confidence, relation = EQUIVALENCE"*.

The three classic *heterogeneity barriers* that schema matching must overcome are:

1. **Syntactic heterogeneity** — different *names* for the same concept (`price` vs. `cost`).
2. **Structural heterogeneity** — different *nestings* (`<addr><city>Kochi</city></addr>` vs. `<location><city>Kochi</city></location>`).
3. **Semantic heterogeneity** — different *meanings* of the same name (`"rate"` as interest-rate vs. heart-rate).

> [!TIP]
> A **schema** is the *blueprint*; an **instance** is the *building*. Schema matching can therefore operate on either the *blueprint alone* (schema-only) or *both blueprint and buildings* (hybrid, instance-based).

---

## 1.4 Master Glossary of Bolded Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise these terms verbatim — KTU 2024 examiners reward the *exact syllabus phrase*.

- **Wrapper** — A program that maps a structured page region to a relational tuple.
- **DOM (Document Object Model)** — The tree representation of an HTML document used by browsers and extractors.
- **Pagination Surface** — The complete set of URL templates that enumerate every page of a result set.
- **Schema** — A formal description of data structure (XML Schema, JSON Schema, RDB table definitions).
- **Mapping Cardinality** — `1:1`, `1:n`, `n:1`, `n:n` correspondences between schema elements.
- **Similarity** — A score `\sigma \in [0,1]` quantifying how alike two elements are.
- **Match Operator** — The functional symbol `\approx`, `\sqsubseteq`, or `\equiv` assigned to a mapping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Single List-Page Pipeline

The pipeline is a **four-stage funnel** in which each stage prunes noise so the next stage operates on a smaller, cleaner candidate set.

### Stage 1 — Tokenisation & DOM Tree Build
The raw HTML string is parsed by a standards-compliant parser (e.g., the **HTML5 parser** in the browser engine) and turned into a tree where the *root* is `document`, children are `<html>`, `<head>`, `<body>`, and so on. Every text node, attribute, and element becomes a node.

### Stage 2 — Repeated Sub-Tree Detection
The system slides a *window* across sibling lists. It computes a *tree-edit distance* between consecutive siblings; siblings whose distance is below a threshold `\tau` are clustered into one *record cluster*. The intuition: **a template generates near-identical sub-trees, but user content makes them not 100 % identical**.

### Stage 3 — Wrapper Induction
For each cluster, the system finds the **Generalised Node Set (GNS)** — the set of XPath expressions that return *one node per record*. For example, on an Amazon-style listing, the GNS may be:

- Title → `/html/body//div[@class="s-result-item"]//h2/a/span`
- Price → `/html/body//div[@class="s-result-item"]//span[@class="a-price-whole"]`
- Rating → `/html/body//div[@class="s-result-item"]//span[@class="a-icon-alt"]`

### Stage 4 — Record Emission
For every cluster element, evaluate every GNS expression and emit a tuple `(record_id, title, price, rating, …)`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why & How":**
> - *Why* detect repetition? Because it is the **only** robust, template-invariant signal in a list page.
> - *How* does repetition arise? Because the server-side template is invoked once per record; identical template code ⇒ identical DOM skeleton with different leaf data.

---

## 2.2 The Multi-Page Pipeline

A multi-page extractor augments the single-page pipeline with two extra layers.

### Layer A — URL Frontier Discovery
The system must find every URL that *might* contain a new record. There are **four frontier-discovery channels**, listed in the priority order recommended by KTU 2024 textbooks:

1. **Anchor Mining** — Parse every `<a href>`. If the href matches a *known result-page template* (e.g., contains `?page=`), enqueue it.
2. **Sitemap Mining** — Fetch `/sitemap.xml`, `/sitemap_index.xml`, and `<head>`'s `<link rel="alternate">`. These are explicitly authored index files.
3. **Pagination Pattern Detection** — From one observed list page, infer the *next-page URL template* using *URL generalisation* (replacing digits with `$1$`).
4. **Form Querying** — Submit GET/POST forms with synthesised input values to reach the deep web.

### Layer B — De-duplication & Merging
Once records are pulled from many pages, identical records may appear twice (a product listed in two categories, for example). A *SimHash* or *MinHash* fingerprint is computed for each record, and records whose fingerprint *Hamming distance* is below `\delta` are merged.

### Why & How of Multi-Page

> [!NOTE]
> - *Why* do we need a separate multi-page layer? Because a single list page is **only a window** into the dataset; a real e-commerce site has *thousands* of pages.
> - *How* do we avoid crawling forever? Two safeguards: (i) **PolitenessPolicy** — sleep `\Delta t` seconds between requests; (ii) **Page-Bound Detection** — stop when frontier returns only *seen* URLs.

---

## 2.3 The Schema-Matching Process

Schema matching is formalised as a **3-tuple input**, a **2-tuple output**, and a **6-step algorithm**.

**Input:**
- Source schema `S = (E_S, R_S)` where `E_S` are elements and `R_S` are relationships.
- Target schema `T = (E_T, R_T)`.
- Optional auxiliary knowledge `K` (dictionary, thesaurus, previous matches).

**Output:**
- Match map `M = \{(e_s, e_t, \rho, \sigma) \mid e_s \in E_S, e_t \in E_T\}`.
- Cardinality map `C` describing whether the match is `1:1`, `1:n`, `n:1`, or `n:n`.

### The Six Logical Steps

1. **Pre-processing** — Stem, tokenise, and lowercase every element label and every data instance.
2. **Schema-only matcher (Linguistic)** — Compare element *names* using string similarity.
3. **Schema-only matcher (Structural)** — Compare element *contexts* (path from root, ancestor/descendant count).
4. **Instance-based matcher** — Compare the *data values* stored under each element.
5. **Hybrid / Reuse matcher** — Combine the three above using a *weighted aggregator* and a *COMA-style* reuse of past match results.
6. **Post-processing** — Filter low-`\sigma` mappings, fix cardinalities, return the *final* `M`.

### Why & How of Each Matcher

> [!NOTE]
> - *Why* linguistic matching? It is **the cheapest and most explainable** matcher; it catches `price ≈ cost` instantly.
> - *Why* structural matching? Two elements with the *same name* but in *different positions* may not be equivalent (e.g., `<book><title>` vs. `<book><chapter><title>`).
> - *Why* instance matching? Names lie: two elements called `"id"` may store very different things. Instance distribution reveals the truth.
> - *Why* hybrid? Because **no single matcher dominates** — a weighted ensemble dominates any individual one (this is the *no-free-lunch theorem in IR*).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects **every** numerical formula KTU 2024 examiners love to test in Module 3. Each row is **exam-ready** — memorise the symbol, the formula, the range, and the use-case.

| Symbol | Formula | Range | When to use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Levenshtein Edit Distance | $lev_{a,b}(i,j) = \min\!\begin{cases} lev(i\!-\!1,j)+1 \\ lev(i,j\!-\!1)+1 \\ lev(i\!-\!1,j\!-\!1)+\mathbf{1}_{a_i \neq b_j} \end{cases}$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ | Compare element *labels* | insertions / deletions / substitutions |
| Normalised Edit Similarity | $sim_{edit}(a,b) = 1 - \dfrac{lev(a,b)}{\max(\vert a \vert, \vert b \vert)}$ | $[0,1]$ | Linguistic matcher | dimensionless |
| Jaccard Similarity (tokens) | $J(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $[0,1]$ | Compare *set* of word-tokens | dimensionless |
| Cosine Similarity (TF-IDF) | $\cos(\vec{x},\vec{y}) = \dfrac{\vec{x}\cdot \vec{y}}{\Vert \vec{x} \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert \vec{y} \Vert_2}$ | $[-1,1]$ | Instance-based matcher | dimensionless |
| Dice Coefficient | $D(A,B) = \dfrac{2\,\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | $[0,1]$ | Bigram overlap for labels | dimensionless |
| SimHash Hamming Distance | $hamming(s_1, s_2) = \sum_{k=1}^{f} s_{1,k} \oplus s_{2,k}$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ | Near-duplicate record detection | bits |
| Combined Similarity (COMA) | $\sigma_{comb} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \sigma_i \quad ; \quad \sum w_i = 1$ | $[0,1]$ | Aggregating matchers | weighted average |
| Path Similarity | $sim_{path}(e_1,e_2) = \dfrac{\vert path(e_1) \cap path(e_2) \vert}{\vert path(e_1) \cup path(e_2) \vert}$ | $[0,1]$ | Structural matcher | dimensionless |
| Repetition Confidence | $rep(block) = 1 - \dfrac{ted(block_i, block_{i+1})}{size(block_i)}$ | $[0,1]$ | Repeated sub-tree detection | dimensionless |
| Cosine of TF-IDF Expansion | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log\!\dfrac{N}{df_t}$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ | Vectorise instances | weight |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility of these formulas:**
> - *Levenshtein* powers **spell-checkers** and **wrapper generators**.
> - *Jaccard* powers **search-engine candidate-generation** and **tag overlap**.
> - *Cosine TF-IDF* powers **scholarly-paper deduplication** (e.g., CiteSeerX).
> - *SimHash* powers **Google News clustering**.
> - *COMA weighted aggregate* powers **enterprise data-fusion** in banking, retail, and healthcare.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Levenshtein Edit Distance Recurrence (Linguistic Matcher)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **most asked** derivation in KTU 2024 Module-3 papers. Practice writing it on a single sheet of foolscap.

### Statement
Given two strings `a = a_1 a_2 ... a_m` and `b = b_1 b_2 ... b_n`, derive the dynamic-programming recurrence for the *edit distance* `lev_{a,b}(m,n)`.

### Derivation Walk-through

Let `lev(i,j)` denote the minimum cost of transforming the prefix `a_1 ... a_i` into the prefix `b_1 ... b_j`, where each insertion, deletion, or substitution costs `+1`, and a match costs `+0`.

**Base Case A** — If `i = 0`, we must insert all `j` characters of `b`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
lev(0,j) &= j \\
\text{Explanation: } & \text{to build a string of length } j \text{ from an empty string, perform } j \text{ insertions.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Base Case B** — If `j = 0`, we must delete all `i` characters of `a`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
lev(i,0) &= i \\
\text{Explanation: } & \text{to destroy a string of length } i, perform } i \text{ deletions.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recursive Case** — Consider the *last* characters of each prefix, `a_i` and `b_j`. There are three actions we can take to make the prefixes agree:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Action 1 — Substitute: } & \text{transform } a_1\ldots a_{i-1} \rightarrow b_1\ldots b_{j-1} \text{ then pay } \mathbf{1}_{a_i \neq b_j}. \\
\text{Action 2 — Delete } a_i: & \text{transform } a_1\ldots a_{i-1} \rightarrow b_1\ldots b_j \text{ then pay } 1. \\
\text{Action 3 — Insert } b_j: & \text{transform } a_1\ldots a_i \rightarrow b_1\ldots b_{j-1} \text{ then pay } 1.
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the minimum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
lev(i,j) &= \min
\begin{cases}
lev(i-1, j-1) + \mathbf{1}_{a_i \neq b_j} \\
lev(i-1, j) + 1 \\
lev(i, j-1) + 1
\end{cases} \\
\text{Explanation: } & \text{the optimal final step is whichever of the three actions is cheapest.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combining base and recursive cases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
lev_{a,b}(i,j) =
\begin{cases}
\max(i,j) & \text{if } \min(i,j) = 0 \\
\min
\begin{cases}
lev(i-1, j-1) + \mathbf{1}_{a_i \neq b_j} \\
lev(i-1, j) + 1 \\
lev(i, j-1) + 1
\end{cases} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The recurrence is **optimal-substructure-correct** because the cost of the best transformation up to `(i,j)` depends only on cheaper sub-problems `(i-1,j-1)`, `(i-1,j)`, `(i,j-1)`, which are already solved when we fill the DP table row-by-row.

### Worked Numerical Example
Strings: `a = "kitten"`, `b = "sitting"`. Compute `lev(6,7)`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{lev(0,0)} &= 0 \\
\text{lev(1,1)} &= \min\bigl(0+1,\; 1+1,\; 1+1\bigr) = 1 \quad (\text{k}\to\text{s}) \\
\text{lev(2,2)} &= \min\bigl(1+0,\; 1+1,\; 2+1\bigr) = 1 \quad (\text{ki}=\text{si}) \\
\text{lev(3,3)} &= \min\bigl(1+0,\; 2+1,\; 3+1\bigr) = 1 \quad (\text{kit}=\text{sit}) \\
\text{lev(4,4)} &= \min\bigl(1+0,\; 2+1,\; 4+1\bigr) = 1 \quad (\text{kitt}=\text{sitt}) \\
\text{lev(5,5)} &= \min\bigl(1+1,\; 2+1,\; 5+1\bigr) = 2 \quad (\text{kitte}\to\text{sitte}) \\
\text{lev(6,6)} &= \min\bigl(2+0,\; 3+1,\; 6+1\bigr) = 2 \quad (\text{kitten}=\text{sitten}) \\
\text{lev(6,7)} &= \min\bigl(2+1,\; 3+1,\; 7+1\bigr) = 3 \quad (\text{insert 'g' at end}) \\
\therefore\ \text{Edit distance} &= 3.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** `lev("kitten","sitting") = 3` because the three required edits are: substitute `k→s`, substitute `e→i`, and insert `g`.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Normalised Edit Similarity (Linguistic Matcher Score)

The raw edit distance depends on the *length* of the strings. To convert it to a *bounded* similarity, divide by the maximum length and subtract from 1.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
sim_{edit}(a,b) &= 1 - \frac{lev(a,b)}{\max(\vert a \vert, \vert b \vert)} \\
\text{Explanation: } & \text{when } a=b,\; lev=0 \Rightarrow sim = 1 \;(\text{perfect}). \\
& \text{when } a,b \text{ are completely disjoint, } lev = \max(\vert a \vert,\vert b \vert) \Rightarrow sim = 0 \;(\text{worst}).
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example
For `a = "kitten"`, `b = "sitting"`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
sim_{edit} &= 1 - \frac{3}{\max(6,7)} = 1 - \frac{3}{7} = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.5714
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cosine TF-IDF Similarity (Instance-Based Matcher)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation tests whether a student can convert a *corpus* of values into a *vector* and then apply the cosine law.

### Step 1 — Term Frequency (TF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
tf(t,d) &= \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}} \\
\text{Explanation: } & f_{t,d} \text{ is the raw count of term } t \text{ in document } d; \text{ divide by document length to normalise.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
idf(t) &= \log\!\frac{N}{df_t} \\
\text{Explanation: } & N \text{ is the total number of documents; } df_t \text{ is the number containing } t. \text{ A rare term gets a high weight.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — TF-IDF Weight

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{t,d} &= tf(t,d) \cdot idf(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Cosine Similarity Between Two Schemas' Instance-Vectors

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos(\vec{e_S},\vec{e_T}) &= \frac{\vec{e_S}\cdot\vec{e_T}}{\Vert\vec{e_S}\Vert_2 \, \Vert\vec{e_T}\Vert_2} \\
&= \frac{\sum_{t \in V} w_{t,e_S} \cdot w_{t,e_T}}{\sqrt{\sum_t w_{t,e_S}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_t w_{t,e_T}^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example
Schema `S` element holds the *instance set* `{"red blue green"}`; schema `T` element holds `{"red yellow green"}`. Treat each word as a term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{red},S} &= tf_{\text{red},S}\cdot \log(N/df_{\text{red}}) = \tfrac{1}{3}\cdot 1 = 0.333 \\
w_{\text{blue},S} &= \tfrac{1}{3}\cdot 1 = 0.333 \\
w_{\text{green},S} &= \tfrac{1}{3}\cdot 1 = 0.333 \\
w_{\text{red},T} &= \tfrac{1}{3}\cdot 1 = 0.333 \\
w_{\text{yellow},T} &= \tfrac{1}{3}\cdot 1 = 0.333 \\
w_{\text{green},T} &= \tfrac{1}{3}\cdot 1 = 0.333 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{S} &= (0.333, 0.333, 0, 0.333) \\
\vec{T} &= (0.333, 0, 0.333, 0.333) \\
\vec{S}\cdot\vec{T} &= 0.333^2 + 0.333^2 = 0.222 \\
\Vert\vec{S}\Vert_2 = \Vert\vec{T}\Vert_2 &= \sqrt{3\cdot 0.333^2} = 0.577 \\
\cos(\vec{S},\vec{T}) &= \frac{0.222}{0.577 \cdot 0.577} = \frac{0.222}{0.333} = 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Cosine similarity `= 0.667`. The two elements are 66.7 % instance-overlapping — they very likely refer to *colour* values.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Jaccard Similarity for Set-Valued Labels

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(A,B) &= \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \{\text{pri, ce, INR}\} \\
B &= \{\text{cos, t, USD}\} \\
A \cap B &= \varnothing \Rightarrow J = 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For element labels `A = {"unit","price"}` and `B = {"unit","cost"}`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \cap B &= \{\text{unit}\} \Rightarrow \vert A\cap B\vert = 1 \\
A \cup B &= \{\text{unit, price, cost}\} \Rightarrow \vert A\cup B\vert = 3 \\
J &= \tfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Combined Similarity (COMA-style Aggregation)

A *hybrid* matcher combines the linguistic, structural, and instance similarities using a *weighted sum*.

### Statement
Given matchers `\sigma_{ling}, \sigma_{struct}, \sigma_{inst}` and non-negative weights `w_1, w_2, w_3` with `\sum w_i = 1`, derive `\sigma_{comb}`.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{comb} &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} w_i \sigma_i \\
&= w_1 \sigma_{ling} + w_2 \sigma_{struct} + w_3 \sigma_{inst}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example
- `\sigma_{ling} = 0.60` (cost ≈ price).
- `\sigma_{struct} = 0.85` (both are *leaf* numeric elements at the same depth).
- `\sigma_{inst} = 0.70` (both contain numbers in `[1, 1000000]`).
- Weights `w = (0.3, 0.2, 0.5)`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{comb} &= 0.3(0.60) + 0.2(0.85) + 0.5(0.70) \\
&= 0.18 + 0.17 + 0.35 \\
&= 0.70
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Threshold rule:** A match is *accepted* if `\sigma_{comb} \geq \theta` where `\theta` is a tunable hyper-parameter (typical `\theta = 0.5` or `0.6`).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — List-Page vs Multi-Page vs Schema Matching

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is **gold for KTU 2024**. Examiners love to ask *"Compare X and Y"* for 7 marks. Memorise the column headers and the entries.

| Dimension | Single List Page | Multi-Page Extraction | Schema Matching |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input unit** | One HTML document | A *frontier* of URLs and a starting seed | Two (or more) schemas |
| **Output unit** | A *table* of records on that one page | A *warehouse* of records across all pages | A *mapping* `M` between elements |
| **Repetition signal** | Repeated sibling sub-trees | Repeated URL template | Repeated element labels or instance patterns |
| **Wrapper type** | XPath / regex | XPath / regex *plus* frontier controller | N/A (operates on schema, not page) |
| **Cardinality** | 1 page → N records | N pages → M records | Mappings: `1:1, 1:n, n:1, n:n` |
| **Failure mode** | Template change across records | Pagination drift / dead URLs | Heterogeneity (syntactic / structural / semantic) |
| **Algorithm family** | Tree-edit-distance, GNS, EXALG | URL-template inference, BFS/DFS, sitemap | Linguistic / structural / instance / hybrid |
| **Time complexity** | `O(n \cdot d^2)` where `n` = siblings, `d` = tree depth | `O(F \cdot (n \cdot d^2))` where `F` = frontier size | `O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert T \vert)` match-pairs |
| **Space complexity** | `O(n \cdot d)` for the DOM | `O(F + n \cdot d)` for frontier + DOM | `O(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert)` for the two schemas |
| **Dominant evaluation metric** | Precision / Recall on record boundaries | Coverage = (retrieved records) / (true records) | F-measure of mappings; low manual effort |
| **Real-world analogue** | One newspaper page | An entire newspaper archive | An international-plug adapter |
| **Difficulty (KTU exam)** | ★★☆☆☆ | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★☆ |
| **Year-asked frequency** | 70 % | 55 % | 90 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Single List-Page Extraction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw HTML String] --> B[HTML5 Parser]
    B --> C[DOM Tree]
    C --> D[Sliding Window over Sibling Lists]
    D --> E[Compute Tree-Edit Distance]
    E --> F{Repetition Confidence >= tau}
    F -- No --> Z[Discard Block]
    F -- Yes --> G[Add to Record Cluster]
    G --> H[Generalised Node Set GNS Induction]
    H --> I[Wrapper Generation XPath Expressions]
    I --> J[Evaluate XPath per Cluster]
    J --> K[Emit Tabular Records]
    K --> L[Persist to CSV or Database]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Node IDs are all alphanumeric (e.g., `node1` style would be used if Mermaid required it; here we use descriptive letters which are still valid identifiers in flowcharts).

## 4.2 Diagram B — Multi-Page Extraction with Frontier Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Seed URL] --> S1[Fetch and Parse]
    S1 --> S2{Is it a List Page or Detail Page?}
    S2 -- List --> S3[Apply Single-List Extractor]
    S3 --> S4[Emit Records]
    S4 --> S5[Discover Pagination Links]
    S5 --> S6[URL Generalisation Replace Digits with Placeholder]
    S6 --> S7[Add to Frontier F]
    S2 -- Detail --> S8[Apply Detail-Page Extractor]
    S8 --> S4
    S7 --> S9[Politeness Delay Delta t]
    S9 --> S10{Frontier Empty?}
    S10 -- No --> S1
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[SimHash De-duplication]
    S11 --> S12[Final Integrated Dataset]
```

## 4.3 Diagram C — Schema Matching Six-Step Pipeline (COMA-style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Schema S] --> P1[Pre-processing Stem and Tokenise]
    B[Schema T] --> P1
    P1 --> M1[Linguistic Matcher sigma_ling]
    P1 --> M2[Structural Matcher sigma_struct]
    P1 --> M3[Instance Matcher sigma_inst]
    M1 --> AGG[Weighted Aggregator sigma_comb]
    M2 --> AGG
    M3 --> AGG
    AGG --> POST{sigma_comb >= theta}
    POST -- No --> DROP[Discard Mapping]
    POST -- Yes --> KEEP[Add to Map M]
    KEEP --> FINAL[Final Match Map M with Cardinalities]
```

## 4.4 Diagram D — Mapping Cardinalities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OneToOne [1:1 Mapping]
      A1[price] --> B1[cost]
    end
    subgraph OneToN [1:n Mapping]
      A2[address] --> B2a[street]
      A2 --> B2b[city]
      A2 --> B2c[zip]
    end
    subgraph NtoOne [n:1 Mapping]
      A3a[street] --> B3[addr_line]
      A3b[city] --> B3
    end
    subgraph NtoN [n:n Mapping]
      A4a[author1] --> B4a[writer]
      A4a[author2] --> B4a
    end
```

## 4.5 Diagram E — End-to-End Web-Mining System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputLayer [Input Layer]
      W1[Website 1]
      W2[Website 2]
      W3[Website 3]
    end
    W1 --> CRAWL[Focussed Crawler]
    W2 --> CRAWL
    W3 --> CRAWL
    CRAWL --> LIST[Single List-Page Extractor]
    CRAWL --> MULTI[Multi-Page Extractor]
    LIST --> WRAP[Wrapper Store]
    MULTI --> WRAP
    WRAP --> DATA[Raw Records]
    DATA --> MATCH[Schema Matching Engine]
    MATCH --> FUSE[Data Fusion and De-duplication]
    FUSE --> WAREHOUSE[Integrated Data Warehouse]
    WAREHOUSE --> MINE[Mining Queries]
    MINE --> INSIGHT[Business Insights]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** When asked to *"draw a block diagram"*, the student is expected to (i) label *every* block, (ii) show *directional arrows*, and (iii) include a brief one-line caption per block. The diagram above is fully captioned and would earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a *Single List Page* and a *Detail Page* in the context of web data extraction. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **Single List Page** is an HTML document that contains a *set* of records generated by the same server-side template. It is identified by *repeated sibling sub-trees* in the DOM. Example: Amazon search results page (`/s?k=laptop`) shows 16 product cards.
- A **Detail Page** contains data for *one* entity only, typically reached by clicking a record on a list page. Example: the individual product page of a single Amazon laptop item.
- Key *differentiator*: list page = *index* (n-ary); detail page = *record* (1-ary). (Award the third mark for a clear differentiator.)

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define *Schema Matching*. List any two *heterogeneity barriers* it must overcome.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Schema Matching** is the (semi-)automatic identification of *semantic correspondences* between elements of two or more heterogeneous schemas, producing a match map with confidence scores. (1 mark)
- **Heterogeneity Barrier 1 — Syntactic:** different *labels* for the same concept, e.g., `price` vs. `cost`. (1 mark)
- **Heterogeneity Barrier 2 — Semantic:** same label for different concepts, e.g., `rate` as *interest-rate* vs. *heart-rate*. (1 mark)
- *(Acceptable third barrier: Structural — different nesting depths.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-3 Internal Choice)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Choice 1]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a multi-page web-data extraction system**. Discuss *URL-template inference* and *sitemap mining* as two frontier-discovery techniques. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **linguistic and instance-based matchers** of a schema-matching system. Show the computation of **Jaccard** and **Cosine** similarities for a worked example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to **Diagram B** and **Diagram E** in SECTION 4. The system has *four* logical blocks:
1. **Focussed Crawler** — receives seed URL, applies URL filters.
2. **Frontier Manager** — keeps the priority queue of to-visit URLs, performs *URL-template inference*.
3. **Page Classifier & Wrapper Dispatcher** — decides whether a fetched page is *list* or *detail*; routes to the right wrapper.
4. **Record Store & De-duplicator** — persists records, applies SimHash fingerprints.

**Step 2 — URL-Template Inference (2.5 marks):**
Given observed URLs such as
- `https://shop.example.com/cat?page=1`
- `https://shop.example.com/cat?page=2`
- `https://shop.example.com/cat?page=47`

The system generalises by **replacing digit runs with a placeholder**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{template} = \texttt{https://shop.example.com/cat?page=}\underbrace{\texttt{([0-9]+)}}_{\text{parameter}} \\
\text{then it instantiates the parameter as } 1, 2, 3, \ldots, 47
\end{aligned}
$$

Generalisation rules: (i) contiguous digits → `\$N\$`; (ii) trailing slashes → optional; (iii) protocol normalised to `https`.

**Step 3 — Sitemap Mining (2.5 marks):**
Many sites expose a *sitemap* at well-known URLs:
- `https://site/robots.txt` (look for `Sitemap:` directive).
- `https://site/sitemap.xml` (sitemap index).
- `<head>`'s `<link rel="alternate" type="application/xml" ...>`.

Parsing the sitemap XML yields every indexable URL — a *near-complete frontier* in a single fetch. The crawler prefers sitemaps because they (i) are *authoritative*, (ii) avoid *spider traps*, and (iii) reduce bandwidth by skipping low-value pages.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Linguistic Matcher (2 marks):**
Compares *element labels* using string similarity. Common metrics: Levenshtein edit distance, Jaccard on token sets, Dice on bigrams, and *soundex/phonetic* similarity. Output: `\sigma_{ling} \in [0,1]`.

**Step 2 — Instance-Based Matcher (2 marks):**
Compares the *data values* stored under each element. Hypothesis: if two elements contain statistically similar value distributions (same min, max, mean, character class), they are *semantically aligned*. Output: `\sigma_{inst} \in [0,1]`.

**Step 3 — Jaccard Worked Example (1.5 marks):**
Element `S.label = "unit_price"`, element `T.label = "unit_cost"`. Token sets:
- `A = {unit, price}`, `B = {unit, cost}`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(A,B) &= \frac{\vert\{\text{unit}\}\vert}{\vert\{\text{unit, price, cost}\}\vert} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cosine Worked Example (1.5 marks):**
Two instance vectors over the *vocabulary* `{red, blue, green, yellow}`:
- `\vec{S} = (0.333, 0.333, 0, 0.333)` (holds `red blue green`).
- `\vec{T} = (0.333, 0, 0.333, 0.333)` (holds `red yellow green`).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos(\vec{S},\vec{T}) &= \frac{0.333^2 + 0.333^2}{\sqrt{3 \cdot 0.333^2} \cdot \sqrt{3 \cdot 0.333^2}} = \frac{0.222}{0.333} \approx 0.667.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Both metrics exceed typical threshold `\theta = 0.5`, so the matcher proposes a *match* with confidence `0.333` (Jaccard) or `0.667` (Cosine) — *Cosine* is preferred for instance vectors.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Choice 2]`

> **(a)** Explain **wrapper induction** for a *single list page*. Discuss the role of *tree-edit distance* and the *Generalised Node Set (GNS)*. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **six-step schema-matching process**. Show how a **COMA-style weighted aggregation** combines three matchers with worked numerical example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — What is a Wrapper (1 mark):**
A *wrapper* is a procedure that takes a region of a structured web page and returns a relational tuple. It is the *interpreter* between the chaotic HTML world and the ordered database world.

**Step 2 — Why List Pages Need Wrapper Induction (1.5 marks):**
Templates are *authored once* and *executed many times*, so list pages contain *near-identical sub-trees*. Wrapper induction learns the *template* by comparing these siblings and emitting a *generalised XPath* per field.

**Step 3 — Tree-Edit Distance (TED) (2 marks):**
TED counts the minimum number of node insert / delete / relabel operations to turn one DOM sub-tree into another. It is used to decide *whether* two siblings belong to the *same template cluster*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
rep(block_i, block_{i+1}) &= 1 - \frac{ted(block_i, block_{i+1})}{\max(\vert block_i \vert, \vert block_{i+1}\vert)} \\
\text{If } rep \geq \tau\ (\text{typically } 0.7): & \text{merge into one cluster.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalised Node Set (GNS) (2.5 marks):**
For each cluster, the system lists all XPath expressions that return *exactly one* node per record. Example for an Amazon-style list:
- Title: `/html/body//div[@class="s-result-item"][\$k\$]//h2/a/span`
- Price: `/html/body//div[@class="s-result-item"][\$k\$]//span[@class="a-price-whole"]`

The variable `\$k\$` ranges from `1` to `N` (number of records). The GNS is the *schema of the extracted table*; the wrapper is the *executor* of the GNS.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — The Six Steps (4 marks, ~0.7 each):**
1. **Pre-processing** — stem, tokenise, lowercase labels and instance values.
2. **Linguistic matching** — compute string similarity on labels.
3. **Structural matching** — compute path/context similarity.
4. **Instance matching** — compute value-distribution similarity.
5. **Hybrid aggregation** — combine via *weighted sum* (COMA).
6. **Post-processing** — threshold, fix cardinalities, return `M`.

**Step 2 — Worked COMA Aggregation (3 marks):**
Match between `S.cost` and `T.price`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{ling} &= 0.40 \quad (\text{edit distance}) \\
\sigma_{struct} &= 0.90 \quad (\text{leaf numeric at same depth}) \\
\sigma_{inst} &= 0.80 \quad (\text{overlapping numeric ranges}) \\
w_1 = 0.2,\; w_2 = 0.3,\; w_3 = 0.5 & \quad (\text{instances weighted highest}) \\
\sigma_{comb} &= 0.2(0.40) + 0.3(0.90) + 0.5(0.80) \\
&= 0.08 + 0.27 + 0.40 \\
&= 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** `0.75 \geq \theta = 0.6`, so the match is *accepted* with confidence `0.75` and relation `\equiv` (equivalence).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — Top 5 Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing list page with detail page (–2 marks).** Always re-state the *cardinality* (list = many, detail = one).
> 2. **Forgetting the threshold `\theta` (–1 mark).** Schema matching *always* has a threshold. If you write `sigma = 0.75` and stop, you lose a mark for not saying *whether it is accepted*.
> 3. **No diagram in 7-mark questions (–2 marks).** Block diagrams are *compulsory* for architecture questions.
> 4. **Mixing up the Jaccard and Cosine formulas (–1 to –2 marks).** Jaccard uses *sets*; Cosine uses *vectors*. State the formula before plugging in numbers.
> 5. **Not mentioning *cardinality* (`1:1`, `1:n`, etc.) in schema matching (–1 mark).** A match map is *incomplete* without the cardinality.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — print this page the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Single list page** = many records, one URL; identified by *repetition*; extracted via **wrapper induction**.
- [ ] **Three wrapper families** — string-pattern, tree-pattern, visual-pattern.
- [ ] **Multi-page** = single list + **frontier discovery** + **iteration** + **de-duplication**.
- [ ] **Four frontier channels** — anchor mining, sitemap mining, URL-template inference, form querying.
- [ ] **Politeness delay** `\Delta t` and **page-bound detection** prevent infinite crawling.
- [ ] **SimHash** is the standard near-duplicate detector for records across pages.
- [ ] **Schema matching** = semantic correspondence discovery; output = match map `M`.
- [ ] **Three matcher families** — *linguistic*, *structural*, *instance-based*.
- [ ] **Hybrid aggregation** uses a *weighted sum* with `\sum w_i = 1`; threshold `\theta` gates acceptance.
- [ ] **Levenshtein recurrence** has *three* min-branches; base cases are `i = 0` and `j = 0`.
- [ ] **Jaccard** is set-based; **Cosine** is vector-based; both return values in `[0,1]`.
- [ ] **Three heterogeneity barriers** — syntactic, structural, semantic.
- [ ] **Four mapping cardinalities** — `1:1`, `1:n`, `n:1`, `n:n` — *must* be reported in the match map.
- [ ] **TF-IDF weight** = `tf \cdot \log(N/df)`; the cosine of two TF-IDF vectors is the *gold-standard* instance similarity.
- [ ] **COMA** = *COMbination of Matchers*; a *framework*, not an algorithm; weights are *learnable* or *hand-tuned*.
- [ ] Always state the *threshold* `\theta` and the *decision* (accept/reject) when computing `\sigma_{comb}`.
- [ ] Use `\vert x \vert` in markdown tables, *not* the bare `|` pipe symbol, to avoid breaking syntax.
- [ ] In Mermaid diagrams, every node ID is *alphanumeric* and labels are *plain uppercase text* without markdown formatting inside double-quoted strings.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Mile Mnemonic — "LISA-M":**
> **L**inguistic, **I**nstance, **S**tructural, **A**ggregation, **M**ap = the five keywords of any 14-mark schema-matching answer. Open with the *definition* of `M`, close with the *cardinality report*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Schema Level Match, Domain and Instance Level Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Schema Level Match, Domain and Instance Level Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Schema Matching

**Schema Matching** is the task of discovering semantic correspondences between schema elements of two or more data sources (e.g., databases, XML schemas, ontologies, web forms). It is the cornerstone of **Data Integration**, **Data Warehousing**, **Schema Alignment**, and **Web Information Extraction**.

In modern web applications, data is scattered across millions of heterogeneous sources (HTML pages, APIs, deep-web databases, RDF triples, etc.). Before a unified view can be built, the system must understand that, for example:

- `<price>` in source A = `<cost>` in source B
- `date_of_birth` = `dob` = `birthDate`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> Schema Matching is the process of identifying **semantically corresponding elements** between two schemas $S_1$ and $S_2$, producing a set of **correspondences** (mappings) of the form $\langle e_1, e_2, sim, R \rangle$, where $e_1 \in S_1$, $e_2 \in S_2$, $sim$ is a similarity score, and $R$ is a relation (e.g., equivalence, subsumption).

The matching can be performed at three distinct abstraction levels, which together form the **Matching Pyramid**:

1. **Schema Level (Element & Structure Level)** — matches based on *names, descriptions, structure, constraints*.
2. **Domain Level** — matches based on *data types, value ranges, units, formats*.
3. **Instance Level (Data Level)** — matches based on *actual data values stored*.

---

## 1.2 The Three Levels — Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Matching Two Library Catalogs (Kerala University Libraries)**
>
> Imagine the Central Library (MG University) and a Private Engineering College library are merging their catalogs:
>
> - **Schema Level** → You compare the **labels** on the cards: "Author Name" vs "Writer", "ISBN" vs "Book Code", and the **hierarchy** (Book → Chapter → Page).
> - **Domain Level** → You compare the **type of data** expected: is it a number, a date, text up to 50 characters? Is the price in INR or USD?
> - **Instance Level** → You compare the **actual values** that fill the cards: the author "Nightingale" appears in both, the ISBN begins with "978-" in both.

This three-pronged strategy is necessary because no single matcher works for every pair. Combining all three produces the most reliable alignments.

---

## 1.3 Schema Level Matching — Definition

**Schema Level Matching** (also called **Element Matching** or **Metadata Matching**) operates purely on the **schema information** — i.e., the labels, descriptions, types, and structural positions of elements — without inspecting the actual data instances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schema Matcher Definition:**
> A schema matcher analyzes the metadata of attributes/elements (names, data types, structural properties, constraints) and returns a similarity score between any two schema elements based on linguistic, structural, and constraint-based criteria.

### Sub-categories of Schema Level Matching

| Sub-type | What it Compares | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Linguistic / String-based** | Element names & descriptions | "city" ≈ "town" |
| **Structural / Graph-based** | Position in hierarchy / parent-child relations | `<book><author>` matched with `<volume><writer>` |
| **Constraint-based** | Data types, keys, nullability, cardinality | `INT` vs `NUMBER` |
| **Semantic / Ontology-based** | Meaning via ontologies (WordNet, domain ontologies) | "car" ≈ "automobile" |

---

## 1.4 Domain Level Matching — Definition

**Domain Level Matching** focuses on the **domain of admissible values** an attribute can take. Two attributes are considered equivalent if their value domains share type, range, format, and unit characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Domain Matcher Definition:**
> A domain matcher analyzes the permissible set of values, their data type, format pattern, and measurement unit. It returns a high similarity if the domains (e.g., "Boolean: 0/1", "Date: yyyy-mm-dd", "Currency: INR") are consistent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Example:**
> In an e-commerce integration system, an attribute called `weight` in one schema (in **kg**, range 0–500) and `mass` in another (in **grams**, range 0–500000) are semantically identical but syntactically mismatched. A domain-level matcher detects this through **unit normalization** and **range scaling**.

---

## 1.5 Instance Level Matching — Definition

**Instance Level Matching** (also called **Data Level Matching** or **Content Matching**) inspects the **actual data values** stored in the underlying data sources. It is particularly powerful when schema names are cryptic, abbreviated, or completely different.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instance Matcher Definition:**
> An instance matcher compares the *set of overlapping data values* between two attributes, using value-overlap statistics, value-distribution profiles, pattern analysis, and statistical characterization to compute similarity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:**
> In one source, a column is labeled `pid` containing values `{101, 102, 103, 104}`. In another, a column is labeled `prod_id` containing values `{101, 102, 104}`. The instance matcher computes a **Jaccard overlap** or **value intersection ratio** and concludes that the two columns refer to the same concept — "Product ID".
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Schema Level Matchers — Detailed Taxonomy

### 2.1.1 Linguistic (String-based) Matchers
Linguistic matchers compare the *linguistic representation* of element names. They are sub-divided into:

**(a) String Equivalence & Edit Distance**
The simplest approach. Two names are similar if they differ by a small number of character edits.

**(b) N-gram Similarity (N-gram Overlap)**
Treats strings as a bag of $n$-length substrings and computes Jaccard/Cosine similarity over them.

**(c) Token-based Similarity**
Breaks the string into tokens (words), normalizes them (lowercasing, stemming, removing stop-words), then computes overlap.

**(d) Phonetic / Soundex Matching**
Compares phonetic representation for matching of names/words that sound alike (e.g., "Smith" vs "Smyth").

**(e) Language-based & Semantic Matchers**
Use NLP tools like **WordNet** to find synonyms, hypernyms, and hyponyms (e.g., "car" ↔ "automobile").

### 2.1.2 Structural (Graph-based) Matchers
These matchers exploit the **hierarchical / graph structure** of schemas.

- If two elements are leaves of similarly-named parents, they are likely similar.
- Schema elements that share similar **paths** (e.g., `/library/book/author` vs `/catalog/volume/writer`) are often matched.

### 2.1.3 Constraint-based Matchers
Constraints include:
- **Data type** (`string`, `int`, `float`, `date`, `boolean`)
- **Cardinality** (1:1, 1:N, N:M)
- **Key constraints** (primary key, foreign key)
- **Nullability / Optionality**
- **Uniqueness**

Two attributes sharing the same type and key constraint are very likely to match.

---

## 2.2 Domain Level Matchers — Detailed Analysis

Domain matchers examine the *data type* and *value domain* of attributes. The matching criteria include:

| Criterion | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Type Equivalence** | Both are `int`, `string`, `date`, etc. | `price: float` ≈ `cost: float` |
| **Format Pattern** | Regular expression describing the format | `dd-mm-yyyy` vs `mm/dd/yyyy` |
| **Value Range** | Min–max bounds | `age: 0–120` vs `years: 1–100` |
| **Unit / Measurement** | Currency, length, mass units | `INR` vs `USD` |
| **Controlled Vocabulary** | Predefined list of allowed values | `{M, F}` vs `{Male, Female}` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Domain Level Matters in Web Mining:**
> Many deep-web forms on the internet (booking sites, e-commerce) restrict inputs through controlled vocabularies. By analyzing the **type-ahead suggestions** and the **value lists**, a web mining system can reverse-engineer the schema and map it to a global reference schema.

---

## 2.3 Instance Level Matchers — Detailed Analysis

Instance matchers use **value analysis** to determine similarity. Key techniques:

### 2.3.1 Value-based Similarity Metrics
Used when the two attributes share overlapping literal values.

- **Jaccard Similarity** — measures the overlap of value sets.
- **Overlap Coefficient** — alternative normalization.
- **Cosine Similarity** — treats value sets as binary vectors.

### 2.3.2 Distribution-based Matchers
Compare the **statistical profile** of values (mean, variance, histogram, pattern frequency). If two attributes have the same data distribution, they are likely the same attribute.

### 2.3.3 Pattern-based Matchers
Identify regular-expression patterns of values. A column containing all `^[A-Z]{2}\d{7}$` strings (passport numbers) and another matching the same regex are likely the same attribute.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table — Save This for KTU University Exam**

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Levenshtein Edit Distance** | $ed(s_1, s_2) = $ min operations to transform $s_1$ to $s_2$ | Character-level edit operations (insert, delete, substitute) |
| 2 | **Normalized Edit Similarity** | $sim_{ed} = 1 - \dfrac{ed(s_1, s_2)}{\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert)}$ | Bounded in $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | **Jaccard Similarity (Instances)** | $J(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Set overlap of values |
| 4 | **Overlap Coefficient** | $OV(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\min(\vert A \vert, \vert B \vert)}$ | Useful when one set is much larger |
| 5 | **Cosine Similarity (Token)** | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{s_1} \cdot \vec{s_2}}{\Vert \vec{s_1} \Vert \cdot \Vert \vec{s_2} \Vert}$ | Token frequency vector dot product |
| 6 | **TF-IDF Weight** | $w_{t,d} = tf_{t,d} \cdot \log \dfrac{N}{df_t}$ | Importance of term $t$ in document $d$ |
| 7 | **Dice Coefficient** | $D = \dfrac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Similar to Jaccard, double-weight on overlap |
| 8 | **Soundex Code** | Phonetic encoding (1 letter + 3 digits) | Names that sound similar share the code |
| 9 | **Path Similarity (Structural)** | $sim_{path}(e_1, e_2) = \dfrac{\vert path(e_1) \cap path(e_2) \vert}{\vert path(e_1) \cup path(e_2) \vert}$ | Jaccard on path nodes |
| 10 | **Type Compatibility Score** | $sim_{type} \in \{0, 0.5, 1\}$ | Same type $\rightarrow 1$, compatible (e.g., int/long) $\rightarrow 0.5$, else $0$ |
| 11 | **Information Content (WordNet)** | $IC(c) = -\log P(c)$ | Semantic similarity in ontologies |
| 12 | **Aggregate Matcher (Composite)** | $sim_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot sim_i$, with $\sum w_i = 1$ | Weighted sum of individual matchers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants / Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
> - All similarity scores are normalized to **range $[0, 1]$**, with **$1$ = perfect match**.
> - **$0.7$ to $0.8$** is typically the threshold for accepting a mapping.
> - **Levenshtein cost** of each insert/delete/substitute operation is **$1$**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Web Mining

| Application | Level Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Deep Web Crawling / Hidden Web Extraction** | All three | Reverse-engineer schemas of query forms to integrate results |
| **Product Comparison Engines** (e.g., shopping aggregators) | Domain + Instance | Match `price` (different currencies), `model` (different codes) |
| **E-commerce Schema Integration** | All three | Unify catalogs from multiple vendors |
| **Web Page Wrapper Induction** | Schema + Instance | Detect field boundaries in HTML tables |
| **Ontology Alignment in Semantic Web** | Schema (semantic) | Align classes/properties in RDF/OWL ontologies |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Jaccard Instance Similarity

**Problem:**
Two e-commerce schemas have the following sets of values for an attribute:

- Schema A `product_id`: $\{101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106\}$
- Schema B `prod_code`: $\{103, 104, 105, 106, 107, 108\}$

Compute the **Jaccard similarity**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the intersection $A \cap B$.

$$A \cap B = \{103, 104, 105, 106\}$$

Count: $\vert A \cap B \vert = 4$.

**Step 2:** Identify the union $A \cup B$.

$$A \cup B = \{101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107, 108\}$$

Count: $\vert A \cup B \vert = 8$.

**Step 3:** Apply the Jaccard formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(A, B) &= \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} \\
&= \frac{4}{8} \\
&= 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result.

A similarity of **$0.5$** indicates **moderate overlap**. With the typical threshold of $0.7$, this mapping is **borderline**; we should confirm with additional domain/schema matchers.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Normalized Edit Distance Similarity

**Problem:**
Compare the schema element names:
- $s_1 =$ `"author"`
- $s_2 =$ `"authour"` (typo, extra 'u')

Compute the **Levenshtein distance** and the **normalized similarity**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute Levenshtein distance using the dynamic programming table.

The minimum number of operations to transform `"author"` to `"authour"` is **$1$** (insert a 'u').

Therefore, $ed(s_1, s_2) = 1$.

**Step 2:** Compute the string lengths.

$\vert s_1 \vert = 6$, $\vert s_2 \vert = 7$, so $\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert) = 7$.

**Step 3:** Apply the normalized formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
sim_{ed} &= 1 - \frac{ed(s_1, s_2)}{\max(\vert s_1 \vert, \vert s_2 \vert)} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{7} \\
&= \frac{6}{7} \\
&\approx 0.857
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret.

With a similarity of **$0.857$**, this is a **strong match** and should be auto-accepted (above $0.7$ threshold).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — TF-IDF Cosine Similarity for Token Matching

**Problem:**
Two column descriptions are:
- $d_1 =$ `"name of the student"`
- $d_2 =$ `"student name"`

Compute the **TF-IDF cosine similarity** over a small corpus.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Tokenize the documents.

- $d_1$ tokens: `{"name", "of", "the", "student"}`
- $d_2$ tokens: `{"student", "name"}`

**Step 2:** Build a vocabulary. Assume corpus size $N = 1000$ documents.

| Term | $df_t$ (in 1000 docs) |
|---|---|
| name | 200 |
| of | 950 |
| the | 1000 |
| student | 50 |

**Step 3:** Compute IDF for each term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IDF}(name) &= \log_{10}\frac{1000}{200} = 0.699 \\
\text{IDF}(of) &= \log_{10}\frac{1000}{950} = 0.022 \\
\text{IDF}(the) &= \log_{10}\frac{1000}{1000} = 0.000 \\
\text{IDF}(student) &= \log_{10}\frac{1000}{50} = 1.301
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute TF-IDF vectors (TF = raw count; here TF = 1 for each present term).

- $\vec{d_1} = [0.699, 0.022, 0.000, 1.301]$ (for `name, of, the, student`)
- $\vec{d_2} = [1.301, 0, 0, 0.699]$ (for `student, name`)

**Step 5:** Compute dot product and magnitudes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{d_2} &= (0.699 \cdot 1.301) + (0.022 \cdot 0) + (0.000 \cdot 0) + (1.301 \cdot 0.699) \\
&= 0.909 + 0 + 0 + 0.909 \\
&= 1.818
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert \vec{d_1} \Vert &= \sqrt{0.699^2 + 0.022^2 + 0.000^2 + 1.301^2} = \sqrt{2.181} \approx 1.477 \\
\Vert \vec{d_2} \Vert &= \sqrt{1.301^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 + 0.699^2} = \sqrt{2.181} \approx 1.477
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Apply cosine formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos(\theta) &= \frac{1.818}{1.477 \times 1.477} = \frac{1.818}{2.181} \approx 0.833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Interpret.

A similarity of **$0.833$** indicates a strong semantic match — the two descriptions refer to the same concept (Student Name).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Composite Multi-Level Matcher

**Problem:**
A web mining system uses three matchers to compare two attributes (`price` vs `cost`):

| Matcher | Similarity | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Linguistic (string) | 0.20 | 0.20 |
| Domain (data type) | 1.00 | 0.30 |
| Instance (Jaccard) | 0.80 | 0.50 |

Compute the **aggregate similarity**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Verify weights sum to 1.

$0.20 + 0.30 + 0.50 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 2:** Apply the weighted sum formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
sim_{agg} &= w_1 \cdot sim_1 + w_2 \cdot sim_2 + w_3 \cdot sim_3 \\
&= (0.20 \times 0.20) + (0.30 \times 1.00) + (0.50 \times 0.80) \\
&= 0.04 + 0.30 + 0.40 \\
&= 0.74
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret.

A composite similarity of **$0.74$** indicates a **reliable match**. The low string similarity is offset by perfect domain similarity and high instance similarity — demonstrating the power of multi-level matching.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Three Levels

| Aspect | Schema Level | Domain Level | Instance Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Element names, descriptions, structure | Data type, format, range, units | Actual data values |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Medium | High (needs data scan) |
| **Robustness to Renaming** | Low (relies on labels) | High (structural facts) | Very High (data doesn't lie) |
| **Useful When…** | Labels are descriptive & meaningful | Units/formats differ but semantics match | Labels are cryptic / data is messy |
| **Output** | Similarity score in $[0, 1]$ | Type compatibility matrix | Value overlap, distribution profile |
| **Limitation** | Fails when labels are abbreviations | Cannot detect semantic equivalence | Requires overlapping instances |
| **Best Combinator** | Combine with Instance | Combine with Schema | Combine with Domain |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define schema matching and explain schema level matching with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Schema matching is the process of identifying semantically corresponding elements between two heterogeneous schemas, producing mappings of the form $\langle e_1, e_2, sim, R \rangle$, where $sim$ is a similarity score and $R$ is the relationship.

**Schema Level Matching Explanation (1.5 marks):** Schema level matching operates purely on the metadata of schema elements — i.e., their *names, descriptions, and structural positions* — without examining actual data values. For example, the matcher would compare the names `product_id` and `prod_code` using edit distance or n-gram similarity and conclude they are likely equivalent, or compare the structural path `/library/book/author` with `/catalog/volume/writer` to infer alignment.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Differentiate between domain level matching and instance level matching.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Domain Level Matching | Instance Level Matching |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Data type, range, format, unit of values | Actual data values stored in the attributes |
| **Input** | Schema metadata (type, range, format) | Sample/overlapping data instances |
| **Example** | `weight: 0–500 kg` matches `mass: 0–1100 lbs` after unit conversion | `pid = {101,102,103}` matches `product_id = {101,102,104}` via Jaccard |
| **Limitation** | Fails if domains coincidentally overlap | Fails if no overlapping instances exist |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | High (requires data access) |

(1.5 marks for definition of each + 1 mark for clear tabular difference)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the three levels of schema matching — schema level, domain level, and instance level — with suitable examples. Discuss the role of similarity aggregation across these levels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the Jaccard similarity and Overlap Coefficient for the following two sets of attribute values. State whether the mapping should be accepted using a threshold of $0.7$. **(7 marks)**

- Attribute A (Schema S1): $\{ 12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27, 30, 33 \}$
- Attribute B (Schema S2): $\{ 15, 18, 24, 27, 33, 36, 39 \}$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Schema Level Matching (2 marks):**
Schema level matching analyzes the *names, descriptions, and structure* of schema elements. It uses linguistic techniques (edit distance, n-gram, WordNet-based) and structural techniques (path similarity, parent-child comparison). Example: `employee_name` matches `emp_nm` with a high edit-distance similarity, or `/university/student/id` matches `/college/learner/rollNo` via path similarity.

**Domain Level Matching (2 marks):**
Domain level matching compares the *data type, range, format, and unit* of admissible values. Example: `salary: 0–10,00,000 INR` (Integer) and `income: 0–125,000 USD` (Float) are matched after currency normalization, since both represent monetary compensation.

**Instance Level Matching (2 marks):**
Instance level matching inspects the *actual data values* of overlapping attributes. Example: Two attributes `country` and `nation` with values `{India, USA, UK}` and `{UK, India, Japan}` have Jaccard $= 2/4 = 0.5$, suggesting a partial match.

**Similarity Aggregation (1 mark):**
The individual scores from the three levels are combined using a weighted sum: $sim_{agg} = \sum w_i \cdot sim_i$, with $\sum w_i = 1$. Typical weights are $0.2$ (schema), $0.3$ (domain), $0.5$ (instance). A threshold (commonly $0.7$) determines acceptance.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Step 1: Identify the intersection.**

$$
A \cap B = \{15, 18, 24, 27, 33\}
$$

So $\vert A \cap B \vert = 5$.

**Step 2: Identify the union and individual set sizes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \cup B &= \{12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27, 30, 33, 36, 39\} \\
\vert A \cup B \vert &= 10 \\
\vert A \vert &= 8 \\
\vert B \vert &= 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Jaccard similarity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(A, B) &= \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Overlap Coefficient.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OV(A, B) &= \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\min(\vert A \vert, \vert B \vert)} = \frac{5}{\min(8, 7)} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Decision.**

Jaccard $= 0.5$ is **below** the $0.7$ threshold → would normally **reject**.
Overlap Coefficient $= 0.714$ is **above** the $0.7$ threshold → suggests **accept**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Many students forget that the Jaccard threshold and the Overlap threshold can lead to **conflicting decisions**. The correct interpretation is that when the two metrics disagree, the system should flag the pair for **human review** rather than blindly accept or reject. A good answer will state this nuance.

**Conclusion:** The mapping is **uncertain** — recommend human review / additional matchers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various schema-level matchers: linguistic, structural, and constraint-based. Provide at least one similarity formula for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two attribute descriptions in a tourism website schema are: $d_1 =$ `"name of the hotel"` and $d_2 =$ `"hotel name"`. Using the TF-IDF cosine similarity method, compute the similarity score. Assume a corpus of $N = 100$ documents with the following document frequencies: $df(\text{name}) = 50$, $df(\text{of}) = 95$, $df(\text{the}) = 100$, $df(\text{hotel}) = 10$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Linguistic Matchers (2.5 marks):**
These matchers compare the *labels* of schema elements using string and semantic techniques.
- *Edit Distance Matcher:* $ed(s_1, s_2) = $ minimum number of character edits.
- *Token-based / TF-IDF Cosine:* $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{s_1} \cdot \vec{s_2}}{\Vert \vec{s_1} \Vert \Vert \vec{s_2} \Vert}$.
- *WordNet-based:* Uses synonym / hypernym graphs.

**Structural Matchers (2.5 marks):**
These matchers exploit the *hierarchical/positional* similarity of elements in the schema tree.
- *Path Similarity:* $sim_{path}(e_1, e_2) = \frac{\vert path(e_1) \cap path(e_2) \vert}{\vert path(e_1) \cup path(e_2) \vert}$.
- Leaves with similar parents are likely to match.

**Constraint-based Matchers (2 marks):**
These matchers use *schema constraints* — data type, key, nullability, cardinality.
- *Type Compatibility:* $sim_{type} = 1$ for same type, $0.5$ for compatible (e.g., `int` and `long`), $0$ otherwise.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Step 1: Tokenize the descriptions.**

- $d_1$ tokens: `{name, of, the, hotel}`
- $d_2$ tokens: `{hotel, name}`

**Step 2: Compute IDF using $\log_{10}(N / df_t)$ with $N = 100$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IDF}(name) &= \log_{10}\frac{100}{50} = 0.301 \\
\text{IDF}(of) &= \log_{10}\frac{100}{95} = 0.022 \\
\text{IDF}(the) &= \log_{10}\frac{100}{100} = 0.000 \\
\text{IDF}(hotel) &= \log_{10}\frac{100}{10} = 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Build TF-IDF vectors (TF = 1).**

- $\vec{d_1} = [0.301, 0.022, 0.000, 1.000]$ (for `name, of, the, hotel`)
- $\vec{d_2} = [1.000, 0, 0, 0.301]$ (for `hotel, name`)

**Step 4: Compute dot product.**

$$
\vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{d_2} = (0.301 \times 1.000) + (0.022 \times 0) + (0.000 \times 0) + (1.000 \times 0.301) = 0.602
$$

**Step 5: Compute magnitudes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert \vec{d_1} \Vert &= \sqrt{0.301^2 + 0.022^2 + 0.000^2 + 1.000^2} = \sqrt{1.0918} \approx 1.045 \\
\Vert \vec{d_2} \Vert &= \sqrt{1.000^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 + 0.301^2} = \sqrt{1.0906} \approx 1.044
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute cosine similarity.**

$$
\cos(\theta) = \frac{0.602}{1.045 \times 1.044} = \frac{0.602}{1.091} \approx 0.552
$$

**Step 7: Interpret.**

Similarity of **$0.552$** is moderate. The lower-than-expected score is because IDF significantly downweights the common words `of` and `the`, and TF-IDF does not capture word-order — "name of the hotel" and "hotel name" are semantically equivalent but lexically differ. In practice, additional matchers (e.g., bigram-based) should be combined.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students often forget to apply the log base correctly. Always state the base explicitly: $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$. Also, students forget that **IDF becomes zero** for terms appearing in **all** documents (like `the` here) — these terms should be **discarded from the vector** entirely, not just given weight zero, to keep the vector clean.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Schema Level, Domain & Instance Level Matching**

- **Schema Matching** is the core task of identifying semantic correspondences between heterogeneous schemas, producing mappings $\langle e_1, e_2, sim, R \rangle$.

- **Three abstraction levels** exist — schema, domain, and instance. **No single level is sufficient**; they must be combined.

- **Schema Level** deals with **names, descriptions, and structure**. Includes:
  - *Linguistic matchers:* Edit Distance, N-gram, TF-IDF, Soundex, WordNet.
  - *Structural matchers:* Path similarity, leaf similarity.
  - *Constraint matchers:* Type, key, nullability, cardinality.

- **Domain Level** deals with the **admissible value space**:
  - Data type equivalence, format pattern (regex), value range, unit (currency, length, mass).
  - Requires **unit normalization** to detect equivalences (e.g., INR↔USD, kg↔lb).

- **Instance Level** deals with **actual data values**:
  - Jaccard: $J = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$.
  - Overlap: $OV = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\min(\vert A \vert, \vert B \vert)}$.
  - Dice: $D = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$.
  - Cosine over value-frequency vectors.
  - Distribution & pattern matchers (mean, variance, regex).

- **Aggregation** uses a **weighted sum** $sim_{agg} = \sum w_i \cdot sim_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$. Typical weights: $0.2$ (schema), $0.3$ (domain), $0.5$ (instance).

- **Threshold** for acceptance is usually **$0.7$**. Below this, the system should defer to human review.

- **Boundary conditions:** All similarity scores normalized to $[0, 1]$; $1$ = perfect match; $0$ = no match.

- **Real-world uses:** Deep web data integration, product comparison engines, e-commerce catalog unification, ontology alignment, HTML wrapper induction.

- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the **formula**, compute **step by step**, and give a **concluding decision** ("Accept / Reject / Review") — this is how the KTU board examiner awards full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extracting and Analysing Web Social Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extracting and Analysing Web Social Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Web Social Network (WSN)** is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ extracted from the World Wide Web where nodes $V$ represent individual entities (web pages, bloggers, social media users, organizations, or any web-resident actor) and edges $E \subseteq V \times V$ represent socially meaningful relationships (hyperlinks, follower/followee ties, co-authorship, friendship, citation, or reply chains).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A *social network* is a social structure made of nodes (individuals or organizations) tied by one or more specific types of interdependency such as friendship, kinship, common interest, financial exchange, dislike, or relationships of beliefs, knowledge, or prestige. In Web Mining, these networks are *extracted* from observable web artifacts (HTML link structures, tags, comments, retweets) and then *analysed* using graph-theoretic, statistical, and machine-learning techniques.

**Key Terminology Mandated by the KTU 2024 Module 3 Syllabus:**

| Term | Mathematical Notation | Meaning |
|------|----------------------|---------|
| Graph | $G = (V, E)$ | Network with vertex set $V$ and edge set $E$ |
| Adjacency Matrix | $A$ where $A_{ij} = 1$ if $(i,j) \in E$ | Encodes all connections |
| Out-degree | $k_i^{out} = \sum_j A_{ij}$ | Number of outgoing edges of node $i$ |
| In-degree | $k_i^{in} = \sum_j A_{ji}$ | Number of incoming edges of node $i$ |
| Path | $P_{u \rightarrow v}$ | A sequence of edges connecting $u$ to $v$ |
| Shortest Path | $d(u, v)$ | Minimum number of hops between $u$ and $v$ |
| Component | $C \subseteq V$ | Maximally connected sub-graph |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> Module 3 of PECDT635 (Web Mining) bridges the gap between raw web data and actionable intelligence. The KTU examiner's pattern from 2024 university cycles confirms that questions on social-network extraction and analysis routinely appear in **ESE Part B (14 marks)**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "City Map" Viewpoint

Imagine the World Wide Web as a **giant city**:
- Every **building** is a person, blog, or web page → this is a *node*.
- Every **road** connecting two buildings is a hyperlink, a "follow," or a "tag" → this is an *edge*.
- The **city planning department** (your mining algorithm) needs to discover:
  1. **Who is the most influential person?** (the building everyone wants to visit) → *Centrality Analysis*.
  2. **Which groups of people always hang out together?** (clusters of tightly connected buildings) → *Community Detection*.
  3. **Are there any bridges between two distant neighborhoods?** (a single road whose removal disconnects the city) → *Betweenness & Bridges*.
  4. **How do ideas/rumors spread through the city?** (paths along which information flows fastest) → *Diffusion & Information Cascades*.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Web Social Network analysis is the discipline of turning the chaotic link structure of the web into a *map* so that we can measure influence, find communities, predict behaviour, and detect anomalies (bots, spam rings, viral cascades).

---

## 1.3 Two Fundamental Network Types in Web Mining

1. **Explicit Social Networks** — Created deliberately by users (Facebook friends, Twitter followers, LinkedIn connections). The platform exposes the adjacency list through APIs.
2. **Implicit Social Networks** — *Inferred* from observable web behaviour. Examples include the *Blogosphere*, *citation networks*, *co-occurrence networks* (two names appearing in the same document), and *reply/mention networks* on forums.
