The KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus explicitly focuses on **extracting implicit networks** from the web, which is computationally harder but academically richer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The End-to-End Pipeline of Web Social Network Mining

Web Social Network analysis is performed in **four logical stages** (this is the canonical KTU 2024 framework):

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition
- **Crawl** the web using a focused/Topical crawler restricted to a domain.
- **Collect** raw artifacts: HTML pages, blog posts, comments, social-media posts, citation records.
- **Persist** in a document store (graph DB or NoSQL).

### Stage 2 — Object Identification (Node Extraction)
- Decide what counts as a *node*: an author? a blog? a page?
- Apply **Named-Entity Recognition (NER)** to extract people, organizations, topics.
- Resolve name ambiguity (e.g., "John Smith" — which one?) using **entity-resolution** heuristics.

### Stage 3 — Relationship Discovery (Edge Extraction)
- Identify *co-occurrence*, *co-citation*, *bibliographic coupling*, or *explicit links*.
- Assign **edge weights** $w_{ij}$ representing strength, frequency, or trust.

### Stage 4 — Network Analysis
- Compute centrality, detect communities, run PageRank/HITS, perform link prediction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 2024 Module 3 anchor is the 4-step pipeline above.** Memorize it — direct 3-mark definition questions test it every cycle.

---

## 2.2 Co-Citation and Bibliographic Coupling — Two Pillars of Implicit Network Construction

These two concepts are heavily tested in KTU exams. Understand them in relation to each other.

### 2.2.1 Co-Citation

Two documents (or authors) A and B are **co-cited** if a third document C cites *both* A and B.

$$
\text{Co-Cite}(A, B) = \vert \{ C \mid A \in \text{refs}(C) \;\land\; B \in \text{refs}(C) \} \vert
$$

The resulting co-citation network has:
- Nodes = cited entities
- Edge weight = number of times they are cited *together*

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** If many papers cite both Einstein and Bohr, those two physicists are *intellectually related* — even if Einstein and Bohr never directly cited each other.

### 2.2.2 Bibliographic Coupling

Two documents A and B are **bibliographically coupled** if they both cite the *same* third document C.

$$
\text{BibCouple}(A, B) = \vert \{ C \mid C \in \text{refs}(A) \;\land\; C \in \text{refs}(B) \} \vert
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:**
> - **Co-citation** → focus on the *citer* (C). Look at the *citers* to discover the link.
> - **Bibliographic coupling** → focus on the *cited* (C). Look at the *cited references* to discover the link.
> - The two are **duals**: if A and B are co-cited by C, then C bibliographically couples A and B.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete, exam-ready formula bank** for the "Extracting and Analysing Web Social Networks" topic. Every KTU 2024 ESE question on this module maps to at least one row below.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Degree Centrality** (in-degree for directed web graphs) | $C_D(v) = k_v^{in} = \sum_{u \in V} A_{uv}$ | $A$ is adjacency matrix; dimensionless count | Ranking web pages by incoming links |
| 2 | **Normalized Degree Centrality** | $C_D^{norm}(v) = \dfrac{k_v^{in}}{\vert V \vert - 1}$ | $\vert V \vert$ is the number of nodes | Comparable across networks of different sizes |
| 3 | **Closeness Centrality** | $C_C(v) = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{u \in V} d(v, u)}$ | $d(v,u)$ is shortest-path length in hops | Identifying the "broadcast hub" |
| 4 | **Betweenness Centrality** | $C_B(v) = \sum_{s \neq v \neq t} \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | $\sigma_{st}$ is the number of shortest paths from $s$ to $t$; $\sigma_{st}(v)$ is the number passing through $v$ | Finding brokers / gate-keepers |
| 5 | **Eigenvector Centrality** | $x_i = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{j} A_{ij} x_j$  i.e. $A x = \lambda x$ | $\lambda$ is the principal eigenvalue | Importance inherited from important neighbours |
| 6 | **PageRank (damping form)** | $PR(p) = \dfrac{1 - d}{N} + d \sum_{q \rightarrow p} \dfrac{PR(q)}{L(q)}$ | $d$ is damping factor $\in [0,1]$ (typically **0.85**), $N = \vert V \vert$, $L(q)$ is out-degree of $q$ | Ranking web pages (Google's algorithm) |
| 7 | **HITS — Authority** | $a(p) = \sum_{q \rightarrow p} h(q)$ | $a$ is authority score, $h$ is hub score | Pages pointed to by many hubs |
| 8 | **HITS — Hub** | $h(p) = \sum_{p \rightarrow q} a(q)$ | $h$ is hub score, $a$ is authority score | Pages that point to many authorities |
| 9 | **Clustering Coefficient (local)** | $C_i = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert \{e_{jk}\} \vert}{k_i(k_i - 1)}$ | $k_i$ is degree of node $i$; $e_{jk}$ are edges among its neighbours | Measures local community density |
| 10 | **Network Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert (\vert V \vert - 1)}$ (undirected); $\rho = \dfrac{\vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert(\vert V \vert - 1)}$ (directed) | Dimensionless ratio in $[0,1]$ | How "wired-up" the network is |
| 11 | **Modularity (Newman)** | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2m} \sum_{ij} \left[ A_{ij} - \dfrac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)$ | $m = \vert E \vert$; $\delta = 1$ if $i,j$ in same community | Quality of a community partition |
| 12 | **HITS Normalization** | $\sum_i a_i^2 = 1,\; \sum_i h_i^2 = 1$ | Unit vectors | Ensures convergence |
| 13 | **Transitivity** | $T = \dfrac{3 \times \text{number of triangles}}{\text{number of connected triples}}$ | Triples of nodes | Global clustering measure |
| 14 | **Adamic-Adar Index** (link prediction) | $AA(u,v) = \sum_{w \in \Gamma(u) \cap \Gamma(v)} \dfrac{1}{\log k_w}$ | $\Gamma(u)$ is neighbour set of $u$ | Predict missing edges in social graphs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The **damping factor $d = 0.85$** in PageRank and the **two mutually-reinforcing equations** of HITS are the *single most-tested* formulas in this module. KTU examiners will provide a small toy graph (3–5 nodes) and ask you to iterate PageRank/HITS 2–3 times by hand.

---

## 2.4 PageRank — The Random Surfer Model (Deep Dive)

The web is modelled as a **Markov chain**. A "random surfer" visits pages by either:
1. Clicking a random outgoing link with probability $d$, **or**
2. Teleporting to a random page with probability $1 - d$.

The **stationary distribution** of this Markov chain is the PageRank vector $\pi$ satisfying:

$$
\pi = d \cdot M \pi + \dfrac{(1 - d)}{N} \mathbf{1}
$$

where $M$ is the **row-stochastic transition matrix** and $\mathbf{1}$ is the all-ones vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $d = 0.85$?**
> Empirically chosen by Brin & Page. Lower $d$ → less propagation along links, faster convergence but worse quality. Higher $d$ → closer to pure eigenvector centrality but slower convergence and susceptible to *rank sinks*. **Always use 0.85 in KTU exam calculations unless told otherwise.**

---

## 2.5 HITS — Hyperlink-Induced Topic Search (Kleinberg, 1999)

For every query $q$, the web is split into two sub-graphs:
- **Root set** $R_q$ — the top-$k$ pages returned by a search engine.
- **Base set** $B_q$ — $R_q$ plus all pages that link to or are linked from $R_q$ (up to a depth of $d$, typically **∞ practical depth**).

Two score vectors are computed via the mutually-reinforcing equations shown in the formula sheet, iterated until convergence (L2 norm change < $\epsilon$, typically $10^{-6}$).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** HITS is used by **citation-indexing services** (e.g., ResearchIndex / CiteSeerX) to surface authoritative papers in a research area, and by **e-commerce recommendation engines** to surface authoritative products within a topic.

---

## 2.6 Community Detection Algorithms (Brief Survey)

| Algorithm | Type | Complexity | Idea |
|-----------|------|-----------|------|
| **Girvan–Newman** | Divisive (edge betweenness) | $O(m^2 n)$ | Iteratively remove highest-betweenness edges |
| **Newman Greedy** | Agglomerative | $O((m+n)n)$ or $O(n^2 \log n)$ with heaps | Maximize modularity $Q$ at each merge |
| **Louvain** | Multi-level greedy | Near linear | Two-phase local moving + community aggregation |
| **Label Propagation** | Semi-synchronous | Near linear | Nodes adopt the most frequent neighbour label |
| **Spectral Clustering** | Eigen-based | $O(n^3)$ | Use eigenvectors of Laplacian $L = D - A$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — PageRank Iteration on a Toy Web Graph (HIGH PRIORITY)

**Problem (typical KTU 14-mark setup):**
Consider a 4-page web graph with the following directed links:
- Page A links to B and C
- Page B links to C
- Page C links to A
- Page D links to A and C

There are no other links. Compute **PageRank** for all pages using $d = 0.85$ and $N = 4$. Perform **3 iterations** starting from $PR_0(A) = PR_0(B) = PR_0(C) = PR_0(D) = 1/N = 0.25$.

### Step 1 — Compute out-degrees

$$
L(A) = 2, \quad L(B) = 1, \quad L(C) = 1, \quad L(D) = 2
$$

### Step 2 — Write down the transition matrix $M$

Each column of $M$ corresponds to a *source* page. The entry $M_{ij}$ is the probability of going from $j$ to $i$.

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1 & 1/2 \\
1/2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
1/2 & 1 & 0 & 1/2 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU pitfall to remember:** A **dangling node** (page D has no out-links) produces a *zero column*. In Google's actual implementation, dangling-node mass is distributed uniformly over all nodes. For the toy exam case, we handle it by *treating the dangling link as teleporting*, which the $(1-d)/N$ term in PageRank already accomplishes.

### Step 3 — Apply the PageRank update rule

$$
PR_i^{(k+1)} = \dfrac{1 - d}{N} + d \sum_{j \rightarrow i} \dfrac{PR_j^{(k)}}{L(j)}
$$

**Iteration 1** (using $PR_j^{(0)} = 0.25$ for all $j$):

For page A (linked from C and D):

$$
PR_A^{(1)} = \dfrac{1 - 0.85}{4} + 0.85 \left( \dfrac{PR_C^{(0)}}{L(C)} + \dfrac{PR_D^{(0)}}{L(D)} \right)
$$

$$
PR_A^{(1)} = \dfrac{0.15}{4} + 0.85 \left( \dfrac{0.25}{1} + \dfrac{0.25}{2} \right)
$$

$$
PR_A^{(1)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot (0.25 + 0.125)
$$

$$
PR_A^{(1)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0.375
$$

$$
PR_A^{(1)} = 0.0375 + 0.31875 = \mathbf{0.35625}
$$

For page B (linked from A only):

$$
PR_B^{(1)} = \dfrac{0.15}{4} + 0.85 \cdot \dfrac{PR_A^{(0)}}{L(A)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \dfrac{0.25}{2} = 0.0375 + 0.10625 = \mathbf{0.14375}
$$

For page C (linked from A, B, D):

$$
PR_C^{(1)} = \dfrac{0.15}{4} + 0.85 \left( \dfrac{PR_A^{(0)}}{L(A)} + \dfrac{PR_B^{(0)}}{L(B)} + \dfrac{PR_D^{(0)}}{L(D)} \right)
$$

$$
PR_C^{(1)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \dfrac{0.25}{2} + \dfrac{0.25}{1} + \dfrac{0.25}{2} \right) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0.75 = 0.0375 + 0.6375 = \mathbf{0.675}
$$

For page D (no incoming links):

$$
PR_D^{(1)} = \dfrac{0.15}{4} + 0 = \mathbf{0.0375}
$$

**Iteration 1 summary:** $(0.35625,\ 0.14375,\ 0.675,\ 0.0375)$ — sum = $1.2125$ (not exactly 1; convergence requires more iterations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Sanity check:** Page C has the most in-links (3 of them) and therefore dominates. This matches intuition. Page D has none, so it should have the lowest score. ✓

**Iteration 2** (using the values from Iteration 1):

For page A:

$$
PR_A^{(2)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \dfrac{0.675}{1} + \dfrac{0.0375}{2} \right) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0.69375 = 0.0375 + 0.5897 = \mathbf{0.6272}
$$

For page B:

$$
PR_B^{(2)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \dfrac{0.35625}{2} = 0.0375 + 0.15141 = \mathbf{0.18891}
$$

For page C:

$$
PR_C^{(2)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \dfrac{0.35625}{2} + \dfrac{0.14375}{1} + \dfrac{0.0375}{2} \right) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0.3406 = 0.0375 + 0.2895 = \mathbf{0.3270}
$$

For page D:

$$
PR_D^{(2)} = 0.0375 + 0 = \mathbf{0.0375}
$$

**Iteration 2 summary:** $(0.6272,\ 0.1889,\ 0.3270,\ 0.0375)$.

**Iteration 3** (only computing page A to illustrate convergence):

$$
PR_A^{(3)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \dfrac{0.3270}{1} + \dfrac{0.0375}{2} \right) = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot 0.34575 = 0.0375 + 0.2939 = \mathbf{0.3314}
$$

| Node | Iter 0 | Iter 1 | Iter 2 | Iter 3 |
|------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| A | 0.2500 | 0.3563 | 0.6272 | 0.3314 |
| B | 0.2500 | 0.1438 | 0.1889 | 0.1778 |
| C | 0.2500 | 0.6750 | 0.3270 | 0.4170 |
| D | 0.2500 | 0.0375 | 0.0375 | 0.0375 |

> [!TIP]
> **Convergence note:** After ~30–50 iterations the values stabilize. In KTU exams, 2–3 iterations are sufficient for full marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — HITS Iteration on a Toy Graph

**Problem:**
Consider a 3-page subgraph where:
- Page P1 links to P2 and P3
- Page P2 links to P3
- Page P3 links to P1

Compute HITS (Authority and Hub) for **2 iterations**, starting from $\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{h}^{(0)} = (1, 1, 1)^T$ with normalization.

### Step 1 — Form the adjacency matrix

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Iteration 1 (no normalization yet)

Authorities: $a^{(1)} = A^T h^{(0)}$

$$
a^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Hubs: $h^{(1)} = A a^{(1)}$

$$
h^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & 1 & 0
\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Normalize (L2 norm = 1)

$$
\|a^{(1)}\|_2 = \sqrt{2^2 + 1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{6} \approx 2.449
$$

$$
a^{(1)}_{norm} = \left( \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{6}},\ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}},\ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \right) \approx (0.816,\ 0.408,\ 0.408)
$$

$$
\|h^{(1)}\|_2 = \sqrt{1 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt{14} \approx 3.742
$$

$$
h^{(1)}_{norm} = \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{14}},\ \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{14}},\ \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{14}} \right) \approx (0.267,\ 0.535,\ 0.802)
$$

### Step 4 — Iteration 2

$$
a^{(2)} = A^T h^{(1)}_{norm} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.267 + 0.535 \\ 0.802 \\ 0.267 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.802 \\ 0.802 \\ 0.267 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
h^{(2)} = A a^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.267 \\ 0.802 \\ 0.802 + 0.802 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.267 \\ 0.802 \\ 1.604 \end{bmatrix}
$$

After normalization these scores would converge to the leading left/right singular vectors of $A$. The page with the **highest authority score is P1** (it is pointed to by the strongest hub P3), and the page with the **highest hub score is P3** (it points to the strongest authority P1). ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Always remember that HITS computes *two* score vectors, while PageRank computes *one*. A common KTU student error is to mix up which equation updates which vector.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Centrality Calculation

**Problem:** For the following undirected graph with 5 nodes and edges $\{(1,2), (1,3), (2,3), (3,4), (4,5)\}$, compute the **degree, closeness, and betweenness centrality** of node 3.

### Degree Centrality

$$
k_3 = 3 \quad (\text{edges to 1, 2, 4})
$$

$$
C_D^{norm}(3) = \dfrac{3}{5 - 1} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$

### Closeness Centrality

Shortest-path distances from node 3:
- $d(3,1) = 1$
- $d(3,2) = 1$
- $d(3,3) = 0$
- $d(3,4) = 1$
- $d(3,5) = 2$ (via 4)

Sum = $1 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 2 = 5$.

$$
C_C(3) = \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.20 \quad ; \quad C_C^{norm}(3) = \dfrac{4}{5} = 0.80
$$

### Betweenness Centrality

Pair (1, 2): both paths go $1 \to 3 \to 2$ and $1 \to 2$. Only 1 of 2 shortest paths goes through 3. Contribution: $1/2 = 0.5$.
Pair (1, 4): shortest path is $1 \to 3 \to 4$. 1 of 1 through 3. Contribution: $1$.
Pair (1, 5): shortest path is $1 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5$. 1 of 1 through 3. Contribution: $1$.
Pair (2, 4): shortest path is $2 \to 3 \to 4$. 1 of 1 through 3. Contribution: $1$.
Pair (2, 5): shortest path is $2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5$. 1 of 1 through 3. Contribution: $1$.
Pair (4, 5): direct edge; 0 of 1 through 3. Contribution: $0$.

$$
C_B(3) = 0.5 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 4.5
$$

Normalized by $\binom{5-1}{2} = 6$ for undirected:

$$
C_B^{norm}(3) = \dfrac{4.5}{6} = 0.75
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** Node 3 sits in the *middle* of the chain, so high betweenness is expected.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PageRank vs HITS

| Property | PageRank | HITS |
|----------|----------|------|
| **Output** | One score per node | Two scores (hub + authority) |
| **Computation** | Global, query-independent | Query-dependent (built from root/base set) |
| **Damping** | Yes ($d = 0.85$) | No damping in basic form |
| **Sensitivity to topic drift** | Low | High (vulnerable to "TKC effect") |
| **Used by** | Google | CiteSeerX, older Yahoo |
| **Principal eigenvector of** | $d M + (1-d)/N \cdot \mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^T$ | $A^T A$ and $A A^T$ |
| **Strength** | Robust against spam | Captures topical authority well |
| **Weakness** | Same score regardless of query | Easily manipulated via link farms |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Web Social Network Mining Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Web Sources: Blogs, Forums, Twitter, Citation DBs] --> B[Focused Crawler]
    B --> C[Raw HTML / JSON / XML Repository]
    C --> D[Pre-processing: Tokenize, Clean, De-duplicate]
    D --> E[Named Entity Recognition NER]
    E --> F[Entity Resolution: Disambiguate Identities]
    F --> G[Node Set V]
    D --> H[Co-occurrence / Co-citation / Bibliographic Coupling / Link Mining]
    H --> I[Edge Set E with Weights w_ij]
    G --> J[Graph Construction: G = V, E]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Network Analysis Engine]
    K --> L[Centrality: Degree, Closeness, Betweenness, Eigenvector]
    K --> M[Ranking: PageRank, HITS]
    K --> N[Community Detection: Girvan-Newman, Louvain]
    K --> O[Link Prediction: Adamic-Adar, Common Neighbours]
    L --> P[Insights Dashboard]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (for the KTU exam):** The pipeline flows **left-to-right** in three zones — *Acquisition → Construction → Analysis*. The examiner may ask you to label each box or explain why a step is necessary.

---

## 4.2 PageRank Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        W[Web Graph G = V, E]
        O[Out-degree Table L_q for all q in V]
    end
    subgraph PRcore[PageRank Engine]
        I1[Initialize PR_v to 1 / N for all v]
        I2{Dangling nodes?}
        I3[Distribute dangling mass uniformly]
        I4[Apply PR_p = 1-d over N + d * sum PR_q over L_q]
        I5[Compute L1 delta between iter k and k-1]
        I6{Converged? delta less than epsilon}
    end
    subgraph Output
        R[Ranked list of pages by stationary distribution]
    end
    W --> I1
    O --> I4
    I1 --> I2
    I2 -- Yes --> I3 --> I4
    I2 -- No --> I4
    I4 --> I5 --> I6
    I6 -- No --> I4
    I6 -- Yes --> R
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam tip:** Draw this as a labeled flowchart if a 14-mark question asks you to "explain the PageRank algorithm with a block diagram". Each block maps to one of the formulas in the Section 2 cheat sheet.

---

## 4.3 HITS Mutual Reinforcement Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RootSet[Root Set Construction]
        Q[User Query Q]
        S[Search Engine Top-k Results]
        Q --> S
        S --> R[Root Set R_q]
    end
    subgraph BaseSet[Base Set Expansion]
        R --> E1[Add all pages that link to pages in R]
        R --> E2[Add all pages linked from pages in R]
        E1 --> B[Base Set B_q]
        E2 --> B
    end
    subgraph HITSloop[Iterative HITS Loop]
        B --> ADJ[Adjacency Matrix A of B]
        ADJ --> AUTH[a_new = A transpose times h_old]
        ADJ --> HUB[h_new = A times a_old]
        AUTH --> N1[Normalize a to unit L2]
        HUB --> N2[Normalize h to unit L2]
        N1 --> CHK{Difference less than epsilon?}
        N2 --> CHK
        CHK -- No --> AUTH
        CHK -- Yes --> OUT[Output a and h vectors]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Community Detection Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Graph G] --> B{Algorithm Class}
    B --> C1[Girvan-Newman: Divisive]
    B --> C2[Newman Greedy: Agglomerative]
    B --> C3[Louvain: Multi-level]
    B --> C4[Spectral: Eigenvectors of L = D - A]
    C1 --> D1[Recompute edge betweenness]
    C2 --> D2[Merge pair maximizing delta Q]
    C3 --> D3[Phase 1: Local move to maximize Q]
    C3 --> D4[Phase 2: Build new coarse graph]
    C4 --> D5[Cluster k smallest eigenvectors of L]
    D1 --> E[Community Partitions]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Modularity Q Score]
    F --> G{Q greater than 0.3?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Acceptable Partition]
    G -- No --> I[Re-run with different parameters]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Modularity Threshold:** Empirically, $Q \in [0.3, 0.7]$ indicates a strong community structure. Below 0.3 → no clear community. Above 0.7 → exceptionally strong (rare in real social networks).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Damping factor slip:** Forgetting to include the $(1-d)/N$ teleportation term in PageRank → **lose 4 of 7 marks** on that sub-part. *Always* show both the teleportation term and the link-propagation term.
> - **HITS confusion:** Mixing up the authority and hub equations → **lose 3 of 7 marks**. Authority = sum of hubs pointing in; Hub = sum of authorities pointed to.
> - **Co-citation vs Bibliographic Coupling:** Writing "they are the same thing" → **lose 3 marks**. The two concepts are *duals* but NOT identical.
> - **Missing normalization:** Failing to normalize HITS scores between iterations → marks deducted under "convergence reasoning" criterion.
> - **Confusing Centralities:** Reporting a degree value where closeness is asked. Read the question verb twice.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. Define a *Web Social Network*. Distinguish between an *explicit* and an *implicit* social network with one example each. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A Web Social Network (WSN) is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ extracted from the web where nodes $V$ represent web-resident entities (users, pages, blogs) and edges $E$ represent socially meaningful relationships (hyperlinks, follower ties, citations).
- **Explicit networks** are user-declared; example — Facebook friend graph.
- **Implicit networks** are inferred from observable web behaviour; example — a co-citation network of research papers in a digital library.

### Q2. What is *co-citation*? How is it different from *bibliographic coupling*? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
*Co-citation* measures the strength of an intellectual relationship between two documents A and B by counting how many *other* documents C cite both. *Bibliographic coupling* measures the same kind of relationship by counting the documents C that are cited by *both* A and B. **Key difference:** co-citation looks at the *citers*; bibliographic coupling looks at the *cited references*. Mathematically, if $C_1$ and $C_2$ co-cite $A$ and $B$, then $A$ and $B$ share a bibliographic-coupling relationship via $C_1$ and $C_2$.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each)

> **ESE Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU 2024 ESE provides a choice between two questions for each module. Both questions below are equally weighted and are completely independent.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the PageRank algorithm. Construct the transition matrix for the following web graph and compute PageRank for 2 iterations using $d = 0.85$. Show all intermediate steps. [7 Marks]**

**Graph:**
- A → B, C
- B → C
- C → A
- D → A, C

**Model Solution (Part a):**

*Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):*
PageRank models a random surfer who, with probability $d$, follows a random outgoing link and, with probability $1 - d$, teleports to a uniformly random page. The stationary distribution of the resulting Markov chain is the PageRank vector. The recurrence is:

$$
PR(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{q \to p} \frac{PR(q)}{L(q)}
$$

*Step 2 — Out-degrees (1 mark):* $L(A)=2,\ L(B)=1,\ L(C)=1,\ L(D)=2$.

*Step 3 — Transition matrix (1 mark):*

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1 & 1/2 \\
1/2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
1/2 & 1 & 0 & 1/2 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 4 — Initial vector (0.5 mark):* $PR^{(0)} = (0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25)^T$.

*Step 5 — Iteration 1 (1.5 marks):*

$$
PR_A^{(1)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \frac{0.25}{1} + \frac{0.25}{2} \right) = 0.35625
$$

$$
PR_B^{(1)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \frac{0.25}{2} = 0.14375
$$

$$
PR_C^{(1)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \frac{0.25}{2} + 0.25 + \frac{0.25}{2} \right) = 0.675
$$

$$
PR_D^{(1)} = 0.0375 + 0 = 0.0375
$$

*Step 6 — Iteration 2 (1 mark):*

$$
PR_A^{(2)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( 0.675 + \frac{0.0375}{2} \right) = 0.6272
$$

$$
PR_B^{(2)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \cdot \frac{0.35625}{2} = 0.1889
$$

$$
PR_C^{(2)} = 0.0375 + 0.85 \left( \frac{0.35625}{2} + 0.14375 + \frac{0.0375}{2} \right) = 0.3270
$$

$$
PR_D^{(2)} = 0.0375 + 0 = 0.0375
$$

**Final PageRank after 2 iterations:** $PR(A) = 0.6272,\ PR(B) = 0.1889,\ PR(C) = 0.3270,\ PR(D) = 0.0375$.

> **Ranking:** $A > C > B > D$.

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the HITS algorithm. Differentiate between HITS and PageRank in terms of (i) number of score vectors produced, (ii) query dependency, and (iii) vulnerability to spam. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

*Step 1 — HITS concept (2 marks):*
HITS (Hyperlink-Induced Topic Search) by Kleinberg assigns **two** scores to every page in a *base set* derived from a search-query's results:
- **Authority score** $a(p)$ — how many good hubs point to $p$.
- **Hub score** $h(p)$ — how many good authorities $p$ points to.

The mutually-reinforcing equations are:

$$
a(p) = \sum_{q \to p} h(q), \qquad h(p) = \sum_{p \to q} a(q)
$$

These are iterated with L2 normalization until convergence.

*Step 2 — Block diagram (2 marks):* Draw the flow: Query → Root set → Base set expansion → Initialize $a^{(0)}, h^{(0)}$ → Iterate $a = A^T h$ and $h = A a$ → Normalize → Converged? Yes → Output. (Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid block.)

*Step 3 — Differences (3 marks):*

| Aspect | PageRank | HITS |
|--------|----------|------|
| (i) Number of score vectors | One (PR) | Two (Authority + Hub) |
| (ii) Query dependency | Query-independent; same rank for a page under all queries | Query-dependent; ranking changes per query's base set |
| (iii) Spam vulnerability | Robust (offline + teleportation dampens link farms) | High (TKC effect — a tightly-knit community can artificially inflate scores) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) What is *betweenness centrality*? Compute betweenness, closeness, and degree centrality for the following graph and identify the most central node. [7 Marks]**

**Graph:** Undirected, $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, $E = \{(1,2), (1,3), (2,3), (3,4), (4,5), (2,5)\}$.

**Model Solution (Part a):**

*Step 1 — Definitions (1.5 marks):*
- **Degree centrality** — number of edges incident on a node.
- **Closeness centrality** — reciprocal of sum of shortest-path distances to all other nodes.
- **Betweenness centrality** — sum over all pairs $(s,t)$ of the fraction of shortest paths from $s$ to $t$ that pass through the node.

*Step 2 — Degree centrality (1 mark):*

| Node | Degree | Normalized $C_D$ |
|------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | 2 | 0.50 |
| 2 | 3 | 0.75 |
| 3 | 3 | 0.75 |
| 4 | 2 | 0.50 |
| 5 | 2 | 0.50 |

*Step 3 — Closeness centrality (1.5 marks):* Compute all-pairs shortest paths first.

| Node | Sum of distances | $C_C^{norm}$ |
|------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $0+1+1+2+2=6$ | $4/6 = 0.667$ |
| 2 | $1+0+1+2+1=5$ | $4/5 = 0.800$ |
| 3 | $1+1+0+1+2=5$ | $4/5 = 0.800$ |
| 4 | $2+2+1+0+1=6$ | $4/6 = 0.667$ |
| 5 | $2+1+2+1+0=6$ | $4/6 = 0.667$ |

*Step 4 — Betweenness centrality (2 marks):*

Pair (1, 2): paths = $\{1\!\to\!2,\ 1\!\to\!3\!\to\!2\}$. Through 3: 1/2. Through 4 or 5: 0.
Pair (1, 4): shortest = $1\!\to\!3\!\to\!4$. Through 3: 1. Through 2: 0.
Pair (1, 5): shortest = $1\!\to\!2\!\to\!5$ (length 2). Through 3: 0. Through 2: 1.
Pair (2, 3): paths = $\{2\!\to\!3,\ 2\!\to\!1\!\to\!3\}$. Through 1: 1/2.
Pair (2, 4): shortest = $2\!\to\!3\!\to\!4$. Through 3: 1.
Pair (2, 5): direct. Through 3: 0.
Pair (3, 5): shortest = $3\!\to\!4\!\to\!5$. Through 3: 0. Through 4: 1.
Other pairs: direct or 0 contributions.

| Node | Un-normalized $C_B$ | Normalized $C_B$ |
|------|---------------------|------------------|
| 1 | 0.5 | 0.5/6 = 0.083 |
| 2 | 0.5 + 1 = 1.5 | 1.5/6 = 0.250 |
| 3 | 0.5 + 1 + 1 = 2.5 | 2.5/6 = 0.417 |
| 4 | 1 | 1/6 = 0.167 |
| 5 | 0 | 0 |

*Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):* **Node 3 is the most central** by betweenness (0.417) and tied with node 2 for closeness (0.800). Degree-wise, nodes 2 and 3 tie at 0.75. Across all three metrics, **node 3 is the overall most central node**.

**(b) Describe the Girvan-Newman algorithm for community detection. How is modularity $Q$ used to choose the best partition? [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

*Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):* Real social networks exhibit dense intra-community links and sparse inter-community links. We want to discover these communities algorithmically.

*Step 2 — Girvan-Newman Algorithm (3 marks):*
1. Compute the **edge betweenness** for every edge — the number of shortest paths between all pairs of nodes that pass through that edge.
2. Remove the edge(s) with the **highest betweenness**.
3. Recompute betweenness on the modified graph.
4. Repeat until the graph is empty (or some stopping criterion).
5. The sequence of removals yields a **dendrogram** of hierarchical communities.

*Step 3 — Modularity $Q$ (2 marks):*
The number of possible partitions is exponential, so we cannot evaluate all. Modularity quantifies the *quality* of a partition as:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{ij} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)
$$

A higher $Q$ indicates more within-community edges than expected by chance. $Q \in [-0.5, 1]$.

*Step 4 — Choosing the best partition (1 mark):* The dendrogram gives $n-1$ cuts. The cut with the **maximum modularity** is selected. Threshold: $Q > 0.3$ indicates meaningful community structure.

---

## Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this the night before the ESE — it is the entire module distilled to 18 bullets.**

- [x] **Web Social Network** = directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = web entities, $E$ = socially meaningful relationships.
- [x] **Pipeline (4 stages):** Crawl → Identify Nodes → Discover Edges → Analyse.
- [x] **Explicit networks** are user-declared (Facebook). **Implicit networks** are inferred (co-citation, blogosphere).
- [x] **Co-citation** of A and B = number of citers C that cite both. **Bibliographic coupling** of A and B = number of references C cited by both. They are *duals*.
- [x] **PageRank** = stationary distribution of random-surfer Markov chain; uses teleportation $(1-d)/N$ with $d = 0.85$.
- [x] **HITS** produces two mutually-reinforcing scores: **Authority** (sum of in-coming hub scores) and **Hub** (sum of out-going authority scores); normalized by L2 norm.
- [x] **PageRank produces ONE score; HITS produces TWO scores.**
- [x] **PageRank is query-independent; HITS is query-dependent.**
- [x] **Degree centrality** = number of incident edges; trivial but cheap.
- [x] **Closeness centrality** = $1 / \sum d(v, u)$ — measures reachability.
- [x] **Betweenness centrality** = $\sum \sigma_{st}(v) / \sigma_{st}$ — measures brokerage.
- [x] **Eigenvector centrality** = principal eigenvector of $A$ — importance inherited from important neighbours.
- [x] **Clustering coefficient** $C_i$ measures local density of triangles.
- [x] **Modularity $Q$** measures partition quality; $Q > 0.3$ = meaningful communities.
- [x] **Girvan-Newman** = divisive algorithm removing highest-betweenness edges.
- [x] **Louvain** = fast greedy modularity optimization in two phases.
- [x] **Link prediction** uses **Adamic-Adar index** = $\sum_{w \in \Gamma(u) \cap \Gamma(v)} 1/\log k_w$.
- [x] **Damping factor $d = 0.85$** is empirical; lower → faster convergence, lower quality.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute mnemonic — "CCPB HEAL":**
> **C**o-citation · **C**entrality (degree, closeness, betweenness, eigenvector) · **P**ageRank · **B**ibliographic coupling · **H**ITS · **E**igenvector · **A**damic-Adar · **L**ouvain.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources and Types of Data, Key Elements of Web Usage Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sources and Types of Data, Key Elements of Web Usage Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Web Mining**, the **sources of data** refer to the heterogeneous origins from which raw information is harvested to enable analytical and predictive modeling over the World Wide Web. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, these sources are broadly classified into three primary domains that mirror the tripartite taxonomy of web mining itself:

1. **Web Content Data** – The actual payload (text, images, audio, video, structured records) served to the user.
2. **Web Structure Data** – The topological skeleton (hyperlinks, sitemaps, DOM hierarchies) that connects content.
3. **Web Usage Data** – The behavioral trace (clickstreams, server logs, cookies, query logs) left behind by users interacting with the web.

The **types of data** further subdivide each source into **structured** (databases, XML), **semi-structured** (HTML pages, JSON APIs), and **unstructured** (free text, multimedia) formats.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (The Restaurant Analogy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Visit**
> Imagine a busy restaurant. The **menu and dishes served** represent *Web Content* (what is shown). The **seating layout and pathways connecting tables** represent *Web Structure* (how things are connected). The **bills, reservation slips, CCTV footage, and waiter notes** represent *Web Usage Data* (what users actually did, when, and how).
> Just as a restaurant manager analyzes bills and CCTV to improve service, a web analyst studies server logs and clickstreams to optimize the website.

### 1.3 Key Elements of Web Usage Data

Web usage data is the most analytically rich and the principal focus of Module 4. Its **key elements** include:

- **IP Address** – Pseudonymous identifier of the requesting client.
- **User ID / Cookie ID** – Persistent identifier enabling session continuity.
- **Timestamp** – Time of request (with millisecond granularity).
- **Requested URL (Resource)** – The exact page or asset accessed.
- **HTTP Status Code** – Outcome of the request (`200`, `404`, `500`).
- **Referrer URL** – The page that linked to the current request.
- **User Agent String** – Browser, OS, and device fingerprint.
- **Method (GET/POST)** – Type of HTTP verb used.
- **Bytes Transferred** – Volume of data sent/received.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 4 weightage heavily emphasizes *preprocessing* of these raw elements into clean sessions, transactions, and user profiles before any mining algorithm (clustering, classification, association) is applied.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Data Sources

The sources of web data can be decomposed into a **three-tier hierarchy**, each tier feeding the next mining paradigm:

| Tier | Source Domain | Raw Artefact | Mining Sub-field |
|:----:|:--------------|:-------------|:-----------------|
| **L1** | Server-Side | Web server access logs, error logs | Web Usage Mining |
| **L2** | Client-Side | Cookies, browser history, JavaScript collectors | Web Usage Mining |
| **L3** | Proxy / Network | Proxy server caches, firewall logs | Web Usage Mining |
| **L4** | Application Layer | Application server logs, database queries | Web Usage / Content |
| **L5** | Semantic Layer | Search engine query logs, click-through data | Web Usage / Structure |

### 2.2 Types of Data — The S³ Paradigm

Data on the web exists in three fundamental types, often referred to as the **S³ Model**:

- **Structured Data** — Highly organized, conforms to a rigid schema. Examples: MySQL records, CSV exports, RDF triples. Easily queried via SQL or SPARQL.
- **Semi-Structured Data** — Carries schema information *within* the data but does not enforce it rigidly. Examples: HTML documents, XML feeds, JSON API responses.
- **Unstructured Data** — No predefined schema; ~**80%** of web data is in this category. Examples: blog posts, tweets, images, video streams, PDFs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** Exam questions frequently test the student's ability to correctly *classify* a given real-world data example into one of the three S³ categories. Misclassification is a common valuation pitfall.

### 2.3 Anatomy of a Web Server Access Log Entry

A single log entry in the **Combined Log Format (CLF)** — the de facto industry standard — contains:

```
127.0.0.1 - frank [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36 -0700] 
"GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2326 
"http://www.example.com/start.html" 
"Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64)"
```

The nine fields map directly to the **key elements** enumerated in §1.3.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Metrics for Usage Data

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. All metrics below are *board-exam friendly* and require no external constants.

| # | Metric / Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:----------------|:------------|:------:|
| 1 | $D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i$ | Total bytes transferred across $n$ requests | Bytes |
| 2 | $\bar{B} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i$ | Mean payload size per request | Bytes |
| 3 | $T_{session} = t_{last} - t_{first}$ | Session duration (seconds) | s |
| 4 | $\overline{T} = \dfrac{1}{\vert S \vert}\sum_{s \in S} T_{session}(s)$ | Average session length across $S$ sessions | s |
| 5 | $PV = \vert \{ p : p \in \text{pageviews} \} \vert$ | Pageview count (cardinality) | Count |
| 6 | $V_{per\_session} = \dfrac{PV}{\vert S \vert}$ | Average pageviews per session | Count |
| 7 | $Bounce\_Rate = \dfrac{\vert S_{single} \vert}{\vert S \vert} \times 100$ | Percentage of single-page sessions | % |
| 8 | $ER_{status} = \dfrac{\vert \{ r : \text{status}(r) \geq 400 \} \vert}{n} \times 100$ | Error rate from HTTP status codes | % |
| 9 | $Hit\_Rate = \dfrac{\text{hits}}{n}$ | Hits-to-request ratio | Ratio |
| 10 | $U_{unique} = \vert \{ u : u \in \text{unique\_IPs} \} \vert$ | Unique visitors (heuristic) | Count |

> Where $S$ = set of identified sessions, $B_i$ = bytes for request $i$, $t_{first}$ / $t_{last}$ = first/last timestamps in a session.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These metrics power **Recommendation Engines** (Amazon, Flipkart), **Anomaly Detection** (fraudulent login pattern mining), **A/B Testing Analytics** (conversion funnel optimization), and **Search Engine Ranking** (dwell-time signals for Google RankBrain).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Session Duration from Raw Timestamps

The session duration is the simplest yet most critical metric. Given a user $u$ who made requests at sorted timestamps:

$$
\mathcal{T}_u = \{ t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots, t_k \}, \quad \text{where } t_1 < t_2 < \dots < t_k
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the temporal boundaries:**

$$
t_{first} = \min(\mathcal{T}_u) = t_1
$$

$$
t_{last} = \max(\mathcal{T}_u) = t_k
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute duration:**

$$
T_{session}(u) = t_{last} - t_{first} = t_k - t_1
$$

**Step 3 — Express in human-readable units (hours-minutes-seconds):**

$$
T_{session}(u) = t_k - t_1 = \Delta t \text{ seconds} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\Delta t}{3600} \right\rfloor : \left\lfloor \dfrac{\Delta t \bmod 3600}{60} \right\rfloor : (\Delta t \bmod 60)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:**

Suppose a user's timestamps (in epoch seconds) are:

$$
\mathcal{T}_u = \{ 1715000000, \; 1715000120, \; 1715000480, \; 1715000900 \}
$$

Compute:

$$
t_{first} = 1715000000
$$

$$
t_{last} = 1715000900
$$

$$
T_{session} = 1715000900 - 1715000000 = 900 \text{ seconds}
$$

Convert:

$$
900 \div 3600 = 0 \text{ h}, \quad 900 \bmod 3600 = 900
$$

$$
900 \div 60 = 15 \text{ m}, \quad 900 \bmod 60 = 0 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\therefore \; T_{session}(u) = 0\text{h}:15\text{m}:0\text{s} = 900 \text{ s}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Pageviews per Session

This metric evaluates *engagement depth*. From a preprocessed log, we identify $N_s$ sessions, each containing a multiset of pages.

**Step 1 — Total Pageviews Aggregation:**

$$
PV_{total} = \sum_{s=1}^{N_s} \vert P_s \vert
$$

where $\vert P_s \vert$ is the number of distinct page requests in session $s$.

**Step 2 — Mean Pageviews:**

$$
V_{per\_session} = \dfrac{PV_{total}}{N_s} = \dfrac{1}{N_s} \sum_{s=1}^{N_s} \vert P_s \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Variance (used in clustering algorithms like K-Means):**

$$
\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{N_s} \sum_{s=1}^{N_s} \left( \vert P_s \vert - V_{per\_session} \right)^2
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Bounce Rate from Categorical Session Labels

Let $S$ be partitioned into $S_{single}$ (sessions with exactly **one** pageview) and $S_{multi}$ (sessions with $\geq 2$ pageviews).

$$
S = S_{single} \cup S_{multi}, \quad S_{single} \cap S_{multi} = \emptyset
$$

$$
Bounce\_Rate = \dfrac{\vert S_{single} \vert}{\vert S_{single} \vert + \vert S_{multi} \vert} \times 100
$$

**Numerical Example:** A website recorded **1200** sessions. Of these, **324** sessions accessed only the landing page and left.

$$
Bounce\_Rate = \dfrac{324}{1200} \times 100 = 27\%
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Sources to Mining Tasks

| Source $\rightarrow$ Task | Clustering | Classification | Association | Anomaly |
|:--------------------------|:----------:|:--------------:|:-----------:|:-------:|
| Server Access Logs        | ✅ Session segmentation | ✅ Bot vs Human | ✅ Page co-visit | ✅ DDoS detection |
| Cookies / Client JS       | ✅ User profiling | ✅ Churn prediction | ✅ Cart patterns | ✅ Account takeover |
| Query Logs                | ✅ Query clustering | ✅ Intent classification | ✅ Query rewriting | ✅ Query bombing |
| Application DB            | ✅ RFM segmentation | ✅ Lifetime value | ✅ Cross-sell rules | ✅ Fraud scoring |
| Click-Through Data        | ✅ SERP clustering | ✅ Ad CTR prediction | ✅ Query-doc pairs | ✅ Click injection |

### 3.5 Data Quality Preprocessing — The **4-C Framework**

Every raw usage log must pass through the **4-C preprocessing pipeline** before mining:

1. **Cleaning** — Remove requests from crawlers, bots, failed status codes (`4xx`, `5xx`).
2. **Filtering** — Eliminate multimedia requests (images, CSS, JS) that inflate counts.
3. **Sessionization** — Segment request stream into logical user sessions (time-window heuristic: $\Delta t \leq 30$ min).
4. **Completion** — Fill missing fields (e.g., unknown referrer via `direct traffic` imputation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **30-minute inactivity threshold** is the de facto industry standard (Catledge & Pitkow, 1995) and is the expected answer in KTU theory questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Web Data Sources

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[WEB DATA SOURCES] --> B[Web Content Data]
    A --> C[Web Structure Data]
    A --> D[Web Usage Data]

    B --> B1[Textual: HTML, XML, JSON]
    B --> B2[Multimedia: Images, Video, Audio]
    B --> B3[Structured: DB Records, RDF]

    C --> C1[Hyperlinks: Inlinks, Outlinks]
    C --> C2[DOM Tree Structure]
    C --> C3[Sitemaps and Navigation]

    D --> D1[Server Access Logs]
    D --> D2[Client-Side Cookies]
    D --> D3[Proxy and Firewall Logs]
    D --> D4[Application Server Logs]
    D --> D5[Search Query Logs]

    D1 --> E[KEY ELEMENTS]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E

    E --> E1[IP Address]
    E --> E2[Timestamp]
    E --> E3[Requested URL]
    E --> E4[HTTP Status Code]
    E --> E5[Referrer URL]
    E --> E6[User Agent String]
    E --> E7[Bytes Transferred]
    E --> E8[Cookie or User ID]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Web Usage Data Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Raw Server Log] --> P2[Cleaning Stage]
    P2 --> P3[Filtering Stage]
    P3 --> P4[User Identification]
    P4 --> P5[Sessionization]
    P5 --> P6[Path Completion]
    P6 --> P7[Transaction Identification]
    P7 --> P8[Clean User Sessions DB]
    P8 --> M1[Pattern Discovery]
    P8 --> M2[Clustering Analysis]
    P8 --> M3[Classification Models]
    P8 --> M4[Association Rules]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC1[Web Server] --> CAPT1[Log Collector Daemon]
    SRC2[Application Server] --> CAPT1
    SRC3[Client Browser Cookies] --> CAPT2[JavaScript Beacon]
    CAPT1 --> STG[Staging Storage HDFS]
    CAPT2 --> STG
    STG --> ETL[ETL Preprocessing Engine]
    ETL --> DW[Data Warehouse Star Schema]
    DW --> OLA[Online Analytical Processing]
    DW --> DM[Data Mining Algorithms]
    OLA --> DASH[Analyst Dashboards]
    DM --> REC[Recommendation Engine]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Aid:** The block diagram above traces data flow from heterogeneous sources through a unified staging area into a star-schema warehouse, from which both OLAP queries and DM algorithms draw. This is the canonical **Lambda-style architecture** for web analytics platforms.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

> Differentiate between **Web Content Data** and **Web Usage Data** with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Web Content Data | Web Usage Data |
|:-------|:-----------------|:----------------|
| **Definition** | The actual information payload served to users | The behavioral trace of user interactions with the web |
| **Origin** | Web pages, databases, multimedia files | Server logs, cookies, clickstreams |
| **Example** | Product description on Amazon.in | Log of clicks a user made on that product page |
| **Mining Goal** | Extract knowledge from content | Discover usage patterns and predict behavior |

*(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for clear differentiation with examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> List and briefly explain any **five key elements** of Web Usage Data as recorded in a server access log.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **IP Address** — Pseudonymous identifier of the requesting client (0.5 mark)
2. **Timestamp** — Date and time of the request with millisecond precision (0.5 mark)
3. **Requested URL** — The exact resource (page, image, API endpoint) fetched (0.5 mark)
4. **HTTP Status Code** — Outcome indicator (`200` success, `404` not found, `500` server error) (0.5 mark)
5. **Referrer URL** — The previous page that linked to the current request (0.5 mark)
6. **User Agent String** — Browser, OS, and device metadata (0.5 mark)

*(Any 5 correctly explained = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **three primary sources of web data** used in Web Mining. For each source, give at least two examples and identify the corresponding mining sub-field. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A web server recorded the following access log entries for a single user. Identify the **key elements** present in each entry and compute the **session duration**, **total pageviews**, and **average bytes per request**.

```
192.168.1.10 - - [15/Mar/2024:09:00:00 +0530] "GET /home HTTP/1.1" 200 5400 
"http://google.com/" "Mozilla/5.0"
192.168.1.10 - - [15/Mar/2024:09:02:30 +0530] "GET /products HTTP/1.1" 200 8200 
"http://example.com/home" "Mozilla/5.0"
192.168.1.10 - - [15/Mar/2024:09:05:15 +0530] "GET /contact HTTP/1.1" 200 3100 
"http://example.com/products" "Mozilla/5.0"
```

**(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Three Primary Sources (7 marks):**

| Source | Description | Examples | Mining Sub-field |
|:-------|:------------|:---------|:-----------------|
| **Web Content** | The textual, visual, and multimedia payload of web pages | HTML pages, news articles, product reviews, video metadata | Web Content Mining |
| **Web Structure** | The hyperlink topology and DOM hierarchy linking pages | Inbound/outbound links, sitemaps, `<a href>` tags, navigation menus | Web Structure Mining |
| **Web Usage** | The behavioral trace of users interacting with web resources | Server access logs, cookies, query logs, click-through data | Web Usage Mining |

**Justification (1 mark per source pair, total 6 marks + 1 mark for coherent explanation):**
- *Web Content* mining extracts useful knowledge from the page contents (e.g., sentiment analysis of reviews).
- *Web Structure* mining exploits link topology (e.g., Google's PageRank algorithm).
- *Web Usage* mining studies user behavior patterns (e.g., recommendation systems on Netflix).

**Part (b) — Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Key Elements (2 marks):**

| Element | Value in 1st Entry |
|:--------|:-------------------|
| IP | `192.168.1.10` |
| Timestamp | `15/Mar/2024:09:00:00 +0530` |
| Method | `GET` |
| URL | `/home` |
| Protocol | `HTTP/1.1` |
| Status | `200` |
| Bytes | `5400` |
| Referrer | `http://google.com/` |
| User Agent | `Mozilla/5.0` |

*(Same structure for the other two entries — 2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Session Duration (2 marks):**

$$
t_{first} = 09{:}00{:}00, \quad t_{last} = 09{:}05{:}15
$$

$$
T_{session} = (9 \times 3600 + 5 \times 60 + 15) - (9 \times 3600 + 0 \times 60 + 0)
$$

$$
T_{session} = 5 \times 60 + 15 = 315 \text{ seconds} = 5 \text{ min } 15 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Pageviews (1 mark):**

$$
PV = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Average Bytes per Request (2 marks):**

$$
\bar{B} = \dfrac{5400 + 8200 + 3100}{3} = \dfrac{16700}{3} \approx 5566.67 \text{ bytes}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]*

> **(a)** Discuss the **S³ classification of data types** (Structured, Semi-Structured, Unstructured) on the web. Provide two examples for each type and explain how the choice of data type influences the mining technique used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a website that recorded **5000 total sessions** in a week, of which **1100 were single-page bounces** and **75 requests returned HTTP status $\geq 400$** out of a total of **25000 requests**, compute: **(i)** the Bounce Rate, **(ii)** the Error Rate, and **(iii)** the Bounce Rate's complement (engagement rate). **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — S³ Classification (7 marks):**

| Type | Definition | Example 1 | Example 2 | Mining Technique |
|:-----|:-----------|:----------|:----------|:------------------|
| **Structured** | Conforms to rigid schema, table-based | MySQL user database | RDF triple store | SQL queries, OLAP |
| **Semi-Structured** | Schema embedded in data, flexible | HTML web page | JSON API response | DOM parsing, XPath |
| **Unstructured** | No predefined schema, free-form | Twitter tweets | YouTube video frames | NLP, Deep Learning |

*(2 marks per type — definition + 2 examples; 1 mark for influence on mining technique)*

The choice of data type dictates the preprocessing pipeline: structured data requires minimal cleaning, semi-structured needs schema extraction (XPath, JSON parsing), and unstructured demands heavy NLP preprocessing (tokenization, stemming, embedding).

**Part (b) — Metric Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Bounce Rate (3 marks):**

$$
Bounce\_Rate = \dfrac{\vert S_{single} \vert}{\vert S \vert} \times 100 = \dfrac{1100}{5000} \times 100 = 22\%
$$

**(ii) Error Rate (2 marks):**

$$
ER_{status} = \dfrac{\vert \{ r : \text{status}(r) \geq 400 \} \vert}{n} \times 100 = \dfrac{75}{25000} \times 100 = 0.3\%
$$

**(iii) Engagement Rate (2 marks):**

$$
Engagement = 100\% - Bounce\_Rate = 100 - 22 = 78\%
$$

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing Web Content with Web Structure** — Many students list hyperlinks under "Content". Hyperlinks belong to **Structure**. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
> 2. **Treating Cookies as Server-Side Data** — Cookies are **client-side** and may be disabled, making them *unreliable* for user identification. Examiners expect this caveat. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Forgetting the `User Agent` field** — This is often overlooked but is a *key element* used for bot filtering. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **Wrong unit conversions in time** — Always convert epoch or HH:MM:SS timestamps consistently before subtraction. Mixing formats leads to negative durations. *(−2 marks)*
> 5. **Misclassification of Semi-Structured** — JSON and XML are **semi-structured**, not structured. *(−1 mark)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Three Sources of Web Data:** Content, Structure, Usage.
- ☐ **Three Types of Data (S³):** Structured, Semi-Structured, Unstructured.
- ☐ **Nine Key Elements of Web Usage Data:** IP, User ID/Cookie, Timestamp, URL, Status, Referrer, User Agent, Method, Bytes.
- ☐ **Primary Log Source:** Server access logs in CLF (Combined Log Format).
- ☐ **Session Duration Formula:** $T_{session} = t_{last} - t_{first}$.
- ☐ **Bounce Rate:** Single-page sessions ÷ Total sessions × 100.
- ☐ **Error Rate:** Failed status (≥ 400) ÷ Total requests × 100.
- ☐ **30-minute Inactivity Threshold** = industry-standard sessionization cutoff.
- ☐ **4-C Preprocessing Pipeline:** Cleaning → Filtering → Sessionization → Completion.
- ☐ **Cookie Limitations:** Client-side, can be disabled → unreliable identifier.
- ☐ **80% of web data is Unstructured** — drives the need for NLP and Deep Learning approaches.
- ☐ **Bot Filtering** uses User Agent + request frequency heuristics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Modelling for Web Usage Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Modelling for Web Usage Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Modelling for Web Usage Mining (WUM)** is the systematic process of transforming raw, semi-structured, and heterogeneous web interaction data (e.g., server logs, browser logs, proxy logs, clickstreams) into clean, structured, and analytically-ready representations. It encompasses the construction of abstract data models — relational, transactional, graph-based, or OLAP-based — that capture the navigational, behavioral, and temporal patterns of users interacting with a web-based system.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Outcome CO4: *Apply data modelling techniques to pre-process and structure web usage data for pattern discovery*), this stage forms the **bridge between data collection and knowledge discovery**, sitting at the heart of the three-stage WUM pipeline: **Preprocessing → Pattern Discovery → Pattern Analysis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** The topic explicitly covers the stages of data preparation — data cleaning, user identification, session identification, path completion, and transaction identification — alongside the formal data models (relational, transactional, graph) used to represent usage data for downstream mining algorithms.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **shopping mall** with hundreds of stores, thousands of visitors, and dozens of CCTV cameras recording every movement. The raw video footage is the **unprocessed web log**. By itself, the footage is just pixels — useless for business decisions. To extract value, mall analysts must:

1. **Clean the footage** (remove glitches, irrelevant frames) — *Data Cleaning*
2. **Identify each unique shopper** using facial recognition or loyalty cards — *User Identification*
3. **Group a shopper's movements into a single "shopping trip"** (entry → exit) — *Session Identification*
4. **Fill in blind spots** when the camera misses a corridor — *Path Completion*
5. **Aggregate trips** into "transactions" (what was bought, in what order) — *Transaction Identification*
6. **Build a structured database** of all trips for analysis — *Data Modelling*

Web Usage Mining operates on exactly the same principle, but with **web pages, users, and sessions** replacing stores, shoppers, and visits. The data model is the **structured ledger** that analysts query to find patterns like "users who bought X also viewed Y."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Without proper data modelling, the mining algorithms (clustering, association rule mining, sequential pattern discovery) would either fail outright or return garbage patterns. As the saying goes: **"Garbage In, Garbage Out (GIGO)."**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value/Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **W3C Extended Log Format (ELF)** | Standard web log schema | Most widely used log format (recommended by KTU) |
| **Common Log Format (CLF)** | 7 fields (host, ident, authuser, date, request, status, bytes) | Older de facto standard |
| **Cookie TTL** | Typically **30–365 days** | Used for long-term user tracking |
| **Session Timeout** | Industry standard: **30 minutes** of inactivity | Defines session boundary |
| **HTTP Status Codes** | **2xx** (success), **3xx** (redirect), **4xx** (client error), **5xx** (server error) | Used in data cleaning filters |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage WUM Pipeline

Data modelling does not exist in isolation. It is the **front-end of a three-stage pipeline**:

1. **Preprocessing** — Cleansing and structuring raw data (the focus of this module)
2. **Pattern Discovery** — Applying statistical, ML, and data mining techniques
3. **Pattern Analysis** — Filtering, visualizing, and interpreting discovered patterns

## 2.2 Phases of Preprocessing (The Core of Data Modelling)

### Phase 1: Data Cleaning

- **Objective:** Remove irrelevant, noisy, and unreliable entries from server logs.
- **Operations:**
  - Remove entries with file extension suffixes like `.gif`, `.jpg`, `.css`, `.js` (multimedia/auxiliary requests that do not represent content views).
  - Remove failed HTTP status codes (e.g., **404 Not Found**, **500 Internal Server Error**).
  - Remove robot/crawler requests (identified by user-agent strings containing "bot", "spider", "crawler").
- **Why it matters:** Reduces log size by **30–80%** and prevents noise propagation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask "List the file extensions filtered during data cleaning." Memorize: `.gif, .jpg, .jpeg, .bmp, .css, .js, .ico, .png, .swf`.

### Phase 2: User Identification

- **Objective:** Distinguish unique visitors from repeat visitors.
- **Techniques (in order of reliability):**
  1. **Cookies** (most reliable, but privacy-sensitive)
  2. **User Authentication** (login IDs — best for personalization)
  3. **IP Address + User-Agent** combination (heuristic, no login required)
  4. **IP Address only** (least reliable, due to NAT/proxies)
- **Conflict resolution rule:** If a new IP address appears, treat as a new user. If a known IP appears with a different user-agent, create a new user (assumes multiple users on one machine).

### Phase 3: Session Identification

- **Objective:** Group a user's page requests into logical "visits" or sessions.
- **Two standard heuristics:**
  1. **Time-oriented:** If the time gap between two consecutive page requests exceeds a threshold (default **30 minutes**), a new session begins.
  2. **Navigation-oriented:** If a user navigates backward (revisits a page) or spends more than a threshold time on a page (**10–15 minutes**), a new session starts.

$$\Delta t = t_{i+1} - t_i$$

- If $\Delta t > T_{threshold}$ (typically **30 min**) → **New Session**

### Phase 4: Path Completion

- **Objective:** Reconstruct missing pages in a user's click path (caused by cached pages, back-button usage, or POST requests).
- **Methods:**
  - Referrer-based completion (use HTTP `Referer` header to chain pages)
  - Site topology consultation (consult the web graph to find likely missing pages)
  - Maximal Forward Reference (MFR) algorithm

### Phase 5: Transaction Identification

- **Objective:** Convert sessions into meaningful transactions (subsets of a session) for mining.
- **Methods:**
  - **Maximal Forward Reference (MFR):** Segment session at backward movements
  - **Reference Length:** Segments based on time spent on each page
  - **Variable-length transaction:** Combines MFR and reference length

## 2.3 Data Models for Web Usage Data

### Model A: Relational Model

- Tables: `Users`, `Sessions`, `PageViews`, `Transactions`
- Suitable for **OLAP** and SQL-based pattern mining
- Supports multi-dimensional analysis (e.g., sessions by region × day)

### Model B: Transactional Model

- Each user session is a **transaction** (set of page views)
- Used directly as input to **association rule mining** (e.g., Apriori algorithm)

$$\text{Transaction} = \{P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n\}$$

### Model C: Graph-Based Model

- Represents usage data as a **directed graph** $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = pages and $E$ = navigation links
- Used for **path analysis** and identifying bottlenecks in site navigation

### Model D: OLAP-based Model

- Multi-dimensional cube with dimensions: **Time, URL, User, Session**
- Supports drill-down/roll-up operations on usage patterns

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula/Metric | Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Session Timeout Rule | $\Delta t = t_{i+1} - t_i > 30$ min | Session identification |
| Log Size Reduction | $\eta = \frac{L_{raw} - L_{clean}}{L_{raw}} \times 100\%$ | Data cleaning efficiency |
| Support (Association) | $\sigma(X) = \frac{\vert\{T \in D : X \subseteq T\}\vert}{\vert D\vert}$ | Frequent page set identification |
| Confidence (Association) | $C(X \rightarrow Y) = \frac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{\sigma(X)}$ | Rule strength |
| Average Session Length | $\bar{S} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert S_i \vert$ | Usage intensity metric |
| Bounce Rate | $BR = \frac{N_{single-page}}{N_{total\ sessions}}$ | Session quality metric |

> [!NOTE]
> The support and confidence formulas listed above are the same ones used in the **Apriori algorithm**, which is applied in the next module (Pattern Discovery). Understanding them now gives you a head start.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Session identification powers recommendation engines.
- **Web Security (SIEM systems):** User identification detects anomalous login patterns.
- **UX Design:** Path completion helps designers identify "dead ends" causing user drop-off.
- **SEO & Marketing:** Transaction identification enables attribution modelling (which page gets credit for a conversion?).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: End-to-End Preprocessing

Consider the following raw web server log entries (CLF format):

```
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:57:12] "GET /products.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2048
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:45:00] "GET /products.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2048
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:46:30] "GET /contact.html HTTP/1.1" 404 512
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:47:00] "GET /about.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1536
203.0.113.78 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:58:00] "GET /logo.gif HTTP/1.1" 200 8192
```

### Step 1: Data Cleaning

Apply cleaning filters:

- Remove `.gif` request → Entry 6 removed
- Remove **404** status → Entry 4 removed

**Cleaned Log:**

```
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:57:12] "GET /products.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2048
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:45:00] "GET /products.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2048
203.0.113.45 - - [10/Oct/2024:14:47:00] "GET /about.html HTTP/1.1" 200 1536
```

### Step 2: User Identification

- IP `203.0.113.45` → **User A**
- IP `203.0.113.78` → was filtered out (logo.gif request)

**Result:** Only User A exists in this dataset.

### Step 3: Session Identification

Compute time deltas between consecutive requests:

$$
\Delta t_1 = 13{:}57{:}12 - 13{:}55{:}36 = 96 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\Delta t_2 = 14{:}45{:}00 - 13{:}57{:}12 = 47 \text{ minutes } 48 \text{ seconds} = 2868 \text{ seconds}
$$

Apply the 30-minute threshold:

- $\Delta t_1 = 96$ s $< 1800$ s → **Same session**
- $\Delta t_2 = 2868$ s $> 1800$ s → **New session begins**

**Session 1:** `[/index.html, /products.html]` (timestamps 13:55 to 13:57)
**Session 2:** `[/products.html, /about.html]` (timestamps 14:45 to 14:47)

### Step 4: Path Completion

Assume `/products.html` (in Session 2) was reached via a missing referrer. The HTTP `Referer` header indicates the previous page was `/index.html` (impossible, since the site topology shows the path is `index.html → category.html → products.html`). Path completion inserts `category.html`:

**Completed Session 2:** `[/index.html, /category.html, /products.html, /about.html]`

### Step 5: Transaction Identification

Apply **MFR (Maximal Forward Reference)**:

- Session 2's reference sequence shows no backward navigation.
- MFR for Session 2 = `[/index.html, /category.html, /products.html, /about.html]`

**Final Transaction (Session 2):** $\{ \text{index}, \text{category}, \text{products}, \text{about} \}$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Session Identification Heuristics

| Heuristic | Threshold | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time-based (30 min)** | $\Delta t > 30$ min | Simple, industry standard | May split "focused" sessions |
| **Navigation-based** | Backward ref = new session | Reflects user intent | Requires topology data |
| **Hybrid** | Time OR navigation | Most accurate | More complex to implement |

## 3.3 Derivation: Log Size Reduction Efficiency

Let $L_{raw}$ = raw log size, $L_{clean}$ = cleaned log size.

$$
\eta = \frac{L_{raw} - L_{clean}}{L_{raw}} \times 100\%
$$

**Example:** If a 10 MB raw log is reduced to 4 MB after cleaning:

$$
\eta = \frac{10 - 4}{10} \times 100\% = 60\%
$$

This means **60% of log entries were noise** (multimedia files, failed requests, crawlers).

## 3.4 Multi-Dimensional OLAP Model Schema

The OLAP cube for web usage has the following dimensions:

- **Time dimension:** Year → Quarter → Month → Day → Hour
- **User dimension:** Anonymous / Registered → Demographics
- **Page dimension:** Category → Subcategory → URL
- **Session dimension:** Session ID → Transaction ID

**Measures (fact table):**

- Page views
- Session duration
- Bounce count
- Conversion count

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "design a data model for web usage mining," include all four dimensions in your OLAP schema diagram. Examiners reward dimensionality.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level WUM Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Web Logs] --> B[Data Cleaning]
    B --> C[User Identification]
    C --> D[Session Identification]
    D --> E[Path Completion]
    E --> F[Transaction Identification]
    F --> G[Structured Data Model]
    G --> H[Pattern Discovery]
    H --> I[Pattern Analysis]
    I --> J[Knowledge]
```

## 4.2 Preprocessing Phase Flow (Detailed Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Raw Log File: CLF/ELF] --> P2[Remove Failed HTTP Codes]
    P2 --> P3[Remove Multimedia Requests]
    P3 --> P4[Remove Robot/Crawler Agents]
    P4 --> P5[Cleaned Log]
    P5 --> P6{User Identification Method}
    P6 -->|Cookies| P7[Cookie-based User ID]
    P6 -->|Auth| P8[Login-based User ID]
    P6 -->|IP+UA| P9[Heuristic User ID]
    P7 --> P10[User Sessions]
    P8 --> P10
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11{Session Boundary Detection}
    P11 -->|Time > 30 min| P12[New Session]
    P11 -->|Navigation Logic| P12
    P12 --> P13[Path Completion: Referrer + Topology]
    P13 --> P14[Transaction Identification: MFR]
    P14 --> P15[Final Transaction Database]
```

## 4.3 OLAP Cube Architecture for Web Usage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DIM_TIME["Time Dimension"]
        T1[Year]
        T2[Quarter]
        T3[Month]
        T4[Day]
    end
    subgraph DIM_USER["User Dimension"]
        U1[Visitor Type]
        U2[Geography]
        U3[Browser]
    end
    subgraph DIM_PAGE["Page Dimension"]
        P1[Category]
        P2[Subcategory]
        P3[URL]
    end
    subgraph FACT["Fact Table: Usage Measures"]
        F1[Page Views]
        F2[Session Duration]
        F3[Bounce Count]
        F4[Conversion Count]
    end
    DIM_TIME --> FACT
    DIM_USER --> FACT
    DIM_PAGE --> FACT
```

## 4.4 Graph-Based Navigation Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1((Home)) --> N2((Category))
    N2 --> N3((Product A))
    N2 --> N4((Product B))
    N3 --> N5((Add to Cart))
    N4 --> N5
    N5 --> N6((Checkout))
    N1 --> N7((About))
    N7 --> N1
    N1 --> N8((Contact))
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** This graph model represents a typical e-commerce site. Algorithms like **PageRank** (adapted for usage) and **HITS** can be applied to identify "hub" pages (e.g., `/Category`) and "authority" pages (e.g., `/Product A`).

## 4.5 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Data Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the mining goal?}
    Q1 -->|Association Rules| M1[Transactional Model]
    Q1 -->|Path Analysis| M2[Graph Model]
    Q1 -->|Multi-dimensional Analysis| M3[OLAP Model]
    Q1 -->|SQL Querying| M4[Relational Model]
    M1 --> R[Optimal Representation]
    M2 --> R
    M3 --> R
    M4 --> R
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q: List and briefly explain the main phases of data preprocessing in web usage mining.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The main phases of data preprocessing are:

1. **Data Cleaning** (1 mark): Removal of irrelevant entries such as failed HTTP requests, multimedia file accesses (`.gif`, `.jpg`, `.css`), and robot/crawler traffic to reduce noise.

2. **User Identification** (1 mark): Distinguishing unique users through cookies, login authentication, or heuristics (IP + User-Agent).

3. **Session, Path Completion, and Transaction Identification** (1 mark): Grouping page views into sessions using a 30-minute timeout, reconstructing missing pages using referrers/site topology, and segmenting sessions into transactions using MFR or reference length.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
**Q: Differentiate between the Common Log Format (CLF) and Extended Log Format (ELF).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | CLF | ELF |
|---|---|---|
| **Fields** | 7 (fixed) | Variable (extensible) |
| **Custom Fields** | Not supported | Supported via directives |
| **Standardization** | De facto | W3C-recommended |
| **Use Case** | Legacy servers | Modern web analytics |

CLF is a **simple, fixed** format with fields like `host, ident, authuser, date, request, status, bytes`. ELF is a **flexible, W3C-standardized** format that allows administrators to define custom fields (e.g., referrer, user-agent, cookie), making it richer for web usage mining.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Module Internal Choice – Option A)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various techniques used for **user identification** in web usage mining. Discuss the merits and demerits of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Maximal Forward Reference (MFR) algorithm** for transaction identification. Apply it to the following session: $\{A, B, C, D, C, E, F, B, G, H\}$ and identify the resulting transactions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

User identification aims to distinguish unique visitors. Four primary techniques exist (arranged by reliability):

1. **Cookie-based identification** (2 marks):
   - A unique identifier is stored in the user's browser as a small text file.
   - **Merits:** Most reliable across sessions; survives multiple visits.
   - **Demerits:** Privacy concerns; users can disable cookies; not available for first-time visitors.

2. **User authentication (login-based)** (2 marks):
   - Users register and log in; user ID is explicit.
   - **Merits:** Highly accurate; supports personalization.
   - **Demerits:** Limited to registered users; many users browse anonymously.

3. **IP + User-Agent combination** (2 marks):
   - Heuristic rule: same IP + same User-Agent = same user.
   - **Merits:** Works without cookies or login.
   - **Demerits:** Fails for NAT, proxies, or shared devices.

4. **IP address only** (1 mark):
   - Coarsest heuristic.
   - **Merits:** Simplest to implement.
   - **Demerits:** Many users may share an IP (corporate networks, ISPs).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**MFR Algorithm (3 marks):**
The MFR algorithm segments a session at every backward reference. A transaction begins at the start of a session or after a backward movement, and ends at the next backward movement or the session's end.

**Application to** $\{A, B, C, D, C, E, F, B, G, H\}$ **(4 marks):**

Step-by-step trace:

- Start at A
- A → B (forward) → C (forward) → D (forward) → **C (backward, already visited)**
- **First MFR ends at D**
- C → E (forward) → F (forward) → **B (backward)**
- **Second MFR ends at F**
- B → G (forward) → H (forward) → end of session
- **Third MFR ends at H**

**Resulting Transactions:**

$$
T_1 = \{A, B, C, D\}
$$

$$
T_2 = \{C, E, F\}
$$

$$
T_3 = \{B, G, H\}
$$

---

### Question B (Module Internal Choice – Option B)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **OLAP-based data model** for web usage mining. List its key dimensions and measures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A web server log contains 8,00,000 raw entries. After data cleaning, the following were removed:
- 2,00,000 multimedia requests
- 50,000 failed HTTP status code requests
- 1,50,000 robot/crawler requests

Calculate the **log size reduction efficiency** $\eta$. Discuss why data cleaning is critical before applying mining algorithms. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The OLAP-based data model represents web usage data as a **multi-dimensional cube** enabling fast analytical queries.

**Key dimensions (4 marks):**

- **Time dimension:** Hierarchy: Year → Quarter → Month → Day → Hour
- **User dimension:** Visitor type (new/returning), geography, browser, OS
- **Page dimension:** Category → Subcategory → URL
- **Session dimension:** Session ID, Transaction ID

**Measures (fact table) (2 marks):**

- Page view count
- Session duration
- Bounce count
- Conversion count

**Diagram (1 mark):** A 3D cube with dimensions on axes and measures in the cell intersections (refer to SECTION 4.3 of these notes for the architecture).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given data (3 marks):**

$$
L_{raw} = 8{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Removed} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
L_{clean} = 8{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000
$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\eta = \frac{L_{raw} - L_{clean}}{L_{raw}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

**Discussion of importance (2 marks):**

Data cleaning is critical because:

1. **Noise reduction:** Multimedia and bot requests are not representative of genuine user behavior, and including them skews pattern discovery.
2. **Computational efficiency:** A 50% reduction halves memory and processing time for downstream mining algorithms.
3. **Pattern quality:** Cleaner data leads to more accurate association rules and clusters; the **GIGO principle** dictates that poor preprocessing yields meaningless patterns.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Session vs. Transaction:** A *session* is the entire user visit, while a *transaction* is a segment of a session (one MFR). Examiners deduct **2 marks** if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting to filter status codes:** Many students forget to remove **4xx/5xx** HTTP codes in the cleaning step. This is a **favourite 2-mark question**.
> 3. **MFR boundary mistake:** The MFR transaction **includes** the page where the backward reference occurs. For example, in $T_1 = \{A, B, C, D\}$, the page D is the last *forward* page before the backward jump to C. Marking it as $\{A, B, C\}$ will lose 1 mark.
> 4. **OLAP diagram must show measures:** Drawing only the dimensions without the central fact table (measures) is incomplete. Examiners expect a **complete star/snowflake schema sketch**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for the night before the exam):**

- ✅ **Three WUM stages:** Preprocessing → Pattern Discovery → Pattern Analysis (Data Modelling is in **Stage 1**).
- ✅ **Five preprocessing phases:** Cleaning → User ID → Session ID → Path Completion → Transaction ID.
- ✅ **Cleaning filter extensions:** `.gif, .jpg, .jpeg, .bmp, .css, .js, .ico, .png`.
- ✅ **Cleaning status codes:** Remove all **non-2xx** (especially **4xx** and **5xx**).
- ✅ **Session timeout:** **30 minutes** of inactivity = new session.
- ✅ **Four user ID methods (ranked by reliability):** Cookie > Login > IP+UA > IP.
- ✅ **MFR algorithm:** Split session at every backward reference.
- ✅ **Four data models:** Relational, Transactional, Graph, OLAP.
- ✅ **OLAP dimensions:** Time, User, Page, Session.
- ✅ **OLAP measures:** Page views, Session duration, Bounces, Conversions.
- ✅ **Log size efficiency formula:** $\eta = \frac{L_{raw} - L_{clean}}{L_{raw}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **CLF = 7 fields (fixed); ELF = variable (W3C standard).**
- ✅ **GIGO principle:** Clean data in = meaningful patterns out.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discovery and Analysis of Web Usage Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Discovery and Analysis of Web Usage Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Web Usage Mining (WUM)** is the automated discovery and analysis of patterns in clickstream data, server logs, browser histories, and user interaction traces to extract meaningful behavioral insights about how users navigate, consume, and interact with web resources.
