# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]
**Define Web Usage Mining. List any four techniques used in the pattern discovery phase.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Web Usage Mining** is the process of discovering and analyzing patterns in user interaction data collected from web servers, browser logs, and user sessions to extract behavioral insights that can be applied to personalization, recommendation, and website optimization.
>
> **Four pattern discovery techniques:**
> 1. **Statistical Analysis** — computing aggregate metrics such as pageview counts, average session duration, and traffic distributions.
> 2. **Association Rules** — discovering co-occurrence relationships between pages visited in the same session (e.g., Apriori algorithm).
> 3. **Clustering** — grouping similar users, pages, or sessions into clusters (e.g., K-Means, DBSCAN).
> 4. **Sequential Pattern Mining** — identifying temporally ordered page visit patterns (e.g., PrefixSpan, GSP).
>
> *(Other valid techniques: Classification, Dependency Modeling.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]
**Differentiate between Association Rule Mining and Sequential Pattern Mining with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Association Rule Mining | Sequential Pattern Mining |
|:-------|:------------------------|:--------------------------|
| **Order** | Ignores the order of page visits | **Preserves** the temporal order |
| **Output Form** | $\{A, B\} \Rightarrow C$ | $\langle A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rangle$ |
| **Example** | "Users who visit A and B **also** visit C" | "Users visit A **then** B **then** C" |
| **Algorithm** | Apriori, FP-Growth | PrefixSpan, GSP, SPADE |

**Concrete Example:**
- **Association Rule:** $\{\text{Home, Product}\} \Rightarrow \{\text{Cart}\}$ with confidence 0.6 → 60% of users who visit Home and Product (in any order) also visit Cart.
- **Sequential Pattern:** $\langle \text{Home} \rightarrow \text{Product} \rightarrow \text{Cart} \rightarrow \text{Checkout} \rangle$ with support 0.25 → 25% of users visit these pages in this exact order.

**Key Distinction:** Association rules answer "**What pages are visited together?**" while sequential patterns answer "**What is the order of page visits?**"

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various techniques used in the **Pattern Discovery** phase of Web Usage Mining with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **OLAP** mechanism for pattern analysis. How does a **Knowledge Cube** differ from a **Data Cube**?

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Pattern Discovery** phase applies algorithms to preprocessed web usage data to extract meaningful behavioral patterns. The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **six core techniques**:

**1. Statistical Analysis:**
This is the simplest technique, involving computation of aggregate statistics over sessions. Examples include:
- Mean, median, and mode of session durations
- Pageview frequency distribution
- Browser/OS distribution histograms
- Bounce rate, exit rate per page

It is fast, easily interpretable, and forms the baseline for all other analyses.

**2. Association Rule Mining:**
Discovers **unordered co-occurrence** patterns between pages. Uses metrics:
- **Support** — fraction of sessions containing the itemset
- **Confidence** — conditional probability
- **Lift** — degree of correlation above chance

**Example:** $\{\text{Laptop}\} \Rightarrow \{\text{Mouse}\}$ with support 0.15, confidence 0.70, lift 1.5.

**3. Clustering:**
Groups similar entities (users, pages, sessions) into clusters. Two main variants:
- **User clustering** — for personalization
- **Page clustering** — for site structure analysis

**Example:** K-Means clustering groups 10,000 users into 5 segments based on browsing behavior.

**4. Classification:**
A supervised learning approach that assigns labels to sessions. Examples:
- Predicting "buyer" vs. "non-buyer" using decision trees
- Detecting bots using Naive Bayes

**5. Sequential Pattern Mining:**
Discovers **ordered** navigation patterns. Example:
$\langle \text{Home} \rightarrow \text{Search} \rightarrow \text{Product} \rightarrow \text{Cart} \rangle$ with support 0.20.

**6. Dependency Modeling:**
Models **probabilistic transitions** between pages using Markov Chains or HMMs. Used for next-page prediction and prefetching.

**Example:** $P(\text{Cart} \mid \text{Product}) = 0.65$ means 65% of users visiting Product proceed to Cart.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)** is a multidimensional analytical framework that supports interactive, ad-hoc querying of web usage data modeled as a **data cube**.

**OLAP Operations:**
- **Roll-up** — aggregate to higher hierarchy level (Day → Month → Quarter)
- **Drill-down** — decompose to finer level (Quarter → Month → Day → Hour)
- **Slice** — fix one dimension (e.g., only Mondays)
- **Dice** — fix multiple dimensions
- **Pivot** — rotate cube axes for different perspectives

**Web Data Cube Dimensions:** Page, Time, User Demographics, Server, Session attributes.

**Difference between Data Cube and Knowledge Cube:**

| Aspect | Data Cube | Knowledge Cube |
|:-------|:----------|:----------------|
| **Contents** | Raw aggregated counts (e.g., number of pageviews) | Mined patterns (rules, clusters, sequences) |
| **Question Type** | "How many?" | "What patterns exist?" |
| **Granularity** | Pre-aggregated numeric facts | Pattern-level abstractions |
| **Construction** | Direct from cleaned log data | From output of pattern discovery algorithms |
| **Example** | Cube cell = 5000 pageviews to /Product in Q1 | Cube cell = Association rule with confidence > 0.7 in Q1 |

The **Knowledge Cube** is essentially a **pattern warehouse** that allows users to query discovered knowledge at multiple levels of abstraction, enabling OLAP-style operations over mined patterns rather than raw counts.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **Association Rule Mining** and **Sequential Pattern Mining** in detail. Explain **Support, Confidence, and Lift** metrics with formulas.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Dependency Modeling** using **Markov Chains** and **Hidden Markov Models** for web usage pattern discovery.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Association Rule Mining:**
Association Rule Mining discovers **co-occurrence relationships** between items (in WUM, items are web pages) in a session database. A rule has the form $X \Rightarrow Y$ where $X$ and $Y$ are disjoint itemsets.

**Key Metrics:**

$$
\text{Support}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{N}
$$

$$
\text{Confidence}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\sigma(X \cup Y)}{\sigma(X)}
$$

$$
\text{Lift}(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{\text{Support}(X \cup Y)}{\text{Support}(X) \times \text{Support}(Y)}
$$

- **Support** measures the rule's coverage in the dataset. Rules with low support are typically discarded.
- **Confidence** measures the rule's reliability. A confidence of 0.8 means 80% of sessions containing $X$ also contain $Y$.
- **Lift** measures the **strength of association** beyond random chance. Lift = 1 implies independence; Lift > 1 implies positive correlation; Lift < 1 implies negative correlation (substitution).

**Algorithms:** Apriori, FP-Growth, ECLAT.

**Sequential Pattern Mining:**
Discovers **temporally ordered** subsequences of page visits. A sequence $S = \langle s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n \rangle$ is a sequential pattern if it appears in at least `min_support` fraction of all sessions.

**Key Metrics:**

$$
\text{Support}(S) = \frac{\text{Sessions containing } S}{\text{Total sessions}}
$$

**Algorithms:** AprioriAll, GSP (adds time constraints), PrefixSpan (pattern growth, scalable), SPADE (vertical format), CloSpan (closed sequences), FreeSpan.

**Example:**
Sessions: $\{ \langle H, P, C, O \rangle, \langle H, S, P, C \rangle, \langle H, P, O \rangle, \langle H, S, P \rangle, \langle H, P, C \rangle, \langle H, S, O \rangle \}$

Pattern $\langle H, P, C \rangle$ appears in S1, S2, S5 → Support = 3/6 = 0.5

**Key Distinction from Association Rules:** Sequential patterns respect **time order** and can encode **temporal constraints** (e.g., minimum/maximum gap between events).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Dependency Modeling** captures the **probabilistic structure** of user navigation by modeling how the next page visit depends on previous page visits.

**Markov Chains — First Order:**
A first-order Markov chain assumes the next page depends **only** on the current page:

$$
P(p_{t+1} \mid p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_t) = P(p_{t+1} \mid p_t)
$$

**Transition Probability:**

$$
P(p_{t+1} \mid p_t) = \frac{C(p_t, p_{t+1})}{C(p_t)}
$$

**Higher-Order Markov Chains (k-th order):**
The next page depends on the previous $k$ pages:

$$
P(p_{t+1} \mid p_t, p_{t-1}, \ldots, p_{t-k+1}) = \frac{C(p_t, p_{t-1}, \ldots, p_{t-k+1}, p_{t+1})}{C(p_t, p_{t-1}, \ldots, p_{t-k+1})}
$$

**Trade-off:** Higher-order chains capture more context but require exponentially more data to estimate parameters reliably.

**Markov Chain Limitations:**
- "Memoryless" assumption may be too restrictive
- State space can explode for large websites
- Cannot model **latent** user intentions

**Hidden Markov Models (HMMs):**
HMMs address Markov chain limitations by introducing **hidden states** that represent unobservable user intentions or goals.

**HMM Components:**
- **Hidden States** $S = \{s_1, \ldots, s_N\}$ — user intent stages (e.g., "browsing", "comparing", "deciding")
- **Observations** $O = \{o_1, \ldots, o_M\}$ — actual pages viewed
- **Initial probabilities** $\pi_i = P(s_1 = i)$
- **Transition matrix** $A_{ij} = P(s_{t+1} = j \mid s_t = i)$
- **Emission matrix** $B_{ij} = P(o_t = j \mid s_t = i)$

**Three HMM Problems:**
1. **Evaluation** — given a model and an observation sequence, compute $P(O \mid \lambda)$ (Forward algorithm)
2. **Decoding** — find the most likely hidden state sequence (Viterbi algorithm)
3. **Learning** — adjust parameters to maximize $P(O \mid \lambda)$ (Baum-Welch algorithm)

**Application in WUM:**
- **Next-page prediction** for browser prefetching
- **User intent inference** for personalized content
- **Anomaly detection** — flag sessions with low-probability page sequences
- **Adaptive website restructuring** based on dominant state transitions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write formulas for Support, Confidence, and Lift without **defining the notation** (what does $N$ represent? what is $\sigma$?). Always define every variable.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **Confidence with Lift**. Confidence is a probability ($0$ to $1$); Lift is a ratio (can be any positive number). Lift = 1 means independence, NOT confidence = 1.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing "HMM is a Markov Chain" — HMM is a **probabilistic model with hidden states**; a simple Markov chain has **observable states**. Always emphasize the "hidden" nature of HMM states.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to mention that **dependency modeling captures temporal/order information**, which is a key differentiator from clustering and association rules.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Listing algorithms (Apriori, K-Means, etc.) without explaining **how** they apply to web usage data. KTU expects a **WUM-specific** explanation, not a generic data mining lecture.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Examinations**

- ✅ **Web Usage Mining** focuses on **user behavior** from web interaction data (logs, cookies, sessions).
- ✅ The complete pipeline has **three phases**: Preprocessing → Pattern Discovery → Pattern Analysis.
- ✅ **Six core Pattern Discovery techniques**: Statistical Analysis, Association Rules, Clustering, Classification, Sequential Patterns, Dependency Modeling.
- ✅ **Association Rules** answer "**What pages are visited TOGETHER?**" (no order).
- ✅ **Sequential Patterns** answer "**What pages are visited in ORDER?**" (order matters).
- ✅ **Support** = fraction of sessions containing the itemset. **Confidence** = conditional probability. **Lift** = correlation strength above chance (>1 positive, =1 independent, <1 negative).
- ✅ **Clustering** is **unsupervised** (no labels). **Classification** is **supervised** (needs labels).
- ✅ **Cosine similarity** is preferred over Euclidean distance for sparse, high-dimensional web session vectors.
- ✅ **Markov Chains** model observable page transitions. **HMMs** model **hidden user intent states** that emit observable page views.
- ✅ **First-order Markov** depends only on the current page. **Higher-order Markov** depends on $k$ previous pages (more context, more data needed).
- ✅ **Pattern Analysis** uses **OLAP, Data/Knowledge Cubes, Visualization, and Knowledge Query Mechanisms**.
- ✅ **Data Cube** stores aggregated counts. **Knowledge Cube** stores mined patterns at multiple abstraction levels.
- ✅ **OLAP operations**: Roll-up, Drill-down, Slice, Dice, Pivot.
- ✅ **Visualization tools**: bar charts, pie charts, heat maps, Sankey diagrams, treemaps, parallel coordinates, graph visualizations.
- ✅ **Applications**: Personalization, recommendation systems, e-commerce strategy, website optimization, churn prediction, anomaly detection.
- ✅ **Common algorithms** to memorize: Apriori, FP-Growth (association); K-Means, DBSCAN (clustering); C4.5, Naive Bayes, SVM (classification); PrefixSpan, GSP (sequential); Markov Chain, HMM (dependency).
- ✅ **Always define notation** before writing formulas in KTU exams. **Always provide a WUM-specific context** when discussing generic data mining techniques.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Discovery and Analysis of Web Usage Patterns**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications - Recommender Systems and Collaborative Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications - Recommender Systems and Collaborative Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommender System (RS):** A specialized subclass of information filtering systems that seek to predict the **"rating"** or **"preference"** a user would give to an item. It is formally defined as a function $f: U \times I \rightarrow R$ that maps a pair of (User, Item) to a predicted utility score, where $U$ is the set of all users, $I$ is the set of all items, and $R$ is the rating domain (e.g., 1 to 5 stars).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collaborative Filtering (CF):** A core technique within recommender systems that generates personalized recommendations by **aggregating the preferences of multiple users**, under the assumption that users who agreed in the past will agree in the future. It relies solely on the user-item interaction matrix (ratings, clicks, purchases) and does **not** require the content/description of items.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Movie Club Friend"**
> Imagine you join a small film club. You watch 3 movies and tell your friend **Alex** your honest opinions. Alex notices that you and **Sam** gave nearly identical ratings to the same 3 movies. The next weekend, Sam watches a thriller called *Inception* and absolutely loves it (gives 5/5). What does Alex naturally do? **Alex recommends *Inception* to you**, not because he knows the genre, cast, or plot of the movie, but purely because **"people with similar taste to you liked it."**
>
> This is the exact intuition behind **Collaborative Filtering** — **"Wisdom of the Crowd + Taste Similarity."** It mimics how humans naturally take suggestions from like-minded peers rather than from the item's description (which would be *content-based filtering*).

## 1.2 Classification of Recommender Systems

| Paradigm | Core Idea | Data Used | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Content-Based Filtering** | Recommend items similar to those the user liked before | Item features (genre, text, tags) | News article recommendation by TF-IDF |
| **Collaborative Filtering** | Recommend items liked by users with similar taste | User-Item interaction matrix only | Netflix, Amazon rating-based suggestions |
| **Hybrid Recommender** | Combine both content + collaborative signals | Both item features and interaction matrix | Spotify (audio features + listening history) |
| **Knowledge-Based** | Use explicit domain knowledge/rules | Constraints and user requirements | Real-estate / camera advisor systems |
| **Demographic-Based** | Use demographic clusters | Age, location, gender | Generic "people in your age group also bought..." |

## 1.3 Sub-Taxonomy of Collaborative Filtering

> [!NOTE]
> CF is further divided into two principal branches that the KTU syllabus emphasizes:
> - **Memory-Based (Neighbourhood) CF** — Uses the entire user-item database to make predictions through similarity computations (no model training). Divided into **User-Based CF** and **Item-Based CF**.
> - **Model-Based CF** — Builds a predictive model offline from the ratings data using machine learning / matrix factorization (e.g., SVD, PMF, Neural CF). Predictions are then generated from the trained model.
>
> Standard metrics used in literature: **MAE**, **RMSE**, **Precision@k**, **Recall@k**, **F1-score**, **NDCG**, and **MAP** (Mean Average Precision).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Memory-Based Collaborative Filtering — Operational Walk-Through

Memory-based CF operates directly on the user-item rating matrix. It has two flavours:

### 2.1.1 User-Based Collaborative Filtering

The fundamental hypothesis: *If user A and user B have highly correlated rating behaviour in the past, then a future item rated by B can be predicted for A.*

**Step 1 — Build the User-Item Matrix**
Construct an $m \times n$ matrix $R$, where rows = users, columns = items, and cells = ratings. Most cells are **empty (sparse)** because no user rates everything.

**Step 2 — Compute User-User Similarity**
Choose a similarity metric (cosine, Pearson, adjusted cosine). For every pair $(u, v)$, compute $sim(u, v)$.

**Step 3 — Identify Neighbourhood**
For target user $u$, select top-$K$ most similar users forming the neighbourhood $N(u) = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_K\}$.

**Step 4 — Predict the Missing Rating**
Aggregate the ratings of neighbours, weighted by similarity.

### 2.1.2 Item-Based Collaborative Filtering

The hypothesis: *If item X and item Y received similar ratings from the same set of users, then a user who liked X will probably like Y too.*

**Step 1 — Compute Item-Item Similarity**
Treat each item as a vector in the user-space and compute similarity across users who rated both.

**Step 2 — Pre-compute and Cache**
Item-item similarities are more **stable over time** than user-user similarities (item ratings change less rapidly), so this approach scales better for large systems like Amazon.

**Step 3 — Predict using Neighbouring Items**
For a target item $i$ the user has not rated, look at the top-K similar items the user has rated, and aggregate.

## 2.2 Model-Based CF — The "Why" Behind Matrix Factorization

Memory-based CF suffers from **sparsity** and **scalability** issues. Model-based CF solves this by **decomposing** the sparse user-item matrix into two low-rank dense matrices that capture latent features (e.g., "action-loving", "comedy-loving").

**Key Idea:** If $R \approx P \cdot Q^T$, where $P$ (user-factor matrix) and $Q$ (item-factor matrix) have $k$ latent dimensions, then the dot product $P_u \cdot Q_i^T$ predicts the rating of user $u$ on item $i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of CF in Modern Web Mining:**
> Modern web platforms (YouTube, Amazon, Netflix, Twitter/X) generate petabytes of behavioural logs. CF algorithms mine these logs to deliver hyper-personalized experiences, increasing user engagement, dwell-time, and revenue. From a KTU/Web Mining perspective, CF is the canonical example of **"knowledge discovery from user-generated web data."**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cosine Similarity** between two users $u, v$ | $\text{sim}_{\cos}(u,v) = \dfrac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} r_{u,i} \cdot r_{v,i}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} r_{u,i}^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}} r_{v,i}^2}}$ | Measures angle between two rating vectors (range 0 to 1) |
| 2 | **Pearson Correlation** (mean-centered) | $sim_{P}(u,v) = \dfrac{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}}(r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)(r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}}(r_{u,i} - \bar{r}_u)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i \in I_{uv}}(r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)^2}}$ | Captures rating **trend similarity**; range $-1$ to $+1$ |
| 3 | **Jaccard Similarity** (for implicit/binary data) | $J(A,B) = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Used for "bought" / "watched" / "clicked" sets |
| 4 | **User-Based CF Prediction** for item $i$ | $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \dfrac{\sum_{v \in N(u)} sim(u,v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \vert sim(u,v) \vert}$ | Predicts unknown rating; mean-centers to remove user bias |
| 5 | **Item-Based CF Prediction** | $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \dfrac{\sum_{j \in N(i)} sim(i,j) \cdot r_{u,j}}{\sum_{j \in N(i)} \vert sim(i,j) \vert}$ | Weights by item-item similarity |
| 6 | **Matrix Factorization Objective** | $\min_{P,Q} \sum_{(u,i) \in K} (r_{u,i} - P_u Q_i^T)^2 + \lambda(\|P\|^2 + \|Q\|^2)$ | Training objective with **L2 regularization** $\lambda$ |
| 7 | **MAE (Mean Absolute Error)** | $MAE = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert r_i - \hat{r}_i \vert$ | Lower is better; in same units as rating |
| 8 | **RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)** | $RMSE = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (r_i - \hat{r}_i)^2}$ | Penalizes large errors more heavily |
| 9 | **Precision@K** | $P@K = \dfrac{\vert \text{relevant} \cap \text{top-K} \vert}{K}$ | Measures top-K recommendation accuracy |
| 10 | **Recall@K** | $R@K = \dfrac{\vert \text{relevant} \cap \text{top-K} \vert}{\vert \text{relevant} \vert}$ | Measures coverage of relevant items in top-K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Used:**
> - $r_{u,i}$: actual rating of user $u$ on item $i$.
> - $\bar{r}_u$: mean rating of user $u$.
> - $I_{uv}$: set of items rated by **both** users $u$ and $v$.
> - $N(u)$: neighbourhood (top-K similar users) of $u$.
> - $K$: set of known (user, item, rating) tuples.
> - $\lambda$: regularization parameter to prevent overfitting.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cosine Similarity Calculation

**Given Problem (KTU-Style):**
Compute the **Cosine Similarity** between User $A$ and User $B$ using the following ratings (1–5 scale). Items rated by both are $I_1$, $I_2$, $I_3$:

| User | $I_1$ | $I_2$ | $I_3$ | $I_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | 3 | 4 | ? |
| B | 4 | 2 | 5 | 5 |

### Step 1 — Identify the Co-Rated Items
Items rated by both A and B (excluding missing entries): $I_{AB} = \{I_1, I_2, I_3\}$. Item $I_4$ is excluded because A has not rated it.

### Step 2 — Form the Rating Vectors
We extract only the co-rated items to avoid skewing the similarity.

$$\vec{A}_{I_{AB}} = [5, 3, 4] \quad ; \quad \vec{B}_{I_{AB}} = [4, 2, 5]$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Dot Product

$$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = (5 \times 4) + (3 \times 2) + (4 \times 5) = 20 + 6 + 20 = 46$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Magnitudes

$$\| \vec{A} \| = \sqrt{5^2 + 3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25 + 9 + 16} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.071$$

$$\| \vec{B} \| = \sqrt{4^2 + 2^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{16 + 4 + 25} = \sqrt{45} \approx 6.708$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Cosine Similarity Formula

$$\text{sim}_{\cos}(A, B) = \frac{46}{\sqrt{50} \cdot \sqrt{45}} = \frac{46}{\sqrt{2250}} = \frac{46}{47.43} \approx 0.970$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A similarity of $\approx 0.970$ is very close to $1$, indicating A and B have **highly aligned preferences** on the co-rated items. They will likely form a strong neighbourhood.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pearson Correlation Similarity

**Given:** Same ratings, but now we use **Pearson Correlation** to capture relative deviation from each user's mean.

### Step 1 — Compute Each User's Mean Rating

$$\bar{r}_A = \frac{5 + 3 + 4}{3} = 4.00 \quad ; \quad \bar{r}_B = \frac{4 + 2 + 5}{3} = 3.67$$

### Step 2 — Mean-Center Each Vector

$$\vec{A}_c = [5 - 4, \; 3 - 4, \; 4 - 4] = [+1, -1, 0]$$

$$\vec{B}_c = [4 - 3.67, \; 2 - 3.67, \; 5 - 3.67] = [+0.33, -1.67, +1.33]$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Pearson Numerator

$$\text{Num} = (+1)(+0.33) + (-1)(-1.67) + (0)(+1.33) = 0.33 + 1.67 + 0 = 2.00$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Pearson Denominator

$$\sum (A_c)^2 = 1^2 + (-1)^2 + 0^2 = 2$$

$$\sum (B_c)^2 = 0.33^2 + (-1.67)^2 + 1.33^2 = 0.11 + 2.79 + 1.77 = 4.67$$

$$\text{Den} = \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{4.67} = \sqrt{9.34} \approx 3.056$$

### Step 5 — Final Pearson Similarity

$$sim_P(A, B) = \frac{2.00}{3.056} \approx 0.654$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Pearson lower than Cosine here?**
> Cosine measures the **angle** (ignoring magnitude differences — e.g., a harsh rater giving 2/5 may align with a generous rater giving 4/5). Pearson **mean-centers** first, capturing the *shape* of agreement independent of scale. KTU exam tip: Use Pearson when users use rating scales differently (some always rate high, others always rate low).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — User-Based CF Rating Prediction

**Scenario:** We want to predict User A's rating for **Item $I_4$**. A's mean is $\bar{r}_A = 4.00$. The two most similar users (neighbours) are B with $sim(A,B) = 0.654$ (rating on $I_4$ = 5) and C with $sim(A,C) = 0.300$ (rating on $I_4$ = 3). C's mean is $\bar{r}_C = 3.50$; B's mean is $\bar{r}_B = 3.67$.

### Step 1 — Apply the Prediction Formula

$$\hat{r}_{A, I_4} = \bar{r}_A + \frac{\sum_{v \in \{B, C\}} sim(A, v) \cdot (r_{v, I_4} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in \{B, C\}} \vert sim(A, v) \vert}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Each Neighbour's Deviation

For B: $r_{B, I_4} - \bar{r}_B = 5 - 3.67 = +1.33$. Weighted contribution: $0.654 \times 1.33 = 0.870$.

For C: $r_{C, I_4} - \bar{r}_C = 3 - 3.50 = -0.50$. Weighted contribution: $0.300 \times (-0.50) = -0.150$.

### Step 3 — Sum the Weighted Deviations

$$\text{Weighted Sum} = 0.870 + (-0.150) = 0.720$$

### Step 4 — Sum the Absolute Similarities (Denominator)

$$\sum \vert sim \vert = 0.654 + 0.300 = 0.954$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Final Predicted Rating

$$\hat{r}_{A, I_4} = 4.00 + \frac{0.720}{0.954} = 4.00 + 0.755 = 4.76 \approx 4.8$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Interpretation:** A's predicted rating for $I_4$ is **4.8 out of 5**, indicating a strong recommendation. The mean-centering correction (the $+0.755$ term) appropriately adjusted A's baseline by considering B's enthusiasm and C's mild negative deviation, weighted by their similarity scores.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Matrix Factorization Intuition (SVD)

**Problem:** Given a sparse user-item matrix $R$ of size $3 \times 3$, factorize it into $P$ ($3 \times 2$) and $Q^T$ ($2 \times 3$) to predict the missing entry.

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 4 & ? \\ 4 & ? & 2 \\ ? & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

After training (SGD), suppose the model converges to latent factors $k=2$:

$$P = \begin{bmatrix} 1.2 & 0.8 \\ 0.9 & 0.4 \\ 0.5 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix} \quad ; \quad Q = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.5 & 0.7 \\ 0.6 & 0.9 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 1 — Compute $\hat{R} = P \cdot Q$

$$\hat{R}_{11} = (1.2)(0.9) + (0.8)(0.6) = 1.08 + 0.48 = 1.56 \quad \text{(×5 scale factor: } \approx 5)$$

### Step 2 — Predict the Missing Cell
For $R_{13}$ (User 1, Item 3):

$$\hat{R}_{13} = P_1 \cdot Q_3 = (1.2)(0.7) + (0.8)(0.3) = 0.84 + 0.24 = 1.08$$

Mapped back to the 1–5 rating scale: $\hat{R}_{13} \approx 5 \times 1.08 / \max(\hat{R}) \approx 4.5$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The two latent dimensions $k=2$ might semantically correspond to (1) "preference for action" and (2) "preference for comedy." Matrix factorization **uncovers hidden semantic axes** automatically from rating patterns — this is the foundational idea behind modern **embedding-based recommenders** and even **Large Language Model** embedding layers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Recommender System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U1[User 1 Behaviour Log] --> DM[Data Acquisition Module]
    U2[User 2 Behaviour Log] --> DM
    U3[User N Behaviour Log] --> DM
    DM --> R[(User-Item Rating Matrix)]
    R --> EDA[Exploratory Data Analysis]
    EDA --> CF1[Memory Based CF Engine]
    EDA --> CF2[Model Based CF Engine]
    CF1 --> HYB[Hybrid Aggregator]
    CF2 --> HYB
    HYB --> TOPK[Top K Item Selector]
    TOPK --> OUT[Personalized Recommendations]
    R --> EVAL[Offline Evaluation MAE RMSE Precision K]
    EVAL --> TUNE[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    TUNE --> CF1
    TUNE --> CF2
```

## 4.2 Collaborative Filtering — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw User Item Ratings] --> B[Normalize Ratings Mean Centering]
    B --> C{Choose CF Type}
    C -->|User Based| D1[Compute User User Similarity]
    C -->|Item Based| D2[Compute Item Item Similarity]
    C -->|Model Based| D3[Train Matrix Factorization Model]
    D1 --> E1[Identify Top K Neighbours]
    D2 --> E2[Identify Top K Similar Items]
    D3 --> E3[Generate Latent Factors P and Q]
    E1 --> F[Predict Unknown Ratings]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Rank Items by Predicted Score]
    G --> H[Output Top N Recommendations]
    H --> I[Evaluate Precision Recall NDCG]
    I --> J{Cold Start?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply Content Based Fallback]
    J -->|No| L[Deploy to Production]
```

## 4.3 User-Item Matrix Sparsity Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Users[User Axis Rows]
        U1[User U1]
        U2[User U2]
        U3[User U3]
        U4[User U4]
    end
    subgraph Items[Item Axis Columns]
        I1[Item I1]
        I2[Item I2]
        I3[Item I3]
        I4[Item I4]
        I5[Item I5]
    end
    U1 --- I1
    U1 -. X .-> I3
    U2 --- I2
    U2 --- I4
    U3 --- I1
    U3 -. X .-> I5
    U4 --- I3
    U4 --- I5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 4.3:**
> - **Solid lines (---)** represent actual user-item ratings.
> - **Dotted lines marked X (-. X .->)** represent **missing entries** (cold cells).
> - In a real e-commerce platform, this matrix can be **more than 99% sparse** (Amazon's product catalogue vs. an average user who views <1% of items). This is exactly why **Matrix Factorization** (model-based CF) is preferred over naive memory-based methods in production.

## 4.4 Cold-Start Problem — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[New User or New Item Arrives] --> Q1{Is the entity a NEW USER?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, New User| Q2{Is demographic data available?}
    Q1 -->|No, Existing User| Q3[Use Collaborative Filtering Normally]
    Q2 -->|Yes| D1[Apply Demographic Based Recommendations]
    Q2 -->|No| D2[Use Content Based Fallback]
    Q2 -->|No demographics and no ratings| D3[Show Trending or Most Popular Items]
    D1 --> MERGE[Merge with CF once ratings accumulate]
    D2 --> MERGE
    D3 --> MERGE
    MERGE --> END[Stable Personalized Recommendations]
    Q3 --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Insight:** The **Cold-Start Problem** is a frequently asked KTU question. A *new user* has no rating history; a *new item* has no rating history. Pure CF cannot handle either. The standard KTU answer is to **hybridize** with content-based or demographic filtering as a bootstrap mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Collaborative Filtering. Differentiate between User-Based and Item-Based Collaborative Filtering.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collaborative Filtering (CF):** A recommendation technique that predicts a user's interest in an item by collecting preferences from many users, under the assumption that users with similar past behaviour tend to agree in the future. **It does not require item content/description.**

| Aspect | User-Based CF | Item-Based CF |
|---|---|---|
| **Similarity computed between** | Two users | Two items |
| **Prediction logic** | Users with similar rating patterns | Items with similar rating patterns |
| **Stability over time** | Low (user behaviour changes) | High (item properties are stable) |
| **Scalability** | Poor for very large user base | Better; pre-computed offline |
| **Example** | "People like you also liked..." | "Customers who bought X also bought Y" |

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for comparison table.)*

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the Cold-Start problem in recommender systems. How is it handled?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Cold-Start Problem** arises when a new user or a new item enters the system with **no prior interaction history** (no ratings, clicks, or purchases). Since pure collaborative filtering depends entirely on the user-item interaction matrix, it cannot generate recommendations for these entities.

**Handling Strategies (any 3):**
1. **Hybridization** — Combine CF with content-based filtering (use item metadata for new items).
2. **Demographic Bootstrap** — Use age, gender, location to cluster new users with similar cohorts.
3. **Onboarding Questionnaires** — Ask new users to rate a small seed set of items during signup.
4. **Popularity Fallback** — Recommend globally trending items until enough data accumulates.
5. **Active Learning** — Proactively ask new users to rate selected items to quickly build a profile.

*(Award 1 mark for problem statement, 2 marks for handling techniques.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 long-answer questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each sub-part is 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the architecture of a Recommender System. With a neat diagram, explain the role of each module in generating personalized recommendations for an e-commerce user.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **Cosine Similarity** and **Pearson Correlation** between two users, User X and User Y, given the following rating matrix (scale 1–5). Items co-rated by both users are to be considered.

| User | Item A | Item B | Item C | Item D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **X** | 5 | 3 | 4 | 2 |
| **Y** | 4 | 2 | 5 | 3 |

(Other users are not involved in this sub-part.)

#### Model Solution to (a) — Architecture [7 Marks]

A recommender system pipeline consists of **five sequential modules** (mapped to Diagram 4.1 above):

1. **Data Acquisition Module (1 mark):** Collects explicit ratings (stars, likes) and implicit feedback (clicks, dwell time, purchase history) from web logs. Output: Raw user-item interaction stream.

2. **Storage & Preprocessing (1 mark):** Stores interactions in a sparse user-item matrix $R$. Handles missing values, applies **mean-centering**, and may perform **dimensionality reduction**.

3. **Recommendation Engine (2 marks):** The core CF module. For **memory-based** approaches, computes cosine / Pearson similarity; for **model-based** approaches, trains matrix factorization (SVD, ALS, BPR). The engine outputs a predicted rating matrix $\hat{R}$.

4. **Ranking & Filtering (1 mark):** Selects top-N items per user, applies business rules (e.g., remove already-purchased, filter out-of-stock, ensure diversity).

5. **Evaluation & Feedback Loop (2 marks):** Computes offline metrics (MAE, RMSE, Precision@K) using held-out test sets, and online A/B tests in production. Feedback from real user interactions is fed back into the data acquisition module to refine the model continuously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip (1 mark reserved for diagram):** Always include a labelled block diagram with arrows showing the data flow from user logs → storage → engine → ranking → output. Refer to the Mermaid architecture diagram provided in Section 4.1.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify Co-Rated Items [1 mark]**
Both X and Y have rated all four items (A, B, C, D). So $I_{XY} = \{A, B, C, D\}$.

**Step 2 — Form Rating Vectors [0.5 mark]**
$$\vec{X} = [5, 3, 4, 2] \quad ; \quad \vec{Y} = [4, 2, 5, 3]$$

**Step 3 — Cosine Similarity Calculation [2.5 marks]**

$$\vec{X} \cdot \vec{Y} = (5)(4) + (3)(2) + (4)(5) + (2)(3) = 20 + 6 + 20 + 6 = 52$$

$$\| \vec{X} \| = \sqrt{25 + 9 + 16 + 4} = \sqrt{54} \approx 7.348$$

$$\| \vec{Y} \| = \sqrt{16 + 4 + 25 + 9} = \sqrt{54} \approx 7.348$$

$$\text{sim}_{\cos}(X, Y) = \frac{52}{\sqrt{54} \cdot \sqrt{54}} = \frac{52}{54} \approx 0.963$$

**Step 4 — Pearson Correlation Calculation [3 marks]**

Mean of X: $\bar{X} = (5+3+4+2)/4 = 14/4 = 3.50$

Mean of Y: $\bar{Y} = (4+2+5+3)/4 = 14/4 = 3.50$

Mean-centered vectors:
$$\vec{X}_c = [+1.5, -0.5, +0.5, -1.5]$$
$$\vec{Y}_c = [+0.5, -1.5, +1.5, -0.5]$$

Numerator:
$$\sum (X_c)(Y_c) = (1.5)(0.5) + (-0.5)(-1.5) + (0.5)(1.5) + (-1.5)(-0.5) = 0.75 + 0.75 + 0.75 + 0.75 = 3.00$$

Denominator:
$$\sum X_c^2 = 2.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25 = 5.00$$
$$\sum Y_c^2 = 0.25 + 2.25 + 2.25 + 0.25 = 5.00$$
$$\text{Den} = \sqrt{5.00} \cdot \sqrt{5.00} = 5.00$$

$$sim_P(X, Y) = \frac{3.00}{5.00} = 0.600$$

**Final Answer [Bonus clarity]:**
- Cosine Similarity = **0.963** (very high — angle between vectors is small).
- Pearson Correlation = **+0.600** (moderate-to-strong positive linear correlation in rating trends).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to **mean-center** the vectors before applying Pearson. Pearson is NOT simply the cosine of the raw vectors; subtracting each user's mean is **mandatory**. Losing this 1 mark is a frequent issue. Also, do not confuse "similarity" with "distance" — they are inversely related.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **User-Based Collaborative Filtering** with the **rating prediction formula**. How does the choice of similarity metric (Cosine vs. Pearson vs. Jaccard) impact recommendation quality?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider three users A, B, C who have rated three items. You are asked to predict A's rating for **Item 3** using **User-Based CF with a 2-neighbour approach**. Given data:

| User | Item 1 | Item 2 | Item 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 5 | 3 | ? |
| **B** | 4 | 2 | 5 |
| **C** | 2 | 4 | 3 |

Means: $\bar{r}_A = 4.00$, $\bar{r}_B = 3.67$, $\bar{r}_C = 3.00$.
Similarities: $sim(A,B) = 0.85$, $sim(A,C) = 0.40$.
Apply the prediction formula and show all steps.

#### Model Solution to (a) — Conceptual [7 Marks]

**User-Based CF — Definition (2 marks):**
User-Based CF predicts the rating of a target user $u$ on an item $i$ by aggregating the ratings of users **most similar to $u$** who have already rated $i$. It is a form of **nearest-neighbour regression** in the user-space.

**Prediction Formula (2 marks):**

$$\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum_{v \in N(u)} sim(u, v) \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N(u)} \vert sim(u, v) \vert}$$

**Choice of Similarity Metric (3 marks):**

| Metric | Strengths | Weaknesses | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cosine** | Fast, scale-invariant, works on sparse data | Ignores rating magnitude differences (e.g., harsh vs. generous raters) | When items have vastly different popularity |
| **Pearson** | Handles user bias (mean-centering), captures linear correlation trends | Fails on very few co-ratings (high variance) | When users rate on different scales (e.g., one always gives 4–5, another 1–3) |
| **Jaccard** | Best for binary / implicit feedback (bought, clicked) | Ignores rating magnitude entirely | Implicit datasets (e.g., "watched" / "purchased" sets) |

**Final synthesis:** A KTU-grade answer should state that **no single metric is universally best**; production systems often **ensemble** multiple similarities using a weighted scheme.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Prediction [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify the Target and Neighbours [1 mark]**
We want $\hat{r}_{A, \text{Item3}}$. Both B and C have rated Item 3, so both qualify as potential neighbours. We use the given top-2 neighbourhood $\{B, C\}$.

**Step 2 — Compute Each Neighbour's Rating Deviation [2 marks]**

For B:
$$r_{B, \text{Item3}} - \bar{r}_B = 5 - 3.67 = +1.33$$

For C:
$$r_{C, \text{Item3}} - \bar{r}_C = 3 - 3.00 = 0.00$$

**Step 3 — Compute Weighted Deviations [1.5 marks]**

Weighted contribution of B: $0.85 \times 1.33 = 1.1305$

Weighted contribution of C: $0.40 \times 0.00 = 0.00$

Sum of weighted deviations: $1.1305 + 0.00 = 1.1305$

**Step 4 — Sum the Absolute Similarities [1 mark]**

$$\sum \vert sim \vert = 0.85 + 0.40 = 1.25$$

**Step 5 — Compute Final Predicted Rating [1.5 marks]**

$$\hat{r}_{A, \text{Item3}} = 4.00 + \frac{1.1305}{1.25} = 4.00 + 0.9044 = 4.9044 \approx 4.90$$

**Final Answer:** A's predicted rating for Item 3 is approximately **4.90 out of 5**, indicating a very strong recommendation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget the **mean-centering** of neighbour ratings ($r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v$). They just plug in $r_{v,i}$ directly. This yields wrong predictions. Lose 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using **raw similarity sum** in the denominator instead of **absolute similarity sum**. If similarities can be negative, the denominator MUST use $\vert sim \vert$ to avoid division instability.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to show intermediate computations. KTU examiners award **step marks** generously — always show the deviation, weighted sum, and denominator separately.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save This for Last-Minute Prep):**

- **Recommender System** = function $f: U \times I \rightarrow R$ that predicts user preferences. Built on the **information filtering** paradigm.

- **Three main paradigms** of RS: **Content-Based**, **Collaborative**, and **Hybrid**. CF is the most widely deployed because it requires no item content.

- **Collaborative Filtering (CF)** has two major branches:
  - **Memory-Based (Neighbourhood):** User-Based CF and Item-Based CF — uses raw rating matrix.
  - **Model-Based:** Matrix Factorization, SVD, ALS, BPR, Neural CF — learns latent factors.

- **Three core similarity metrics:** Cosine, Pearson Correlation, Jaccard. Each has trade-offs.

- **User-Based CF Prediction Formula** uses **mean-centering** to correct for user rating bias: $\hat{r}_{u,i} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum sim \cdot (r_{v,i} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum \vert sim \vert}$.

- **Item-Based CF** is more **scalable** than user-based for large e-commerce platforms because item relationships are more stable.

- **Matrix Factorization (SVD/ALS)** addresses **sparsity** and **scalability** by decomposing $R$ into low-rank $P \cdot Q^T$. It reveals **latent semantic features** automatically.

- **Cold-Start Problem:** New users / new items have no rating history → CF fails. Solution: **hybridize** with content-based or demographic filtering; use **onboarding questionnaires**; fall back to **trending/popular** items.

- **Evaluation Metrics** — Offline: MAE, RMSE (rating accuracy); Precision@K, Recall@K, F1@K, NDCG, MAP (top-K ranking quality). Online: CTR, dwell time, conversion rate.

- **Sparsity Issue:** Real-world user-item matrices are typically **> 99% empty**. This is the central motivation for moving from memory-based to model-based CF.

- **Implicit vs. Explicit Feedback:** Ratings (1–5 stars) are **explicit**; clicks, views, and purchases are **implicit**. CF can work on both, but implicit feedback requires algorithms like **BPR (Bayesian Personalized Ranking)** or **ALS-WR**.

- **KTU Exam Hot Topics:** Cold start, similarity formula derivations, prediction formula with worked numerical, comparison tables between User-based vs. Item-based vs. Content-based, and the role of CF in web mining.

- **One-line exam-ready definition:** *"Collaborative filtering leverages the collective intelligence of similar users to recommend items without requiring any prior knowledge about the items themselves."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Query Log Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECDT635 - WEB MINING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Query Log Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 – Query Log Mining

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Query Log Mining* is the sub-domain of **Web Usage Mining (WUM)** that focuses on the systematic extraction, preprocessing, transformation, and analytical modeling of historical query records (search engine logs, intranet search logs, e-commerce search logs) submitted by users to information retrieval systems, with the objective of discovering latent search intent, usage patterns, click-through behaviour, query-reformulation strategies, and personalized recommendation patterns.

In simple terms, when a user types something into Google, Bing, or even a university library portal, the system silently keeps a small diary entry. That diary entry contains **who searched, what they searched, when they searched, which result they clicked, and sometimes how long they stayed**. *Query Log Mining* is the act of opening millions of such diary entries, cleaning them, joining them together, and finding useful stories inside them.

### Conceptual Analogy – The Coffee Shop Register

Imagine a small coffee shop that writes down every order on a paper slip. Over a year, the owner has 50,000 slips. If she just stares at them, she sees chaos. But if she *mines* them, she notices:

- 70 % of people who order **"latte"** on a Monday morning also order **"croissant"** within 5 minutes.
- Queries like **"decaf latte"** frequently get *reformulated* into **"herbal tea"** because the original result was poor.
- Power-users submit **3 to 5 queries per session** before satisfaction.

The slips are the **query logs**. The patterns are the **mining output**. That is exactly what happens in search engines, only at a scale of **billions of records per day**.

### Why Query Log Mining Matters in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECDT635 M4):**
> Query log mining bridges *Information Retrieval (IR)*, *Web Usage Mining*, and *Recommendation Systems*. KTU expects students to understand log structure, preprocessing, session segmentation, query intent, and personalization applications.

| Aspect | Web Server Log Mining | Query Log Mining |
|---|---|---|
| Source | HTTP requests, page hits | Search engine query submissions |
| Granularity | Page-level | Query-intent level |
| Primary Goal | Traffic analysis | Search personalization |
| Privacy Risk | IP addresses | Search intent + identity |
| KTU Module | Web Usage Mining (Module 3) | **Web Usage Mining (Module 4)** |

### Components Present in a Typical Query Log Entry

A query log record is far richer than a normal server log entry. A canonical record contains the following fields, each of which becomes a *feature* for mining algorithms:

1. **User Identifier** – cookie-based or login-based pseudo ID.
2. **Query String** – the raw textual query submitted by the user.
3. **Timestamp** – exact time of submission.
4. **Session ID** – grouping identifier spanning multiple queries.
5. **Result List Returned** – top-N URLs or document IDs.
6. **Clicked Result** – URL/Doc ID actually clicked.
7. **Click Position** – rank of the clicked result.
8. **Dwell Time** – time spent on the result page.
9. **Query Reformulation Chain** – parent query, modified query, expanded query.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Tip:** In KTU board answers, always mention *at least 6 fields* of a query log entry to show structural awareness. Examiners specifically test this enumeration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Stages of Query Log Mining

The end-to-end pipeline of a Query Log Mining system can be decomposed into **five sequential stages**. KTU frequently asks students to draw or list these stages – remember the order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5-Stage Pipeline (High-Yield KTU Topic):**
> 1. **Log Collection** – raw aggregation from search front-ends.
> 2. **Preprocessing & Cleaning** – noise, spam, and bot removal.
> 3. **Session Identification** – grouping queries into meaningful user sessions.
> 4. **Pattern Discovery** – association rules, clustering, classification.
> 5. **Evaluation & Application** – recommendation, ranking, personalization.

#### Stage 1 – Log Collection
Logs are typically collected at three possible layers of the search stack:

- **Client-Side Logs** – collected via browser toolbars or JavaScript instrumentation. *Most accurate* for dwell time, but *biased* (only users with toolbar installed).
- **Proxy / Network Logs** – aggregated at ISP or corporate proxy. *Most coverage*, but cannot identify user beyond IP.
- **Server-Side Logs** – collected by the search engine backend. *Most authoritative*, but anonymized.

#### Stage 2 – Preprocessing & Cleaning

Raw logs are notoriously dirty. KTU expects you to know the cleanup operations:

- **Bot Filtering** – remove entries from crawlers, automated agents (identified via user-agent strings).
- **Spam Query Removal** – drop queries with zero clicks and zero dwell time repeatedly from same IP.
- **Anonymization** – hash user IDs to protect privacy (GDPR compliance).
- **Stop-word Normalization** – lowercase, trim, expand abbreviations.
- **Duplicate Removal** – same user, same query, identical timestamp.

#### Stage 3 – Session Identification

A **session** is the unit of mining. The most common algorithms are:

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Canonical Session Identification Methods:**
> - **Time-Based (30-minute rule):** If gap between two consecutive queries from the same user exceeds **30 minutes**, a new session begins. (Standard heuristic proposed by Catledge & Pitkow.)
> - **Navigation-Based:** Use referrer URL chains; if a query leads to a click whose referrer matches the prior result, extend the session.
> - **Query-Content Based:** Use semantic similarity (cosine, Jaccard) between consecutive queries; threshold $\tau$ determines new-session boundary.

#### Stage 4 – Pattern Discovery Techniques

Once sessions are built, mining techniques from data-mining are applied:

- **Frequent Query Set Mining** – Apriori-like algorithm to find frequently co-occurring queries within sessions.
- **Query Clustering** – group semantically similar queries using TF-IDF + cosine or Word2Vec embeddings.
- **Query Classification** – map each query to a category (navigational / informational / transactional – the **Broder taxonomy**).
- **Association Rule Mining** – $X \Rightarrow Y$ where $X$ and $Y$ are queries within same session.
- **Markov Chain Modeling** – predict next query from current query.

#### Stage 5 – Application Layer

Discovered patterns drive:

- **Query Recommendation** – "Users who searched X also searched Y".
- **Query Auto-Completion (QAC)** – predict next characters.
- **Personalized Ranking** – re-rank SERP using user history.
- **Query Reformulation Assistance** – "Did you mean ...?"
- **User Profiling** – build interest vectors.
- **Trending Topic Detection** – burst detection in query frequency.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All quantities in the table below are examiner-favourite. Memorize units and variable meanings.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variable Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Support of query pair** | $\text{Sup}(q_i, q_j) = \dfrac{\vert \{ s \in S : q_i \in s \wedge q_j \in s \} \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | $S$ = set of all sessions | dimensionless, $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | **Confidence of rule $q_i \Rightarrow q_j$** | $\text{Conf}(q_i \Rightarrow q_j) = \dfrac{\text{Sup}(q_i, q_j)}{\text{Sup}(q_i)}$ | Strength of co-occurrence | dimensionless, $[0,1]$ |
| 3 | **Lift (interestingness)** | $\text{Lift}(q_i \Rightarrow q_j) = \dfrac{\text{Conf}(q_i \Rightarrow q_j)}{\text{Sup}(q_j)}$ | $>1$ = positive correlation | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Cosine similarity between queries** | $\cos(q_i, q_j) = \dfrac{\vec{v}_{q_i} \cdot \vec{v}_{q_j}}{\vert \vec{v}_{q_i} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{v}_{q_j} \vert}$ | Vector form of queries | $[0,1]$ for normalized |
| 5 | **Jaccard coefficient for sessions** | $J(s_a, s_b) = \dfrac{\vert s_a \cap s_b \vert}{\vert s_a \cup s_b \vert}$ | Overlap of two session query sets | $[0,1]$ |
| 6 | **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** | $\text{CTR}(r) = \dfrac{\text{Clicks}(r)}{\text{Impressions}(r)}$ | Relevance feedback metric | $[0,1]$ |
| 7 | **Average Dwell Time** | $\bar{D} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i$ | Mean time on clicked result | seconds |
| 8 | **TF-IDF weight** | $w_{t,q} = \text{tf}(t,q) \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{\vert Q \vert}{\text{df}(t)}\right)$ | Term weight in query $q$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Mutual Information of queries** | $I(q_i; q_j) = \sum_{x,y} P(x,y) \log\!\left(\dfrac{P(x,y)}{P(x)P(y)}\right)$ | Co-occurrence information | nats or bits |
| 10 | **Session Boundary Time Threshold** | $\Delta T = 30 \text{ minutes (default)}$ | Gap threshold for new session | minutes |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Support-Confidence-Lift framework is borrowed directly from *market basket analysis* and is the bedrock of association rule mining used in e-commerce recommendation engines such as Amazon and Flipkart query-to-query suggestion systems.

### 2.3 Broder's Query Intent Taxonomy (Mandatory KTU Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Broder (2002) classified queries into three canonical intents:**
> - **Navigational** – the user wants to reach a *specific website* (e.g., "facebook login", "ktu student login").
> - **Informational** – the user wants to *learn about a topic* (e.g., "what is web mining", "define query log mining").
> - **Transactional** – the user wants to *perform an action* (e.g., "buy iphone 15", "download nptel pdf").

Mining logs to classify queries into these three categories is itself a mining task – called **Query Intent Classification** – and uses features like click distribution, session length, dwell time, and POS tagging.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Query Log Mining directly powers:

- **Google Autocomplete** – uses aggregated log frequencies.
- **YouTube "Search Suggestions"** – session-based sequential pattern mining.
- **Amazon "Customers who searched X also searched Y"** – association rule mining on query logs.
- **Spotify "Recommended Searches"** – collaborative filtering + query logs.
- **KTU Internal ERP Search** – university portal can use the same principles for student behaviour analytics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example – Computing Support, Confidence and Lift

Let us construct a complete KTU-style numerical walkthrough. This is the *single most asked* computation in board exams.

**Given Data (Mining Query Log):**

Consider the following four user sessions extracted from a search engine query log:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \{ \text{``web mining''}, \text{``query log mining''}, \text{``data preprocessing''} \} \\
S_2 &= \{ \text{``web mining''}, \text{``query log mining''} \} \\
S_3 &= \{ \text{``machine learning''}, \text{``data preprocessing''} \} \\
S_4 &= \{ \text{``web mining''}, \text{``data preprocessing''} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total number of sessions $\vert S \vert = 4$.

**Step 1 – Compute Support of itemset $\{ \text{``web mining''}, \text{``data preprocessing''} \}$**

This itemset appears in $S_1$ and $S_4$. So it occurs in **2 sessions** out of 4.

$$
\text{Sup}(\{\text{web mining}, \text{data preprocessing}\}) = \dfrac{2}{4} = 0.50
$$

**Step 2 – Compute Support of single query $\{\text{``web mining''}\}$**

Appears in $S_1, S_2, S_4$. Occurs in **3 sessions**.

$$
\text{Sup}(\{\text{web mining}\}) = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$

**Step 3 – Compute Support of single query $\{\text{``data preprocessing''}\}$**

Appears in $S_1, S_3, S_4$. Occurs in **3 sessions**.

$$
\text{Sup}(\{\text{data preprocessing}\}) = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75
$$

**Step 4 – Confidence of rule $\{\text{web mining}\} \Rightarrow \{\text{data preprocessing}\}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Conf}(\text{web mining} \Rightarrow \text{data preprocessing}) &= \dfrac{\text{Sup}(\text{web mining}, \text{data preprocessing})}{\text{Sup}(\text{web mining})} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.50}{0.75} = 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Lift of the same rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lift}(\text{web mining} \Rightarrow \text{data preprocessing}) &= \dfrac{\text{Conf}}{\text{Sup}(\text{data preprocessing})} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.667}{0.75} = 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 – Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lift $< 1$ implies negative correlation.** Even though support (50 %) and confidence (66.7 %) seem high, lift being **0.889 < 1** means the two queries co-occur *less often than chance*. A real-world recommender would *reject* this rule.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Algorithm Selection Guide

KTU sometimes asks: "Which mining technique would you use for scenario X?" Use the matrix below as your cheat-sheet.

| Mining Objective | Best-Suited Algorithm | Input Data Structure | Output Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Find related query suggestions | Apriori / FP-Growth | Transactional sessions | Frequent itemsets |
| Group similar user queries | K-Means / DBSCAN | TF-IDF vectors of queries | Query clusters |
| Predict next query in session | Markov Chain (order-2) | Sequential query stream | Probability distribution |
| Classify query intent | SVM / Naïve Bayes | Labelled query log sample | Intent label |
| Recommend URL for new query | Collaborative Filtering | User-Query-URL matrix | Ranked URL list |
| Detect trending queries | Burst Detection (Kleinberg) | Query frequency time series | Burst intervals |
| Detect query reformulations | Edit Distance + Session Join | Consecutive queries | Rewrite pairs |
| Personalize ranking | Learning to Rank (RankNet) | Click + dwell features | Re-ranked SERP |

### 3.3 Derivation – Cosine Similarity for Two Query Vectors

Suppose we represent each query as a TF-IDF vector across a vocabulary of 4 terms: *web*, *mining*, *query*, *log*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_1 &= \text{``query log mining''} \rightarrow \vec{v}_{q_1} = (0.00,\; 0.41,\; 0.51,\; 0.51) \\
q_2 &= \text{``web mining''} \rightarrow \vec{v}_{q_2} = (0.61,\; 0.51,\; 0.00,\; 0.00)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 – Dot Product**

$$
\vec{v}_{q_1} \cdot \vec{v}_{q_2} = (0.00)(0.61) + (0.41)(0.51) + (0.51)(0.00) + (0.51)(0.00)
$$

$$
\vec{v}_{q_1} \cdot \vec{v}_{q_2} = 0 + 0.2091 + 0 + 0 = 0.2091
$$

**Step 2 – Magnitudes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \vec{v}_{q_1} \vert &= \sqrt{0.00^2 + 0.41^2 + 0.51^2 + 0.51^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.00 + 0.1681 + 0.2601 + 0.2601} \\
&= \sqrt{0.6883} = 0.8297
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \vec{v}_{q_2} \vert &= \sqrt{0.61^2 + 0.51^2 + 0.00^2 + 0.00^2} \\
&= \sqrt{0.3721 + 0.2601} = \sqrt{0.6322} = 0.7951
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Cosine Similarity**

$$
\cos(q_1, q_2) = \dfrac{0.2091}{(0.8297)(0.7951)} = \dfrac{0.2091}{0.6597} = 0.317
$$

**Interpretation:** The two queries are *weakly related* in vector space, which makes sense because one mentions *web* and the other does not. A clustering algorithm using threshold $\tau = 0.6$ would keep them in separate clusters.

### 3.4 Session Identification – 30-Minute Rule Walkthrough

Given raw queries from a single user with timestamps (in minutes):

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_1 &: 09{:}00 \\
q_2 &: 09{:}12 \\
q_3 &: 09{:}45 \\
q_4 &: 11{:}10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 – Compute consecutive time gaps**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{1\to 2} &= 12 \text{ min} \\
\Delta T_{2\to 3} &= 33 \text{ min} \\
\Delta T_{3\to 4} &= 85 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Apply 30-minute threshold**

- $\Delta T_{1 \to 2} = 12 \le 30$ → same session.
- $\Delta T_{2 \to 3} = 33 > 30$ → **new session begins at $q_3$**.
- $\Delta T_{3 \to 4} = 85 > 30$ → **new session begins at $q_4$**.

**Step 3 – Final Session Segmentation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Session A} &= \{q_1, q_2\} \\
\text{Session B} &= \{q_3\} \\
\text{Session C} &= \{q_4\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a routine KTU Part-A short numerical; practice similar ones.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Query Log Mining System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Query Log Source] --> B[Log Collector Layer]
    B --> C[Bot and Spam Filter]
    C --> D[Anonymization Module]
    D --> E[Session Identifier 30 min Rule]
    E --> F[Pattern Discovery Engine]
    F --> F1[Frequent Query Mining]
    F --> F2[Query Clustering]
    F --> F3[Sequential Markov Model]
    F1 --> G[Pattern Repository]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Recommendation Engine]
    G --> I[Query Auto Complete]
    G --> J[Personalized Ranker]
    G --> K[Analytics Dashboard]
    H --> L[End User SERP]
    I --> L
    J --> L
```

**Reading the Diagram (for KTU answers):**
- **Top Row** – data ingestion.
- **Middle Row** – preprocessing and transformation.
- **Lower Middle Row** – mining algorithms running in parallel.
- **Bottom Row** – real-time applications serving the user Search Engine Results Page (SERP).

### 4.2 Session Segmentation State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ActiveSession
    ActiveSession --> ActiveSession: Gap less than or equal to 30 min
    ActiveSession --> NewSession: Gap greater than 30 min
    ActiveSession --> ClosedSession: User logout or timeout
    NewSession --> ActiveSession
    ClosedSession --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Broder's Query Intent Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Incoming Query] --> P1{Contains brand or URL keyword}
    P1 -- Yes --> Nav[Navigational Intent]
    P1 -- No --> P2{Click on single dominant result}
    P2 -- Yes --> Nav
    P2 -- No --> P3{Contains action verb buy download}
    P3 -- Yes --> Tr[Transactional Intent]
    P3 -- No --> Inf[Informational Intent]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Architecture – Click-Through Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User submits query] --> S[Search Engine returns ranked list]
    S --> C[User clicks result]
    C --> L[Log entry query, clicked URL, rank, dwell time written]
    L --> M[Query Log Mining pipeline]
    M --> R[Updated ranking model]
    R --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This *closed feedback loop* is what makes modern search engines self-improving. Every click becomes a tiny vote that retrains the ranker. KTU loves diagrams that show such feedback loops – they earn full marks in 7-mark sub-parts.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define query log mining. List any four fields stored in a typical search engine query log entry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Query log mining is the process of extracting useful patterns, associations, and knowledge from the historical records of user-submitted search queries stored in search engine logs. (1 mark)

Four fields of a typical query log entry: (½ mark each)
1. User Identifier (cookie or login ID)
2. Query String (raw text submitted)
3. Timestamp of submission
4. Clicked Result URL and Click Rank
5. Dwell Time on clicked result

> [!TIP]
> Examiner awards full marks only if *all four fields are technically named* (e.g., say "dwell time" not "time spent").

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Explain the 30-minute rule used in query log session identification.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The 30-minute rule is a heuristic proposed by Catledge and Pitkow (1995) for identifying user sessions from query logs. (1 mark)

According to this rule, all consecutive queries submitted by the same user within a time gap of **30 minutes or less** are grouped into a single session. (1 mark)

If the time gap between two consecutive queries exceeds 30 minutes, a new session is started. The threshold of 30 minutes has been empirically validated as a balance between over-segmentation and under-segmentation of sessions. (1 mark)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Module 4 internal-choice pattern: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a typical query log mining system. List the major stages and explain any three stages in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A query log mining system is organized as a multi-stage pipeline. The architecture consists of the following stages:

1. **Log Collection Layer** – gathers raw queries from client toolbars, proxies, or search-engine server logs.
2. **Preprocessing Layer** – removes bots, normalizes text, anonymizes users.
3. **Session Identification Layer** – segments the cleaned stream into user sessions using the 30-minute rule or navigation-based heuristics.
4. **Pattern Discovery Layer** – runs mining algorithms such as Apriori, clustering, and Markov models.
5. **Application Layer** – deploys discovered patterns into query recommendation, auto-complete, and personalization.

**Detailed explanation of three stages:**

**Stage 1 – Preprocessing:** This stage performs critical cleaning. Bot queries are identified using user-agent strings and IP repetition patterns. Stop-words and duplicates are removed. User IDs are hashed using SHA-256 to comply with privacy regulations such as GDPR. Without this stage, the mined patterns would be dominated by crawler noise.

**Stage 2 – Session Identification:** This stage uses the **30-minute time threshold** as the default. For each user, queries are sorted by timestamp and grouped into a session. A new session begins whenever the gap between consecutive queries exceeds 30 minutes. More advanced systems use *content-based segmentation* where semantic similarity between consecutive queries is measured, and a new session starts if similarity falls below a threshold $\tau = 0.3$.

**Stage 3 – Pattern Discovery:** In this stage, sessions are mined using:

- **Association Rule Mining (Apriori/FP-Growth):** Finds frequently co-occurring queries.
- **Query Clustering:** Groups semantically similar queries.
- **Sequential Pattern Mining:** Predicts next query in a session using Markov chains.

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes):**
The diagram illustrates the flow from raw log to pattern repository and finally to recommendation engine, autocomplete, and personalized ranker.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often draw only **3 boxes** and skip the Application Layer. The Application Layer (Recommendation / AutoComplete / Personalization) is **mandatory** for full 7 marks. Always end your diagram with the end-user SERP block.

---

**(b)** Given the following query log sessions from a search engine, mine all frequent query pairs using the Apriori algorithm with **minimum support = 50 %**. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Sessions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \{\text{``data mining''}, \text{``web mining''}, \text{``query log''}\} \\
S_2 &= \{\text{``data mining''}, \text{``query log''}\} \\
S_3 &= \{\text{``web mining''}, \text{``query log''}\} \\
S_4 &= \{\text{``data mining''}, \text{``web mining''}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total sessions $\vert S \vert = 4$. Minimum support count = $0.50 \times 4 = 2$ sessions.

**Step 1 – Candidate 1-itemsets $C_1$:**

Count frequency of each single query:
- data mining → $S_1, S_2, S_4$ = **3** ✓
- web mining → $S_1, S_3, S_4$ = **3** ✓
- query log → $S_1, S_2, S_3$ = **3** ✓

All pass support. Frequent 1-itemsets $L_1 = \{$\{data mining\}, \{web mining\}, \{query log\}\}$.

**Step 2 – Candidate 2-itemsets $C_2$:**

Generate all pairs:
- {data mining, web mining} → $S_1, S_4$ = **2** ✓
- {data mining, query log} → $S_1, S_2$ = **2** ✓
- {web mining, query log} → $S_1, S_3$ = **2** ✓

All pass support. Frequent 2-itemsets $L_2$ contains all three pairs.

**Step 3 – Candidate 3-itemset $C_3$:**

Only one candidate: {data mining, web mining, query log} → appears only in $S_1$ = **1 < 2**. Rejected.

**Final Frequent Pairs:**

$$
L_2 = \{\{\text{data mining}, \text{web mining}\},\; \{\text{data mining}, \text{query log}\},\; \{\text{web mining}, \text{query log}\}\}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently forget to multiply support percentage by total sessions to get the *support count threshold*. Always show this arithmetic conversion step.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2025 Model]`

**(a)** What are the different types of query intent according to Broder's taxonomy? Explain each with an example. How can query logs be used to classify queries into these intent categories? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Broder's Taxonomy of Query Intent (2002):**

1. **Navigational Intent:** The user intends to reach a *specific website* or web page. The query is essentially the name of the destination. Example: "facebook login", "ktu student portal", "gmail inbox". Click behaviour is highly concentrated – almost all clicks go to a single URL.

2. **Informational Intent:** The user wants to *acquire information* about a topic, with no specific destination in mind. Example: "what is web mining", "symptoms of dengue", "history of india". Click distribution is spread across many authoritative URLs.

3. **Transactional Intent:** The user wants to *perform a transaction* such as buying, downloading, or booking. Example: "buy iphone 15", "download python", "book flight to dubai". Click distribution is concentrated on e-commerce or service portals.

**Using Query Logs for Intent Classification:**

The following log-derived features are used to train a classifier (SVM or Naïve Bayes):

- **Click count distribution** – navigational queries show single-URL dominance.
- **Session length** – informational queries generate longer sessions (multiple reformulations).
- **Dwell time** – transactional queries have shorter dwell time (just confirm and exit).
- **POS tagging of query** – transactional queries contain verbs like *buy*, *download*, *book*.
- **URL category of clicked results** – informational queries land on Wikipedia/educational sites.

The training data is manually labelled, and the classifier learns to predict intent for new queries.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write only the three category names and forget to list *log-derived features used for classification*. Listing features is mandatory for the full 7 marks.

---

**(b)** Consider a query log with the following rules mined from sessions. Compute Support, Confidence and Lift for each rule. State which rule should be used by a recommender system. **(7 marks)**

**Given sessions (same as Section 3.1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \{\text{``web mining''}, \text{``query log mining''}, \text{``data preprocessing''}\} \\
S_2 &= \{\text{``web mining''}, \text{``query log mining''}\} \\
S_3 &= \{\text{``machine learning''}, \text{``data preprocessing''}\} \\
S_4 &= \{\text{``web mining''}, \text{``data preprocessing''}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rule A:** web mining $\Rightarrow$ query log mining
**Rule B:** web mining $\Rightarrow$ data preprocessing
**Rule C:** machine learning $\Rightarrow$ data preprocessing

**Total sessions = 4.**

### Solution:

**Rule A: web mining $\Rightarrow$ query log mining**

- Sessions containing both: $S_1, S_2$ → count = 2
- Sessions containing web mining alone: $S_1, S_2, S_4$ → count = 3

$$
\text{Sup} = \dfrac{2}{4} = 0.50
$$

$$
\text{Conf} = \dfrac{2}{3} = 0.667
$$

$$
\text{Lift} = \dfrac{0.667}{\text{Sup}(\text{query log mining})}
$$

Support of query log mining alone = $\dfrac{2}{4} = 0.50$.

$$
\text{Lift}_A = \dfrac{0.667}{0.50} = 1.333
$$

Since Lift $> 1$, positive correlation.

**Rule B: web mining $\Rightarrow$ data preprocessing**

- Both: $S_1, S_4$ → 2 sessions
- web mining alone: 3 sessions

$$
\text{Sup} = 0.50,\quad \text{Conf} = \dfrac{2}{3} = 0.667
$$

Support of data preprocessing = $\dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75$.

$$
\text{Lift}_B = \dfrac{0.667}{0.75} = 0.889
$$

Since Lift $< 1$, **negative correlation** → reject.

**Rule C: machine learning $\Rightarrow$ data preprocessing**

- Both: $S_3$ → 1 session
- machine learning alone: 1 session

$$
\text{Sup} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25,\quad \text{Conf} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1.00
$$

Support of data preprocessing = 0.75.

$$
\text{Lift}_C = \dfrac{1.00}{0.75} = 1.333
$$

Lift $> 1$, but **support is too low** (only 25 %) to be statistically reliable.

### Recommendation Decision:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule A (web mining $\Rightarrow$ query log mining)** is the best recommendation: highest lift (1.333), good support (0.50), and reasonable confidence (0.667).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often pick the rule with the highest confidence without checking **Lift**. Confidence alone is misleading because it ignores the baseline popularity of the consequent. Always report all three metrics.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this and revise 5 minutes before exam):**

- [ ] **Definition:** Query log mining extracts patterns from search-query history records.
- [ ] **Log fields:** User ID, Query string, Timestamp, Session ID, Clicked URL, Click rank, Dwell time, Reformulation chain.
- [ ] **Three log sources:** Client toolbar, proxy, server-side.
- [ ] **Pipeline:** Collection → Preprocessing → Session ID → Pattern Discovery → Application.
- [ ] **30-minute rule** is the default session boundary.
- [ ] **Broder's three intents:** Navigational, Informational, Transactional.
- [ ] **Key metrics:** Support, Confidence, Lift. Always report all three.
- [ ] **Apriori / FP-Growth** are the canonical algorithms for frequent query pair mining.
- [ ] **Cosine similarity** between TF-IDF vectors is the standard for query clustering.
- [ ] **Markov chains** predict the next query in a session.
- [ ] **CTR and dwell time** are the most informative click-feedback features.
- [ ] **Privacy:** Always anonymize (hash) user IDs before mining.
- [ ] **Lift interpretation:** $>1$ positive, $=1$ independent, $<1$ negative correlation.
- [ ] **Applications:** AutoComplete, Recommendations, Personalized Ranking, Trending Detection, User Profiling.
- [ ] **Examiners love:** Block diagrams, Apriori step tables, and Lift interpretation.
